topics.title: Functional Units - Basic Operational Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Functional Units - Basic Operational Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Computer Organization & Architecture — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Architecture** refers to the attributes of a system as seen by the programmer — i.e., the conceptual structure and functional behavior of the computer. It deals with **what** the system does (instruction set, addressing modes, data types, I/O mechanisms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer Organization** refers to the operational units and their interconnections that realize the architectural specifications. It deals with **how** the features are implemented (control signals, interfaces, memory technology, bus structures).

A clear distinction:
- **Architecture** is *logical* and remains programmer-visible.
- **Organization** is *physical* and remains transparent to the programmer.

## 1.2 Functional Units of a Computer — Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A digital computer consists of **five functionally independent units**: *Input, Memory, Arithmetic & Logic, Output, and Control*. Together, the ALU + CU form the **CPU**, which is the brain of the system.

| # | Unit | Function |
|---|------|----------|
| 1 | **Input Unit** | Accepts coded data & instructions from external environment (keyboard, mouse, scanner) |
| 2 | **Memory Unit** | Stores programs, data, and intermediate results (Primary RAM, ROM; Secondary HDD/SSD) |
| 3 | **Arithmetic & Logic Unit (ALU)** | Performs arithmetic (+,−,×,÷) and logical (AND, OR, NOT, XOR) operations |
| 4 | **Output Unit** | Returns processed results to the outside world (monitor, printer) |
| 5 | **Control Unit (CU)** | Directs & coordinates all units; fetches, decodes, and generates control signals |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen Model 🍳

Imagine a busy **restaurant kitchen**:
- **Input Unit** = Waiter taking the customer's order.
- **Memory Unit** = The recipe book and the counter where ingredients are kept.
- **ALU** = The chef chopping, mixing, and cooking.
- **Output Unit** = The plated dish served back to the customer.
- **Control Unit** = The head chef shouting instructions, timing each dish, coordinating all stations.

The **CPU (ALU + CU)** is the entire kitchen staff working in sync, with **registers** acting as the small trays next to the chef holding currently-needed ingredients (temporary operands).

## 1.4 Basic Operational Concepts

> [!NOTE]
> Every computer is built on the **Stored Program Concept** (proposed by John von Neumann, 1945): *Both instructions and data reside in memory*. The CPU repeatedly **Fetches → Decodes → Executes** instructions.

Key operational principles:
- **Sequential instruction execution** (in a single-core machine) governed by the **Program Counter (PC)**.
- **Registers** provide high-speed temporary storage inside the CPU.
- **Buses** act as shared communication highways (Data, Address, Control).
- **Clock signal** synchronizes every micro-operation; its rate is measured in **Hertz (Hz)** — typically **GHz** in modern processors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *CPU time* with *Elapsed (wall-clock) time*. CPU time excludes I/O wait and other-process interference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Functional Units — Detailed Anatomy

### 2.1.1 Input Unit
- Converts external information (human/machine readable) → binary form acceptable to the computer.
- Example devices: **Keyboard, Mouse, Scanner, MIC, Touchpad**.
- Interface logic: typically a **buffer register** holds the data before transferring to CPU/Memory.

### 2.1.2 Memory Unit
A two-level hierarchy inside the computer:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Memory (Fast, Volatile/Cache-RAM)** — directly accessible by CPU, low capacity.
> - **RAM (Random Access Memory)** — read/write, volatile.
> - **ROM (Read Only Memory)** — non-volatile, stores boot firmware.
> - **Cache** — ultra-fast SRAM between CPU and RAM (L1, L2, L3).
>
> **Secondary Memory (Slow, Non-volatile, High Capacity)** — HDD, SSD, Optical Disks.

### 2.1.3 Arithmetic & Logic Unit (ALU)
- Performs **data processing** operations.
- **Arithmetic:** Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division, Increment, Decrement.
- **Logic:** AND, OR, XOR, NOT, Shift-left, Shift-right, Rotate, Compare.
- Operates on operands fetched from **registers** or **memory**; stores result in an **Accumulator (AC)**.

### 2.1.4 Output Unit
- Reverses the input process — converts binary → human-readable form.
- Examples: **Monitor, Printer, Speakers, LEDs**.

### 2.1.5 Control Unit (CU)
- The "conductor of the orchestra" — it does not process data itself.
- Functions:
  1. **Fetch** the next instruction from memory (address provided by PC).
  2. **Decode** the instruction (opcode + operand).
  3. **Generate control signals** for ALU, registers, memory, and I/O.
  4. **Update the Program Counter** to point to the next instruction.
- Implementation styles: **Hardwired CU** (fast, rigid) vs. **Microprogrammed CU** (flexible, slower).

### 2.1.6 Internal CPU Registers (Mano's Model)
These registers appear frequently in KTU exam questions:

| Register | Symbol | Function |
|----------|:------:|----------|
| Program Counter | **PC** | Holds address of next instruction |
| Address Register | **AR** | Holds memory address being accessed |
| Data Register | **DR** | Buffers data going to/from memory |
| Accumulator | **AC** | Main ALU working register |
| Instruction Register | **IR** | Holds the currently executing instruction |
| Temporary Register | **TR** | Scratchpad for temporary operations |
| Input Register | **INPR** | Receives data from input device |
| Output Register | **OUTR** | Holds data destined for output device |

## 2.2 System Bus Architecture

A **bus** is a shared communication path consisting of multiple parallel lines.

- **Data Bus** — carries actual data (bidirectional). Width = number of bits transferred per cycle (8, 16, 32, **64 bits** typical).
- **Address Bus** — carries source/destination memory address (unidirectional). Width determines **maximum addressable memory**.
- **Control Bus** — carries timing & control signals (Read/$\overline{\text{Write}}$, IRQ, Bus Request/Grant).

> [!NOTE]
> A bus with *n* address lines can address **$2^n$ unique memory locations**.

## 2.3 Performance Terminology

- **Clock Cycle Time (T)** = $1/f$ where *f* is clock rate in Hz.
- **Clock Rate (f)** = cycles per second (e.g., **3.0 GHz** = $3 \times 10^9$ cycles/sec).
- **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** = average cycles needed per instruction.
- **Instruction Count (IC)** = total instructions in a program.
- **CPU Execution Time** = time the CPU spends on a specific program.
- **Throughput** = number of tasks/results produced per unit time.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $T_{\text{cycle}} = 1/f$ | Clock cycle time | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | CPU execution time | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{\text{CPU}} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$ | Alternate form | seconds |
| 4 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{IC}{T_{\text{CPU}} \times 10^6} = \dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions / Second | $10^6$ inst/s |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{T_{\text{old}}}{T_{\text{new}}}$ | Speedup factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + f/k}$ | **Amdahl's Law** — speedup with fraction $f$ improved by factor $k$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{\text{Tasks}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Task completion rate | tasks/sec |
| 8 | $N_{\text{addr}} = 2^{n_{\text{lines}}}$ | Addressable locations with $n$ address lines | locations |
| 9 | $T_{\text{bus}} = \dfrac{\text{Data bytes}}{\text{Bus width (bytes)}} \times T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Bus transfer time | seconds |
| 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{\text{useful}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Processor utilisation / efficiency | percent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Performance Equation** $T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times CPI \times T$ is the single most-tested formula across all KTU examinations on this module. Master it.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPU designers** minimise CPI via pipelining and parallelism.
- **Compiler engineers** reduce IC via instruction-level optimisations.
- **System architects** lower $T$ via smaller CMOS nodes (e.g., 5 nm, 3 nm).
- **Amdahl's Law** guides GPU offloading decisions in **HPC clusters**.
- Bus-width choice affects the cost-performance trade-off in **embedded SoCs** (ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CPU Performance Equation

We start from the most basic unit of measurement — one **clock cycle**.

**Step 1 — Time per cycle.**
A clock of frequency $f$ ticks $f$ times every second. Therefore, the time for one complete cycle is:

$$
T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{f}
$$

The reasoning is: if the clock ticks $f$ times per second, each tick consumes a $1/f$ slice of that second.

**Step 2 — Time per instruction.**
A single instruction consumes, on average, **CPI** clock cycles. Hence:

$$
T_{\text{inst}} = CPI \times T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{CPI}{f}
$$

Multiplying by CPI scales the per-cycle time by the average number of cycles each instruction needs.

**Step 3 — Time for the full program.**
If the program contains $IC$ instructions, total CPU time is the sum over all instructions:

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times T_{\text{inst}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting back.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f}
$$

This is the **fundamental CPU performance equation** used in every performance comparison.

## 3.2 Derivation of MIPS Rating

MIPS measures instruction throughput in **millions of instructions per second**.

**Step 1 — Start from definition.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{\text{Instruction Count (IC)}}{T_{\text{CPU}} \times 10^6}
$$

The $10^6$ converts raw instructions-per-second into the "millions" scale.

**Step 2 — Substitute $T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times CPI / f$.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{IC}{\left(\dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}\right) \times 10^6}
$$

The $IC$ in the numerator and denominator cancel.

**Step 3 — Simplify.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}
$$

This elegant form is what KTU examiners expect you to write.

## 3.3 Derivation of Amdahl's Law

Suppose a fraction $f$ of total execution time benefits from an enhancement of speedup factor $k$, while the remaining fraction $(1-f)$ is unchanged.

**Step 1 — Old execution time.**
Without enhancement, let the total execution time be $T$. The improved portion takes time $f \cdot T$, and the unchanged portion takes $(1-f) \cdot T$.

**Step 2 — New improved time.**
The improved portion becomes $\dfrac{f \cdot T}{k}$; the unchanged portion is still $(1-f) \cdot T$. So:

$$
T_{\text{new}} = (1-f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $T$.**

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T \left[ (1-f) + \frac{f}{k} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Define speedup $S = T / T_{\text{new}}$.**

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

This is **Amdahl's Law** — used to bound the maximum theoretical improvement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $k \to \infty$, $S \to 1/(1-f)$. Even infinite improvement on one part cannot overcome a serial bottleneck. This is the **"Law of Diminishing Returns"** central to parallel computing.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Performance Equation

**Problem.** A processor runs at **2.5 GHz**. A benchmark program contains $200 \times 10^6$ instructions with an average CPI of **4**. Find: (a) CPU execution time, (b) MIPS rating.

**Part (a) — CPU Time.**

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f} = \frac{200 \times 10^6 \times 4}{2.5 \times 10^9}
$$

Multiplying numerator: $200 \times 4 = 800$, so $800 \times 10^6$ cycles.

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{800 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^9} = \frac{800}{2500} = 0.32 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Part (b) — MIPS.**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^6} = 625 \text{ MIPS}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #2 — Amdahl's Law

**Problem.** A program executes in **100 seconds** on a serial processor. **30%** of the time is spent on a function that can be parallelised and made **10 times faster**. Compute overall speedup.

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.** $f = 0.30$, $k = 10$, $T = 100$ s.

**Step 2 — Apply Amdahl's Law.**

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.30) + \dfrac{0.30}{10}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.03} = \frac{1}{0.73}
$$

$$
S \approx 1.370
$$

**Step 3 — New execution time.**

$$
T_{\text{new}} = \frac{100}{1.370} \approx 73.0 \text{ seconds}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even a 10× boost on 30% of the code yields only **~37%** total speedup. This explains why KTU and real-world architects push for parallelism across the *entire* program, not just hot-spots.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Problem Type | Inputs Given | Outputs Required | Governing Formula |
|--------------|--------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Basic CPU time | $IC$, $CPI$, $f$ | $T_{\text{CPU}}$ | $T = IC \times CPI / f$ |
| MIPS from specs | $f$, $CPI$ | MIPS | $f / (CPI \times 10^6)$ |
| Speedup analysis | $f$, $k$ | Overall $S$ | $1/[(1-f) + f/k]$ |
| Bus addressability | $n$ address lines | Max memory | $2^n$ |
| Bus transfer | Bytes, bus width, $T_{\text{cycle}}$ | Transfer time | Bytes/(width) × T |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Basic Computer Functional Units (Mano's Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT_AREA[INPUT SIDE]
        in_dev[INPUT DEVICE] --> inpr[INPR INPUT REGISTER]
    end

    subgraph CORE[CPU CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT]
        pc[PC PROGRAM COUNTER] --> ar[AR ADDRESS REGISTER]
        ar --> mem_bus[MEMORY BUS]
        mem_bus --> dr[DR DATA REGISTER]
        dr --> ir[IR INSTRUCTION REGISTER]
        ir --> dec[DECODER]
        dec --> cu[CU CONTROL UNIT]
        cu --> alu[ALU ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT]
        alu --> ac[AC ACCUMULATOR]
        cu --> tr[TR TEMPORARY REGISTER]
        cu --> pc_upd[UPDATES PC]
    end

    subgraph MEMORY_AREA[MAIN MEMORY RAM ROM]
        ram[RAM PRIMARY] --- rom[ROM BOOT]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_AREA[OUTPUT SIDE]
        outr[OUTR OUTPUT REGISTER] --> out_dev[OUTPUT DEVICE]
    end

    inpr --> cu
    ac --> outr
    mem_bus <--> ram
    rom --> pc
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Five Independent Functional Units

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYS[COMPUTER SYSTEM OVERVIEW]
        iu[INPUT UNIT KEYBOARD SCANNER MOUSE]
        mu[MEMORY UNIT RAM ROM CACHE]
        ou[OUTPUT UNIT MONITOR PRINTER]
    end

    subgraph CPU_BOX[CPU]
        alu_block[ALU]
        cu_block[CONTROL UNIT]
        reg_block[REGISTER FILE PC IR AC DR AR TR]
    end

    iu -->|data bus| reg_block
    reg_block <-->|address and data bus| mu
    alu_block <--> reg_block
    cu_block -.->|control signals| iu
    cu_block -.->|control signals| mu
    cu_block -.->|control signals| alu_block
    cu_block -.->|control signals| ou
    reg_block -->|results| ou
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — System Bus Interconnection (3-Bus Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERIPHERALS[IO AND MEMORY]
        mem[MEMORY]
        io[IO DEVICES]
    end

    subgraph CPU_INTERIOR[CPU]
        reg[INTERNAL REGISTERS]
    end

    DB[(DATA BUS 64 BIT)]
    AB[(ADDRESS BUS 32 BIT)]
    CB[(CONTROL BUS RD WR BUSREQ]

    mem --- DB
    mem --- AB
    mem --- CB
    io --- DB
    io --- AB
    io --- CB
    reg --- DB
    reg --- AB
    reg --- CB
```

## 4.4 Instruction Execution Flow — Fetch Decode Execute

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([START]) --> fetch1[STEP 1 FETCH INSTRUCTION FROM MEMORY]
    fetch1 --> fetch2[AR HOLDS ADDRESS PC TO AR READ SIGNAL]
    fetch2 --> fetch3[INSTRUCTION LOADED INTO IR]
    fetch3 --> decode1[STEP 2 DECODE INSTRUCTION]
    decode1 --> decode2[CU DECODES OPCODE IDENTIFIES OPERANDS]
    decode2 --> exec1[STEP 3 EXECUTE INSTRUCTION]
    exec1 --> branch{CONTROL FLOW BRANCH OR JUMP}
    branch -->|YES| update_pc[UPDATE PC WITH TARGET ADDRESS]
    branch -->|NO| update_npc[INCREMENT PC NATURALLY]
    update_pc --> result[STORE RESULT IN DESTINATION REGISTER]
    update_npc --> result
    result --> end_node([REPEAT FOR NEXT INSTRUCTION])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **PC → AR → Memory → DR → IR** sequence is the canonical *instruction fetch micro-operation sequence* that KTU examiners love to test. Memorise it in order.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*Define computer architecture. Differentiate between computer architecture and computer organisation with two relevant examples for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Computer Architecture** *(1 mark)* — The set of attributes of a computer as seen by the programmer, i.e., the logical/abstract view of the system including instruction set, addressing modes, data representation, and I/O mechanisms.

**Differences** *(2 marks)*:

| Aspect | Architecture | Organisation |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Concern | *What* the system does | *How* it is implemented |
| Transparency | Visible to programmer | Transparent to programmer |
| Example 1 | Instruction set (RISC vs CISC) | Hardwired vs microprogrammed CU |
| Example 2 | Addressable memory size (32-bit vs 64-bit) | Use of cache, RAM type, pipelining |
| Volatility | Long-term design | Changes with technology |

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
*A processor has a clock rate of **3.2 GHz** and an average CPI of **5** for a benchmark program. Compute the MIPS rating.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall the MIPS formula.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^6} = \frac{3.2 \times 10^3}{5} = 640
$$

**Step 3 — Result.** *(1 mark)*

$$
\boxed{\text{MIPS} = 640 \text{ MIPS}}
$$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Internal Choice Pattern:** *Answer ANY ONE full question. Each question carries 14 marks split as 7 + 7.*

---

### 🔹 QUESTION A

#### Q A (a) **[7 Marks]**
*Explain in detail the basic functional units of a digital computer with a neat block diagram. Describe the role of the Control Unit in coordinating these units.* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted]*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction** *(1 mark)*
A digital computer is an electronic device that accepts data (input), processes it according to a stored program, and produces output. It is functionally partitioned into five independent units.

**2. Description of Units** *(4 marks)*

- **Input Unit:** Accepts raw data and instructions from the user via devices such as a keyboard, mouse, or scanner. Converts human-readable information into binary form. Holds the value in a buffer register (e.g., INPR) before transferring to CPU.

- **Memory Unit:** Stores programs, data, and results. Two-level hierarchy — *primary* (fast, volatile RAM/ROM and cache) and *secondary* (high-capacity HDD/SSD). The CPU interacts with primary memory through MAR and MDR.

- **Arithmetic & Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs all data manipulation. *Arithmetic:* add, subtract, multiply, divide, increment, decrement. *Logic:* AND, OR, XOR, NOT, shifts, rotates, comparisons. Operands are fetched from registers (typically AC and DR), and the result is stored back in AC.

- **Output Unit:** Converts binary results into human-perceivable form via monitor, printer, or speakers. The CPU writes the data into an output register (OUTR), which feeds the output device.

- **Control Unit (CU):** Acts as the central coordinator. Fetches the instruction from memory, decodes the opcode, generates timing and control signals for every unit, and updates the Program Counter. It does not process data itself — it *directs* the flow.

**3. Block Diagram** *(1 mark)*
[Refer to Section 4.2 block diagram of CPU + Input/Output/Memory]

**4. Role of the Control Unit in Detail** *(1 mark)*
The CU operates the **Fetch–Decode–Execute** cycle:
- **Fetch:** $AR \leftarrow PC$; $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$; $IR \leftarrow DR$.
- **Decode:** Examine opcode bits in IR; identify operand-fetch mechanism.
- **Execute:** Generate signals like *Read/Write* for memory, *Add/Sub* for ALU, *Shift* for registers.

---

#### Q A (b) **[7 Marks]**
*A program contains $500 \times 10^6$ instructions. The processor clock rate is **2.0 GHz**, and the average CPI for this program is **3.5**. Compute: (i) CPU execution time, (ii) MIPS rating, (iii) If the program is to run in half the time, by what factor must the CPI be reduced (assuming IC and clock remain constant)?* **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CPU Execution Time** *(2 marks)*

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{IC \times CPI}{f} = \frac{500 \times 10^6 \times 3.5}{2.0 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{\text{CPU}} = \frac{1750 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^9} = 0.875 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(ii) MIPS Rating** *(2 marks)*

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f}{CPI \times 10^6} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^9}{3.5 \times 10^6} \approx 571.43 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**(iii) CPI Reduction for Half Time** *(3 marks)*

Let new CPI be $CPI_{\text{new}}$. For half the time:

$$
\frac{IC \times CPI_{\text{new}}}{f} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{IC \times CPI}{f}
$$

Cancelling common terms $IC$ and $f$:

$$
CPI_{\text{new}} = \frac{CPI}{2} = \frac{3.5}{2} = 1.75
$$

**Reduction factor:**

$$
\text{Factor} = \frac{CPI}{CPI_{\text{new}}} = \frac{3.5}{1.75} = 2
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CPI must be reduced by a factor of } 2 \ (\text{i.e., } CPI_{\text{new}} = 1.75)}
$$

---

### 🔹 QUESTION B (Alternative)

#### Q B (a) **[7 Marks]**
*Explain the **Von Neumann / Stored Program Concept** with a neat diagram. List any four key features of this architecture and state one major limitation.* **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition** *(1 mark)*
The **Stored Program Concept** (proposed by John von Neumann, 1945) states that *both instructions and data of a program are stored in the same memory unit* and accessed by the CPU in the same way.

**2. Architecture Description** *(3 marks)*

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| Memory | Holds both instructions and data (single address space) |
| CU | Fetches instructions sequentially, decodes & controls |
| ALU | Performs arithmetic and logic operations |
| I/O | Mediates between user and memory |
| PC | Tracks location of the next instruction |

The CPU repeatedly:
1. Fetches the instruction pointed to by PC.
2. Decodes it.
3. Executes the operation.
4. Updates PC (increment or branch).

**3. Diagram** *(1 mark)*
[Refer to Section 4.4 instruction execution flow diagram]

**4. Key Features** *(1 mark — any four)*
- Single memory for instructions and data.
- Sequential instruction execution.
- Central processing unit with ALU + CU.
- Data and instructions share the same bus (a bottleneck).
- Program can modify itself (since it is in writable memory).

**5. Limitation** *(1 mark)*
**Von Neumann Bottleneck** — Both instructions and data share the same bus, so the CPU spends time waiting for memory access, severely limiting throughput. Modern computers mitigate this with *Harvard architecture* (separate instruction & data caches).

---

#### Q B (b) **[7 Marks]**
*State **Amdahl's Law**. A computer program takes **80 seconds** to run. **25%** of the time is spent in a section that can be made **8 times faster** with an optimisation. Calculate the overall speedup and the new execution time. Comment on the result.* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Statement of Amdahl's Law** *(2 marks)*

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}
$$

where:
- $S$ = overall speedup,
- $f$ = fraction of execution time affected by the enhancement,
- $k$ = speedup factor of the enhanced portion.

**2. Identification of Parameters** *(1 mark)*

$$
f = 0.25, \quad k = 8, \quad T_{\text{old}} = 80 \text{ s}
$$

**3. Computation of Speedup** *(2 marks)*

$$
S = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.25) + \dfrac{0.25}{8}} = \frac{1}{0.75 + 0.03125}
$$

$$
S = \frac{1}{0.78125} = 1.28
$$

**4. New Execution Time** *(1 mark)*

$$
T_{\text{new}} = \frac{T_{\text{old}}}{S} = \frac{80}{1.28} = 62.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

**5. Comment** *(1 mark)*
Even an **8× boost on 25%** of the program yields only a **1.28×** overall speedup — a 28% reduction in execution time. This demonstrates Amdahl's central insight: *system speedup is limited by the unoptimised (serial) portion*. The remaining 75% of the program must also be accelerated to gain meaningful wall-clock improvements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing units:** MIPS is *millions* of instructions per second. Forgetting the $10^6$ factor is an instant zero.
> 2. **Mismatched Amdahl's Law denominator:** Students sometimes write $\frac{f \cdot k}{1}$ or $(f + k)$; the correct form is $1 / [(1-f) + f/k]$.
> 3. **Missing the PC update:** In fetch-decode-execute diagrams, students forget to show PC being incremented or loaded with a branch target — KTU deducts 1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing CPU time with elapsed time:** Wall-clock time includes I/O wait, OS overhead, and other processes. Always specify **CPU execution time** unless told otherwise.
> 5. **No diagrams in long answers:** A block diagram is *mandatory* in any 7-mark question about computer organisation. Marks are reserved for it.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Five functional units:** Input, Memory, ALU, Output, Control (CU + ALU = CPU).
- **Stored Program Concept** (Von Neumann, 1945) — *single memory* holds both instructions and data.
- **Mano's essential registers** — PC, AR, DR, AC, IR, TR, INPR, OUTR (memorise symbols & roles).
- **Three buses** — Data (bidirectional), Address (unidirectional), Control (timing/handshake).
- **CPU Performance Equation:** $T_{\text{CPU}} = IC \times CPI \times T_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{IC \times CPI}{f}$.
- **MIPS formula:** $\dfrac{f}{CPI \times 10^6}$ — *not* $\dfrac{IC}{T}$.
- **Amdahl's Law:** $S = 1/[(1-f) + f/k]$; bound on speedup dictated by serial fraction $(1-f)$.
- **Instruction cycle = Fetch + Decode + Execute**, repeated indefinitely.
- **Bus addressability:** $n$ address lines → $2^n$ uniquely addressable memory words.
- **Clock cycle time** is the smallest unit of work; modern CPUs operate in the **GHz** range.
- **CPI** is *average* over the entire instruction mix; RISC aims for CPI = 1, CISC may have CPI > 1.
- **CPU time ≠ Elapsed time** — the former excludes I/O wait and OS interference.
- **Speedup limit:** Even infinite $k$ yields $S_{\max} = 1/(1-f)$.
- **Bus width** affects per-transfer throughput; doubling width halves the bus transfer time for a fixed payload.
- **The Von Neumann bottleneck** is the central architectural weakness motivating *Harvard* and *modified Harvard* designs (e.g., modern x86 with separate L1 I-cache and D-cache).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bus Structures - Bus Operations - Arbitration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bus Structures - Bus Operations - Arbitration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Bus Structure — Formal Definition

In the architecture of a computer system, a **bus** is defined as a **shared communication pathway** that consists of a set of common lines (wires/traces) through which data, addresses, and control signals are transmitted between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Main Memory**, and **Input/Output (I/O) devices**. A *bus structure* refers to the **topological arrangement** of these lines, the way devices are physically and logically interconnected, and the **protocols** that govern signal exchange.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the bus system is broadly classified into three functional groups:

| Bus Type | Function | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus** | Carries the memory/I/O location address | **Unidirectional** (CPU → Memory/I/O) |
| **Data Bus** | Carries the actual data payload | **Bidirectional** |
| **Control Bus** | Carries command & timing signals (Read/Write, IRQ, Clock) | Mixed (Bidirectional/Uni) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The aggregate of Address, Data, and Control lines is collectively called the **System Bus** (also called the *Internal Bus* or *Front-Side Bus*). The bus that connects the system to peripheral devices is called the **I/O Bus** (e.g., PCI, USB, SATA).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Roadway" Model

Imagine a computer as a **bustling city**:

- The **Data Bus** is the **wide multi-lane highway** that physically carries the cars (data).
- The **Address Bus** is the **road sign / GPS system** that tells traffic *where* to go.
- The **Control Bus** is the **traffic police** (signals, lights) that say *when* and *how* traffic should move.
- The **CPU, Memory, and I/O devices** are the **buildings** (origins/destinations) connected by the road network.
- **Bus Arbitration** is the **traffic signal controller** — when two cars want to cross the same intersection at once, the controller decides who goes first.

Just as adding more lanes (wider bus) or a faster speed limit (higher clock frequency) increases city traffic flow, increasing **bus width** or **bus clock frequency** increases the **data transfer rate** of the system.

## 1.3 Types of Bus Architectures

A bus structure can be organized in different ways based on the number of parallel pathways available:

- **Single Bus Structure:** All components (CPU, Memory, I/O) share **one common bus**. Simple and inexpensive, but creates a **bottleneck** because only two devices can communicate at a time.
- **Multiple Bus Structure:** Uses **two or more** parallel buses (e.g., a *Local Bus* for CPU–Memory and a *System Bus* for I/O). Improves throughput by allowing **concurrent operations**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Most modern systems use a **hierarchical bus structure** combining high-speed local buses (e.g., Front-Side Bus) with slower peripheral buses (e.g., PCIe, USB), a design called the **"Northbridge/Southbridge"** or modern **SoC fabric** architecture.

---

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Decomposition of Bus Operations

A bus operation (also called a **bus cycle** or **bus transaction**) is the *complete sequence of events* required to transfer a single unit of data between a **master** (initiator) and a **slave** (responder). It consists of the following structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Arbitration Phase:** When multiple masters request the bus, the *arbiter* grants access to the highest-priority requester. This ensures **mutual exclusion**.
- **Step 2 — Address Phase:** The master places the target memory/I/O address on the **Address Bus** and asserts the appropriate **Control Signal** (e.g., `M/IO#`, `RD#`, `WR#`).
- **Step 3 — Data Phase:** Data is transferred across the **Data Bus** — either master→slave (Write) or slave→master (Read).
- **Step 4 — Termination / Handshake:** The slave acknowledges completion (e.g., `READY#` or `ACK#` signal), and the master releases the bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Why"** behind these steps: Without arbitration, two devices driving the data bus simultaneously would cause **bus contention** (electrical short circuit). Without handshaking, the master might read *stale* data before the slave has placed valid data on the bus.

## 2.2 Synchronous vs. Asynchronous Bus Operations

| Property | Synchronous Bus | Asynchronous Bus |
|---|---|---|
| Timing Reference | **Fixed clock** line | **Handshake signals** (REQ/ACK) |
| Speed | Faster (no waiting) | Slower (overhead of handshaking) |
| Reliability | Sensitive to clock skew | Highly reliable, self-timed |
| Use Case | Internal CPU–Memory | I/O peripherals (USB, I²C) |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bus Bandwidth** (Maximum Data Transfer Rate) | $B = \dfrac{W \times f}{8}$ | Bytes/sec | $W$ = bus width (bits), $f$ = clock frequency (Hz) |
| 2 | **Data Transfer Time** | $T_{transfer} = \dfrac{N}{B}$ | seconds | $N$ = number of bytes to transfer |
| 3 | **Bus Cycle Time** | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | seconds | Time per bus transaction |
| 4 | **Effective Bandwidth** (with wait states) | $B_{eff} = \dfrac{W}{(T_{cycle} + T_{wait}) \times 8}$ | Bytes/sec | $T_{wait}$ = wait-state delay |
| 5 | **Arbitration Latency** | $T_{arb} = (P_{prop} \times N_{devices}) + T_{logic}$ | seconds | $P_{prop}$ = propagation delay per stage |
| 6 | **Number of Bus Cycles for N words** | $C = \lceil N / W_{word} \rceil$ | cycles | $W_{word}$ = words per bus width |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The bus bandwidth formula is critical when designing **real-time embedded systems**, **memory controllers**, and **high-performance computing (HPC) interconnects** (e.g., InfiniBand, NVLink) where data throughput is a primary design constraint.

## 2.4 Bus Arbitration — Deep Concept

**Bus arbitration** is the process of resolving **contention** when two or more devices (bus masters) request control of the bus simultaneously. Without arbitration, **data corruption** and **electrical damage** can occur.

### Arbitration Schemes (KTU High-Yield):

- **Daisy-Chain Arbitration (Priority by Physical Position):** Devices are connected in series. The bus grant signal propagates through a chain. The *closest* device to the arbiter wins. **Disadvantage:** Fixed priority, single point of failure.
- **Centralized Parallel Arbitration:** A dedicated **arbiter chip** (e.g., Intel 8259, 8289) collects all request lines and generates a parallel grant based on a priority encoder. **Fastest** but **not scalable**.
- **Distributed Arbitration (Self-Arbitration):** Each device has a unique arbitration ID. All devices simultaneously assert their ID onto dedicated arbitration lines, and a **wired-OR / conflict-detection** circuit resolves the highest ID. **Scalable**, used in **PCI, SCSI, Ethernet**.

---

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Bus Bandwidth from First Principles

Let us derive the maximum theoretical data throughput of a bus system.

**Step 1 — Define Bus Width.**
A bus of width $W$ can carry $W$ bits of data in **one clock cycle**.

**Step 2 — Define Clock Frequency.**
The bus operates at a clock frequency $f$ (cycles per second). Therefore, in 1 second, the bus completes $f$ cycles.

**Step 3 — Total Bits Transferred Per Second.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits per second} &= W \; \text{(bits/cycle)} \times f \; \text{(cycles/sec)} \\
&= W \cdot f \quad \text{bits/second}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert Bits to Bytes (Engineering Standard).**
Since 1 Byte = 8 bits, divide by 8:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B \; (\text{Bytes/sec}) &= \dfrac{W \cdot f}{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Maximum Bus Bandwidth Formula**.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A 32-bit system bus operates at a clock frequency of **200 MHz**. The memory subsystem inserts **2 wait states** per access. Calculate:
(a) Maximum theoretical bandwidth
(b) Effective bandwidth with wait states
(c) Time to transfer a 1 MB file

### Solution:

**Given:** $W = 32$ bits, $f = 200 \text{ MHz} = 200 \times 10^6$ Hz, $T_{wait} = 2 \times T_{cycle}$.

**Step (a) — Maximum Bandwidth:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{max} &= \dfrac{W \times f}{8} \\
&= \dfrac{32 \times 200 \times 10^6}{8} \\
&= 4 \times 200 \times 10^6 \\
&= 800 \times 10^6 \; \text{Bytes/sec} \\
&= 800 \; \text{MB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The bus *could* theoretically move 800 MB every second under ideal conditions.

**Step (b) — Effective Bandwidth:**

Each bus cycle is now extended by 2 wait cycles. So the effective cycle time becomes $T_{eff} = T_{cycle} + 2 \cdot T_{cycle} = 3 \cdot T_{cycle}$.

Effective frequency $f_{eff} = f / 3 = 66.67$ MHz.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{eff} &= \dfrac{W \times f_{eff}}{8} \\
&= \dfrac{32 \times 66.67 \times 10^6}{8} \\
&= 266.67 \; \text{MB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Transfer Time for 1 MB:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{transfer} &= \dfrac{N}{B_{eff}} \\
&= \dfrac{1 \; \text{MB}}{266.67 \; \text{MB/sec}} \\
&= 3.75 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{seconds} \\
&= 3.75 \; \text{ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The 2 wait states reduce performance by **66.67%** (from 800 MB/s to 266.67 MB/s). This shows why KTU examiners love asking: *"What is the performance penalty of wait states?"* — Answer: it scales **inversely** with the number of wait states.

## 3.3 Arbitration Latency Derivation

In a **daisy-chain** of $N$ devices, the grant signal must propagate sequentially through all $N$ stages before reaching the requesting device. The total arbitration latency is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{arb} &= N \cdot t_{prop} + t_{logic} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $t_{prop}$ is the propagation delay per gate stage, and $t_{logic}$ is the arbiter's internal decision time.

**Example:** If $N = 8$ devices, $t_{prop} = 5$ ns, $t_{logic} = 10$ ns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{arb} &= 8 \times 5 + 10 = 50 \; \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Arbitration Methods

| Criterion | Daisy-Chain | Centralized Parallel | Distributed (Self-Arb.) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Priority Flexibility** | Fixed (positional) | Programmable | Dynamic (by ID) |
| **Scalability** | Poor (linear latency) | Limited (~16 lines) | Excellent |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Low (single chain) | Medium (arbiter fails) | High (no single point) |
| **Speed** | Slowest | Fastest | Medium |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High (more lines) |
| **Example Use** | Old 8086 systems | Intel 8289 / PCI | Multi-processor SoCs |

---

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 System Bus Interconnection — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU - Master Device]
    MEM[Main Memory - RAM/ROM]
    IO1[I/O Device 1 - Keyboard]
    IO2[I/O Device 2 - Disk]
    IO3[I/O Device 3 - Display]

    subgraph SYS_BUS[SYSTEM BUS - 3 Sub-Buses]
        AB[Address Bus - Unidirectional]
        DB[Data Bus - Bidirectional]
        CB[Control Bus - Read/Write/IRQ/Clock]
    end

    CPU --- AB
    CPU --- DB
    CPU --- CB
    MEM --- AB
    MEM --- DB
    MEM --- CB
    IO1 --- AB
    IO1 --- DB
    IO1 --- CB
    IO2 --- AB
    IO2 --- DB
    IO2 --- CB
    IO3 --- AB
    IO3 --- DB
    IO3 --- CB
```

## 4.2 Bus Operation — Read Transaction Timing (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Arbitration - Arbiter grants bus to Master] --> B[Step 2: Master places Address on Address Bus]
    B --> C[Step 3: Master asserts RD - Read Control Signal]
    C --> D[Step 4: Slave decodes address and prepares data]
    D --> E[Step 5: Slave places data on Data Bus]
    E --> F[Step 6: Slave asserts READY - ACK Signal]
    F --> G[Step 7: Master latches data and releases bus]
```

## 4.3 Centralized Parallel Arbitration — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Master 1 - Request Line R1]
    M2[Master 2 - Request Line R2]
    M3[Master 3 - Request Line R3]
    M4[Master 4 - Request Line R4]

    ARB[Central Arbiter - Priority Encoder]

    G1[Grant Line G1]
    G2[Grant Line G2]
    G3[Grant Line G3]
    G4[Grant Line G4]

    M1 --> ARB
    M2 --> ARB
    M3 --> ARB
    M4 --> ARB
    ARB --> G1
    ARB --> G2
    ARB --> G3
    ARB --> G4
```

## 4.4 Daisy-Chain Arbitration — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Arbiter - Source of Grant] --> B[Device 1 - Highest Priority]
    B --> C{Is Device 1 Requesting?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Device 1 Gets Bus - Grant Stops]
    C -- No --> E[Device 2 - Next Priority]
    E --> F{Is Device 2 Requesting?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Device 2 Gets Bus - Grant Stops]
    F -- No --> H[Device 3 - Lowest Priority]
    H --> I{Is Device 3 Requesting?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Device 3 Gets Bus]
    I -- No --> K[No Grant Issued - Idle]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Explain the three main types of buses in a computer system. How does the address bus differ from the data bus in terms of directionality and function?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A computer system uses three fundamental buses grouped under the **System Bus**:

1. **Address Bus (1 Mark):** Carries the **memory or I/O location address** from the CPU to memory or peripherals. It is **unidirectional** because only the CPU (master) generates addresses. Its width determines the **maximum addressable memory** (e.g., 32-bit address bus → $2^{32} = 4$ GB addressable).

2. **Data Bus (1 Mark):** Carries the **actual data** being read or written. It is **bidirectional** since data can flow from CPU→Memory (Write) or Memory→CPU (Read). Its width determines the **data transfer granularity** (e.g., 64-bit bus transfers 8 bytes per cycle).

3. **Control Bus (1 Mark):** Carries **timing and command signals** such as Read (`RD#`), Write (`WR#`), Memory/IO (`M/IO#`), Bus Request (`BR#`), Bus Grant (`BG#`), Clock, Reset, and Interrupt Request (`INTR`). It is a **mixed-direction** bus.

**Key Difference:** The address bus is **unidirectional and CPU-driven** (no other device places addresses on it), while the data bus is **bidirectional** since both masters and slaves can drive it during different phases of a transaction.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Why is bus arbitration necessary in a multi-master system? List any two arbitration methods.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Necessity of Bus Arbitration (2 Marks):**
In a **multi-master system**, more than one device (e.g., CPU, DMA controller, multiple processor cores) can act as a **bus master** and request control of the shared bus simultaneously. If two masters drive the data bus at the same instant, a condition called **bus contention** occurs, which causes:
- Electrical short circuits (high current draw)
- Data corruption (unpredictable voltage levels on shared lines)
- System instability or hardware damage

**Bus arbitration** is a hardware mechanism that ensures **mutual exclusion** — only one master uses the bus at any given time. An **arbiter** (a dedicated logic circuit) evaluates simultaneous requests and grants the bus to the **highest-priority** requester based on a fixed or programmable policy.

**Two Arbitration Methods (1 Mark):**
1. **Daisy-Chain Arbitration** — Priority is fixed by physical position in the chain.
2. **Centralized Parallel Arbitration** — A dedicated arbiter chip uses a priority encoder to grant access in parallel.

*(Alternative: Distributed Arbitration using self-arbitration IDs.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1 Pattern]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **single bus structure** of a computer system. Discuss its advantages and limitations. How does a **multiple bus structure** overcome these limitations?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 64-bit system bus operates at **400 MHz** clock frequency. Calculate:
(i) The maximum data transfer rate in MB/s.
(ii) The time required to transfer a **256 KB** block of data (in microseconds).
(iii) The percentage reduction in throughput if the memory introduces **3 wait states** per access.

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Single vs. Multiple Bus Structure (7 Marks)

**Single Bus Structure (3 Marks):**

In a **single bus structure**, all major components of the computer system — the **CPU, Main Memory, and all I/O devices** — are connected to **one common shared bus**. This bus carries address, data, and control signals. The CPU acts as the master, while memory and I/O devices act as slaves.

**Block Diagram (drawn in exam):**

```
+------+      +--------+      +------+
| CPU  |<---->|        |<---->| I/O  |
+------+      | SYSTEM |      |  D1  |
+------+      |  BUS   |      +------+
|Memory|<---->|        |<---->| I/O  |
+------+      +--------+      |  D2  |
                              +------+
```

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
1. **Simple and inexpensive** — only one set of bus lines to design and route on the PCB.
2. **Easy to add new devices** — just connect them to the existing bus.
3. **Flexible architecture** — all devices use the same communication protocol.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
1. **Bus bottleneck** — only one transaction can occur at a time; performance degrades as devices are added.
2. **Limited speed** — slowest device dictates the bus clock.
3. **Bus contention risk** — multiple masters may simultaneously request the bus.

**Multiple Bus Structure (1 Mark — How it overcomes limitations):**
A multiple bus structure uses **two or more parallel buses** (e.g., a high-speed *Local Bus* between CPU and memory, and a slower *Expansion Bus* for I/O). This **decouples** high-speed CPU–Memory traffic from slower I/O traffic, allowing **concurrent operations** and eliminating the bottleneck of the single-bus design. Examples include the classic **Northbridge/Southbridge** architecture used in modern PCs.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- Bus width $W = 64$ bits
- Clock frequency $f = 400 \text{ MHz} = 400 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Data block size $N = 256 \text{ KB} = 256 \times 1024 \text{ Bytes} = 262144 \text{ Bytes}$
- Wait states $W_s = 3$

**(i) Maximum Data Transfer Rate (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{max} &= \dfrac{W \times f}{8} \\
&= \dfrac{64 \times 400 \times 10^6}{8} \\
&= 8 \times 400 \times 10^6 \\
&= 3200 \times 10^6 \; \text{Bytes/sec} \\
&= 3200 \; \text{MB/sec} = 3.2 \; \text{GB/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Transfer Time for 256 KB (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{transfer} &= \dfrac{N}{B_{max}} \\
&= \dfrac{262144 \; \text{Bytes}}{3200 \times 10^6 \; \text{Bytes/sec}} \\
&= 81.92 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{seconds} \\
&= 81.92 \; \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Percentage Reduction with 3 Wait States (2 Marks):**

With 3 wait states, each cycle takes $1 + 3 = 4$ clock periods. Effective frequency $f_{eff} = f/4 = 100$ MHz.

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{eff} &= \dfrac{64 \times 100 \times 10^6}{8} = 800 \; \text{MB/sec} \\
\text{Reduction} &= \dfrac{B_{max} - B_{eff}}{B_{max}} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{3200 - 800}{3200} \times 100 \\
&= 75\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The throughput drops by **75%**, leaving only 25% of peak performance.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **centralized parallel arbitration** and **distributed arbitration** schemes with diagrams. Compare them in terms of speed, scalability, and reliability.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **complete sequence of events** during a **bus read operation** in a synchronous system, clearly distinguishing between the **arbitration phase, address phase, data phase, and termination phase**. What is the role of the `READY#` signal?

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Centralized vs. Distributed Arbitration (7 Marks)

**Centralized Parallel Arbitration (3 Marks):**

A dedicated hardware unit called the **arbiter** (e.g., Intel 8289 Bus Arbiter) is placed between the CPU and all potential bus masters. Each master has a dedicated **Request line** (REQ1, REQ2, …, REQn) going to the arbiter, and the arbiter has corresponding **Grant lines** (GNT1, GNT2, …, GNTn) returning to each master.

The arbiter contains a **priority encoder** that:
1. Samples all request lines simultaneously.
2. Selects the highest-priority active request.
3. Asserts the corresponding grant line.
4. Other masters see their grant line inactive and wait.

**Diagram (from Section 4.3 above).**

**Distributed Arbitration (3 Marks):**

In distributed (or *self-arbitration*), there is **no central arbiter**. Each master has a **unique 4-bit arbitration ID**. When two or more devices request the bus, all requesting devices simultaneously place their IDs onto dedicated arbitration lines. A **wired-OR** circuit compares the IDs — the device with the **highest ID wins**, and all others withdraw. Used in **PCI bus** and **SCSI** chains.

**Comparison (1 Mark):**

| Criterion | Centralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster (parallel decision) | Slower (serial ID comparison) |
| Scalability | Limited by arbiter pin count | Highly scalable |
| Reliability | Arbiter is a single point of failure | No single point of failure |

---

#### Part (b) — Bus Read Operation Sequence (7 Marks)

A **bus read operation** transfers data *from* a slave (memory/I/O) *to* the master (CPU). The transaction proceeds in **four phases**:

**Phase 1 — Arbitration Phase (2 Marks):**
- All masters that need the bus assert their `BR#` (Bus Request) line.
- The arbiter evaluates priorities and asserts `BG#` (Bus Grant) to the winner.
- The winning master proceeds; others de-assert `BR#` and wait.

**Phase 2 — Address Phase (2 Marks):**
- The master places the **target memory address** on the **Address Bus**.
- The master asserts the **Read control signal** (`RD# = 0`, `M/IO#` line set appropriately).
- The master activates `ADS#` (Address Strobe) to signal that valid address is present.

**Phase 3 — Data Phase (1 Mark):**
- The addressed slave decodes the address and retrieves the data.
- The slave places the data on the **Data Bus**.
- The slave prepares to assert `READY#` once data is stable.

**Phase 4 — Termination Phase (2 Marks):**
- The slave asserts **`READY#`** to indicate data is valid and stable on the bus.
- The master **latches** (reads) the data from the Data Bus at the rising edge of the clock.
- The master de-asserts `RD#` and releases the Address Bus.
- The master de-asserts `BR#` (or the arbiter rotates priority).
- The bus becomes free for the next transaction.

**Role of `READY#` Signal:**
The `READY#` (active-low) signal is a **slave-driven handshake** that tells the master that the data is **valid and stable** on the bus. In systems with slow memory, the slave can **de-assert `READY#`** to insert **wait states** — the master extends its cycle duration until `READY#` is asserted. This synchronizes fast CPUs with slow peripherals without data loss.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to label bus directionality** (1–2 marks lost): Students often write "Data Bus carries data" but fail to mention it is **bidirectional**, while the Address Bus is **unidirectional**. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Unit conversion mistakes in bandwidth problems:** Confusing **Mb (megabits) vs MB (megabytes)** costs full marks. Always divide by 8 when the bus width is in bits.
> 3. **Confusing arbitration schemes:** Students frequently mix up **Daisy-Chain** (priority by position) with **Distributed** (priority by ID). Remember: Daisy-Chain uses *serial grant propagation*, Distributed uses *parallel ID comparison*.
> 4. **Not drawing the `READY#` signal timing** in bus cycle diagrams: A common 1-mark loss in 14-mark read/write operation questions.
> 5. **Forgetting wait-state penalty in numericals:** Many students compute only maximum bandwidth and skip the effective bandwidth part, losing 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 1: Bus Systems):**

- **Bus = Shared communication pathway** (Address + Data + Control).
- **Address Bus is UNIDIRECTIONAL**; **Data Bus is BIDIRECTIONAL**; **Control Bus is mixed**.
- **System Bus** = CPU–Memory interconnect; **I/O Bus** = peripheral interconnect.
- **Single bus** = simple but creates a bottleneck; **Multiple bus** = faster, allows concurrency.
- **Bus Operation Phases:** Arbitration → Address → Data → Termination.
- **Synchronous bus** uses a clock; **Asynchronous bus** uses REQ/ACK handshaking.
- **Bus Bandwidth Formula:** $B = \dfrac{W \times f}{8}$ Bytes/sec.
- **Wait states** *reduce* bandwidth linearly: $B_{eff} = B_{max} / (1 + W_s)$.
- **Arbitration** prevents **bus contention** in multi-master systems.
- **Three Arbitration Methods:**
  1. **Daisy-Chain** (serial, fixed priority, low cost, not scalable).
  2. **Centralized Parallel** (arbiter chip, fastest, scalable to ~16 devices).
  3. **Distributed / Self-Arbitration** (ID-based, scalable, no single point of failure — used in PCI, SCSI).
- **`READY#` signal** inserts wait states to synchronize fast masters with slow slaves.
- **Number of bus cycles for N words** = $\lceil N / W_{word} \rceil$.
- **KTU-favorite numerical:** Given bus width, clock frequency, and wait states, compute (i) max bandwidth, (ii) transfer time, (iii) effective bandwidth.
- **Real-world examples to memorize:** PCI bus (parallel arbitration), USB (synchronous + arbitration), DDR Memory bus (synchronous), I²C (asynchronous with arbitration).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Instruction Cycle** (also called the **Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle** or **Machine Cycle**) is the fundamental operational sequence performed by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) to retrieve a single machine-level instruction from main memory, interpret it, and carry out the specified operation. It constitutes the smallest indivisible unit of work that a processor performs and is the bedrock of all program execution in a stored-program computer (Von Neumann architecture).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020)**, the instruction cycle is formally defined as the complete chronological sequence of micro-operations—clocked, register-transfer-level (RTL) operations—that the CPU's datapath performs from the moment a program counter (PC) supplies an address to the moment the next instruction is about to be fetched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Exam-Savvy):** The instruction cycle is a *repeating* sequence comprising the **Fetch Phase**, **Decode Phase**, **Operand Fetch Phase** (if addressing mode demands), **Execute Phase**, and **Store/Write-Back Phase**, terminated (in some implementations) by an **Interrupt Cycle** before the next instruction cycle begins.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Chef's Kitchen Model

Imagine a master chef (the **CPU**) working inside a locked kitchen (the **processor chip**). Outside the kitchen is a massive walk-in refrigerator (**Main Memory / RAM**) holding thousands of recipe cards (**instructions**) and ingredient boxes (**data**). The chef cannot leave the kitchen, so he relies on a tray system (**System Bus**) and a personal notepad (**Registers**).

| Kitchen Element | Computer Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Recipe card in fridge | Machine instruction in RAM | The "what to do" |
| Chef's hand pointing at recipe | **Program Counter (PC)** | Holds address of *next* recipe card |
| Tray to fetch recipe | **Memory Address Register (MAR) + MDR** | Brings card into kitchen |
| Recipe card in chef's hand | **Instruction Register (IR)** | Holds currently-being-executed instruction |
| Chef's brain interpreting recipe | **Control Unit (CU) + Decoder** | Decodes opcode, generates control signals |
| Mixing bowl / oven | **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** | Where the actual cooking (computation) happens |
| Plate served to customer | **Result written back to memory or register** | Output of execution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Lock-in:** Every single program you have ever run—whether a calculator app or a deep-learning training script—is simply the CPU performing *billions* of these tiny instruction cycles one after another. The "speed" of your laptop is essentially how *fast* it can do this loop.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Clock Cycle Time (T):** Typically measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**. Modern CPUs operate in the **GHz** range, meaning T ≈ **0.25 ns** to **1 ns**.
- **Cycles Per Instruction (CPI):** A dimensionless ratio (often between **1 and 5+** for simple CPUs).
- **Instruction Length:** **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits** depending on ISA (e.g., ARM uses 32-bit fixed; x86 uses variable).
- **Bus Width:** **8, 16, 32, 64 bits** standard (matches datapath width).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme (Module 1) explicitly expects students to draw the **complete instruction cycle flowchart**, label the **Von Neumann registers** (PC, AR, IR, DR, AC), and state the **register transfer language (RTL)** micro-operations for each phase.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of the Instruction Cycle — Six Logical Phases

The instruction cycle is not monolithic; it is decomposed into a strict sequence of **micro-operations**, each gated by the master clock. Below is the exhaustive, exam-grade breakdown aligned with the **Mano's Classical CPU Model** (the reference architecture KTU follows).

#### Phase 1: Fetch Instruction (T₀ → T₂)
The *only* mandatory phase in every instruction cycle. Always begins with the PC.

1. **Address Transfer:** PC contents are moved to the **Memory Address Register (AR)**.
2. **Memory Read:** The control unit issues a **READ** signal to memory; the addressed instruction is placed on the bus.
3. **Data Transfer:** The bus contents are latched into the **Memory Data Register (DR/MDR)**.
4. **Instruction Transfer:** The DR contents are copied into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
5. **PC Increment:** The PC is incremented to point to the *next* sequential instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PC increments here?** Because after fetch, the *current* instruction is safely in IR. Failing to increment PC would create an infinite loop executing the *same* instruction—classic 8051 students will recognize this as a "stuck on one line" bug.

#### Phase 2: Decode (T₃)
- The **opcode** field of the IR is routed to the **Control Unit / Instruction Decoder**.
- The decoder activates exactly **one** of N control output lines (one-hot encoding) corresponding to the operation.
- The **addressing-mode bits** are simultaneously decoded to determine if operands need to be fetched.

#### Phase 3: Effective Address Calculation (Conditional)
- Required **only for memory-reference instructions** with indirect or indexed addressing (e.g., `ADD @X` in 8086, or any `LDR` with displacement).
- The **Effective Address (EA)** is computed in a dedicated ALU path, not the main ALU.

#### Phase 4: Fetch Operands (Conditional)
- **Register operands:** Already inside the CPU, fetched in **0 extra memory cycles** from the **Register File**.
- **Memory operands:** One extra memory READ cycle is needed → contents go to DR, then copied to AC (Accumulator).

#### Phase 5: Execute (T₄)
- The ALU performs the operation: `ADD`, `SUB`, `AND`, `OR`, `SHIFT`, `JUMP-check`, etc.
- For branch instructions, the result of the condition test (Zero flag, Sign flag) determines the *next* PC value.

#### Phase 6: Store Result (Conditional)
- For arithmetic/logic instructions: result written back to **Accumulator (AC)** or **destination register**.
- For store instructions: result written to memory via DR → Memory WRITE cycle.
- For branch instructions: PC is loaded with branch target.

### 2.2 Interrupt Cycle — The "Polite Interruption"

After the *current* instruction completes, but *before* the next fetch, the CPU may honor an **interrupt request (IRQ)** from I/O devices.

1. Save current PC (and sometimes status flags) to a known memory location (stack or reserved address).
2. Load PC with the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** entry address from a fixed vector.
3. Resume after ISR via a `RTI` (Return from Interrupt) instruction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Concept:** "Differentiate between instruction cycle and interrupt cycle." Answer: *The interrupt cycle is NOT a part of the instruction cycle—it is a separate cycle that is interleaved between two consecutive instruction cycles. It saves state, services the device, and restores state, allowing the original program to resume seamlessly.*

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Performance Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute-value and conditional notations use `\vert` and `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Clock Cycle Time | $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ | Seconds (s). $f_{clk}$ in **Hz**. |
| 2 | CPU Clock Rate | $f_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk}}$ | **Hz** (often GHz = $10^9$ Hz). |
| 3 | CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI \times T_{clk}$ | Seconds. $N$ = total instruction count. |
| 4 | Alternative Form | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N \times CPI}{f_{clk}}$ | Convenient when $f_{clk}$ is given. |
| 5 | Average CPI | $CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( CPI_i \times \dfrac{N_i}{N} \right)$ | Weighted average over instruction mix. |
| 6 | MIPS Rating | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second. |
| 7 | Total Cycles | $C_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \times N_i)$ | Dimensionless clock cycles. |
| 8 | Instruction Count | $N = \sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i$ | Sum over all instruction classes. |
| 9 | Speedup Formula | $S = \dfrac{T_{old}}{T_{new}}$ | Always dimensionless $\mid S \geq 1 \mid$. |
| 10 | Amdahl's Law | $S_{overall} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{part}}}$ | $f$ = fraction enhanced. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Design:** Compilers use CPI knowledge to schedule high-latency instructions around slow ones.
- **Processor Design (Pipelining):** The instruction cycle is the unit that gets *overlapped* in a pipeline. A 5-stage pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) essentially allows **5 instructions** to be in *different phases* of their cycles simultaneously—boosting throughput without reducing per-instruction time.
- **Performance Engineering:** Datacenter operators tune $f_{clk}$ and $CPI$ (via SIMD/AVX) to optimize the cost-per-instruction metric.
- **Embedded Systems (KTU specialization):** ARM Cortex-M cores have *deterministic* instruction cycles (e.g., `ADD` always takes 1 cycle). This is why RTOS developers can compute worst-case execution time exactly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Fundamental CPU Performance Equation

**Starting Point (raw work):** Suppose a program contains $N$ machine instructions. Each instruction $i$ takes $CPI_i$ clock cycles on average. Each clock cycle lasts $T_{clk}$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Total clock cycles consumed by the program:**

$$
C_{total} = N \times CPI_{avg}
$$

**Explanation:** If every instruction had a uniform CPI, the product would trivially give the total cycles. Because instructions are heterogeneous, we use the *weighted average* CPI.

**Step 2 — Substitute the weighted-average definition:**

$$
CPI_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( CPI_i \times \frac{N_i}{N} \right)
$$

**Explanation:** Here $N_i$ is the count of instructions of class $i$, and $\dfrac{N_i}{N}$ is their *frequency* in the instruction mix.

**Step 3 — Total execution time = total cycles × time per cycle:**

$$
T_{CPU} = C_{total} \times T_{clk}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting back:**

$$
T_{CPU} = N \times \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( CPI_i \times \frac{N_i}{N} \right) \right] \times T_{clk}
$$

**Step 5 — Canceling $N$ from numerator and denominator inside the sum:**

$$
T_{CPU} = T_{clk} \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( CPI_i \times N_i \right)
$$

**Final Canonical Form:**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{CPU} = T_{clk} \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \cdot N_i) = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} (CPI_i \cdot N_i)\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most-asked derivation** in KTU 2024 ESE Module 1. Memorize the *final form*, but in the exam, you **must show Steps 1–4** to score full marks.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical: KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement:** A processor runs at **$2.5 \text{ GHz}$** clock frequency. A benchmark program consists of **$4$ instruction classes** with the following statistics:

| Class | $N_i$ (count) | $CPI_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| A (ALU) | $50{,}000$ | 1 |
| B (Load) | $30{,}000$ | 5 |
| C (Store) | $15{,}000$ | 3 |
| D (Branch) | $5{,}000$ | 2 |

**Compute:** (i) $CPI_{avg}$, (ii) $T_{CPU}$ in seconds, (iii) MIPS rating.

**Step 1 — Total instruction count $N$:**

$$
N = 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 15{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 100{,}000 \text{ instructions}
$$

**Step 2 — Weighted CPI sum:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} (CPI_i \cdot N_i) = (1 \cdot 50{,}000) + (5 \cdot 30{,}000) + (3 \cdot 15{,}000) + (2 \cdot 5{,}000)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluating each product:**

$$
= 50{,}000 + 150{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 255{,}000 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Average CPI:**

$$
CPI_{avg} = \frac{\sum (CPI_i \cdot N_i)}{N} = \frac{255{,}000}{100{,}000} = 2.55
$$

**Step 5 — Clock cycle time:**

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.4 \text{ ns} = 4 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Total CPU time:**

$$
T_{CPU} = N \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} = 100{,}000 \times 2.55 \times 4 \times 10^{-10}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation:**

$$
T_{CPU} = 100{,}000 \times 2.55 \times 4 \times 10^{-10} = 1.02 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 8 — MIPS rating:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{2.55 \times 10^{6}} \approx 980.39 \text{ MIPS}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trivia:** Students often forget to convert GHz to Hz in MIPS calculations. The denominator **must** be in Hz, not GHz. One missed power of $10^9$ = 6 marks lost.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Phase-by-Phase Register Transfer Language (RTL)

This is the **gold-standard table** KTU examiners expect. Memorize this RTL micro-operation set; it forms the backbone of Module 1 questions.

| Phase | Time Tag | Register Transfer Statement | Control Signals Active | Bus Operations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fetch T0** | T0 | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | $PC_{out}, AR_{in}$ | 1 internal xfer |
| **Fetch T1** | T1 | $IR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ | $AR_{out}, Read, DR_{in}, DR_{out}, IR_{in}, +1, PC_{in}$ | 1 memory read |
| **Decode T2** | T2 | $D0...D7 \leftarrow \text{Decode } IR(12\text{-}14)$ | $IR_{out}, D_{in}$ | 1 internal xfer |
| **EA Calc T3** | T3 | $AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11) + \text{or} \; I(AR)$ | $IR_{out}, I, AR_{in}$ (or $AC_{out}$) | 1 ALU op |
| **Operand Fetch T3** | T3 | $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$ | $AR_{out}, Read, DR_{in}$ | 1 memory read |
| **Execute (ADD)** | T4 | $AC \leftarrow AC + DR$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ | $DR_{out}, AC_{in}, Add$ | 1 ALU op |
| **Execute (AND)** | T4 | $AC \leftarrow AC \wedge DR$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ | $DR_{out}, AC_{in}, And$ | 1 ALU op |
| **Execute (LDA)** | T4 | $AC \leftarrow DR$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ | $DR_{out}, AC_{in}$ | 1 internal xfer |
| **Execute (STA)** | T4 | $M[AR] \leftarrow AC$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ | $AR_{out}, AC_{out}, Write$ | 1 memory write |
| **Branch (BUN)** | T4 | $PC \leftarrow AR$, $SC \leftarrow 0$ | $AR_{out}, PC_{in}$ | 1 internal xfer |
| **Branch (BSA)** | T4 | $M[AR] \leftarrow PC, AR \leftarrow AR+1, PC \leftarrow AR, SC \leftarrow 0$ | Multiple | 1 mem write + 1 ALU |
| **Interrupt** | T0* | $AR \leftarrow 0$, $TR \leftarrow PC$ | $INT\_ACK$ | Save state |
| **Interrupt** | T1* | $M[AR] \leftarrow TR$, $PC \leftarrow 0$ | $Write$ | Jump to ISR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decoding the table:** The "Time Tag" column reflects the textbook clock-cycle T0, T1, T2, T3, T4 sequence. The "SC" variable is the **Sequence Counter**—a 2-bit register that tells the control unit which phase the CPU is currently in. When SC = 0, the CPU returns to T0 to begin fetching the next instruction.

### 3.4 Derivation: Pipelined Speedup

**Starting Point:** A non-pipelined processor takes $k$ stages × CPI = $k$ cycles per instruction. A pipelined processor *ideally* completes 1 instruction per cycle after the pipeline is full.

**Step 1 — Non-pipelined time for N instructions:**

$$
T_{np} = N \times k \times T_{clk}
$$

**Step 2 — Pipelined time for N instructions (assuming N >> k):**

$$
T_{p} = (k + N - 1) \times T_{clk}
$$

**Step 3 — Speedup ratio:**

$$
S = \frac{T_{np}}{T_{p}} = \frac{N \times k}{k + N - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — As $N \to \infty$:**

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S = k
$$

**Conclusion:** A $k$-stage pipeline offers a **maximum theoretical speedup of $k$**—a foundational result for Module 5 (Pipelining) but rooted in Module 1 (Instruction Cycle).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Instruction Cycle Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Power ON or Reset]) --> B[Initialize PC to First Instruction Address]
    B --> C[SC = 0 T0 AR gets PC]
    C --> D[SC = 1 T1 IR gets M AR PC gets PC plus 1]
    D --> E{SC = 2 T2 Decode IR opcode bits}
    E --> F[Determine Addressing Mode and Operand Need]
    F --> G{Is Operand in Memory}
    G -->|Yes| H[SC = 3 T3 DR gets M AR Effective Address Calc]
    G -->|No| I[SC = 3 T3 Operand Already in Register]
    H --> J[SC = 3 T4 Execute Operation in ALU]
    I --> J
    J --> K[SC = 0 Store Result Write Back if Needed]
    K --> L{Interrupt Pending}
    L -->|Yes| M[Execute Interrupt Cycle Save PC Jump to ISR]
    L -->|No| C
    M --> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this diagram top-to-bottom following the arrows.** The three orange-ish decision diamonds (`{}`) are *branch points* in the micro-sequence. The dashed line back to `SC=0` represents the loop—the instruction cycle is by definition cyclic.

### 4.2 Von Neumann CPU Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU[Central Processing Unit]
        PC1[Program Counter PC] --> MAR1[Memory Address Register MAR]
        MAR1 --> CTRL[Control Unit CU]
        IR1[Instruction Register IR] --> DEC[Instruction Decoder]
        DEC --> CTRL
        CTRL --> ALU1[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU]
        ACC[Accumulator AC] <--> ALU1
        DR1[Data Register DR MDR] <--> ALU1
        SC1[Sequence Counter SC] --> CTRL
    end
    subgraph MEM[Main Memory RAM]
        CELL[(Addressable Memory Cells 0 to 2 to the power n minus 1)]
    end
    MAR1 <-->|Address Bus n bits| CELL
    DR1 <-->|Data Bus n bits| CELL
    PC1 -.->|Fetched instruction opcode| IR1
    CTRL -->|Read Write signals| CELL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture is called "Von Neumann":** Look at how *both* instructions AND data travel over the *same* bus lines connecting CPU to memory. This single-bus design is what causes the **Von Neumann Bottleneck**—the bus becomes a traffic jam as the CPU demands instructions and data faster than the bus can deliver. Modern CPUs solve this with **separate L1 instruction cache and L1 data cache (Harvard architecture inside the chip).**

### 4.3 Instruction Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Fetch
    Fetch --> Decode: T1 complete SC=1
    Decode --> OperandFetch: Memory reference or indirect
    Decode --> Execute: Register operand or immediate
    OperandFetch --> Execute: Operand in DR
    Execute --> StoreResult: Write back required
    Execute --> Fetch: No write back SC=0
    StoreResult --> InterruptCheck: Result stored SC=0
    InterruptCheck --> InterruptService: IRQ signal high
    InterruptCheck --> Fetch: No interrupt
    InterruptService --> Fetch: ISR complete RTI
    Fetch --> [*]: HALT instruction
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The state diagram above is *exactly* what your Module 1 textbook shows. If the examiner asks "draw the instruction cycle state diagram," replicate this structure node-for-node. Any missing state = loss of 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part A questions expect a **definition + 2 supporting points + a diagram keyword**. Each question is 3 marks; aim for 3–4 crisp sentences.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 1]**
**Define the instruction cycle. List and briefly explain any three phases of the instruction cycle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **instruction cycle** is the complete sequence of operations performed by the CPU to fetch, decode, and execute a single machine instruction, returning the CPU to a state ready to fetch the next instruction.

*Three key phases:*

1. **Fetch Phase (T0–T1):** The CPU copies the Program Counter (PC) into the Memory Address Register (AR), reads the addressed memory location, and loads the instruction into the Instruction Register (IR). The PC is simultaneously incremented to point to the next sequential instruction.

2. **Decode Phase (T2):** The opcode field of the IR is sent to the control unit's instruction decoder, which activates the appropriate control signals and determines the addressing mode and operand locations.

3. **Execute Phase (T4):** The ALU performs the actual computation—arithmetic, logical, shift, or branch decision—based on the decoded control signals. The result is either written to a register, stored in memory, or used to update the PC (in case of a branch).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Differentiate between the instruction cycle and the interrupt cycle. When does the interrupt cycle occur?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Instruction Cycle | Interrupt Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Executes the *current* program instruction | Services an *external* I/O or hardware request |
| **Trigger** | Automatic, sequential (PC increment) | Asynchronous, triggered by `INT` signal |
| **State Saved?** | Not necessarily | Always saves PC (and often status flags) |
| **PC loaded with** | PC + 1 (or branch target) | ISR vector address |
| **Relationship** | Primary cycle | Interleaved *between* two instruction cycles |

The **interrupt cycle** occurs *after* the current instruction cycle completes but *before* the next instruction's fetch phase begins. It allows the CPU to honor urgent hardware requests (e.g., disk I/O, timer tick, network packet arrival) without losing the program's progress—state is saved and restored seamlessly via the stack or a reserved memory location.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B carries **14 marks** and offers internal choice between two questions. Both sub-parts are typically **7 marks each**. Show all RTL micro-operations and control signals for full credit.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of the basic computer (Von Neumann architecture) and explain the function of each register. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the complete fetch and decode phases of the instruction cycle using Register Transfer Language (RTL). Show the timing/sequence of control signals. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

*Block diagram description (students should draw):*

The basic computer consists of three main units:

1. **Memory Unit:** $4096 \times 16$-bit word-addressable RAM, addressed by the 12-bit AR.
2. **CPU Registers:**
   - **PC (Program Counter, 12 bits):** Holds the address of the *next* instruction to be fetched.
   - **AR (Address Register, 12 bits):** Specifies the memory address for read/write operations.
   - **IR (Instruction Register, 16 bits):** Holds the *currently executing* instruction.
   - **DR (Data Register, 16 bits):** Temporary buffer for data transferred to/from memory.
   - **AC (Accumulator, 16 bits):** The primary ALU working register; holds one operand and receives results.
   - **TR (Temporary Register, 16 bits):** Internal scratchpad used during interrupt handling.
   - **INPR (Input Register, 8 bits)** and **OUTR (Output Register, 8 bits):** Interface with peripheral devices.
   - **SC (Sequence Counter, 2 bits):** Tracks the current phase (T0, T1, T2, T3) of the instruction cycle.
3. **ALU and Control Unit:** Performs arithmetic/logic operations and generates timing signals.

**Functions table (must be in answer):**

| Register | Size | Function |
|---|---|---|
| PC | 12 bits | Points to next instruction |
| AR | 12 bits | Memory addressing |
| IR | 16 bits | Holds fetched instruction |
| DR | 16 bits | Memory data buffer |
| AC | 16 bits | ALU's primary operand/result |
| TR | 16 bits | Temporary storage in interrupt cycle |
| INPR | 8 bits | Receives input from peripheral |
| OUTR | 8 bits | Sends output to peripheral |
| SC | 2 bits | Tracks micro-operation phase |

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for register identification, 2 marks for functions, 1 mark for neatness/labels.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Fetch Phase (occurs in clock cycles T0 and T1):**

| Time | RTL Statement | Control Signals |
|---|---|---|
| T0 | $AR \leftarrow PC$ | $PC_{out}, AR_{in}$ |
| T1 | $IR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | $AR_{out}, Read, DR_{in}, DR_{out}, IR_{in}, INC\_PC, PC_{in}$ |

**Explanation of Fetch:**
At T0, the PC's content (the address of the instruction to be fetched) is transferred to AR. At T1, the memory is read at the address held in AR; the 16-bit instruction appears on the bus and is latched first into DR, then copied to IR. Simultaneously, the PC is incremented to point to the next instruction in sequence. This phase is *universal*—it occurs for every instruction without exception.

**Decode Phase (occurs in clock cycle T2):**

| Time | RTL Statement | Control Signals |
|---|---|---|
| T2 | $D_7 D_6 D_5 D_4 D_3 D_2 D_1 D_0 \leftarrow \text{Decode } IR(12\text{-}14)$, $AR \leftarrow IR(0\text{-}11)$ | $IR_{out}, D_{in}, AR_{in}$ |

**Explanation of Decode:**
At T2, the three opcode bits (`IR[12]`, `IR[11]`, `IR[10]`) are routed to the 3-to-8 decoder, which activates exactly one of the eight outputs $D_0$ through $D_7$ corresponding to the instruction type. Simultaneously, the 12-bit address field `IR[0–11]` is transferred to AR (this is *preliminary*—for memory-reference instructions, this address is used or modified in T3; for register-reference and I/O instructions, this is unused).

The decoder output $D_i$ remains active until SC is reset, and it controls all subsequent micro-operations in T3 and T4.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for fetch RTL, 2 marks for fetch explanation, 2 marks for decode RTL, 1 mark for control signal annotation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]`
