**(a)** With a neat flowchart, explain all the phases of the instruction cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A processor has a clock rate of **$3 \text{ GHz}$** and executes a program with the following instruction mix:

| Instruction Type | Count | CPI |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | $1{,}20{,}000$ | 1 |
| Load | $45{,}000$ | 4 |
| Store | $30{,}000$ | 3 |
| Branch | $5{,}000$ | 2 |

Calculate: (i) Average CPI, (ii) CPU execution time, (iii) MIPS rating. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

*Refer to the flowcharts in SECTION_4.1 and SECTION_4.3 of these notes.* The answer should present the following six logical blocks in a top-down flowchart:

1. **START → T0: AR ← PC** (initialize fetch)
2. **T1: IR ← M[AR], PC ← PC + 1** (memory read for instruction)
3. **T2: Decode opcode bits IR[12–14]** (determine instruction class)
4. **T3: Effective address / operand fetch (if needed)** (for memory-reference or indirect)
5. **T4: Execute operation in ALU** (computation / branch decision)
6. **Store result (if needed) → Check for interrupt → Loop to T0**

Marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for phase labels, 1 for control signals, 1 for return-to-T0 loop.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $f_{clk} = 3 \text{ GHz} = 3 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$, hence $T_{clk} = \frac{1}{3 \times 10^9} \approx 0.333 \text{ ns}$.

**Step 1 — Total instruction count $N$:**

$$
N = 1{,}20{,}000 + 45{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Weighted cycle sum:**

$$
\sum (CPI_i \cdot N_i) = (1 \cdot 1{,}20{,}000) + (4 \cdot 45{,}000) + (3 \cdot 30{,}000) + (2 \cdot 5{,}000)
$$

$$
= 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}80{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3 (i) — Average CPI:**

$$
CPI_{avg} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{2{,}00{,}000} = 2.0
$$

**Step 4 (ii) — CPU execution time:**

$$
T_{CPU} = N \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 2.0 \times \frac{1}{3 \times 10^9}
$$

$$
T_{CPU} = \frac{4{,}00{,}000}{3 \times 10^9} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ seconds} = 133.33 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 5 (iii) — MIPS rating:**

$$
\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{9}}{2.0 \times 10^{6}} = 1500 \text{ MIPS}
$$

*(Marks: 1 for setup, 2 for $CPI_{avg}$, 2 for $T_{CPU}$, 2 for MIPS — show all unit conversions explicitly.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the PC increment:** Many students describe the fetch phase but omit "$PC \leftarrow PC + 1$." This costs **1 full mark** every single time. The PC *must* advance to avoid an infinite loop.
>
> 2. **Confusing AR and IR:** The AR holds a *memory address*; the IR holds an *instruction*. Mixing them up in a register table = 1 mark lost.
>
> 3. **Skipping RTL annotation:** Simply drawing a flowchart without writing the **register transfer statements** in each box is incomplete. KTU 2024 explicitly requires RTL micro-operations for full marks.
>
> 4. **MIPS unit confusion:** MIPS = Million Instructions Per Second. The denominator MUST have $\times 10^6$. Forgetting this = answer is off by 6 orders of magnitude = **2 marks lost**.
>
> 5. **Interrupt cycle misuse:** Students often describe the interrupt cycle as *part of* the instruction cycle. KTU marking scheme explicitly penalizes this — they are *separate* cycles. Always say "the interrupt cycle is interleaved *between* two instruction cycles."
>
> 6. **Effective Address confusion:** The EA is computed *only* for memory-reference instructions with indirect/indexed addressing. Do NOT apply it to direct or immediate addressing. This is a 1-mark trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before ESE:**

- **Definition:** The instruction cycle is the *complete* sequence: **Fetch → Decode → Operand Fetch → Execute → Store → (Interrupt Check)**.
- **Von Neumann Registers (in order of appearance):** **PC → AR → IR → DR → AC**.
- **Universal Phase:** *Only* the fetch phase (T0, T1) executes for **every** instruction without exception.
- **Decode Phase:** Opcode bits are `IR[12–14]`; address field is `IR[0–11]` (in the classical 16-bit ISA).
- **RTL for Fetch T0–T1:** $AR \leftarrow PC$; then $IR \leftarrow M[AR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$.
- **Sequence Counter SC:** A 2-bit register that tracks micro-phase (00, 01, 10, 11) and resets to 0 to begin a new cycle.
- **Performance Equation (must-memorize):** $T_{CPU} = N \times CPI_{avg} \times T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}} \times \sum (CPI_i \cdot N_i)$.
- **MIPS Formula:** $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{CPI_{avg} \times 10^6}$.
- **Interrupt Cycle:** A *separate* cycle, *not* part of the instruction cycle. It saves PC to memory and jumps to the ISR.
- **Pipeline Speedup (advanced):** Maximum theoretical speedup = number of pipeline stages $k$.
- **Von Neumann Bottleneck:** Single shared bus for instructions and data — solved by modern Harvard caches.
- **Key Constant:** $T_{clk} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clk}}$ — never forget this inverse relationship.
- **CPI Range:** Typically **1 to 5+** for simple CPUs; can be **< 0.5** in superscalar processors (more than 1 instruction per cycle).
- **Drawing Rule:** Always label all 9 registers (PC, AR, IR, DR, AC, TR, INPR, OUTR, SC) in any CPU block diagram for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Sequencing and Execution - Fundamental Concepts - Execution of a Complete Instruction - Data Path - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization - Hardwired Control - Microprogrammed Control
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Sequencing and Execution - Fundamental Concepts - Execution of a Complete Instruction - Data Path - Single Bus and Multiple Bus Organization - Hardwired Control - Microprogrammed Control

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Instruction Sequencing and Execution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (NEP 2020 Aligned)**
> **Instruction Sequencing** is the systematic process by which the Central Processing Unit (CPU) fetches, decodes, and executes instructions from main memory in a deterministic order governed by the Program Counter (PC). **Instruction Execution** refers to the operational phase where the decoded instruction is carried out by the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) using data from registers or memory, producing a result that is stored back into a register, memory, or used to alter control flow.

**Key Constituents of the CPU involved in this process:**

- **Program Counter (PC):** Holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
- **Instruction Register (IR):** Stores the currently fetched instruction.
- **Memory Address Register (MAR):** Holds the address of the memory location being accessed.
- **Memory Data Register (MDR):** Temporarily buffers data being transferred to/from memory.
- **Accumulator (AC) / General Purpose Registers:** Hold operands and results.
- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs arithmetic and logic operations.
- **Control Unit (CU):** Generates the timing and control signals to orchestrate all operations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a **CPU as a high-end restaurant kitchen** preparing dishes (instructions) one after another:
> - The **Order Ticket Stack** = Main Memory (recipes stored sequentially)
> - The **Head Chef's Pointer** = Program Counter (PC) pointing to the next order
> - The **Reading Counter** = Instruction Register (IR) holding the current order
> - The **Waiter** = System Bus (carries ingredients — data — between counter, fridge, and stove)
> - The **Stainless Steel Counters** = Registers (workspace)
> - The **Stove and Oven** = ALU (where actual cooking/computation happens)
> - The **Head Chef** = Control Unit (dictates what each station does, in what order, and for how long)
> 
> Just as a chef must **read the order → understand the dish → grab ingredients → cook → plate** before moving to the next ticket, the CPU performs **Fetch → Decode → Operand Fetch → Execute → Store Result** in a precise sequence for every single instruction.

---

### 1.3 Execution of a Complete Instruction — The Phases

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Instruction Cycle** consists of **four major phases** (KTU Standard):

| Phase | Purpose | Key Registers Used |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Fetch Instruction** | Read instruction from memory at address PC | MAR, MDR, IR, PC |
| **2. Decode Instruction** | Determine opcode and addressing mode | IR, Control Unit |
| **3. Fetch Operand(s)** | Retrieve data from register/memory/I/O | MAR, MDR, Registers |
| **4. Execute & Store Result** | Perform ALU op and write back | ALU, AC, Memory, Flags |

---

### 1.4 Data Path — Definition and Variants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition**
> A **Data Path** is the collection of functional units (ALU, registers, multiplexers, decoders), data buses, and control paths through which data flows during the execution of an instruction. It is the "physical wiring" of the CPU that moves bits between storage, computation, and output.

#### **Two Architectural Variants of Data Path Organization:**

1. **Single Bus Organization**
   - All registers share **one common bus**.
   - **Only one data transfer per clock cycle** is possible.
   - Simpler hardware, lower cost, but **slower** because operand fetches must be serialised.
   - Used in **simpler processors** and educational architectures (e.g., the basic Mano/Morris Mano CPU).

2. **Multiple Bus Organization**
   - **Two or three internal buses** allow **simultaneous** transfers.
   - Faster execution since source and destination can move data in parallel.
   - More complex control logic and higher hardware cost.
   - Common in modern pipelined and superscalar processors.

---

### 1.5 Control Unit — Two Design Philosophies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Control Unit (CU) is the "brain" of the CPU. KTU 2024 syllabus mandates thorough understanding of **two distinct design styles**:

#### **A. Hardwired Control**
- Built from **combinational logic gates, decoders, flip-flops, and PLA/PAL** devices.
- Control signals are generated by **fixed Boolean expressions** over the current state and instruction opcode.
- **Speed:** Very fast (gate-level propagation only).
- **Flexibility:** Difficult to modify or correct design errors post-fabrication.
- **Used in:** RISC processors (MIPS, ARM, SPARC).

#### **B. Microprogrammed Control**
- Each machine instruction is **broken down into a sequence of microinstructions** stored in a special **Control Memory (ROM)**.
- A **microsequencer** fetches the next microinstruction based on the current state.
- **Speed:** Slower (extra memory access cycles).
- **Flexibility:** Easy to modify, debug, and extend — just change the microprogram.
- **Used in:** CISC processors (Intel x86, VAX).

---

### 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics Used in This Module:**
> - **CPU Clock Frequency (f):** Measured in **Hz (Hertz)**, typically **MHz to GHz**.
> - **Clock Cycle Time (T):** T = 1/f, measured in **nanoseconds (ns)** or **picoseconds (ps)**.
> - **Control Word Width (k):** Number of bits in a microinstruction, typically **20 to 100 bits**.
> - **Control Memory Size:** 2^n × k, where n = microinstruction address bits.
> - **Instruction Word Length:** Typically **16, 32, or 64 bits**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Phases of Instruction Execution (Operational Logic)

> [!NOTE]
> Below is a **step-by-step operational breakdown** that examiners expect for full marks in any 7 or 14-mark question on instruction execution.

---

#### **Phase 1: Fetch Instruction (Common to ALL instructions)**

The fetch phase is **identical for every instruction** regardless of its type. It uses the address in the PC to retrieve the instruction from memory.

| Step | Action | Control Signals Asserted |
|---|---|---:|
| 1 | **MAR ← PC** | PCout, MARin |
| 2 | **MDR ← Memory[MAR]** (Read operation) | Read, MDRinE |
| 3 | **IR ← MDR** | MDRout, IRin |
| 4 | **PC ← PC + 1** (Increment for next instruction) | PCin, +1 |

> **Result:** The instruction is now loaded in IR, and PC points to the next sequential instruction.

---

#### **Phase 2: Decode Instruction**

| Step | Action | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| 5 | **Opcode bits of IR are sent to Control Unit** | Instruction Decoder (3-to-8, 4-to-16) |
| 6 | **CU determines the instruction type** | Combinational decode logic |
| 7 | **Mode bits determine addressing mode** | Direct, Indirect, Immediate, Register |

---

#### **Phase 3: Fetch Operand(s)**

This phase **varies** based on the addressing mode:

- **Direct Memory Address:** `MAR ← IR(address)`, then `MDR ← Memory[MAR]`
- **Indirect Memory Address:** `MAR ← MDR(address)`, then `MDR ← Memory[MAR]`
- **Register Operand:** Just select the register (no memory access).
- **Immediate Operand:** Operand is part of the IR itself.

---

#### **Phase 4: Execute and Store Result**

**Three classes of instructions in a typical KTU textbook CPU:**

| Class | Action |
|---|---|
| **Memory-Reference (MRI)** | Read/Write/Arithmetic/Logic with memory operand |
| **Register-Reference (RRI)** | Operations on AC or other registers (no memory) |
| **Input-Output Reference (IOI)** | I/O operations (IN, OUT) |

**Example:** For `ADD` instruction with direct addressing:

| Step | Action |
|---|---|
| 8 | `DR ← M[AR]` (Read memory operand into Data Register) |
| 9 | `AC ← AC + DR, SC ← 0` (Perform addition, reset Sequence Counter) |

---

### 2.2 The Sequence Counter (SC) — Heart of Timing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Sequence Counter (SC)** is a 2-bit or 3-bit register that generates timing signals **T0, T1, T2, T3** in sequence. Each clock cycle advances SC. Control signals are gated by these timing signals to produce micro-operations at the correct instant.

**Signal Generation Logic:**

$$\text{Micro-operation} = T_i \cdot \text{Function Bit}_j \cdot \text{Bus Driver}$$

For example:

$$PC_{in} = T_0 \cdot (+1) \cdot \overline{Reset}$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Equations and Definitions for KTU Board Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Clock Cycle Time** | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Seconds (s); f = Hz |
| 2 | **CPU Execution Time** | $T_{exec} = N \times CPI \times T$ | N = instruction count; CPI = cycles/instruction |
| 3 | **Average CPI** | $CPI_{avg} = \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} CPI_i \times \dfrac{I_i}{I_{total}}$ | Weighted by instruction frequency |
| 4 | **Throughput** | $Throughput = \dfrac{1}{CPI \times T}$ | Instructions per second (IPS) |
| 5 | **Control Memory Size** | $CM_{size} = 2^k \times n$ | k = address bits; n = microinstruction width |
| 6 | **Microinstruction Address** | $Next Address = \mu PC + 1 \text{ or Branch}$ | Determined by conditional bits |
| 7 | **Speedup (Hardwired vs Microprogrammed)** | $S = \dfrac{T_{micro}}{T_{hardwired}}$ | Hardwired is typically **2x–5x faster** |
| 8 | **Effective Address (Direct)** | $EA = \text{Address field of IR}$ | Single memory reference |
| 9 | **Effective Address (Indirect)** | $EA = M[\text{Address field of IR}]$ | Two memory references |
| 10 | **Amdahl's Limit (Control Unit)** | $S_{max} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{S_{component}}}$ | f = fraction improved |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - For **Single Bus**: at most **one register-microoperation** per clock cycle.
> - For **Three Bus**: up to **two source operands + one destination** per cycle.
> - **Hardwired Control** has no memory access delay.
> - **Microprogrammed Control** has **1 extra memory cycle** per microinstruction fetch.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility and Real-World Application

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this knowledge applies in industry:**
> - **Processor Design:** Intel, AMD, ARM, and Apple Silicon engineers design data paths using these exact principles.
> - **Embedded Systems:** Microcontrollers (8051, PIC, AVR) use **hardwired** or **microprogrammed** control.
> - **FPGA Design:** HDL (Verilog/VHDL) implementations of CPUs follow single/multi-bus architectures.
> - **Performance Engineering:** Understanding CPI and clock cycles is critical for **compiler optimization** and **instruction scheduling**.
> - **Verification Engineers:** Use microprogrammed models to test CPU correctness before silicon tape-out.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Execution Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Execution of a Complete Instruction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example: ADD Instruction (Direct Addressing)**
> 
> **Instruction:** `ADD 500` — Adds the contents of memory location **500** to the **Accumulator (AC)**.
> 
> **Assume:** PC currently holds 200 (address of this ADD instruction).

We will use a **standard Mano-type CPU** with 3-bit opcode, 12-bit address, and a Sequence Counter (SC) that cycles through T0, T1, T2, T3.

---

#### **Step 1 — T0: Place PC on Address Bus**

**Action:** `AR ← PC`

**Detailed Reasoning:** The CPU begins every instruction cycle by transferring the contents of the Program Counter (PC) into the Address Register (AR). This is necessary because the AR is the only register connected to the external memory address bus. Until this transfer, memory does not know which location to read from.

**Control Signals:** `PCout, ARin` (both active HIGH during T0)

**Result:** AR = 200 (binary: `0000 1100 1000`)

---

#### **Step 2 — T1: Read Instruction from Memory**

**Action:** `IR ← M[AR]`, `PC ← PC + 1`

**Detailed Reasoning:** Once AR is loaded, the memory read operation is initiated. The memory places the contents of address 200 (the encoded ADD instruction) onto the data bus, and this is latched into the Instruction Register (IR). Simultaneously, the PC is incremented to 201 so the next fetch can begin from the next location. Some implementations separate these into T1 and T2.

**Control Signals:** `Read, IRin, PCin, +1`

**Result:** IR = ADD 500 (opcode + address field), PC = 201

---

#### **Step 3 — T2: Decode and Load Effective Address**

**Action:** `D0...D7 ← decode IR(12-14)`, `AR ← IR(0-11)`

**Detailed Reasoning:** The 3-bit opcode (bits 12–14) of the IR is fed to the instruction decoder, which activates the `D0` output line (assuming ADD has opcode `001`). The lower 12 bits (the address field) of the IR are transferred into the AR, which now holds the effective address of the operand (500 in our example). The decoder output is used to gate the next cycle's control signals.

**Control Signals:** `D0, IRout(0-11), ARin`

**Result:** AR = 500 (binary: `0001 1111 0100`), D0 = 1 (active)

---

#### **Step 4 — T3: Fetch the Operand from Memory**

**Action:** `DR ← M[AR]`

**Detailed Reasoning:** With AR = 500, the memory is read again. This time, the data at address 500 (the actual number to be added, say `X`) is fetched and stored in the Data Register (DR). The DR is used as a temporary holding register so that AC and DR can both feed the ALU in the next cycle.

**Control Signals:** `Read, DRin`

**Result:** DR = X (the operand)

---

#### **Step 5 — T4: Perform the Addition**

**Action:** `AC ← AC + DR`, `SC ← 0`

**Detailed Reasoning:** The ALU is configured for an addition operation. The contents of AC and DR are routed to the ALU inputs. The sum `AC + DR` is computed and stored back into the AC, replacing the old AC value. The Sequence Counter is reset to 0, marking the end of the current instruction. The CPU is now ready to begin the next instruction cycle at T0.

**Control Signals:** `DRout, ACin, ADD operation, SCreset`

**Result:** AC = AC(old) + X

**Final Mathematical Expression:**

$$AC_{new} = AC_{old} + M[500]$$

---

### 3.2 Timing Diagram Representation (Text-Based Analytical Matrix)

> [!NOTE]
> **Micro-operation Sequence Matrix for `ADD 500`**

| Timing Signal | Micro-operation | Active Control Signals | Purpose |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| **T0** | `AR ← PC` | `PCout, ARin` | Load address of instruction |
| **T1** | `IR ← M[AR]`, `PC ← PC + 1` | `Read, IRin, PCin, +1` | Fetch instruction |
| **T2** | `D0 ← Decode(IR)`, `AR ← IR(addr)` | `D0, IRout, ARin` | Decode & load operand address |
| **T3** | `DR ← M[AR]` | `Read, DRin` | Fetch operand |
| **T4** | `AC ← AC + DR`, `SC ← 0` | `DRout, ACin, ADD` | Execute & reset |

**Total Clock Cycles for ADD:** **5** (T0 to T4)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hardwired Control vs. Microprogrammed Control

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark comparison question**.

| Parameter | **Hardwired Control** | **Microprogrammed Control** |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Combinational logic gates, PLA, PAL, FSM | Control Memory (ROM/RAM) with microinstructions |
| **Speed** | **Very fast** (gate delay only) | **Slower** (1 extra memory access per μ-instruction) |
| **Flexibility** | Rigid; changes require redesign | **Highly flexible**; modify the microprogram |
| **Design Complexity** | Complex for large instruction sets | Simpler for complex ISAs |
| **Cost** | Higher gate count for complex CISC | Lower design cost |
| **Debugging** | Difficult; signals are buried in gates | Easy; just inspect microprogram |
| **Instruction Set Support** | Better for small RISC | Better for large CISC |
| **Pipelining Compatibility** | Naturally compatible | Less natural |
| **Storage Required** | None (logic only) | $2^k \times n$ bits of control memory |
| **Failure Recovery** | Hard to patch (microcode fixes impossible) | **Microcode patches** can fix silicon bugs |
| **Used In** | MIPS, ARM (early), RISC-V | Intel x86, IBM mainframe, VAX |
| **Clock Cycle Per μ-op** | 1 cycle (direct decode) | 1 cycle (with CM access overhead) |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Single Bus vs. Multiple Bus Organization

| Parameter | **Single Bus** | **Two-Bus** | **Three-Bus** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Buses Used** | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| **Simultaneous Transfers** | 1 | 2 | 3 (2 src + 1 dest) |
| **ALU Inputs** | Sequential (one at a time) | Parallel | Parallel |
| **Clock Cycles per ADD** | 5–7 | 3–4 | 2–3 |
| **Hardware Cost** | **Low** | Medium | **High** |
| **Mux/Demux Logic** | Minimal | Moderate | Extensive |
| **Used In** | Educational CPUs | Mid-range | Modern RISC, DSPs |

---

### 3.5 Performance Derivation for Multi-Bus Speedup

**Single Bus ADD Execution Time:**

$$T_{single} = N_{cycles}^{single} \times T = 6 \times T$$

**Three Bus ADD Execution Time:**

$$T_{triple} = N_{cycles}^{triple} \times T = 2 \times T$$

**Speedup:**

$$S = \frac{T_{single}}{T_{triple}} = \frac{6 \times T}{2 \times T} = 3.0$$

**Interpretation:** A three-bus organization is **3x faster** for this ADD instruction, with the trade-off of **roughly 3x more bus hardware and control complexity**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs are prefixed with letters and contain **no reserved Mermaid keywords**. Labels are **clean uppercase alphanumeric text** with no markdown formatting inside the quotes.

---

### 4.2 Figure 1: General CPU Data Path Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef register fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef alu fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef memory fill:#F48FB1,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    PC[PROGRAM COUNTER]:::register
    AR[ADDRESS REGISTER]:::register
    IR[INSTRUCTION REGISTER]:::register
    DR[DATA REGISTER]:::register
    AC[ACCUMULATOR]:::register
    TR[TEMPORARY REGISTER]:::register
    ALU[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT]:::alu
    CU[CONTROL UNIT]:::control
    MEM[MAIN MEMORY]:::memory
    SEQ[SEQUENCE COUNTER]:::control

    PC --> AR
    AR --> MEM
    MEM --> DR
    DR --> IR
    IR --> CU
    CU --> SEQ
    SEQ --> CU
    IR --> DR
    IR --> AR
    DR --> ALU
    AC --> ALU
    TR --> ALU
    ALU --> AC
    ALU --> DR
    CU -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> PC
    CU -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> AR
    CU -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> IR
    CU -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> ALU
    CU -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> MEM
```

---

### 4.3 Figure 2: Single Bus Organization Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef bus fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:4px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef alu fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    BUS[COMMON INTERNAL BUS]:::bus
    PC2[PC]:::reg
    AR2[AR]:::reg
    IR2[IR]:::reg
    DR2[DR]:::reg
    AC2[AC]:::reg
    TR2[TR]:::reg
    ALU2[ALU]:::alu
    INPR[INPR]:::reg
    OUTR[OUTR]:::reg

    PC2 --> BUS
    AR2 --> BUS
    IR2 --> BUS
    DR2 --> BUS
    AC2 --> BUS
    TR2 --> BUS
    BUS --> ALU2
    ALU2 --> BUS
    INPR --> BUS
    OUTR --> BUS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Observation:** All registers connect to **one common bus**. Only **one** register can drive the bus at any time, and only **one** can latch from the bus at any time. This requires careful timing via the Sequence Counter.

---

### 4.4 Figure 3: Three Bus Organization (Modern CPU Style)

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef busA fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef busB fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef busC fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef alu fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef reg fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    BUSA[SOURCE A BUS]:::busA
    BUSB[SOURCE B BUS]:::busB
    BUSC[DESTINATION BUS]:::busC

    R1[R1]:::reg
    R2[R2]:::reg
    R3[R3]:::reg
    ALU3[ALU]:::alu
    RDEST[RESULT REGISTER]:::reg

    R1 --> BUSA
    R2 --> BUSB
    BUSA --> ALU3
    BUSB --> ALU3
    ALU3 --> BUSC
    BUSC --> R1
    BUSC --> R2
    BUSC --> R3
    BUSC --> RDEST
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **three-bus design**, ALU can accept **two operands simultaneously** from BUSA and BUSB, and place the result on BUSC in **a single clock cycle** — the foundation of RISC processor performance.

---

### 4.5 Figure 4: Hardwired Control Unit

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef input fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    OPCODE[INSTRUCTION OPCODE]:::input
    TIMING[TIMING SIGNALS T0 T1 T2 T3]:::input
    FLAGS[STATUS FLAGS]:::input
    DECODER[INSTRUCTION DECODER]:::logic
    COMBO[COMBINATIONAL LOGIC GATES AND PLA]:::logic
    FSM[FINITE STATE MACHINE]:::logic
    OUTCTRL[CONTROL SIGNALS TO CPU]:::output

    OPCODE --> DECODER
    DECODER --> COMBO
    TIMING --> COMBO
    FLAGS --> COMBO
    COMBO --> FSM
    FSM --> OUTCTRL
```

> [!NOTE]
> The hardwired CU is essentially a **Boolean function** of opcode, timing, and flags: `Control Signal = f(Opcode, T_i, Flags)`.

---

### 4.6 Figure 5: Microprogrammed Control Unit

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef store fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logic fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef seq fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    CM[CONTROL MEMORY ROM]:::store
    CD[CONTROL DATA STORE]:::store
    CAR[CONTROL ADDRESS REGISTER]:::seq
    NEXT[LOGIC CIRCUIT FOR BRANCH]:::logic
    MS[MICROSEQUENCER]:::seq
    OPC[INSTRUCTION OPCODE]:::logic
    COND[CONDITION FLAGS]:::logic
    OUT[CONTROL WORD OUTPUT]:::output

    OPC --> CAR
    CD --> CAR
    CAR --> CM
    CM --> OUT
    OUT --> CPU_DEST[CPU COMPONENTS]
    COND --> NEXT
    OUT --> NEXT
    NEXT --> CAR
    MS --> CAR
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** The microprogrammed CU contains a **Control Memory (ROM)** that stores the microprogram. Each machine instruction is **decoded into a starting address** in the control memory, and the microsequencer walks through the corresponding microinstructions.

---

### 4.7 Figure 6: Microinstruction Format

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef field fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef label fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    F1[CONTROL FIELD F1]:::field
    F2[CONTROL FIELD F2]:::field
    F3[CONTROL FIELD F3]:::field
    CD2[CONDITION CD]:::field
    BR[BRANCH BR]:::field
    AD[ADDRESS AD]:::field

    F1 --- F2 --- F3 --- CD2 --- BR --- AD
```

**Field Meaning:**
- **F1, F2, F3:** Select which register/function is activated.
- **CD:** Condition for branch (e.g., `00 = always`, `01 = if AC = 0`, etc.).
- **BR:** Branch type (`0 = next`, `1 = jump to AD`).
- **AD:** Branch target address.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Format:** Direct, definition/conceptual, **2-mark answer + 1-mark diagram or example**.

---

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

> **Differentiate between single bus and multiple bus organization of a CPU data path.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Single Bus | Multiple Bus |
|---|---|---|
| **Buses** | One common internal bus | Two or three internal buses |
| **Transfers per cycle** | Only one | Two or three (simultaneous) |
| **Speed** | Slower | Faster |
| **Hardware cost** | Low | High |
| **Example** | Basic Mano CPU | Modern RISC processors |

**Conclusion:** Single bus is simple and economical; multiple bus is faster and used in modern high-performance CPUs. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

> **Compare hardwired control and microprogrammed control units.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hardwired | Microprogrammed |
|---|---|---|
| **Implementation** | Logic gates / PLA | Control memory (ROM) |
| **Speed** | Faster | Slower |
| **Flexibility** | Hard to modify | Easy to modify |
| **Used in** | RISC | CISC |

**Conclusion:** Hardwired is faster but rigid; microprogrammed is flexible but slower due to extra memory access. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between **Question A and Question B**.

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the execution of a complete instruction in a basic computer with a neat block diagram. Show the sequence of micro-operations for an ADD instruction with direct addressing. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
The execution of an instruction in a basic computer (Mano's design) occurs in **two major phases**: the **Fetch phase** (common to all instructions) and the **Execute phase** (instruction-specific). The fetch phase consists of 4 micro-operations, and the execute phase varies.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw the CPU block diagram with PC, AR, IR, DR, AC, TR, ALU, Memory, and Control Unit connected by a common bus. (Refer to Figure 1 in Section 4.2)

**Step 3 — Fetch Micro-operations (2 marks):**

| Signal | Micro-operation |
|---|---|
| T0 | `AR ← PC` |
| T1 | `IR ← M[AR], PC ← PC + 1` |
| T2 | `D0...D7 ← Decode IR(12-14), AR ← IR(0-11)` |
| T3 | `DR ← M[AR]` |

**Step 4 — Execute for ADD (Direct) (2 marks):**

| Signal | Micro-operation |
|---|---|
| T4 | `AC ← AC + DR, SC ← 0` |

**Conclusion:** The ADD instruction completes in **5 clock cycles** (T0 to T4). The first 3 are common fetch-decode, T3 fetches operand, T4 performs the addition. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the microprogrammed control unit. Discuss the format of a microinstruction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw the microprogrammed control unit showing: Control Memory, Control Address Register (CAR), Microsequencer, Control Data Register (CDR), and next-address logic. (Refer to Figure 5 in Section 4.6)

**Step 2 — Working Principle (2 marks):**
- The opcode of the IR is mapped to a **starting microaddress** in the control memory.
- The microsequencer fetches successive microinstructions from control memory.
- Each microinstruction is a **control word** that activates specific control signals.
- After execution, the next-address logic determines the next microaddress (sequential or branch).

**Step 3 — Microinstruction Format (2 marks):**
A typical microinstruction has:
- **F1, F2, F3:** Control fields (select registers / operations).
- **CD:** Condition field (`00 = always`, `01 = AC=0`, `10 = AC>0`, `11 = I bit`).
- **BR:** Branch field (`0 = next address`, `1 = jump to AD`).
- **AD:** Branch address field.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The microprogrammed control unit is **flexible, easy to design for complex ISAs**, and is the preferred choice for **CISC processors** like x86. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) Discuss the single bus organization of the data path. Explain the timing and control signals for fetching and executing an instruction. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition & Diagram (2 marks):**
In single bus organization, **all CPU registers share a common internal bus**. Only one register can drive the bus at a time. (Refer to Figure 2 in Section 4.3)

**Step 2 — Timing Signals (2 marks):**
The **Sequence Counter (SC)** generates timing signals T0, T1, T2, T3. Each cycle advances by one. The control unit uses these signals to gate register read/write and ALU operations.

**Step 3 — Fetch Example (2 marks):**
- `T0: PCout, ARin` — AR ← PC
- `T1: Read, IRin, PCin, +1` — IR ← M[AR], PC ← PC+1

**Step 4 — Execute Example (1 mark):**
- For `ADD` direct: `T2: D0, ARin` (AR ← IR(addr)), `T3: Read, DRin`, `T4: ACin, ADD`.

**Conclusion:** Single bus is simple, but requires **serial data transfers**, making it slower than multi-bus designs. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

**(b) Explain the design of a hardwired control unit with an example. Compare it with microprogrammed control. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A hardwired control unit uses **combinational logic circuits (gates, decoders, PLA, FSM)** to generate control signals as Boolean functions of the current instruction opcode, timing signal, and status flags.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Draw the hardwired CU showing: Instruction Decoder, PLA, Timing Generator, Status Flags, Control Signal Outputs. (Refer to Figure 4 in Section 4.5)

**Step 3 — Example Control Logic (2 marks):**
For the control signal `ACin` (enable AC input):

$$AC_{in} = T_4 \cdot D_0 + T_5 \cdot D_1 \cdot I$$

Where:
- $T_4$ is the timing signal for ADD execution.
- $D_0$ is the decoder output for ADD opcode.
- $T_5 \cdot D_1 \cdot I$ is for an indirect instruction using I-bit.

**Step 4 — Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Hardwired | Microprogrammed |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Faster (no memory access) | Slower (1 extra cycle) |
| Flexibility | Rigid | Easy to modify |
| Used in | RISC | CISC |

**Conclusion:** Hardwired control offers **maximum speed** at the cost of **design complexity and flexibility**. **(Total: 7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (Read Carefully!)**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the Fetch and Execute phases** — The fetch is *common* to all instructions; the execute phase *differs*. Don't merge them in your answer.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting PC ← PC + 1** — Examiners specifically check if you have incremented the PC during T1 or T2. Missing this **costs 1 mark**.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up single bus and three bus** — In single bus, you **cannot** fetch two operands in one cycle. Don't write "fetch both operands simultaneously" for single bus.
> 
> 4. **In microprogrammed control**, students often forget to mention the **CAR (Control Address Register)** and the **branch logic**. This loses **2 marks** in 7-mark questions.
> 
> 5. **Control Signal Boolean expression** — For hardwired control, always show the equation in **canonical form** (e.g., `Signal = T_i · D_j · I_bit`). Writing only the signal name is **incomplete**.
> 
> 6. **Diagram without labels** — Block diagrams must have **all registers and bus lines labeled**. An unlabeled diagram gets **0 marks** even if the structure is correct.
> 
> 7. **In comparison tables**, students often list features but forget to state **which is better and why**. Always end with a **justification sentence**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 (Topic: Instruction Sequencing and Control)**

- ✅ **Instruction Cycle Phases:** Fetch → Decode → Operand Fetch → Execute.
- ✅ **Fetch is always 3–4 cycles** regardless of instruction type.
- ✅ **PC increment happens during T1** (or T2 in some designs) — **never forget it**.
- ✅ **Sequence Counter (SC)** generates timing signals T0, T1, T2, T3.
- ✅ **Single bus** = 1 transfer per cycle, **Multi bus** = up to 3 transfers per cycle.
- ✅ **Three-bus ALU** can read 2 sources and write 1 destination in **one** cycle.
- ✅ **Hardwired CU** = Logic gates + FSM + PLA. **Fast, rigid, RISC**.
- ✅ **Microprogrammed CU** = ROM + Microsequencer. **Slow, flexible, CISC**.
- ✅ **Microinstruction format:** F1, F2, F3, CD, BR, AD.
- ✅ **Control Memory size** = $2^k \times n$ where $k$ = address bits, $n$ = word width.
- ✅ **Key Boolean equation:** $Control\_Signal = T_i \cdot D_j \cdot Flags \cdot I\_bit$.
- ✅ **ADD instruction timing** (single bus, direct addressing): **5 cycles** (T0 to T4).
- ✅ **Effective address** (direct) = address field of IR; (indirect) = `M[address field]`.
- ✅ **Speedup of three-bus over single-bus** for ALU ops: typically **2x to 3x**.
- ✅ **Famous processors using each style:**
  - Hardwired → **MIPS, ARM, RISC-V**
  - Microprogrammed → **Intel x86, VAX, IBM z/Architecture**
- ✅ **Examiner's hot topic:** "Compare hardwired and microprogrammed control" — appears **every year** in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Must-know example:** Show the timing/micro-operations for at least one **ADD, LDA, STA, BUN, BSA** instruction from Morris Mano's basic computer.
- ✅ **Don't confuse** "micro-operation" (single register transfer) with "microinstruction" (control word).

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — PERUT418 — Computer Organisation and Architecture**

topics.title: Assembly Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Assembly Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Assembly Language** is a low-level programming language that uses **mnemonic codes** (short, human-readable symbolic abbreviations) to represent the machine-level instructions executed by a computer's **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**. Each assembly language statement corresponds directly — typically in a **1:1 (one-to-one)** mapping — to a single **machine instruction** in the processor's **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 2 of PERUT418), Assembly Language is studied as the bridge between high-level programming constructs and the underlying digital hardware. It is the *human-translatable face* of **machine code** (binary).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT418, Module 2)**
> Assembly Language introduces students to instruction formats, addressing modes, symbolic mnemonics, assembler directives, and the translation process from mnemonic source code → object code (machine language).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Triad to Remember:**
> 1. **Machine Language** → pure binary (0s and 1s), the only language the CPU truly understands.
> 2. **Assembly Language** → human-readable mnemonics, 1:1 mapped to machine language.
> 3. **High-Level Language (HLL)** → abstract, portable, requires compiler/interpreter (1:N mapping).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **master chef working in a professional kitchen**:

- The **CPU** is the *chef*. It only understands very specific, simple gestures (chop, stir, boil, plate).
- **Machine language** is the chef's *neural signal* — invisible, unrecognizable, and fast but impossible to communicate directly.
- **Assembly language** is the *recipe card* the chef reads: "CHOP ONION 2CM," "BOIL WATER 5MIN," "PLATE CENTER." Each instruction is short, specific, and tells the chef exactly one thing to do.
- **High-level language** is the *customer's order*: "Make me a delicious soup." The compiler/translator (the *kitchen manager*) must break that into many recipe-card steps.

Just as a recipe card gives the chef *precise* control over every ingredient, assembly language gives the programmer **direct, exact control** over **registers, memory, and the program counter (PC)** — control that high-level languages deliberately hide.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature (Bolded)

- **Mnemonic**: A short alphabetic code such as `ADD`, `MOV`, `SUB`, `JMP`, representing one machine operation.
- **Opcode (Operation Code)**: The binary pattern that specifies which operation the CPU performs.
- **Operand**: The data or address on which the operation acts.
- **Assembler**: The system software that translates assembly source code into machine object code.
- **Instruction Format**: The standardized binary layout specifying opcode, operands, and addressing mode bits.
- **One Address / Two Address / Three Address Instructions**: Classification based on the number of explicit operands in the instruction.
- **Word length**: Typically **8, 16, 32, or 64 bits** for typical KTU-referenced architectures.
- **Memory Address**: Uniquely identifies a storage location, commonly expressed in **hexadecimal** in KTU papers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statement for 2-Mark Definition:** "Assembly language is a symbolic representation of the machine language of a specific processor, where each statement corresponds to one machine instruction and provides direct control over CPU registers, memory, and hardware resources."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of an Assembly Language Statement

A typical assembly language line is composed of **four fields**, written in the form:

```
[LABEL:]  MNEMONIC  [OPERAND1]  [, OPERAND2]  [; COMMENT]
```

- **Label** (optional): A symbolic name bound to a memory address (e.g., `LOOP:`).
- **Mnemonic**: The operation to perform (e.g., `ADD`, `MOV`).
- **Operands**: Source and destination of data (registers, constants, or memory locations).
- **Comment**: Begins with `;` in most assemblers; ignored by the translator.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU questions, the assembler syntax `LABEL:  OP  DST, SRC` is most commonly used (Intel/MASM style) — but be prepared to recognize **AT&T** and **ARM** variants as well.

## 2.2 Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) Components

The **Instruction Set** is the complete vocabulary of a CPU. It is typically partitioned into functional groups:

- **Data Transfer Instructions** — `MOV`, `LOAD`, `STORE`, `PUSH`, `POP`
- **Data Manipulation (Arithmetic)** — `ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`, `INC`, `DEC`
- **Data Manipulation (Logical)** — `AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, `SHL`, `SHR`
- **Branch / Control Transfer** — `JMP`, `JZ`, `JNZ`, `CALL`, `RET`
- **I/O and System** — `IN`, `OUT`, `NOP`, `HLT`

## 2.3 Instruction Format (Bit-Level)

A standard KTU-referenced instruction format with a **single address / accumulator** reference contains:

$$
\boxed{\text{INSTRUCTION} = \underbrace{OPCODE}_{\text{operation bits}} \; \big\vert \; \underbrace{MODE}_{\text{addressing bits}} \; \big\vert \; \underbrace{ADDRESS}_{\text{operand field}}}
$$

For a **three-address instruction** (common in KTU numericals):

$$
\boxed{\text{INSTRUCTION} = \underbrace{OP}_{\text{opcode}} \; \big\vert \; \underbrace{DEST}_{\text{destination reg}} \; \big\vert \; \underbrace{SRC1}_{\text{source 1 reg}} \; \big\vert \; \underbrace{SRC2}_{\text{source 2 reg}}}
$$

## 2.4 Addressing Modes — The Heart of the Module

Addressing modes describe **how the operand is interpreted** to fetch the effective address. KTU Module 2 places heavy weight on these:

| # | Addressing Mode | Effective Address (EA) Computation | Example (Intel Syntax) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | EA = value in instruction itself | `MOV AX, 5` |
| 2 | **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field of instruction | `MOV AX, [2000H]` |
| 3 | **Indirect (Register)** | EA = Contents of register | `MOV AX, [BX]` |
| 4 | **Register** | Operand is the register itself | `MOV AX, BX` |
| 5 | **Displacement (Indexed)** | EA = Base/Index $\pm$ Offset | `MOV AX, [BX + 4]` |
| 6 | **Relative** | EA = PC $\pm$ Offset | `JNC LOOP` |
| 7 | **Implied / Implicit** | Operand assumed (e.g., ACC) | `CLC`, `CMA` |
| 8 | **Auto-increment / Auto-decrement** | EA = (R), then R $\pm$ 1 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The word "Effective Address (EA)" is the *final physical address* the CPU uses to read/write the operand. Always derive the EA *first* before evaluating the instruction.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Instruction Size | $S = \text{Opcode bits} + \sum \text{Operand fields}$ | bits |
| Memory Address Range | $N = 2^{n}$ locations | n = address bits |
| Memory Capacity | $C = N \times w$ | w = word size (bits) |
| Effective Address (Direct) | $EA = A$ | A is address field |
| Effective Address (Indirect) | $EA = (R)$ | (R) means contents of R |
| Effective Address (Indexed) | $EA = (R) + \text{Offset}$ | Indexed addressing |
| Effective Address (Relative) | $EA = (PC) + \text{Offset}$ | Branch instructions |
| 1-Address Program Length | $L_1 = N \times W_1$ | $W_1$ = 1-address word |
| 3-Address vs 1-Address | $L_3 \approx 1.5 \times L_1$ | (typical for same problem) |
| PC Update (Sequential) | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | (in word-addressed) |
| PC Update (Branch) | $PC \leftarrow PC + \text{Offset}$ | on taken branch |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is used in the table to keep markdown table syntax intact. Students writing answers on paper may freely use the standard `|`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems & IoT**: Firmware in microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M, AVR) is written in assembly for deterministic timing and minimal memory footprint.
- **Device Drivers**: OS-level hardware communication often needs hand-optimized assembly.
- **Compilers**: Modern compilers like GCC emit assembly as an intermediate stage; understanding it aids in **code optimization**.
- **Reverse Engineering / Cybersecurity**: Malware analysts decode machine code by reading its assembly equivalent.
- **DSP & Real-Time Control**: Signal processing loops are sometimes hand-tuned in assembly for cycle-exact execution.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Instruction Memory Size for a Given ISA

**Problem Framework (KTU-style):** A processor has **64 instructions**, supports **8 registers**, and uses a **one-address format** where the address field is **16 bits** wide. Compute the total instruction size in bits.

### Step 1: Determine Opcode Field Width

The number of unique opcodes requires:

$$
n_{opcode} = \lceil \log_2(64) \rceil
$$

Evaluating:

$$
n_{opcode} = \log_2(2^6) = 6 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 2: Identify Operand Fields

The instruction is **one-address**, meaning the format is `OPCODE | ADDRESS`. No register field is required. The address field is **given as 16 bits**.

$$
n_{address} = 16 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Total Instruction Size

$$
S = n_{opcode} + n_{address}
$$

Substituting:

$$
S = 6 + 16 = 22 \text{ bits}
$$

### Step 4: Round to Standard Word Size

KTU convention typically rounds to the **nearest multiple of 8 bits** (one byte) for storage efficiency:

$$
S_{padded} = 24 \text{ bits} = 3 \text{ bytes}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Address Computation Across Modes

Suppose a processor has registers: `PC = 3000H`, `AC = A5H`, `R1 = 4000H`, and an instruction `ADD @R1` is fetched from memory location `2000H`. The memory at `4000H` contains `1250H`. Find the result stored in `AC`.

### Step 1: Identify Addressing Mode

`@R1` denotes **register indirect addressing** in classical notation. The `@` symbol means *contents of*.

### Step 2: Compute Effective Address (EA)

$$
EA = (R1) = 4000H
$$

### Step 3: Fetch Operand at EA

$$
\text{Operand} = M[EA] = M[4000H] = 1250H
$$

### Step 4: Execute Operation

The `ADD` operation adds the operand to the accumulator:

$$
AC_{new} = AC_{old} + M[EA]
$$

Substituting the hex values:

$$
AC_{new} = A5H + 1250H
$$

Performing hexadecimal addition (in decimal for verification): $165 + 4688 = 4853$, which equals `12F5H` in hex.

$$
AC_{new} = 12F5H
$$

### Step 5: Verify via Binary (Optional Rigour)

$$
A5H = 1010\;0101_2
$$
$$
1250H = 0001\;0010\;0101\;0000_2
$$

Bit-by-bit (16-bit) sum yields `0001\;0010\;1111\;0101_2 = 12F5H` ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Number of Instructions vs. Memory Footprint

KTU frequently asks students to compare **1-address, 2-address, and 3-address** instruction formats for the same algorithm. Consider evaluating `X = (A + B) * (C - D)`:

| Format | Required Instructions | Instruction Count | Memory Footprint (assumed 16-bit/instr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1-Address (Accumulator) | `LDA A`, `ADD B`, `STA T1`, `LDA C`, `SUB D`, `MUL T1`, `STA X` | 7 | 7 words |
| 2-Address | `MOV R1, A`, `ADD R1, B`, `MOV R2, C`, `SUB R2, D`, `MUL R1, R2`, `MOV X, R1` | 6 | 6 words |
| 3-Address | `ADD R1, A, B`, `SUB R2, C, D`, `MUL R3, R1, R2`, `MOV X, R3` | 4 | 4 words |

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Memory Savings

Ratio of 1-address to 3-address footprint:

$$
R = \frac{7}{4} = 1.75
$$

This means the 3-address format uses only:

$$
\text{Saving} = (1 - \tfrac{4}{7}) \times 100\% = 42.86\%
$$

less memory for the same logical task — a frequent KTU reasoning question.

## 3.4 Derivation: Branch Target Address

Suppose at address `2000H`, an instruction `JNC LOOP` is executed, where `LOOP` is a label at address `1FF0H` and the instruction occupies **3 bytes**. Compute the offset that is encoded in the branch.

### Step 1: Identify PC After Fetch

After fetching a 3-byte instruction, the PC points to the **next instruction**:

$$
PC_{next} = 2000H + 3H = 2003H
$$

### Step 2: Compute Offset

$$
\text{Offset} = \text{Target} - PC_{next}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\text{Offset} = 1FF0H - 2003H
$$

Performing hexadecimal subtraction:

$$
1FF0H - 2003H = -13H
$$

Expressed in two's complement 8-bit representation:

$$
\text{Offset} = EDH
$$

The instruction encodes `EDH` (or `-13H` in signed 8-bit), confirming the **relative branch** mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Assembly Language Translation Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Assembly Source File .asm] --> B1[Lexer and Tokenizer]
    B1 --> C1[Parser Builds Symbol Table]
    C1 --> D1[Two Pass Assembler Pass 1 Resolve Labels]
    D1 --> E1[Pass 2 Generate Machine Code]
    E1 --> F1[Linker Combines Object Files]
    F1 --> G1[Loader Places Code in Memory]
    G1 --> H1[CPU Executes Machine Instructions]

    C1 --> C2[Symbol Table Symbol equals Address]
    D1 --> D2[Intermediate Code with Pending Labels]
    E1 --> E2[Object File .obj With Relocation Info]
    F1 --> F2[Executable File .exe]
    G1 --> G2[Memory Image in RAM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Passes?** A forward jump (e.g., `JMP FORWARD`) references a label not yet defined. **Pass 1** scans the entire program to build the **symbol table** (label → address). **Pass 2** performs the actual mnemonic → opcode substitution using the resolved table.

## 4.2 CPU Execution Cycle of an Assembly Instruction (Fetch–Decode–Execute)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[PC sends address to MAR] --> node2[Memory returns Instruction to IR]
    node2 --> node3[IR decoded by Control Unit]
    node3 --> node4{Determine Addressing Mode}
    node4 --> node5[Compute Effective Address EA]
    node5 --> node6[Fetch Operand from Memory or Register]
    node6 --> node7[ALU performs operation ADD SUB MOV etc]
    node7 --> node8[Store result in Destination Register or Memory]
    node8 --> node9[Update PC Sequential or Branch target]
    node9 --> node10[Repeat for next instruction]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture: Memory, CPU, Registers, and Bus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPUblock[CPU Block]
        nodeA[Accumulator AC]
        nodeB[General Purpose Registers R0 to R7]
        nodeC[Program Counter PC]
        nodeD[Instruction Register IR]
        nodeE[ALU Arithmetic Logic Unit]
        nodeF[Control Unit CU]
    end
    nodeM[Main Memory holds Instructions and Data]
    nodeB1[System Bus Data Address Control Lines]

    nodeC -.sends address.-> nodeM
    nodeM -.returns instruction.-> nodeD
    nodeF --> nodeE
    nodeE <--> nodeA
    nodeE <--> nodeB
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeM --- nodeB1
    nodeB1 --- nodeA
```

## 4.4 Symbolic Mapping: Mnemonic to Opcode (Sample Table Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Mnemonic ADD] --> B[Opcode 0001]
    C[Mnemonic SUB] --> D[Opcode 0010]
    E[Mnemonic MOV] --> F[Opcode 0011]
    G[Mnemonic JMP] --> H[Opcode 0100]
    I[Mnemonic AND] --> J[Opcode 0101]
    K[Mnemonic OR] --> L[Opcode 0110]
    M[Mnemonic NOP] --> N[Opcode 1111]
    B --> O[Assembled to Machine Code]
    D --> O
    F --> O
    H --> O
    J --> O
    L --> O
    N --> O
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** When asked "draw the block diagram of an assembly process," the **two-pass assembler** and the **fetch-decode-execute** cycles are the two highest-scoring diagrams. Master both for guaranteed marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Differentiate between machine language and assembly language. Why is assembly language preferred for writing device drivers?"** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

- **Machine Language** consists of binary codes (0s and 1s) directly executed by the CPU. It is hardware-dependent and extremely difficult for humans to read, write, or debug.
- **Assembly Language** uses **mnemonics** (e.g., `ADD`, `MOV`) that map **1:1** to machine instructions, making the program readable while retaining low-level control.
- **Why preferred for device drivers:**
  1. Direct access to **hardware registers** and **memory-mapped I/O**.
  2. Deterministic execution time — essential for real-time hardware responses.
  3. Minimal code size — fits tight memory constraints of kernel/driver space.
  4. Ability to use **specialized CPU instructions** not exposed by high-level languages.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**"List any four addressing modes with one example for each."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Immediate Addressing** — `MOV AX, 10`  (operand `10` is part of the instruction).
2. **Direct Addressing** — `MOV AX, [2000H]`  (address `2000H` specified in the instruction).
3. **Register Addressing** — `MOV AX, BX`  (operand is the register `BX`).
4. **Indirect (Register) Addressing** — `MOV AX, [BX]`  (operand's address is in `BX`).

*(Additional valid modes for extra credit: Indexed, Relative, Implied, Auto-increment.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question 1A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2]

**(a)** Explain the **various addressing modes** in assembly language with examples. Highlight how the **Effective Address (EA)** is calculated in each case. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** For a processor with **32 instructions**, **16 registers**, and a **single-address** format with a **12-bit address field**, calculate the **total instruction size in bits** and the **maximum memory addressable** in bytes. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

The primary addressing modes in assembly language are:

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is embedded in the instruction.
   Example: `MVI A, 05H` → The value `05H` is the operand itself.
   $$
   EA = \text{not applicable (value is literal)}
   $$

2. **Direct (Absolute) Addressing** — The address field of the instruction contains the effective address.
   Example: `LDA 2000H` → Loads `AC` from memory location `2000H`.
   $$
   EA = A = 2000H
   $$

3. **Register Addressing** — Operand is a CPU register.
   Example: `MOV AX, BX`.
   $$
   EA = R_{\text{name}}
   $$

4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — The register contains the *address* of the operand.
   Example: `LDA @R1` (where R1 holds `4000H`).
   $$
   EA = (R1) = 4000H
   $$

5. **Indexed Addressing** — EA = base register content + offset.
   Example: `LDA 2050H (IX)` with `IX = 1000H`.
   $$
   EA = (IX) + \text{Offset} = 1000H + 2050H = 3050H
   $$

6. **Relative Addressing** — Used in branch instructions; EA = PC + offset.
   Example: `JNC LOOP` with `PC = 2003H`, offset = `-13H`.
   $$
   EA = (PC) + \text{Offset}
   $$

7. **Implied (Implicit) Addressing** — Operand is assumed (often the accumulator).
   Example: `CMA` (complement accumulator).

> **Conclusion:** The addressing mode determines how the operand's effective address is derived, which directly impacts instruction length, execution time, and flexibility of memory access.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Opcode Width**

$$
n_{op} = \lceil \log_2(32) \rceil = 5 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2: Register Field Width**

$$
n_{reg} = \lceil \log_2(16) \rceil = 4 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3: Address Field (given)**

$$
n_{addr} = 12 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Total Instruction Size**

Since it is a *single-address* format with register reference for the other operand:

$$
S = n_{op} + n_{reg} + n_{addr}
$$

$$
S = 5 + 4 + 12 = 21 \text{ bits}
$$

Rounded to the nearest multiple of 8:

$$
S_{padded} = 24 \text{ bits} = 3 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 5: Maximum Addressable Memory**

The address field is 12 bits, so the addressable memory locations are:

$$
N = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ words}
$$

Assuming each word is 8 bits (1 byte):

$$
C = 4096 \times 1 = 4096 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ KB}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Instruction size = **24 bits (3 bytes)**, Maximum addressable memory = **4 KB**.

---

### Question 1B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Module 2] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Describe the **structure of an assembly language statement**. Explain the role of an **assembler** and the difference between **one-pass** and **two-pass** assembly. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A program uses **three-address instructions** to evaluate `Z = (P * Q) + (R - S)`. Compare the **number of instructions and memory words** required with the equivalent **one-address (accumulator-based)** program. Assume each instruction occupies **1 word (16 bits)**. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks

**Structure of an Assembly Language Statement:**

$$
[LABEL:]\;\;\text{MNEMONIC}\;\;[OPERAND1]\;[, \text{OPERAND2}]\;[;COMMENT]
$$

- **Label** — symbolic name for a memory address (e.g., `START:`).
- **Mnemonic** — operation code symbol (e.g., `ADD`, `MOV`).
- **Operands** — source and destination of data; can be registers, constants, or addresses.
- **Comment** — documentation only; ignored by the assembler.

**Role of the Assembler:**

The assembler is system software that:
1. Translates mnemonics into **binary opcodes**.
2. Resolves symbolic labels into **absolute addresses**.
3. Allocates memory for **data and instructions**.
4. Detects **syntax errors** and reports them with line numbers.
5. Produces **relocatable object code** for the linker.

**One-Pass vs Two-Pass Assembler:**

| Aspect | One-Pass | Two-Pass |
|---|---|---|
| Passes | 1 | 2 |
| Forward references | Not allowed (or filled with placeholders) | Allowed (resolved in Pass 2) |
| Speed | Faster (less I/O) | Slightly slower |
| Symbol table | Built as encountered | Fully populated in Pass 1 |
| Use case | Simple, restricted programs | General-purpose, KTU-standard |

**Two-Pass Procedure:**
- **Pass 1:** Scan source → assign addresses to all labels → build **symbol table** (label ↔ address).
- **Pass 2:** Substitute mnemonics with opcodes → resolve operands using symbol table → emit object code.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks

**Three-Address Program for `Z = (P * Q) + (R - S)`:**

1. `MUL  T1, P, Q`        — T1 = P * Q
2. `SUB  T2, R, S`        — T2 = R - S
3. `ADD  Z,  T1, T2`      — Z  = T1 + T2

$$
\text{Instruction Count} = 3, \quad \text{Memory Used} = 3 \text{ words}
$$

**One-Address (Accumulator) Program:**

1. `LDA  P`               — AC = P
2. `MUL  Q`               — AC = P * Q
3. `STA  T1`              — T1 = AC
4. `LDA  R`               — AC = R
5. `SUB  S`               — AC = R - S
6. `ADD  T1`              — AC = (R-S) + T1
7. `STA  Z`               — Z = AC

$$
\text{Instruction Count} = 7, \quad \text{Memory Used} = 7 \text{ words}
$$

**Comparison Matrix:**

| Parameter | 3-Address | 1-Address | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instructions | 3 | 7 | +4 |
| Memory Words (16-bit each) | 3 | 7 | +4 |
| Memory Footprint (bytes) | 6 | 14 | +8 |
| Program Compactness Ratio | 1.0 | 2.33× | — |

**Efficiency Saving:**

$$
\text{Saving} = \left(1 - \frac{3}{7}\right) \times 100\% = 57.14\%
$$

The 3-address format is **57.14% more compact** for this expression.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Mixing up opcode and address widths:** A common error is using $\log_2(32) = 6$ bits instead of the correct `5 bits`. Always re-verify with $2^n \geq N$.
> 2. **Forgetting to add register field width:** In hybrid instruction formats (opcode + register + address), students often sum only two of the three fields.
> 3. **Wrong Effective Address derivation:** Many students compute `EA = 4000H` for `[R1]` but forget that in *register indirect*, `EA = (R1)`, which *retrieves the address* — not the operand itself. Fetch from that EA in a separate step.
> 4. **Not rounding to byte boundaries:** When total bits = 21, you **must** round to 24 bits (3 bytes) for realistic storage. Examiners deduct 1 mark for omitting this.
> 5. **Confusing assembler passes:** Saying "one-pass is faster" alone is incomplete; mention the *trade-off in handling forward references*.
> 6. **Ignoring labels in relative branches:** Always state `PC = PC + 1` (or word size) *after* the fetch, *before* computing the offset.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Assembly Language = Mnemonic representation of machine code**, with a strict 1:1 correspondence.
- **Mnemonics** are translator-friendly symbols; **Opcodes** are the CPU-friendly binary forms.
- **Assembler** is the translator; it runs in **one or two passes** to handle label resolution.
- **Instruction Format** = `Opcode + Operand fields`; total size = sum of all bit fields, rounded up to a multiple of 8.
- **Addressing Modes** determine how the **Effective Address (EA)** is calculated — this is the single most important concept in Module 2.
- **Most-Used Modes (must know for KTU):** Immediate, Direct, Register, Register Indirect, Indexed, Relative, Implied.
- **EA Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Direct: `EA = A`
  - Register Indirect: `EA = (R)`
  - Indexed: `EA = (R) + Offset`
  - Relative: `EA = (PC) + Offset`
- **Memory Capacity Formula:** $C = 2^n \times w$ where $n$ = address bits, $w$ = word size.
- **Instruction Count Comparison:** 1-address > 2-address > 3-address for the *same* program (3-address is most compact).
- **Use of Assembler:** Mnemonic → Opcode translation, label resolution, syntax checking, object code generation.
- **Real-world applications:** Embedded firmware, OS kernels, device drivers, real-time control loops, reverse engineering, and compiler back-ends.
- **Key Trade-off:** Assembly offers maximum control and efficiency at the cost of **portability, readability, and development time**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Know the addressing mode → compute the EA → execute the instruction → update the PC."* Repeat this for every assembly problem you solve in the exam hall.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Instructions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Machine Instruction? A **machine instruction** is a binary-coded command that is fetched from memory, decoded by the control unit, and executed by the processor to perform a specific operation on data stored in registers, memory locations, or I/O devices. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, an instruction is the fundamental unit of work that bridges software (programs) and hardware (CPU datapath). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > An instruction in a computer is a binary pattern that specifies a micro-operation to be performed, the operands involved, and the addressing mode used to locate the operands. Formally, a machine instruction consists of the following **logical fields**: $$\text{Instruction} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{\text{Operation}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Operands}}_{\text{Data Reference}}$$ Where: - **Opcode (Operation Code):** Specifies the operation to be performed (e.g., ADD, MOV, LOAD). - **Operand(s):** Specify the registers, memory addresses, or immediate data on which the operation acts. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Factory Worker" Model Imagine the CPU as a **highly disciplined factory worker** who can only understand a fixed set of orders written on standardized forms. Each "form" (instruction) contains: 1. **Action Code (Opcode):** "Paint the wall", "Drill a hole", "Polish the surface" 2. **Materials List (Operands):** "Use blue paint, bucket from shelf #3, brush from drawer #7" The worker picks up one form at a time from a stack (memory), reads the action code, fetches the materials (operand resolution via addressing modes), performs the work (execution), and then moves to the next form. This sequential, form-based processing is exactly how a CPU executes instructions. ## 1.3 Instruction Word Length in KTU Standard Architecture In typical KTU textbook problems (Mano's style architecture), instruction word length is **16 bits**, distributed between opcode and operand fields. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Reference Architecture:** > - Memory Word Size: **16 bits** > - Instruction Word Size: **16 bits** > - Register Size (Accumulator-based): **16 bits** > - Number of Registers: 8 (R0 to R7) or accumulator + 7 general-purpose registers > - Maximum Memory Addressing: **2^12 = 4096 words** (for single-address instructions) or **2^8 = 256 words** depending on addressing scheme ## 1.4 Why Instructions Matter in Computer Architecture Instructions form the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** — the contract between hardware and software. The design of instructions directly affects: - **Performance** (CPI, clock cycle time) - **Code density** (program size in memory) - **Compiler complexity** (orthogonality of instruction set) - **Hardware cost** (control unit complexity, datapath width) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Instruction Format — Structural Breakdown A **16-bit instruction** in a basic accumulator-based KTU architecture is divided into: | Field | Size (bits) | Purpose | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Opcode | 3 | Specifies the operation (8 possible operations) | | Address / Operand | 13 | Specifies memory address or register reference | | Mode Bit / I-bit | 1 | Distinguishes direct from indirect addressing | For a **3-address instruction format**: $$\text{16 bits} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{6 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Dest}}_{3 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Src1}}_{3 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Src2}}_{3 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Function}}_{1 \text{ bit}}$$ ## 2.2 Types of Machine Instructions (KTU Classification) The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes instructions into **five major classes**: ### 2.2.1 Data Transfer Instructions Move data between registers, memory, and I/O without changing the data content. - **Examples:** LOAD, STORE, MOVE, PUSH, POP, IN, OUT - **Operation:** `R1 ← M[address]` or `M[address] ← R1` ### 2.2.2 Data Processing Instructions Perform arithmetic or logical operations on data. - **Arithmetic:** ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, INC, DEC, ADI (Add Immediate) - **Logical:** AND, OR, XOR, NOT, SHL, SHR - **Operation:** `R1 ← R1 OP R2` or `AC ← AC OP M[address]` ### 2.2.3 Control Transfer Instructions Change the program execution sequence by modifying the Program Counter (PC). - **Unconditional:** JMP, CALL, RET - **Conditional:** JZ, JNZ, JC, JNC, JN, JP - **Operation:** `PC ← target_address` (if condition met) ### 2.2.4 I/O Instructions Interact with peripheral devices through dedicated I/O addresses. - **Examples:** IN (Read from input port), OUT (Write to output port) ### 2.2.5 System Control Instructions Control processor behavior and system state. - **Examples:** HLT (Halt), NOP (No operation), EI/DI (Enable/Disable interrupts), IRET > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Tip:** In Mano-style architecture, the opcode **0 (000)** is typically reserved for **HLT/INDIRECT/REFERENCE** and the opcode **7 (111)** for **Control/IO** operations. Always check the specific opcode table provided in the question. ## 2.3 Addressing Modes — The Heart of Instruction Design Addressing modes specify **how the operand is located**. They are critical for KTU problems. | Mode | Effective Address (EA) | Example Instruction | Use Case | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Direct (Absolute)** | EA = Address field | LDA 2000 | Accessing fixed variables | | **Indirect** | EA = M[Address field] | LDA @2000 | Pointer-based access | | **Immediate** | Operand = Address field | ADI 05 | Constant operations | | **Register** | EA = Register | ADD R1 | Fast operations | | **Register Indirect** | EA = R[Register] | ADD (R1) | Array traversal | | **Displacement (Indexed)** | EA = Address + R[Index] | LDA 1000(XR) | Array base+offset | | **Relative** | EA = PC + Offset | JNC +05 | Branching | | **Auto-increment** | EA = R[Reg], R[Reg] ← R[Reg]+1 | LDA (R1)+ | Stack operations | | **Auto-decrement** | R[Reg] ← R[Reg]-1, EA = R[Reg] | LDA -(R1) | Reverse stack | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Instruction & Memory Calculations | Concept | Formula | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Memory Addressing | $\text{Addresses} = 2^{n}$ | n = address bits | | Instruction Size | $S = O + A$ | O = opcode bits, A = address bits | | Max Instructions | $I_{max} = 2^{O}$ | O = opcode bits | | Effective Address (Direct) | $EA = A$ | A = address field value | | Effective Address (Indirect) | $EA = M[A]$ | M = memory contents | | Effective Address (Relative) | $EA = PC + A$ | PC = current program counter | | Effective Address (Indexed) | $EA = A + \text{XR}$ | XR = index register | | Stack Growth (Full Descending) | $\text{Top} = \text{Base} - \text{Offset}$ | For PUSH/POP | | PC Update (Branch) | $PC_{new} = PC_{old} + \text{displacement}$ | For branch instructions | | Memory Capacity | $C = 2^{n} \times w$ | n = address bits, w = word size | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** Addressing modes are the foundation of high-level language features. Pointers in C use **register indirect** addressing. Arrays use **indexed/displacement** addressing. Switch statements and loops use **PC-relative** addressing. Function calls use a combination of **absolute** (for the function entry) and **stack-based** addressing for parameters. ## 2.5 RISC vs CISC — KTU Perspective | Feature | RISC (Reduced Instruction Set) | CISC (Complex Instruction Set) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Instruction Size | Fixed (usually 32 bits) | Variable (8 to 120 bits) | | Instruction Count | Small (50–100) | Large (200–300) | | Addressing Modes | Few (3–5) | Many (12–24) | | Memory Access | Load/Store only | Any instruction can access memory | | Clock Cycles per Instruction | 1 CPI (pipelined) | Variable (1–20) | | Examples | ARM, MIPS, SPARC | Intel x86, Motorola 68000 | | Pipelining | Easy and efficient | Complex and difficult | ## 2.6 Instruction Cycle (Fetch–Decode–Execute) The complete execution of an instruction involves **six micro-operations** (Mano's classic formulation): 1. **T0:** `AR ← PC` — Address of instruction loaded into Address Register 2. **T1:** `IR ← M[AR], PC ← PC + 1` — Instruction fetched, PC incremented 3. **T2:** `D0..D7 ← Decode IR(12-14), AR ← IR(0-11)` — Decode opcode, load address 4. **T3:** `AR ← M[AR]` (for indirect mode) — Resolve effective address 5. **T4:** `DR ← M[AR]` or `DR ← AC` — Read operand from memory or register 6. **T5:** `AC ← AC OP DR, SC ← 0` — Perform the operation, reset sequence counter # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Memory References for Each Addressing Mode This is a **classic KTU question** — calculating total memory accesses to fetch an instruction and its operand. ### 3.1.1 Direct Addressing Mode **Step 1:** Define memory reference as a single read or write to memory. **Step 2:** For instruction fetch, the CPU must access memory once to read the instruction itself. **Step 3:** For direct addressing, the address field of the instruction **contains the actual memory address** of the operand — no extra memory access is needed to resolve the address. **Step 4:** To read the operand, the CPU must access memory once more. $$ \text{Total Memory References (Direct)} = 1 \text{ (instruction fetch)} + 1 \text{ (operand fetch)} = 2 $$ ### 3.1.2 Indirect Addressing Mode **Step 1:** Instruction fetch requires **1 memory reference**. **Step 2:** For indirect addressing, the address field contains a **pointer** to another memory location. To get the actual address of the operand, the CPU must perform a memory read at the pointer location. **Step 3:** After obtaining the effective address, the CPU performs another memory read to fetch the operand. $$ \text{Total Memory References (Indirect)} = 1 \text{ (instruction)} + 1 \text{ (pointer fetch)} + 1 \text{ (operand fetch)} = 3 $$ ### 3.1.3 Immediate Addressing Mode **Step 1:** Instruction fetch requires **1 memory reference**. **Step 2:** The operand value is **embedded in the instruction itself** — no memory access needed for operand retrieval. $$ \text{Total Memory References (Immediate)} = 1 \text{ (instruction)} + 0 \text{ (operand is in instruction)} = 1 $$ ### 3.1.4 Register Addressing Mode **Step 1:** Instruction fetch requires **1 memory reference**. **Step 2:** The operand is in a CPU register — no memory access needed. $$ \text{Total Memory References (Register)} = 1 + 0 = 1 $$ ### 3.1.5 Relative Addressing Mode **Step 1:** Instruction fetch requires **1 memory reference**. **Step 2:** Effective Address is computed as `PC + offset` in hardware — no memory access needed. **Step 3:** The branch target address is computed in the CPU; the actual instruction at the target is fetched later. $$ \text{Total Memory References (Relative)} = 1 \text{ (instruction)} + 0 \text{ (EA computed in hardware)} = 1 $$ ## 3.2 Derivative Matrix — Addressing Mode vs Memory Access | Addressing Mode | Instruction Fetch | Address Resolution | Operand Fetch | Total Refs | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Direct | 1 | 0 | 1 | **2** | | Indirect | 1 | 1 | 1 | **3** | | Immediate | 1 | 0 | 0 (in IR) | **1** | | Register | 1 | 0 | 0 | **1** | | Register Indirect | 1 | 0 | 1 | **2** | | Indexed/Displacement | 1 | 0 (EA = A+XR in HW) | 1 | **2** | | Relative | 1 | 0 (EA = PC+offset) | 0 (target later) | **1** | | Auto-increment | 1 | 0 | 1 | **2** | ## 3.3 Worked Example: Instruction Format Design (KTU Standard) **Problem:** Design a 32-bit instruction format for a machine with 64 operations, 32 registers, and 64 K memory addresses. Use 2-address format with opcode, register fields, and immediate operand. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Calculate bits required for opcode. Since we need 64 operations: $$ O = \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate bits required for register references. Since we have 32 registers (each register field needs to identify one of 32): $$ R = \log_2(32) = 5 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate bits for immediate operand. We need to determine the address field size. Given the total instruction is 32 bits: $$ \text{Address bits} = 32 - O - 2R = 32 - 6 - 2(5) = 32 - 6 - 10 = 16 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 4:** Verify the memory addressability. With 16 address bits, the machine can directly address: $$ 2^{16} = 65536 = 64 \text{ K memory words} \checkmark $$ **Step 5:** Present the final instruction format. $$ \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{6 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Reg1 (Dest)}}_{5 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Reg2 (Src)}}_{5 \text{ bits}} \mid \underbrace{\text{Immediate/Address}}_{16 \text{ bits}} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Example: Effective Address Calculation **Given:** A computer uses PC-relative addressing for branch instructions. A branch instruction located at address 200 (decimal) has an offset field of 35 (decimal). What is the effective address of the branch target? ### Solution: **Step 1:** Identify the addressing mode. The instruction is at memory location 200, and the address field is an offset, so the addressing mode is **PC-relative**. **Step 2:** Recall the effective address formula for PC-relative mode. $$ EA = PC + \text{offset} $$ **Step 3:** Substitute the values. The PC typically holds the address of the **next instruction** (after the branch instruction has been fetched and PC incremented). Assuming this is a single-word instruction: $$ PC_{next} = 200 + 1 = 201 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the effective address. $$ EA = 201 + 35 = 236 $$ **Step 5:** State the result clearly. The branch target is memory address **236 (decimal)**.
