The CPU will fetch the next instruction from this address if the branch condition is true. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Micro-Operations for Each Instruction Class | Instruction Type | T0 | T1 | T2 | T3 (if needed) | T4 | T5 | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **LDA (Direct)** | AR ← PC | IR ← M[AR], PC←PC+1 | AR ← IR(0-11) | (skipped) | DR ← M[AR] | AC ← DR, SC ← 0 | | **LDA (Indirect)** | AR ← PC | IR ← M[AR], PC←PC+1 | AR ← IR(0-11) | AR ← M[AR] | DR ← M[AR] | AC ← DR, SC ← 0 | | **ADD (Direct)** | AR ← PC | IR ← M[AR], PC←PC+1 | AR ← IR(0-11) | (skipped) | DR ← M[AR] | AC ← AC+DR, SC←0 | | **STA (Direct)** | AR ← PC | IR ← M[AR], PC←PC+1 | AR ← IR(0-11) | (skipped) | M[AR] ← AC, SC←0 | (skipped) | | **BUN (Branch)** | AR ← PC | IR ← M[AR], PC←PC+1 | AR ← IR(0-11) | (skipped) | PC ← AR, SC←0 | (skipped) | | **BSA (Call)** | AR ← PC | IR ← M[AR], PC←PC+1 | AR ← IR(0-11) | M[AR] ← PC, AR←AR+1 | PC ← AR, SC←0 | (skipped) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Instruction Format Layout — Three-Address Format ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Opcode: 6 bits
Specifies Operation"] nodeB["Dest Reg: 5 bits
Target Register"] nodeC["Src1 Reg: 5 bits
First Source"] nodeD["Src2 Reg: 5 bits
Second Source"] nodeE["Funct/Imm: 11 bits
Function or Immediate"] nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE ``` ## 4.2 Instruction Cycle — Fetch-Decode-Execute State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> T0_Fetch T0_Fetch: T0 AR from PC T0_Fetch --> T1_Read T1_Read: T1 Read Memory to IR Increment PC T1_Read --> T2_Decode T2_Decode: T2 Decode Opcode Load AR T2_Decode --> T3_Resolve T3_Resolve: T3 Indirect Address Resolution T3_Resolve --> T4_Fetch T4_Fetch: T4 Read Operand into DR T4_Fetch --> T5_Execute T5_Execute: T5 Perform Operation Write Result T5_Execute --> [*] ``` ## 4.3 Addressing Mode Resolution Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Start Instruction] --> decodeA{Decode Operand Field} decodeA -->|Mode 00| modeA[Register Direct: EA = R field] decodeA -->|Mode 01| modeB[Register Indirect: EA = M at R field] decodeA -->|Mode 10| modeC[Immediate: Operand in IR] decodeA -->|Mode 11| modeD[Direct/Indexed: EA = Address plus XR] modeA --> resultA[Use Register Value] modeB --> memA[Memory Access at EA] modeC --> resultB[Use Immediate Value] modeD --> memB[Memory Access at Computed EA] resultA --> execA[Execute Operation] memA --> execA resultB --> execA memB --> execA execA --> endA[Write Result and Update PC] ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Instruction Execution Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Memory Unit] -->|Instruction Word| node2[Instruction Register IR] node2 -->|Opcode Field| node3[Instruction Decoder] node2 -->|Address Field| node4[Effective Address Logic] node3 -->|Control Signals| node5[Control Unit] node4 -->|Effective Address| node6[Arithmetic Logic Unit ALU] node5 -->|Control Signals| node6 node6 -->|Result| node7[Accumulator AC] node7 -->|Writeback| node1 node5 -->|PC Update| node8[Program Counter PC] node8 -->|Next Address| node1 ``` ## 4.5 RISC vs CISC Pipeline Compatibility ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Instruction Set] --> nodeB{Variable Length?} nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC[CISC: Complex Decode Stages] nodeB -->|No| nodeD[RISC: Single Cycle Decode] nodeC --> nodeE[Deep Multi-Cycle Pipeline] nodeD --> nodeF[Shallow High-Frequency Pipeline] nodeE --> nodeG[High CPI Variable Latency] nodeF --> nodeH[CPI equals 1 Ideal Throughput] ``` ## 4.6 Instruction Class Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeRoot[Machine Instructions] --> nodeA1[Data Transfer] nodeRoot --> nodeA2[Data Processing] nodeRoot --> nodeA3[Control Transfer] nodeRoot --> nodeA4[Input Output] nodeRoot --> nodeA5[System Control] nodeA1 --> nodeB1[Load Store Move] nodeA2 --> nodeB2[Arithmetic ADD SUB MUL DIV] nodeA2 --> nodeB3[Logical AND OR XOR NOT] nodeA3 --> nodeB4[Unconditional Jump Call Return] nodeA3 --> nodeB5[Conditional Branch JZ JNZ JC JNC] nodeA4 --> nodeB6[IN OUT] nodeA5 --> nodeB7[HLT NOP Interrupt Control] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define an instruction. List the three essential components of a machine instruction with an example.** **Model Answer:** A machine instruction is a binary code that specifies a micro-operation to be performed by the CPU and the operands on which the operation acts. The three essential components of a machine instruction are: 1. **Operation Code (Opcode):** Specifies the operation to be performed. For example, in an ADD instruction, the opcode is `001` (assuming binary encoding). 2. **Source Operand Reference:** Specifies the location of the source data. It can be a register, a memory address, or an immediate value. 3. **Destination Operand Reference:** Specifies where the result of the operation will be stored. This is typically a register or a memory address. **Example:** `ADD R1, R2` → Opcode = ADD, Source = R2, Destination = R1. ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Distinguish between direct and indirect addressing modes. State one advantage of each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Direct Addressing | Indirect Addressing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Effective Address | EA = Address field of instruction | EA = M[Address field] | | Memory Access | One access to fetch operand | Two accesses (pointer + operand) | | Speed | Faster | Slower | | Flexibility | Limited to fixed address | Can access variable addresses via pointer | **Advantage of Direct Addressing:** Simpler hardware, faster execution, requires only 1 memory reference for operand fetch. **Advantage of Indirect Addressing:** Greater programming flexibility — a single instruction can access data at different memory locations by changing the pointer content. Useful for implementing pointers, arrays, and dynamic data structures. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of machine instructions with examples for each category. Draw the instruction format for a basic computer with 16-bit instruction word, 12-bit address, 1-bit addressing mode, and 3-bit opcode. **(b) [7 Marks]** A computer has a 16-bit instruction format with 3-bit opcode, 12-bit memory address, and 1-bit mode field. An instruction at address 250 in hexadecimal is fetched. The mode bit is 1 (indirect). The content of memory location 250 is EA50 (hex), and the content of memory location EA50 is A0B1 (hex). Determine: (i) The effective address of the operand, (ii) The value of the operand, (iii) The total number of memory references for this instruction execution. #### Model Solution for Question A (a): **Types of Machine Instructions:** 1. **Data Transfer Instructions:** Move data between registers, memory, and I/O without modification. - Examples: `LOAD` (AC ← M[AR]), `STORE` (M[AR] ← AC), `MOVE` (R1 ← R2) 2. **Data Processing Instructions:** Perform arithmetic and logical operations. - Arithmetic examples: `ADD` (AC ← AC + DR), `SUB` (AC ← AC − DR), `INC` - Logical examples: `AND` (AC ← AC ∧ DR), `OR` (AC ← AC ∨ DR), `CMA` (Complement AC) 3. **Control Transfer Instructions:** Alter the sequence of program execution. - Unconditional: `BUN` (Branch Unconditionally), `BSA` (Branch and Save Return Address) - Conditional: `BZA` (Branch if Zero), `BNE` (Branch if Not Zero) 4. **Input/Output Instructions:** Interface with peripheral devices. - Examples: `IN` (Read from input device to AC), `OUT` (Write AC to output device) 5. **System Control Instructions:** Control processor-level operations. - Examples: `HLT` (Halt), `NOP` (No operation), `ION`/`IOF` (Interrupt on/off) **Instruction Format (16-bit basic computer):** | I (1 bit) | Opcode (3 bits) | Address (12 bits) | | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Mode bit | Operation code | Operand/Address | The I-bit distinguishes between direct (0) and indirect (1) addressing. #### Model Solution for Question A (b): **Given Information:** - Instruction address: `250` (hex) - Mode bit: 1 (indirect addressing) - M[250] = `EA50` (hex) — content of location 250 - M[EA50] = `A0B1` (hex) — content of location EA50 **(i) Effective Address of the Operand:** Since the mode bit is 1, indirect addressing applies. The effective address is the content of the memory location pointed to by the address field of the instruction. **Step 1:** The address field of the instruction at location 250 points to location 250 itself (per the given data structure) — or we follow the pointer chain as specified. **Step 2:** The content of the address field (M[250]) = `EA50` (hex). This is the effective address. $$ EA = \text{M[250]} = \text{EA50}_{16} = 60000_{10} $$ **(ii) Value of the Operand:** The operand value is the content of the effective address location. $$ \text{Operand} = \text{M[EA]} = \text{M[EA50]} = \text{A0B1}_{16} $$ Converting to decimal: $$ \text{A0B1}_{16} = (10 \times 16^3) + (0 \times 16^2) + (11 \times 16^1) + (1 \times 16^0) = 40960 + 0 + 176 + 1 = 41137 $$ **(iii) Total Number of Memory References:** **Step 1:** Instruction fetch: **1 reference** to read the instruction at address 250. **Step 2:** Address resolution (indirect): **1 reference** to read the pointer at address 250 (which gives EA50). **Step 3:** Operand fetch: **1 reference** to read the operand at the effective address EA50. $$ \text{Total Memory References} = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to include the instruction fetch in memory reference count. Always count **all** accesses to M[]. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing "address field of instruction" with "effective address". The address field is *inside* the instruction; the EA is what it *resolves to* after addressing mode logic. > - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert hex to decimal when the question asks for a numeric value. KTU expects unit consistency. --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the instruction cycle of a basic computer. List all micro-operations involved in the fetch and execute phases for a direct-mode ADD instruction. **(b) [7 Marks]** A computer system has a 32-bit instruction word. The instruction format has 6-bit opcode, 5-bit register field for destination, 5-bit register field for source 1, and the remaining bits for an immediate operand. (i) How many different operations can the processor support? (ii) How many registers are in the register file? (iii) What is the size of the immediate operand? (iv) If the same machine were redesigned with 64 operations, what would be the new size of the immediate operand? #### Model Solution for Question B (a): **Instruction Cycle Overview:** The instruction cycle consists of two main phases: 1. **Fetch Phase:** Common to all instructions (T0, T1, T2). 2. **Execute Phase:** Instruction-specific (T3, T4, T5 if needed). **Micro-operations for Direct-Mode ADD Instruction:** | Clock | Micro-operation | Description | | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **T0** | `AR ← PC` | Load PC into Address Register | | **T1** | `IR ← M[AR]`, `PC ← PC + 1` | Fetch instruction, increment PC | | **T2** | `D0..D7 ← Decode IR(12-14)`, `AR ← IR(0-11)` | Decode opcode, load address from IR | | **T3** | *(skipped for direct mode)* | No address resolution needed | | **T4** | `DR ← M[AR]` | Read operand from memory into Data Register | | **T5** | `AC ← AC + DR`, `SC ← 0` | Add DR to AC, reset Sequence Counter | The `SC (Sequence Counter)` is reset to 0 at the end, which causes the processor to return to T0 for the next instruction, thus starting a new fetch phase. #### Model Solution for Question B (b): **Given:** 32-bit instruction word with the following format: - Opcode: 6 bits - Destination Register: 5 bits - Source 1 Register: 5 bits - Immediate Operand: remaining bits **(i) Number of Operations:** The number of unique operations is determined by the opcode field size: $$ N_{ops} = 2^{\text{opcode bits}} = 2^6 = 64 \text{ operations} $$ **(ii) Number of Registers:** The number of unique registers is determined by the register field size: $$ N_{regs} = 2^{\text{register bits}} = 2^5 = 32 \text{ registers} $$ **(iii) Size of Immediate Operand:** Total instruction size = 32 bits. Subtract the sizes of the other fields. $$ I_{imm} = 32 - O - R_{dest} - R_{src1} = 32 - 6 - 5 - 5 = 16 \text{ bits} $$ So the immediate operand is **16 bits** wide, allowing signed values in the range: $$ \text{Range} = -2^{15} \text{ to } +2^{15} - 1 = -32768 \text{ to } +32767 $$ **(iv) Redesign with 64 Operations:** For 64 operations, the opcode must be: $$ O = \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits (unchanged)} $$ Since the opcode size is unchanged, the immediate operand size remains: $$ I_{imm} = 32 - 6 - 5 - 5 = 16 \text{ bits} $$ **Conclusion:** The redesign does not affect the immediate operand size because 64 operations require exactly 6 bits, which is what was already allocated. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to include PC increment in the fetch phase. PC must be incremented **after** the instruction is read in T1. > - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing `AC ← DR + M[AR]` instead of `AC ← AC + DR` after fetching into DR. The DR is the buffer; the operation is on AC. > - **Common Mistake 3:** For Part (b), students often compute `log_2(N)` incorrectly or fail to apply the formula `N = 2^n` in the right direction. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up signed and unsigned ranges for immediate values — KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to recognize 2's complement signed representation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - **Instruction Components:** Every machine instruction has an **Opcode** (what to do) and **Operand(s)** (where the data is). Some instructions (like HLT) have no operand. - **Standard 16-bit Format:** Opcode (3) + Address (12) + Mode bit (1) = 16 bits. This is the **Mano's basic computer** format most KTU questions use. - **Addressing Mode Memorization Priority:** 1. Immediate → Operand is in instruction (fastest, 1 memory ref) 2. Register → Operand is in CPU register (1 memory ref) 3. Direct → Operand at given address (2 memory refs) 4. Indirect → Operand at address stored at given address (3 memory refs) 5. Relative → PC + offset (used for branches) 6. Indexed → Address + XR (used for arrays) - **Memory Reference Counts (MUST MEMORIZE):** - Immediate: **1** (instruction fetch only) - Register: **1** (instruction fetch only) - Direct: **2** (instruction + operand) - Indirect: **3** (instruction + pointer + operand) - **Instruction Cycle Phases:** T0 (AR←PC) → T1 (Fetch, PC++) → T2 (Decode, AR←addr) → T3 (Indirect resolve, if needed) → T4 (Read operand) → T5 (Execute, SC←0). - **Branch Instructions (BUN):** Modify PC to jump to a new address. The execute phase is `PC ← AR` (no memory read for operand). - **Subroutine Call (BSA):** Stores return address at AR, increments AR, then jumps. This is the foundation of function calls. - **RISC vs CISC:** RISC = fixed length, register-register ops, load-store architecture, easy pipelining. CISC = variable length, memory-memory ops, complex instructions, dense code. - **PC Update Rule:** PC is always incremented to point to the next instruction **before** the execute phase. The PC value used for relative addressing is the **incremented** value (PC of next instruction). - **Effective Address Quick Formulas:** - Direct: `EA = A` - Indirect: `EA = M[A]` - Indexed: `EA = A + XR` - Relative: `EA = PC + A` - Register Indirect: `EA = R[reg]` - **Common Exam Traps:** - Mode bit I=0 is **Direct**, I=1 is **Indirect** (in Mano architecture). - Opcode **7 (111)** is reserved for **I/O and register reference** operations. - Opcode **0 (000)** is reserved for **HLT** or **reference** depending on the I-bit. - **Format Design Rule of Thumb:** Always verify that `Opcode bits + Address bits + Mode bits = Total instruction size`. Sum must match exactly.

topics.title: Operands
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Operands # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Operands ## 1.1 What is an Operand? In computer architecture, an **operand** is the data (or the memory location containing the data) upon which an instruction operates. Every machine instruction in any instruction set architecture (ISA) is essentially built from two parts: 1. An **Opcode** (Operation Code) — specifies *what* operation to perform (e.g., ADD, SUB, MOV). 2. One or more **Operands** — specify *where* the data is, or *what* values to use for the operation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** An operand is the part of a computer instruction that denotes the data to be manipulated or the address of the data. The method used to specify the operand is called the **Addressing Mode**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are at a hotel reception and you want the receptionist to **"Deliver a parcel to Room 302"**. - The **instruction** is: *Deliver a parcel*. - The **operand** is: *Room 302* (a *reference* to the data — the parcel is inside). Now imagine three ways the receptionist can act: | Instruction Style | What Happens | Analogy to Addressing | |---|---|---| | "Deliver the parcel kept in **Box 12** of the storage rack" | Direct fetch from a known slot | **Direct Addressing** | | "Deliver the parcel. The room number is written in **Register A**" | Look up the reference, then go there | **Register Indirect Addressing** | | "Deliver **2 parcels more** than whatever is in Box 5" | The operand is the *value* itself (2) | **Immediate Addressing** | This is exactly how a CPU treats operands — either it **contains the actual data**, or it **contains a pointer (address)** to the data. ### 1.3 Types of Operands Computer instructions operate on four fundamental classes of data: - **Addresses** — memory locations (e.g., pointer to a variable in RAM). - **Numbers** — integers (signed/unsigned) and floating-point values. - **Characters** — ASCII / Unicode character codes. - **Logical Data** — bits and bit-fields representing boolean states or flags. > [!NOTE] > In a 32-bit CPU, each operand is typically **32 bits (4 bytes)** wide. In a 64-bit CPU, it grows to **64 bits (8 bytes)**. KTU questions often assume a **32-bit architecture** unless specified otherwise. ### 1.4 The Role of Addressing Modes The CPU never stores data inside the instruction directly (except in special cases). It uses **Addressing Modes** to interpret the bits of the instruction and figure out *where the operand lives* — in a register, in memory, or embedded inside the instruction itself. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **effective address (EA)** is the *final, actual memory location* of the operand after all addressing mode calculations are performed. This is the address that the CPU's memory unit (MU) ultimately places on the address bus. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Do We Need Multiple Addressing Modes? Different programming situations demand different ways of locating data: - **Constant values** → Immediate addressing. - **Single variables** → Direct addressing. - **Arrays/tables** → Indexed addressing. - **Pointers** → Indirect addressing. - **Stack operations** → Stack addressing. A single fixed mode would make every program either too long (no immediates) or too rigid (no indirection). Thus, modern ISAs like **x86, ARM, and MIPS** provide a rich set of modes. ## 2.2 Comprehensive Classification of Addressing Modes ### A. Immediate Addressing - The operand *itself* is stored inside the instruction. - No memory reference is required. - **Speed:** Fastest possible (operand is in the Instruction Register). - **Drawback:** Limited operand size (fits in instruction word). ### B. Direct (Absolute) Addressing - The instruction contains the **full memory address** of the operand. - Only **one** memory reference is required to fetch the operand. - **Drawback:** Large address size → large instruction size. ### C. Indirect Addressing - The instruction points to a memory cell that **contains the address** of the operand. - Requires **two** memory references (one to fetch the pointer, one to fetch the operand). - **Advantage:** A single instruction can refer to many different addresses by changing the pointer. ### D. Register Addressing - The operand is contained in a CPU register (named in the instruction). - **Zero** memory references — fastest after immediate. - **Drawback:** Very limited address space (only as many registers as the CPU has, e.g., 16 or 32). ### E. Register Indirect Addressing - The instruction specifies a register that **contains the memory address** of the operand. - Requires **one** memory reference. - **Used for:** Pointers, array traversal, table lookups. ### F. Displacement (Indexed) Addressing - Combines a constant offset with a register value. - **EA = A + (R)** or **EA = (R) + A**, where `A` is a constant offset in the instruction and `R` is a register. - **Used for:** Array access, record access in structures. - **Variants:** - **Relative Addressing** — offset is relative to the **Program Counter (PC)**. Used for branch instructions. - **Base-Register Addressing** — `R` is a base register pointing to the start of a data structure. - **Index Addressing** — `R` is an index register that auto-increments/decrements. ### G. Stack Addressing - The operand is on the top of the **stack** (pointed to by the **Stack Pointer SP**). - Instructions like PUSH and POP implicitly use stack addressing. - **Used for:** Subroutine calls, expression evaluation, interrupt handling. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Addressing Mode** | **Effective Address (EA) Formula** | **Memory References** | **Use Case** | |---|---|---|---| | Immediate | Operand is in the instruction | 0 | Constants | | Direct (Absolute) | $\text{EA} = A$ | 1 | Global variables | | Indirect | $\text{EA} = (A)$ | 2 | Pointers, dynamic dispatch | | Register | $\text{EA} = R$ | 0 | Fast ALU operations | | Register Indirect | $\text{EA} = (R)$ | 1 | Pointer dereferencing | | Displacement | $\text{EA} = A + (R)$ | 1 | Arrays, structures | | Relative | $\text{EA} = A + (\text{PC})$ | 1 | Branch instructions | | Indexed | $\text{EA} = A + (R)$ (R auto-modifies) | 1 | Loop-based array access | | Stack | $\text{EA} = (\text{SP})$ | 0 (implicit) | Subroutine calls, PUSH/POP | > [!NOTE] > **Legend used in the table:** > - `A` = constant address/offset field inside the instruction. > - `R` = register whose number is specified in the instruction. > - `(X)` = "contents of memory location/register X" (a pointer dereference). > - `PC` = Program Counter. > - `SP` = Stack Pointer. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Addressing Modes - **Compiler Design:** Compilers map high-level constructs (`a[i]`, `*p`, `&x`) onto specific addressing modes for performance. - **Operating Systems:** Indirect and base-register addressing are used for virtual memory translation and process isolation. - **Embedded Systems:** Stack and immediate modes dominate to keep code small and deterministic. - **Network Processors:** Indexed addressing with auto-increment is used to walk through packet headers efficiently. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Effective Address (EA) — Universal Concept The **Effective Address** is the final, calculated memory address that the CPU uses to fetch or store an operand. The derivation of EA depends on the addressing mode. For a 32-bit architecture, the Memory Address Register (MAR) eventually holds the EA, which is a 32-bit unsigned integer in the range: $$ 0 \leq \text{EA} \leq 2^{32} - 1 $$ This corresponds to a maximum addressable memory of **4 GB**. ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of EA for Each Mode ### Derivation 1 — Direct Addressing The instruction's address field `A` is the EA itself. $$ \text{EA} = A $$ **Example:** `LOAD 2000` → CPU places `2000` on the address bus, memory returns the word at address 2000. ### Derivation 2 — Indirect Addressing The instruction's address field `A` points to a memory cell whose *contents* are the EA. $$ \text{EA} = (A) $$ **Example:** `LOAD @3000` → CPU reads address 3000, finds the value `5000` stored there, then goes to address 5000 to get the operand. ### Derivation 3 — Register Indirect Addressing The instruction names a register `R`. The *contents* of `R` form the EA. $$ \text{EA} = (R) $$ **Example:** If `R1 = 4000`, then `LOAD (R1)` fetches the operand from memory location 4000. ### Derivation 4 — Displacement Addressing The EA is the sum of a constant offset `A` (in the instruction) and the contents of a register `R`. $$ \text{EA} = A + (R) $$ **Example:** If `A = 100` (offset) and `R2 = 5000` (base), then `LOAD 100(R2)` fetches the operand from address `100 + 5000 = 5100`. ### Derivation 5 — Relative Addressing The offset `A` is treated as a **signed** value (2's complement) and added to the PC. This makes branches position-independent. $$ \text{EA} = A + (\text{PC}) $$ **Example:** If `PC = 1000` and the instruction contains `+50`, then `EA = 1000 + 50 = 1050` (branch forward by 50). ### Derivation 6 — Indexed Addressing The EA is the sum of the constant `A` and the index register `IX`, but the register is typically *auto-modified* (incremented/decremented) after access. $$ \text{EA} = A + (IX) $$ $$ (IX) \leftarrow (IX) \pm d \quad \text{(after the access)} $$ where `d` is the step size (often the operand size in bytes). ### Derivation 7 — Stack Addressing The operand is implicitly at the top of the stack. $$ \text{EA} = (\text{SP}) $$ For **PUSH**: First, `SP` is decremented, then data is stored at the new `(SP)`. $$ (\text{SP}) \leftarrow (\text{SP}) - 1 $$ $$ \text{Memory}[(\text{SP})] \leftarrow \text{data} $$ For **POP**: Data is read from `(SP)`, then `SP` is incremented. $$ \text{data} \leftarrow \text{Memory}[(\text{SP})] $$ $$ (\text{SP}) \leftarrow (\text{SP}) + 1 $$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) **Problem:** A 32-bit CPU has a memory of 4 GB. The instruction `ADD (R5), 1000(R6)` is executed. Given that `R5 = 3000H` and `R6 = 5000H`, find the effective address of each operand. Assume the instructions and data are stored starting at address `2000H`. **Step 1: Decode the first operand `(R5)` — Register Indirect Addressing** $$ \text{EA}_1 = (R5) = 3000_\text{H} $$ **Step 2: Decode the second operand `1000(R6)` — Displacement Addressing** $$ \text{EA}_2 = 1000_\text{H} + (R6) = 1000_\text{H} + 5000_\text{H} $$ Perform the hex addition: $$ 1\,000_\text{H} + 5\,000_\text{H} = 6\,000_\text{H} $$ $$ \therefore \text{EA}_2 = 6000_\text{H} $$ **Step 3: Final answer** $$ \text{EA}_1 = 3000_\text{H}, \quad \text{EA}_2 = 6000_\text{H} $$ **Step 4: Total memory references** - `EA_1` needs **1** memory reference (read operand at 3000H). - `EA_2` needs **1** memory reference (read operand at 6000H). - Plus **1** instruction fetch = **3** total memory references. > [!IMPORTANT] > When the same register appears in both operands, KTU examiners often ask for the **number of memory references** explicitly. Always add **+1** for the instruction fetch. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | **Mode** | **EA Calculation Complexity** | **Hardware Cost** | **Speed** | **Flexibility** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Immediate | None (data in IR) | Very Low | Fastest | Low | | Direct | None (direct use) | Low | Fast | Medium | | Indirect | 1 dereference | Medium | Slow | High | | Register | None (direct use) | Very Low | Fastest | Low | | Register Indirect | 1 dereference | Low | Fast | High | | Displacement | 1 ALU add | Medium | Fast | High | | Relative | 1 ALU add (with PC) | Medium | Fast | Medium | | Indexed | 1 ALU add + auto-update | High | Medium | Highest | | Stack | Implicit (SP-based) | Low (in PUSH/POP unit) | Fast | Medium | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Addressing Modes The following Mermaid flowchart captures the taxonomy of addressing modes taught under the KTU 2024 scheme. It begins with the **Operand** node, branches into the three primary operand-storage categories, and then expands each into the canonical KTU modes. ```mermaid graph TD A["OPERAND LOCATION"] --> B["DATA IN INSTRUCTION"] A --> C["DATA IN REGISTER"] A --> D["DATA IN MEMORY"] B --> B1["IMMEDIATE
Operand is the constant itself"] C --> C1["REGISTER
EA = R"] C --> C2["REGISTER INDIRECT
EA = (R)"] D --> D1["DIRECT
EA = A"] D --> D2["INDIRECT
EA = (A)"] D --> D3["DISPLACEMENT
EA = A + R"] D --> D4["RELATIVE
EA = A + PC"] D --> D5["INDEXED
EA = A + IX auto-step"] D --> D6["STACK
EA = SP via PUSH POP"] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Effective Address Computation The figure below illustrates the **data path** used by the CPU's Control Unit to compute the Effective Address. Three sources (instruction constant, register, and PC) feed into an **EA Calculator (Adder)**, whose output drives the **Memory Address Register (MAR)** for operand fetch. ```mermaid graph LR IR["INSTRUCTION REGISTER
Constant Field A"] --> MUX1["MUX"] RG["GENERAL REGISTER
Contents of R"] --> MUX1 PC["PROGRAM COUNTER"] --> MUX1 MUX1 --> ADD["EA CALCULATOR
Adder Unit"] ADD --> MAR["MEMORY ADDRESS REGISTER"] MAR --> BUS["ADDRESS BUS to MEMORY"] MEM["MAIN MEMORY"] -->|"|--| Operand Returned to MDR"|BUS2["DATA BUS"] BUS2 --> ALU["ALU executes Operation"] ``` ## 4.3 Stack PUSH/POP Flow Diagram The schematic below shows the implicit operation of the Stack Pointer during PUSH and POP — a key exam favorite in KTU question papers. ```mermaid graph TD PUSH["PUSH instruction"] --> P1["Step 1: Decrement SP"] P1 --> P2["Step 2: Store operand at Memory SP"] P2 --> P3["Operand now on Top of Stack"] POP["POP instruction"] --> Q1["Step 1: Read operand from Memory SP"] Q1 --> Q2["Step 2: Increment SP"] Q2 --> Q3["Operand transferred to destination register"] ``` > [!NOTE] > In architectures like the **8086**, the stack grows **downward** (toward lower addresses). In the **ARM** and **MIPS**, the convention is also "full-descending" — SP is decremented *before* the store during PUSH. KTU questions often test this growth-direction convention explicitly. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define an *operand*. List any **four types of operands** recognized in a typical computer architecture. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** An *operand* is the data on which a machine instruction performs its specified operation. It is specified either as a constant value, as a CPU register, or as a memory location. The four types of operands are: 1. **Addresses** — references to memory locations. 2. **Numbers** — integer or floating-point numeric values. 3. **Characters** — ASCII/Unicode encoded characters. 4. **Logical data** — individual bits or bit-fields representing boolean conditions. *(Full 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four types.)* --- ### **Q2.** Distinguish between **Immediate Addressing** and **Direct Addressing** modes. State one advantage of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | **Feature** | **Immediate** | **Direct** | |---|---|---| | Where is the operand? | Inside the instruction word | In memory at address `A` | | Memory references | 0 | 1 | | Operand size | Limited by instruction width | Limited by memory word size | | Typical use | Constants, masks | Global variables | - **Advantage of Immediate:** Fastest — no memory access required. - **Advantage of Direct:** Simple — one memory reference gives the operand; useful for named variables. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule (Module 2 Choice):** Answer **any ONE** of the following two full questions. Each carries **14 marks**, split as Part (a) — 7 marks and Part (b) — 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** Explain in detail the following addressing modes with a suitable diagram and EA equation: **(i) Displacement Addressing** **(ii) Relative Addressing** **(iii) Indexed Addressing** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **Part (a) Model Solution** **(i) Displacement Addressing:** The Effective Address is computed by adding a constant offset `A` (encoded in the instruction) to the contents of a register `R`. Equation: $$ \text{EA} = A + (R) $$ The register `R` is usually called the **base register**. The offset `A` may be positive or negative (2's complement signed). It requires **1 memory reference** for the operand fetch and is widely used to access fields within a data structure. **(ii) Relative Addressing:** A special case of displacement where the register used is the **Program Counter (PC)**. The offset `A` is a signed displacement relative to the current instruction. $$ \text{EA} = A + (\text{PC}) $$ This is the foundation of **position-independent code** and is used in branch and jump instructions. A negative offset allows backward branches (loops); a positive offset allows forward branches (skip blocks). **(iii) Indexed Addressing:** The address is again `EA = A + (IX)` where `IX` is an **index register** that is *automatically incremented or decremented* by a fixed step `d` after each access. $$ \text{EA}_{\text{new}} = A + (IX), \quad (IX) \leftarrow (IX) \pm d $$ This is the natural mode for traversing arrays and tables using a loop counter. Step `d` is typically the operand size in bytes. *(Diagrams should show the EA computation block with three inputs: A, R/PC/IX, feeding an adder whose output is the MAR.)* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** A 32-bit CPU has registers R1 = `1000H` and R2 = `3000H`. Memory location `1000H` contains the value `5000H`, and memory location `3000H` contains the value `2000H`. Find the operand value fetched for each of the following addressing modes (assume the instruction is at address `4000H`): **(a)** Immediate `5000H` **(b)** Direct `1000H` **(c)** Register (R1) **(d)** Register Indirect `(R2)` **(e)** Displacement `1000H(R1)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Part (b) Model Solution** **(a) Immediate `5000H`:** The operand *is* the constant `5000H`. **Operand = 5000H** (0 memory references). **(b) Direct `1000H`:** EA = `1000H`. CPU reads memory `1000H` → finds `5000H`. **Operand = 5000H** (1 memory reference). **(c) Register (R1):** R1 = `1000H`, so the operand *is* the value of R1. **Operand = 1000H** (0 memory references). **(d) Register Indirect `(R2)`:** EA = `(R2)` = `3000H`. CPU reads memory `3000H` → finds `2000H`. **Operand = 2000H** (1 memory reference). **(e) Displacement `1000H(R1)`:** $$ \text{EA} = 1000_\text{H} + (R1) = 1000_\text{H} + 1000_\text{H} = 2000_\text{H} $$ CPU reads memory `2000H` → operand value is whatever is stored at that address (not specified, so we report the EA). **EA = 2000H** (1 memory reference). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - In **Register Indirect**, students frequently confuse `(R2)` with "R2 itself". Remember: parentheses mean **"contents of the memory location whose address is R2"** — this requires a *memory read*. > - In **Displacement**, do *not* multiply the offset by 2 or 4; just add it in hexadecimal. > - Always state the **number of memory references** explicitly — examiners award partial credit for that. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Question A* #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** What is an *addressing mode*? Explain **Indirect Addressing** and **Register Indirect Addressing** with neat diagrams. Compare their speed and use cases. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` #### **Part (a) Model Solution** **Definition:** An addressing mode is the rule used by the CPU to interpret the operand field(s) of an instruction to locate the actual data — whether in a register, in memory, or embedded as a constant. **Indirect Addressing:** The instruction's address field `A` points to a memory cell. The *contents* of that cell become the Effective Address. $$ \text{EA} = (A) $$ It requires **2 memory references** (one to read the pointer, one to read the operand). Used when the target address is computed at runtime (e.g., dynamic dispatch tables, pointers to pointers). **Register Indirect Addressing:** A register `R` holds the address. The contents of `R` are the EA. $$ \text{EA} = (R) $$ It requires only **1 memory reference**, since the pointer is already in a fast register. Used heavily for arrays, pointer dereferencing in C, and stack-relative access. **Comparison:** | **Parameter** | **Indirect** | **Register Indirect** | |---|---|---| | Pointer stored in | Memory | CPU Register | | Memory references per fetch | 2 | 1 | | Speed | Slower | Faster | | Register pressure | None | Yes (uses one register) | | Typical use | Pointers to pointers | Array traversal, `*p` in C | --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** Consider a 32-bit processor with the following register and memory state at address `1000H`: | Register | Value | |---|---| | PC | `1000H` | | R1 | `400H` | | R2 | `500H` | | R3 | `600H` | | Memory Address | Content | |---|---| | `400H` | `1000H` | | `500H` | `2000H` | | `600H` | `3000H` | | `3000H` | `ABCDH` | Find the operand value fetched for the instruction `LOAD 400H(R2)` and compute the total memory references required to execute it. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` #### **Part (b) Model Solution** **Step 1: Identify the addressing mode.** `400H(R2)` is a **Displacement (Base-Register) Addressing** mode. The constant offset is `400H` and the base register is `R2`. **Step 2: Compute the Effective Address.** $$ \text{EA} = \text{offset} + (R2) = 400_\text{H} + 500_\text{H} $$ $$ \text{EA} = 900_\text{H} $$ **Step 3: Fetch the operand.** The CPU reads memory address `900H`. Since the table only specifies contents up to `600H`, we treat the operand as `MEM[900H]`. For scoring, report the **EA** as the final answer: $$ \boxed{\text{EA} = 900_\text{H}, \quad \text{Operand} = \text{Memory}[900_\text{H}]} $$ **Step 4: Total memory references.** - **1** reference to fetch the instruction from `PC = 1000H`. - **1** reference to fetch the operand from `900H`. $$ \text{Total Memory References} = 1 + 1 = 2 $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Do not confuse **Register Indirect** (`(R2)`) with **Displacement** (`400H(R2)`). The former has *no* constant term. > - Always convert hex values carefully: `400H + 500H = 900H`, **not** `9000H`. > - Memory references for the *instruction fetch* and *operand fetch* must be **added together** — this is a common 1-mark deduction spot. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Operands** - ✅ An **operand** is the data manipulated by an instruction; it can be a value, a register, or a memory reference. - ✅ The **Effective Address (EA)** is the *final* memory location of the operand used by the memory unit. - ✅ **Immediate mode** has the operand in the instruction itself — **zero** memory references. - ✅ **Direct mode** uses one memory reference; operand is at address `A`. - ✅ **Indirect mode** uses two memory references; operand is at `(A)`. - ✅ **Register mode** is the fastest after immediate — zero memory references. - ✅ **Register Indirect** is `(R)` — one memory reference. - ✅ **Displacement** is `A + (R)` — used for arrays, structures. - ✅ **Relative** is `A + (PC)` — used for branch instructions, position-independent code. - ✅ **Indexed** auto-increments the index register — used in loops over arrays. - ✅ **Stack** uses the **SP** implicitly — PUSH decrements, POP increments. - ✅ Always state the **number of memory references** in KTU derivations — it carries marks. - ✅ In a 32-bit architecture, the address space is **$2^{32} = 4$ GB**. - ✅ Draw the **EA computation block** (MUX → Adder → MAR) in diagrams — examiners reward labeled diagrams. - ✅ Stack grows **downward** in 8086, ARM, MIPS — SP is decremented *before* PUSH, incremented *after* POP. - ✅ Endianness (little vs. big) affects how multi-byte operands are stored — relevant for direct addressing questions. --- **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 2 / Topic: Operands** *Prepared in alignment with KTU Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels L1–L4) and OBE Course Outcomes CO2–CO3.*

topics.title: Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Registers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **register** is a high-speed, discrete storage element constructed from a group of **flip-flops** (or latches), physically located *inside* the Central Processing Unit (CPU). Each flip-flop stores **one bit** of binary information, so an *n*-bit register is implemented as a parallel bank of *n* flip-flops sharing a common clock.

Registers are the **fastest and most expensive** storage elements in the computer hierarchy, holding operands, instructions, addresses, and control information that are in immediate use by the processor.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *“A register is a group of flip-flops capable of storing one word of binary information. Registers are used to store temporary data, instructions, memory addresses, and control information inside the CPU.”*

The number of bits a register can hold is called its **word length** or **register width**. Modern processors use **32-bit, 64-bit, or 128-bit** registers, while the textbook-level KTU examples typically use **8-bit or 16-bit** registers for design clarity.

## 1.2 Physical & Performance Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage element** | D-flip-flop / latch | 1 bit per element |
| **Register width** | 8 / 16 / 32 / 64 bits | Processor-dependent |
| **Access time** | **~1 nanosecond (ns)** | On the CPU silicon die |
| **Capacity** | $2^n$ values for *n* bits | Binary encoding |
| **Cost per bit** | Highest in memory hierarchy | Few hundred to thousands of times costlier than DRAM |
| **Power** | Active (high), Static (low) | Clock-gated in low-power designs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Registers reside **on-chip** and operate at the **processor clock speed**. They are the only storage that the ALU can read from and write to *directly* in a single clock cycle.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The *Workbench vs. Warehouse*

Imagine a carpenter’s workshop:

- The **workbench** is small, keeps only the tools being used *right now*, and is reached instantly. This is a **Register**.
- The **tool-rack on the wall** has more tools, but takes a few seconds to grab. This is **Cache (L1/L2/L3)**.
- The **storeroom** down the road has everything, but requires a trip. This is **Main Memory (RAM)**.
- The **central warehouse in another city** is the slowest — this is the **Hard Disk / SSD**.

When the carpenter (CPU) is hammering, the hammer must be *on the workbench* (in a register). A computer program succeeds or fails based on how cleverly the carpenter keeps the right tools (operands and addresses) on the workbench at the right time — this is what *register allocation* in compilers tries to optimise.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> *n* flip-flops → $2^n$ distinct binary states
> Therefore, *k* address/select lines can address exactly $2^k$ registers.

## 1.4 Why Registers Exist — The *Why* Question

The CPU executes an instruction cycle (Fetch → Decode → Execute → Store). At every micro-step, the following must be available **without delay**:

1. The **next instruction address** to fetch.
2. The **current instruction** being decoded.
3. The **operands** for the ALU.
4. The **destination** for the ALU result.
5. **Status flags** (zero, carry, sign) from the previous operation.

If the CPU had to fetch any of these from main memory (DRAM, ~50–100 ns access time), performance would collapse. Registers solve this by providing **single-cycle** access. This is why *every* CPU — from the 8085 to Apple M-series — dedicates silicon real estate to dozens of registers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Registers

Registers are classified into **two broad families** in the KTU syllabus, and understanding the distinction is critical for exam answers.

### 2.1.1 User-Visible Registers

The programmer (or compiler) can directly reference these via machine instructions. They include:

**a) General-Purpose Registers (GPRs)**
- Used for any function — data, addresses, or scratchpad operations.
- Examples: `EAX, EBX, ECX, EDX` (Intel 8086), `R0–R15` (ARM).
- Modern designs split them into **integer** and **floating-point** sub-pools.

**b) Data Registers**
- Hold intermediate ALU operands (e.g., Accumulator `ACC`).

**c) Address Registers**
- Dedicated to memory addressing (segment, index, base pointer).
- Examples: `SP (Stack Pointer)`, `BP (Base Pointer)`, `SI (Source Index)`.

**d) Condition-Code / Flag Registers**
- Hold single-bit status: `Zero (Z)`, `Carry (C)`, `Sign (N)`, `Overflow (V)`, `Parity (P)`.
- Set automatically by the ALU after every arithmetic operation.

### 2.1.2 Control & Status Registers (CSR)

These are *privileged* — the programmer cannot read/write them directly under normal execution. They are managed by the control unit.

| Register | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **PC** | Program Counter | Holds address of the next instruction to fetch. |
| **IR** | Instruction Register | Holds the instruction currently being decoded. |
| **MAR** | Memory Address Register | Holds the memory location to be accessed. |
| **MDR / MBR** | Memory Data / Buffer Register | Holds the data being read from / written to memory. |
| **SP** | Stack Pointer | Points to the top of the hardware stack. |
| **PSW / FR** | Program Status Word / Flag Register | Holds condition codes, interrupt enable, mode bits. |
| **IBR** | Instruction Buffer Register | Prefetch buffer (used in older designs). |
| **AC** | Accumulator | Implicit operand of most ALU operations. |

## 2.2 Internal CPU Bus Organization

Multiple registers inside the CPU must be interconnected so data can be moved between them and the ALU. The **bus system** provides this interconnection. KTU commonly tests three topologies.

### 2.2.1 Common Bus System with Multiplexers

This is the **most-asked KTU topic** in Module 2.

**Design Principle:**
- *n* registers, each *k* bits wide, share a single common bus.
- For each bit position *i* (1 ≤ *i* ≤ *k*), an **n-to-1 multiplexer** selects which register’s bit *i* drives the bus.
- A set of **select lines** $S = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ controls all multiplexers simultaneously.

**Control Logic:**
$$
S_{\text{select}} = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil
$$

**Number of Multiplexers Required:**
$$
M_{\text{total}} = n \times k
$$

**Example:** 4 registers × 8 bits → $\lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ select lines, 8 multiplexers (one per bit).

### 2.2.2 Three-Bus Internal Organization

A high-performance design with **three internal buses**:
- **Bus A** → carries operand from source register R1 to ALU input *A*.
- **Bus B** → carries operand from source register R2 to ALU input *B*.
- **Bus C** → carries ALU output to the destination register Rd.

**Advantage:** A complete register-to-register operation (R3 ← R1 + R2) completes in **one clock cycle** because two operands are sourced *simultaneously*.

### 2.2.3 Two-Bus Organization

- **Bus A** → source 1 to ALU-A; ALU output also flows to a destination register.
- **Bus B** → source 2 to ALU-B.
- A **temporary register (Y)** stores ALU output for one sub-cycle.
- Operations take **two clock cycles**.

### 2.2.4 Single-Bus Organization

- One common bus shared by *all* registers and both ALU inputs.
- ALU input requires a **buffer register** (since both operands cannot be placed on the same bus simultaneously).
- Operations take **three clock cycles** — slowest, but cheapest in hardware.

## 2.3 Stack Organization

A **stack** is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) structure. Hardware stacks use a fixed memory region pointed to by the **Stack Pointer (SP)**.

### 2.3.1 PUSH Operation
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} - 1 \\
\text{M[SP]} &\leftarrow \text{DR}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.2 POP Operation
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DR} &\leftarrow \text{M[SP]} \\
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.3.3 Four Stack Variants

| Type | SP Direction | Full/Empty | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Ascending** | Increases | Full at SP | x86 (real mode) |
| **Full Descending** | Decreases | Full at SP | ARM, RISC-V |
| **Empty Ascending** | Increases | Empty at SP | Rare |
| **Empty Descending** | Decreases | Empty at SP | DSP processors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The order of `SP` update and `M[SP]` write **matters** in RTL. Decrementing **before** writing is *pre-decrement*; incrementing **after** writing is *post-increment*. The KTU preferred convention is the **Full Descending** model used by ARM.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bits per register | $k$ | Number of flip-flops = register width |
| 2 | Addressable registers | $2^s$ | Where $s$ = number of select lines |
| 3 | Select lines needed | $s = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | For $n$ registers |
| 4 | Multiplexers required | $M = n \times k$ | For $n$ registers of $k$ bits each |
| 5 | Distinct binary states | $2^k$ | Per $k$-bit register |
| 6 | Addressable memory | $2^m$ | Where $m$ = address bus width |
| 7 | Register file size | $n \times k$ | Total flip-flops in a register file |
| 8 | Memory address division | $2^{m-k}$ | Memory words of $k$ bits each |
| 9 | Effective address | $\text{EA} = \text{Base} + \text{Offset}$ | Indexed / based addressing |
| 10 | Stack size | $2^s$ | When SP is $s$ bits wide |

> **Units Note:** Register width is in **bits**. Memory capacity is in **bytes, KB, MB, GB, TB**. Address range is a *count* (dimensionless).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Compiler Optimisation** — Register allocation (graph colouring, linear scan) minimises costly spills to main memory.
2. **Context Switching** — OS kernels save general-purpose and CSR registers to memory during process switches.
3. **Interrupt Handling** — The PC, PSW, and GPRs are *pushed* onto a system stack; on `IRET` they are *popped* back.
4. **Digital Signal Processing (DSP)** — Dedicated accumulator and barrel-shifter registers accelerate convolution and FFT kernels.
5. **Hardware Verification** — RTL (Register Transfer Language) descriptions using VHDL/Verilog model the data path at the register level, then synthesise down to flip-flops and gates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Select-Line Requirement for Common Bus

**Problem:** Derive the number of select lines $s$ required to choose one register out of $n$ registers sharing a common bus.

**Step 1 — State the requirement.**  
We need a unique binary code for every register.

**Step 2 — Number of possible codes with $s$ bits.**  
$$
\text{Codes available} = 2^s
$$

**Step 3 — Require uniqueness.**  
$$
2^s \geq n
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $s$.**  
$$
s \geq \log_2 n
$$

**Step 5 — Apply ceiling (since $s$ must be an integer).**  
$$
s = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil
$$

**Example:** $n = 4$ registers → $s = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = 2$ select lines ($S_1 S_0$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Multiplexer Count for an 8-bit, 8-Register File

**Given:** $n = 8$ registers, $k = 8$ bits each.

**Step 1 — Select lines.**  
$$
s = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3
$$

**Step 2 — Size of each multiplexer.**  
$$
\text{MUX size} = 8\text{-to-}1 \quad (\text{one per bit})
$$

**Step 3 — Number of multiplexers required (one per bit position).**  
$$
M = k = 8
$$

**Step 4 — Total number of data inputs into multiplexers.**  
$$
\text{Inputs} = M \times n = 8 \times 8 = 64
$$

**Step 5 — Hardware complexity comparison.**  
A decoder-based selection would require $n = 8$ AND gates with $s + 1 = 4$ inputs each, in addition to the OR-gate merge tree. Multiplexer-based selection is the **standard KTU design**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RTL for the Micro-Operation `R3 ← R1 + R2` in Three-Bus Architecture

**Step 1 — Identify the source operands.**  
- R1 must appear on **Bus A**.
- R2 must appear on **Bus B**.

**Step 2 — Specify the ALU function.**  
- ALU performs `ADD`.

**Step 3 — Identify the destination.**  
- ALU output is placed on **Bus C**, which is connected to R3’s input.

**Step 4 — Express the gate-level control signals.**  
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SelA} &= 01 \quad (\text{select R1 onto Bus A}) \\
\text{SelB} &= 10 \quad (\text{select R2 onto Bus B}) \\
\text{ALU\_OP} &= \text{ADD} \\
\text{LoadR3} &= 1 \quad (\text{enable write to R3})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Register Transfer Language statement.**  
$$
\text{R3} \leftarrow \text{R1} + \text{R2}
$$

**Step 6 — Time complexity.** This completes in **one clock cycle** because all three buses are active in parallel.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stack Pointer Arithmetic — PUSH Operation

**Problem:** A 16-bit processor has a stack that grows *downward* (descending). Show the RTL for a PUSH of register R0.

**Step 1 — Define the convention.**  
- The stack grows toward lower addresses (descending).
- The top of the stack is at the current value of SP.
- The PUSH operation must make room *before* writing (full-descending convention).

**Step 2 — Decrement the stack pointer.**  
$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1
$$

**Step 3 — Write the operand to the new top of stack.**  
$$
\text{M[SP]} \leftarrow \text{R0}
$$

**Step 4 — Combined RTL.**  
$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1; \quad \text{M[SP]} \leftarrow \text{R0}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**  
Suppose initial SP = 0x1004, and the word size is 16 bits (2 bytes).  
- After decrement: SP = 0x1002.  
- Memory write: M[0x1002] ← R0.  
- Next PUSH will decrement to 0x1000.

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks (e.g., Stallings) define the SP update *after* the memory write for ascending stacks. Always state the convention **before** writing the RTL.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Bus Organisations

| Organisation | Buses | Clock Cycles per R–R op | Temporary Regs | Hardware Cost | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Single-Bus** | 1 | 3 | 2 (Y, Z) | Lowest | Low (1–2 marks) |
| **Two-Bus** | 2 | 2 | 1 (Y) | Medium | Medium (3–5 marks) |
| **Three-Bus** | 3 | 1 | 0 | Highest | High (7–14 marks) |
| **Common Bus + MUX** | 1 + MUX | 1 (read) / 1 (write) | 0 | Medium | **High (7–14 marks)** |

> [!TIP]
> The *most frequent 14-mark KTU question* combines **common bus with multiplexers** for a specified register file size — expect a calculation of select lines, MUX count, and a neat diagram.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem (Typical KTU Style)

**Question:** A CPU has **32 general-purpose registers**, each **16 bits wide**. Design the common bus system using multiplexers. Calculate the number of select lines, the multiplexer size, and the total number of MUXes required.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Number of registers:** $n = 32$.

**Step 2 — Select lines:**  
$$
s = \lceil \log_2 32 \rceil = \lceil 5 \rceil = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplexer size:**  
Since $n = 32$, we need a **32-to-1 multiplexer** for each bit position.

**Step 4 — Multiplexers required (one per bit):**  
$$
M = k = 16
$$

**Step 5 — Total MUX input count:**  
$$
I_{\text{total}} = M \times n = 16 \times 32 = 512
$$

**Step 6 — Decoder (alternative implementation):**  
A 5-to-32 decoder produces 32 enable lines, one per register, eliminating the need for 32-input MUXes but requiring a wired-OR merge per bit (rarely used in practice).

**Answer:** 5 select lines, 16 multiplexers of size 32-to-1.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Internal CPU Register Organisation

The following Mermaid diagram shows how user-visible and control/status registers are interconnected inside a typical single-cycle CPU data path. Note: all node IDs are alphanumeric, and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

```mermaid
graph TD
    pcA[Program Counter PC]
    marA[Memory Address Register MAR]
    mdrA[Memory Data Register MDR]
    irA[Instruction Register IR]
    accA[Accumulator AC]
    gprA[General Purpose Registers R0 to Rn]
    spA[Stack Pointer SP]
    pswA[Program Status Word PSW]
    aluA[ALU]
    memA[Main Memory]
    busA[Internal Common Bus]
    ctrlA[Control Unit]

    pcA --> busA
    marA --> busA
    mdrA --> busA
    irA --> busA
    accA --> busA
    gprA --> busA
    spA --> busA
    pswA --> busA

    busA --> aluA
    aluA --> busA

    busA --> memA
    memA --> busA

    ctrlA -.controls.-> pcA
    ctrlA -.controls.-> aluA
    ctrlA -.controls.-> memA
```

> **Reading Guide:** Solid arrows represent *data flow*. Dotted arrows represent *control signals* issued by the Control Unit. Every register and the ALU can both *source* and *sink* data on the common bus, but only one source drives the bus at a time.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Common Bus System with Multiplexers

This diagram illustrates the multiplexer-based common bus for **4 registers of 8 bits each**, matching the canonical KTU textbook figure (Mano / Stallings).

```mermaid
graph TD
    r0a[Register R0 8 bits]
    r1a[Register R1 8 bits]
    r2a[Register R2 8 bits]
    r3a[Register R3 8 bits]
    mux0[MUX 4 to 1 bit 0]
    mux1[MUX 4 to 1 bit 1]
    mux7[MUX 4 to 1 bit 7]
    busout[Common Bus 8 bits]
    s0[S1 S0 Select Lines]

    r0a --> mux0
    r1a --> mux0
    r2a --> mux0
    r3a --> mux0

    r0a --> mux1
    r1a --> mux1
    r2a --> mux1
    r3a --> mux1

    r0a --> mux7
    r1a --> mux7
    r2a --> mux7
    r3a --> mux7

    mux0 --> busout
    mux1 --> busout
    mux7 --> busout

    s0 -.controls.-> mux0
    s0 -.controls.-> mux1
    s0 -.controls.-> mux7
```

> **Key Observation:** Each bit position has its *own* MUX. All MUXes share the same select lines, ensuring the *same* register is selected bit-by-bit to drive the bus coherently.

## 4.3 Three-Bus Internal CPU Organisation

The high-performance three-bus design permits two source registers to feed the ALU *in parallel* and a destination register to capture the result in the *same* clock cycle.

```mermaid
graph TD
    r1b[Source Register R1]
    r2b[Source Register R2]
    rdA[Destination Register Rd]
    ybA[Temporary Register Y]
    aluB[ALU]
    busAline[Bus A]
    busBline[Bus B]
    busCline[Bus C]

    r1b --> busAline
    busAline --> aluB
    r2b --> busBline
    busBline --> aluB
    aluB --> busCline
    busCline --> rdA
    busCline --> ybA
```

> **Engineering Note:** The three-bus design is the foundation of RISC pipelines (e.g., classic MIPS 5-stage pipeline). The temporary register *Y* is used only for forwarding in the EX stage and is *not* part of the programmer-visible state.

## 4.4 Register-File Read/Write Functional Block

The following diagram abstracts a generic *n × k* register file with two read ports and one write port, a common feature in modern CPUs.

```mermaid
graph LR
    addrA[Read Address A]
    addrB[Read Address B]
    addrW[Write Address W]
    dataW[Write Data D]
    wen[Write Enable WE]
    clk[Clock]
    rfileX[Register File n x k]
    dataA[Data Out A]
    dataB[Data Out B]

    addrA --> rfileX
    addrB --> rfileX
    addrW --> rfileX
    dataW --> rfileX
    wen --> rfileX
    clk --> rfileX
    rfileX --> dataA
    rfileX --> dataB
```

> **Key Point:** The read ports are *asynchronous combinational* (no clock needed to read), but the write port is *synchronous* (data is committed on the clock edge). This split is critical for pipeline performance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
*Define a register. Distinguish between user-visible registers and control-status registers with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

A **register** is a high-speed storage element inside the CPU, built from a group of flip-flops, that holds binary information (data, instructions, or addresses) during instruction execution. Each flip-flop stores one bit, so an *n*-bit register requires *n* flip-flops.

| Aspect | User-Visible Registers | Control & Status Registers |
|---|---|---|
| **Access** | Programmer can directly reference | Privileged access; managed by hardware / OS |
| **Purpose** | Hold operands, addresses, intermediate results | Track CPU state — instruction flow and status |
| **Examples** | GPRs (EAX, R0–R12), Accumulator, Index registers | PC, IR, MAR, MDR, SP, PSW |

User-visible registers support the *data path* of computation, while CSRs govern the *control path* and machine state.

### Question A2
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
*What is the function of the Program Counter (PC) and the Instruction Register (IR)? Why are they classified as control registers?*

**Model Answer:**

- **Program Counter (PC):** Holds the memory address of the *next* instruction to be fetched. After every instruction fetch, the PC is incremented (or replaced by a branch target) by the control unit. Width of PC = width of the address bus (e.g., 16, 32, or 64 bits).
- **Instruction Register (IR):** Holds the instruction word that has just been fetched from memory and is *currently* being decoded and executed. Its width equals the instruction word length.

Both are **control registers** because:
1. They are not used to hold data manipulated by the program.
2. They are managed entirely by the control unit, not by user instructions.
3. They are essential for the *fetch–decode–execute* cycle, which is the CPU’s fundamental control flow.

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Draw and explain the **common bus system using multiplexers** for a CPU that has **8 registers, each 8 bits wide**. Calculate the number of select lines, multiplexer size, and total multiplexers required.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters.**
- Number of registers, $n = 8$.
- Register width, $k = 8$ bits.

**Step 2 — Select lines required.**
$$
s = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil = \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil = 3
$$
We need select lines $S_2, S_1, S_0$.

**Step 3 — Multiplexer size.**
Since $n = 8$, each MUX must be **8-to-1**.

**Step 4 — Multiplexers required.**
One MUX per bit position, hence:
$$
M = k = 8 \text{ multiplexers of size 8-to-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Construction.**
- The bit 0 of all 8 registers is fed to MUX-0; the bit 1 of all 8 registers to MUX-1; … and the bit 7 of all 8 registers to MUX-7.
- The 3 select lines $S_2 S_1 S_0$ are tied to *all* 8 multiplexers.
- The output of all MUXes forms the 8-bit common bus.

**Step 6 — Function.**
Any one of the 8 registers can be gated onto the bus by setting the appropriate 3-bit select code. The remaining 7 registers are isolated (tri-stated). Other registers can latch this bus value into themselves if their *load* signal is asserted by the control unit.

**Step 7 — Diagram (refer to Section 4.2).** A 4-register version is shown; the 8-register version is a 3-bit extension of the same topology.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Explain the **three-bus internal organisation** of the CPU. Show how the operation `R3 ← R1 + R2` is executed in a single clock cycle.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Topology.** Three internal buses exist:
- **Bus A** → connects source register R1 to ALU input *A*.
- **Bus B** → connects source register R2 to ALU input *B*.
- **Bus C** → carries ALU output to the destination register R3.

**Step 2 — Control signals for `R3 ← R1 + R2`.**

| Signal | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `SelA` | Code for R1 | R1 drives Bus A |
| `SelB` | Code for R2 | R2 drives Bus B |
| `ALU_Op` | ADD | ALU performs addition |
| `LoadR3` | 1 | R3 latches Bus C at the next clock edge |

**Step 3 — Clock cycle execution.**
- *Cycle begins:* R1, R2 outputs are placed on Bus A and Bus B respectively (parallel action).
- *Combinational phase:* ALU computes R1 + R2 combinationally and presents the result on Bus C.
- *Cycle end:* R3’s flip-flops latch Bus C value.

**Step 4 — Why single-cycle?** The three buses allow *two source operands* and *one destination operand* to move **simultaneously**, eliminating the need for intermediate temporary registers. Hence the operation completes in **one clock cycle**, the theoretical maximum throughput.

**Step 5 — Reference diagram (Section 4.3).** The three-bus diagram is the canonical illustration; the temporary register *Y* may be present for the EX-to-MEM forwarding path in pipelined designs.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*With a neat diagram, explain **stack organization** in a computer. Define the **PUSH** and **POP** operations using RTL, assuming a **full-descending** stack.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept.** A stack is a LIFO memory region. A dedicated register, the **Stack Pointer (SP)**, always points to the *top* element. In a **full-descending** stack, the SP points to the *last written* location, and the stack grows toward *lower* addresses.

**Step 2 — PUSH operation.** To push the contents of a register (say `DR`) onto the stack:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} - 1 \\
\text{M[SP]} &\leftarrow \text{DR}
\end{aligned}
$$

The stack pointer is decremented *first* (making room), and then the data is written.

**Step 3 — POP operation.** To pop the top of stack into `DR`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DR} &\leftarrow \text{M[SP]} \\
\text{SP} &\leftarrow \text{SP} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

The data is read *first*, and then SP is incremented to “remove” the entry logically.

**Step 4 — Hardware implementation.** A typical KTU answer includes a 16-bit SP, address lines to a memory module, and the memory data register (MDR) acting as the data sink/source. Overflow (stack full) and underflow (stack empty) are detected by checking SP against the stack-base and stack-limit registers.

**Step 5 — Stack variants summary (already in Section 2.3.3).**

**Step 6 — Use cases.** Subroutine return addresses, local variable storage, interrupt context saving, expression evaluation in compilers (postfix).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*A processor has **16 general-purpose registers, each 32 bits wide**. Design the common bus system using multiplexers. Also list the micro-operations and the corresponding select-line codes for three operations: (i) `R2 ← R5`, (ii) `R0 ← R0 + R4`, (iii) `R7 ← R2 - R3`.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Parameters.** $n = 16$, $k = 32$.

**Step 2 — Select lines.**
$$
s = \lceil \log_2 16 \rceil = 4
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplexer count.**
$$
M = k = 32 \text{ multiplexers of size 16-to-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Micro-operation (i): `R2 ← R5`.**

| Signal | Value |
|---|---|
| `SelBus` | 0101 (binary for R5) |
| `ALU_Op` | PASS-A (no operation) |
| `LoadR2` | 1 |
| `LoadR0,R1,R3,...,R15` | 0 |

**Step 5 — Micro-operation (ii): `R0 ← R0 + R4`.**

| Signal | Value |
|---|---|
| `SelA` (Bus A) | 0000 (R0) |
| `SelB` (Bus B) | 0100 (R4) |
| `ALU_Op` | ADD |
| `LoadR0` | 1 |

> **Note:** This operation requires a *two-bus* or *three-bus* internal architecture because both R0 and R4 must be sourced simultaneously. In a *single-bus* design, two sub-cycles are needed: first move R4 to a temporary register, then add R0 to it.

**Step 6 — Micro-operation (iii): `R7 ← R2 - R3`.**

| Signal | Value |
|---|---|
| `SelA` | 0010 (R2) |
| `SelB` | 0011 (R3) |
| `ALU_Op` | SUB |
| `LoadR7` | 1 |

**Step 7 — Diagram reference.** The bus structure is identical to the canonical 4-register design (Section 4.2) but extended to 16 inputs per MUX with 4 select lines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing the bus topology with the register file.** A “common bus” is an *interconnection*, not a register. Drawing registers *as if they were* the bus is the #1 deduction trigger.
> 2. **Incorrect select-line counts.** A frequent mistake is $s = n$ (number of registers) instead of $s = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$. For 8 registers, $s = 3$, **not** 8.
> 3. **Mixing up PUSH and POP RTL order.** The decrement *must precede* the write in PUSH (full-descending). Reversing the order causes SP to be off by one location, costing 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to label bit positions on the multiplexer diagram.** Examiners explicitly look for the MUX *per bit* (i.e., one MUX per bit of the register). A single “MUX block” without bit-level decomposition loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the explicit RTL for every micro-operation.** KTU answers *must* include the symbolic RTL (e.g., `R3 ← R1 + R2`) for every operation discussed. Do not describe only in prose.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Registers**

- **Definition:** A register is a parallel bank of flip-flops; *n* flip-flops = *n*-bit register.
- **Speed:** Registers operate at **CPU clock speed (~1 ns)**, the fastest storage in the hierarchy.
- **User-Visible Registers:** GPRs, Accumulator, Index/Address registers, Condition codes.
- **Control & Status Registers:** PC, IR, MAR, MDR, SP, PSW, IBR.
- **Common Bus System:** Built using one **n-to-1 multiplexer per bit**; select lines = $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$.
- **Multiplexer Count Formula:** $M = n \times k$ inputs total, or $k$ MUXes of size $n$-to-1.
- **Three-Bus Architecture:** R3 ← R1 + R2 in **one clock cycle**; uses Bus A, Bus B, Bus C in parallel.
- **Two-Bus Architecture:** Requires **two clock cycles**; one temporary register Y holds intermediate ALU output.
- **Single-Bus Architecture:** Requires **three clock cycles**; two temporary registers Y and Z needed.
- **Stack Variants:** Full / Empty × Ascending / Descending — *four* combinations, of which **Full Descending** is the KTU / ARM default.
- **PUSH RTL (Full Descending):** `SP ← SP - 1; M[SP] ← DR`.
- **POP RTL (Full Descending):** `DR ← M[SP]; SP ← SP + 1`.
- **RTL Symbols:** `←` = transfer; `:` = conditional; `,` = simultaneous; `;` = sequential.
- **Register File:** A hardware unit containing an array of registers with two read ports and one write port.
- **Modern Widths:** 32-bit and 64-bit registers dominate desktop / server CPUs; 128-bit SIMD registers (SSE, AVX) extend the data path.
- **Examiners’ Favourite 14-Mark Question:** Design a common bus system for a given *n × k* register file, with select-line and MUX count derivations.
- **Examiners’ Favourite 7-Mark Question:** Explain PUSH/POP with RTL for a *specific* stack variant.

> **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Aspirants:** When asked to “design” a bus system, *always* state the parameters first, then derive the formulas, *then* draw the diagram. Marks are split as: **2 marks — formulas, 2 marks — diagram, 2 marks — explanation, 1 mark — neatness.** Miss any one and you lose the proportional slice.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory and Constants; Machine Language: R-Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Memory and Constants; Machine Language: R-Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Memory Organization in MIPS

**Memory** in the MIPS architecture is a large, single-dimensional array of storage locations, conceptually treated as an array of bytes. For MIPS-32, the logical address space is **2³² = 4 GB (Giga Bytes)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **word** in MIPS is defined strictly as **4 bytes = 32 bits**. Word addresses must be **word-aligned**, meaning they are multiples of 4 (0, 4, 8, 12, …). Misaligned accesses cause hardware exceptions.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Hotel Mailbox
Imagine a long corridor of numbered mailboxes (memory). Each mailbox holds exactly one letter (1 byte). For efficiency, when you need a stack of 4 letters (a word), you always grab mailboxes **#0–#3**, or **#4–#7**, etc. You would *never* grab mailboxes #3–#6 because they are not aligned to a clean 4-box boundary. The mailroom supervisor (CPU) refuses to deliver misaligned letters.

### 1.2 Memory Access Instructions (Load/Store Architecture)

MIPS uses a strict **Load/Store Architecture** — only `lw` and `sw` (and their byte/halfword variants) may access memory. ALU instructions never touch memory directly.

| Instruction | Meaning | Size Transferred | Address Range Accessed |
|-------------|---------|------------------|------------------------|
| `lb rt, offset(rs)` | **Load Byte** | 1 byte (8 bits), sign-extended to 32 bits | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |
| `lbu rt, offset(rs)` | **Load Byte Unsigned** | 1 byte, zero-extended to 32 bits | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |
| `lh rt, offset(rs)` | **Load Halfword** | 2 bytes (16 bits), sign-extended | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |
| `lhu rt, offset(rs)` | **Load Halfword Unsigned** | 2 bytes, zero-extended | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |
| `lw rt, offset(rs)` | **Load Word** | 4 bytes (32 bits) | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |
| `sb rt, offset(rs)` | **Store Byte** | 1 byte (lowest 8 bits of `rt`) | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |
| `sh rt, offset(rs)` | **Store Halfword** | 2 bytes (lowest 16 bits of `rt`) | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |
| `sw rt, offset(rs)` | **Store Word** | 4 bytes (full 32 bits of `rt`) | `R[rs] + sign_ext(offset)` |

> [!NOTE]
> The 16-bit **offset** is **sign-extended** to 32 bits before being added to the base register. This is why offsets are limited to approximately ±32 KB from the base register.

### 1.3 Endianness: Big vs. Little

**Endianness** defines the byte-ordering convention used when storing a multi-byte word in memory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Big-Endian:** The **most significant byte (MSB)** is stored at the **lowest** memory address. (Used in older MIPS, SPARC, PowerPC, networking protocols.)
> - **Little-Endian:** The **least significant byte (LSB)** is stored at the **lowest** memory address. (Used in x86, ARM, modern MIPS-32 cores like the M4K.)

### 1.4 Constants and Immediates

A **constant** (or **immediate**) is a fixed numerical value embedded directly inside the instruction's binary encoding. The challenge: most useful constants (memory addresses, large numbers) are **32 bits wide**, but MIPS instructions are also **32 bits wide** — leaving no room for a 32-bit constant in a single instruction.

The MIPS solution is a **two-instruction sequence** using the **Load Upper Immediate (`lui`)** instruction.

### 1.5 Machine Language: R-Type Introduction

**Machine language** is the binary representation of an instruction as it physically resides in memory and is fetched by the CPU. The **R-Type (Register-type)** format is used for instructions that perform arithmetic and logical operations purely on registers (no memory access, no immediate operand in the arithmetic sense).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Word Storage Mechanics

When a 32-bit word is stored at address `A`:
- **Big-Endian:** Byte at address `A+0` = bits 31–24, byte at `A+1` = bits 23–16, byte at `A+2` = bits 15–8, byte at `A+3` = bits 7–0.
- **Little-Endian:** Byte at address `A+0` = bits 7–0, byte at `A+1` = bits 15–8, byte at `A+2` = bits 23–16, byte at `A+3` = bits 31–24.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board problems, the value of a word is **invariant under endianness** — only the byte storage order differs. So `lw $t0, 0($s0)` returns the *same* numeric value regardless of endianness.

## 2.2 Generating Large Constants — The `lui` Trick

The `lui` (Load Upper Immediate) instruction loads a 16-bit immediate into the **upper 16 bits** of a register and **zeroes the lower 16 bits**.

`lui $t0, 0xABCD`  →  `$t0` becomes `0xABCD0000`

To load the 32-bit constant `0xABCD1234`, use:

```mips
lui  $t0, 0xABCD      # $t0 = 0xABCD0000
ori  $t0, $t0, 0x1234  # $t0 = 0xABCD0000 | 0x00001234 = 0xABCD1234
```

## 2.3 The R-Type Machine Language Format

Every R-Type instruction is exactly **32 bits wide**, divided into **six fields**:

| Field | Full Name | Width (bits) | Position (bit index) | Purpose |
|-------|-----------|--------------|----------------------|---------|
| `op` | Operation code | 6 | 31:26 | Identifies the instruction format. For R-Type, this is **always 000000** |
| `rs` | Source register | 5 | 25:21 | First source operand register number |
| `rt` | Source register | 5 | 20:16 | Second source operand register number |
| `rd` | Destination register | 5 | 15:11 | Destination (result) register number |
| `shamt` | Shift amount | 5 | 10:6 | Shift amount for shift instructions (0 otherwise) |
| `funct` | Function code | 6 | 5:0 | Specifies the exact arithmetic/logic operation |

### Visual Field Layout

```
  31       26 25    21 20    16 15    11 10     6 5      0
 ┌──────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┐
 │   op     │   rs   │   rt   │   rd   │ shamt  │ funct  │
 │ (6 bits) │(5 bits)│(5 bits)│(5 bits)│(5 bits)│(6 bits)│
 └──────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┘
   000000    src1     src2      dest     00000     code
```

## 2.4 The MIPS Register File

MIPS has **32 general-purpose registers**, each 32 bits wide, named `$0` to `$31`.

| Number | Name | Conventional Use | Preserved Across Calls? |
|--------|------|------------------|--------------------------|
| 0 | `$zero` (or `$0`) | Constant value 0 (hardwired) | N/A |
| 1 | `$at` | Assembler temporary | No |
| 2–3 | `$v0`, `$v1` | Function return values | No |
| 4–7 | `$a0`–`$a3` | Function arguments | No |
| 8–15 | `$t0`–`$t7` | Temporary (caller-saved) | No |
| 16–23 | `$s0`–`$s7` | Saved (callee-saved) | **Yes** |
| 24–25 | `$t8`–`$t9` | Temporaries | No |
| 26–27 | `$k0`–`$k1` | Kernel reserved | No |
| 28 | `$gp` | Global pointer | **Yes** |
| 29 | `$sp` | Stack pointer | **Yes** |
| 30 | `$fp` | Frame pointer | **Yes** |
| 31 | `$ra` | Return address | **Yes** |

## 2.5 Common R-Type Instructions and Their `funct` Codes

| Instruction | Operation | `op` | `funct` | Syntax | Meaning |
|-------------|-----------|------|---------|--------|---------|
| `add` | Addition | 000000 | 100000 | `add rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← rs + rt` |
| `addu` | Add unsigned | 000000 | 100001 | `addu rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← rs + rt` (no overflow trap) |
| `sub` | Subtraction | 000000 | 100010 | `sub rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← rs − rt` |
| `and` | Bitwise AND | 000000 | 100100 | `and rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← rs & rt` |
| `or` | Bitwise OR | 000000 | 100101 | `or rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← rs \vert rt` |
| `xor` | Bitwise XOR | 000000 | 100110 | `xor rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← rs ^ rt` |
| `nor` | Bitwise NOR | 000000 | 100111 | `nor rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← ~(rs \vert rt)` |
| `slt` | Set less than | 000000 | 101010 | `slt rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← (rs < rt) ? 1 : 0` (signed) |
| `sltu` | Set less than unsigned | 000000 | 101011 | `sltu rd, rs, rt` | `rd ← (rs < rt) ? 1 : 0` (unsigned) |
