| `sll` | Shift left logical | 000000 | 000000 | `sll rd, rt, shamt` | `rd ← rt << shamt` |
| `srl` | Shift right logical | 000000 | 000010 | `srl rd, rt, shamt` | `rd ← rt >> shamt` (zeros filled) |
| `sra` | Shift right arithmetic | 000000 | 000011 | `sra rd, rt, shamt` | `rd ← rt >> shamt` (sign-extended) |
| `jr` | Jump register | 000000 | 001000 | `jr rs` | `PC ← rs` |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet — Memorize for Exam**

| Concept | Equation / Rule |
|---------|------------------|
| Word size | 1 word = **4 bytes = 32 bits** |
| Word address alignment | Address mod 4 = 0 (i.e., A = 0, 4, 8, 12, …) |
| Number of registers | **32** (`$0` to `$31`) |
| `$zero` value | Always **0** (hardwired, read-only) |
| `$sp` initial value | Typically **0x7FFFEFFC** (top of stack area) |
| Memory space (MIPS-32) | **2³² bytes = 4 GB** |
| R-Type `op` field | Always **000000** (binary) / **0x00** (hex) |
| Effective address | EA = R[rs] + sign_extend(offset) |
| `lui` effect | R[rt] ← (immediate << 16) |
| Load 32-bit const `C` | `lui $t, upper16(C); ori $t, $t, lower16(C)` |
| Hex ↔ Binary | 1 hex digit = 4 binary bits |
| Decimal → Hex for KTU | Repeated division-by-16 (Euclidean) |

> [!WARNING]
> The `funct` field is **6 bits wide**, not 8. Students often confuse `funct` for `opcode`. **The `op` field is 6 bits wide and equals 0 for R-Type; the `funct` field is the secondary 6-bit code that selects the actual ALU operation.**

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

R-Type and memory organization are the **fundamental abstraction** of every modern processor — RISC-V, ARM, PowerPC, and even the RISC micro-ops executed by x86 CPUs (Intel's micro-ops are RISC-like). Mastering the 32-bit binary format enables students to read disassembly, understand instruction decoders, write efficient compilers, perform reverse engineering, and design hardware datapaths (the classic Patterson & Hennessy 5-stage pipeline).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Encoding an R-Type Instruction

**Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):** Encode the MIPS instruction `add $t1, $s2, $s3` into its 32-bit binary machine language.

### Step 1: Identify the operation and look up `op` and `funct`

The instruction is `add` (signed addition). Refer to the R-Type table:
- `op` = `000000` (6 bits)
- `funct` = `100000` (6 bits)

### Step 2: Look up the register numbers

Using the standard MIPS register numbering convention:
- `$t1` = register **9**
- `$s2` = register **18**
- `$s3` = register **19**

### Step 3: Map operands to fields

The MIPS syntax `add rd, rs, rt` means `rd ← rs + rt`. So:
- `rd` = `$t1` = 9 → destination
- `rs` = `$s2` = 18 → first source
- `rt` = `$s3` = 19 → second source

### Step 4: Convert all decimal numbers to 5-bit binary

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{rs} = 18_{10} &= 10010_2 \\
\text{rt} = 19_{10} &= 10011_2 \\
\text{rd} = 9_{10}  &= 01001_2 \\
\text{shamt} = 0    &= 00000_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Concatenate the six fields in the correct order

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Machine Code} &= \underbrace{000000}_{\text{op}} \; \underbrace{10010}_{\text{rs}} \; \underbrace{10011}_{\text{rt}} \; \underbrace{01001}_{\text{rd}} \; \underbrace{00000}_{\text{shamt}} \; \underbrace{100000}_{\text{funct}} \\
&= 0000\ 0010\ 0101\ 1001\ 0100\ 0010\ 0000 \; (32 \text{ bits})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Express as hexadecimal (group into 4 bits from left)

$$
0000\ 0010\ 0101\ 1001\ 0100\ 0010\ 0000_2 \;=\; 0x02598020
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The full 8-digit hex form `0x02598020` is the canonical answer expected in KTU board examinations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Decoding a 32-bit R-Type Instruction

**Problem:** Decode the following MIPS machine language instruction (in hex): `0x02324020`.

### Step 1: Convert hex to binary (1 hex digit = 4 binary bits)

$$
0x02324020 \;=\; 0000\ 0010\ 0011\ 0010\ 0100\ 0000\ 0010\ 0000_2
$$

### Step 2: Slice the 32 bits into the six R-Type fields

$$
\underbrace{000000}_{op=0} \; \underbrace{00010}_{rs} \; \underbrace{01100}_{rt} \; \underbrace{01000}_{rd} \; \underbrace{00000}_{shamt} \; \underbrace{100000}_{funct}
$$

### Step 3: Convert each 5/6-bit field to decimal

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{op}      &= 000000_2 = 0_{10} \quad \Rightarrow \text{R-Type} \\
\text{rs}      &= 00010_2  = 2_{10} \quad \Rightarrow \$v0 \\
\text{rt}      &= 01100_2  = 12_{10} \quad \Rightarrow \$t4 \\
\text{rd}      &= 01000_2  = 8_{10}  \quad \Rightarrow \$t0 \\
\text{shamt}   &= 00000_2  = 0_{10}  \quad \Rightarrow \text{not a shift} \\
\text{funct}   &= 100000_2 = 32_{10} \quad \Rightarrow \text{ADD}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Construct the assembly instruction

Since `op = 0` and `funct = 100000`, the operation is `add`. The format is `add rd, rs, rt`:

$$
\texttt{add \$t0, \$v0, \$t4}
$$

Meaning: `$t0 ← $v0 + $t4`

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Loading a Large 32-bit Constant

**Problem:** Write a MIPS code sequence to load the 32-bit constant `0xDEAD1234` into register `$t0`.

### Step 1: Split the constant into upper 16 bits and lower 16 bits

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{upper 16 bits} &= 0xDEAD \\
\text{lower 16 bits} &= 0x1234
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Use `lui` to place the upper 16 bits in the upper half of `$t0`

After `lui $t0, 0xDEAD`, the register contains:

$$
R[\$t0] = 0xDEAD\,0000
$$

### Step 3: Use `ori` to OR-in the lower 16 bits

`ori` performs a bitwise OR with a zero-extended 16-bit immediate. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R[\$t0] &= 0xDEAD0000 \;\vert\; 0x00001234 \\
       &= 0xDEAD1234
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Assembly Code

```mips
lui  $t0, 0xDEAD      # $t0 = 0xDEAD0000
ori  $t0, $t0, 0x1234 # $t0 = 0xDEAD1234  (OR with 0x1234)
```

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Memory Storage and Endianness

**Problem:** A 32-bit word value `0xA1B2C3D4` is stored at memory address `0x100`. Show how the bytes are arranged in (a) Big-Endian and (b) Little-Endian systems.

### (a) Big-Endian (MSB at lowest address)

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
\text{Address} & \text{Byte Stored} \\
\hline
0x100 & \text{0xA1 (MSB, bits 31–24)} \\
\hline
0x101 & \text{0xB2 (bits 23–16)} \\
\hline
0x102 & \text{0xC3 (bits 15–8)} \\
\hline
0x103 & \text{0xD4 (LSB, bits 7–0)} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

### (b) Little-Endian (LSB at lowest address)

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|}
\hline
\text{Address} & \text{Byte Stored} \\
\hline
0x100 & \text{0xD4 (LSB, bits 7–0)} \\
\hline
0x101 & \text{0xC3 (bits 15–8)} \\
\hline
0x102 & \text{0xB2 (bits 23–16)} \\
\hline
0x103 & \text{0xA1 (MSB, bits 31–24)} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In both schemes, an `lw` from address `0x100` returns the same numeric value `0xA1B2C3D4`. Endianness only affects the **byte layout in memory**, not the logical value.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Byte-Load Sign Extension

**Problem:** Suppose memory byte at address `0x2000` contains `0x9A`. What is the value placed in `$t0` after executing `lb $t0, 0($s0)` (where `$s0 = 0x2000`)?

### Step 1: Read the 8-bit byte

Byte = `0x9A` = `1001 1010` in binary.

### Step 2: Sign-extend to 32 bits

Since the MSB of `0x9A` is `1`, sign-extension replicates the sign bit (1) through the upper 24 bits:

$$
\underbrace{1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1001\ 1010}_{32 \text{ bits}} = \text{0xFFFFFF9A}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the signed decimal value

$$
\text{0xFFFFFF9A} = -102 \quad \text{(in two's complement)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If `lbu` (unsigned) had been used, `$t0` would equal `0x0000009A` = **+154** (unsigned). The choice of `lb` vs `lbu` matters for KTU problems involving ASCII or byte manipulation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 R-Type Instruction Decoding Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fetch 32-bit Instruction from Memory] --> B[Send to Instruction Decoder]
    B --> C{Inspect op field bits 31-26}
    C -->|op = 000000| D[R-Type Format Detected]
    C -->|op != 000000| E[I-Type or J-Type Format]
    D --> F[Read rs register bits 25-21]
    D --> G[Read rt register bits 20-16]
    D --> H[Read rd register bits 15-11]
    D --> I[Read shamt bits 10-6]
    D --> J[Read funct bits 5-0]
    F --> K[Read R rs from Register File]
    G --> L[Read R rt from Register File]
    H --> M[Route to Writeback rd destination]
    I --> N[Pass to Barrel Shifter]
    J --> O[ALU Operation Selector]
    K --> P[ALU Inputs]
    L --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q[ALU Performs Operation]
    Q --> M
    M --> R[Result Written to R rd]
    R --> S[PC increments PC + 4]
```

## 4.2 Memory Access Datapath (Load/Store)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IM[Instruction Memory] --> ID[Instruction Decoder]
    ID --> RS[Read rs base register]
    RS --> ADD[32-bit Adder]
    ID --> SE[Sign-Extend 16-bit offset]
    SE --> ADD
    ADD --> EA[Effective Address EA]
    EA --> DM[Data Memory]
    DM --> LB[Load Path: lb, lh, lw]
    DM --> SB[Store Path: sb, sh, sw]
    LB --> ZSE[Optional Zero/Sign Extend]
    ZSE --> RD[Write to rt register]
    SB --> DM
    ID2[Instruction Decoder for rt] --> SB
```

## 4.3 MIPS Register File Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RF[MIPS 32 x 32-bit Register File]
    RF --> R0[$0  $zero = 0 always]
    RF --> R1[$1  $at assembler temp]
    RF --> R2[$2-$3  $v0-$v1 return values]
    RF --> R3[$4-$7  $a0-$a3 arguments]
    RF --> R4[$8-$15 $t0-$t7 temporaries]
    RF --> R5[$16-$23 $s0-$s7 saved registers]
    RF --> R6[$24-$25 $t8-$t9 extra temporaries]
    RF --> R7[$26-$27 $k0-$k1 kernel]
    RF --> R8[$28 $gp global pointer]
    RF --> R9[$29 $sp stack pointer]
    RF --> R10[$30 $fp frame pointer]
    RF --> R11[$31 $ra return address]
```

## 4.4 Endianness Byte Storage Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[32-bit Word 0xA1B2C3D4] --> ST{Storage Order}
    ST -->|Big-Endian| BE[Addr 0x100: 0xA1 MSB]
    BE --> BE2[Addr 0x101: 0xB2]
    BE2 --> BE3[Addr 0x102: 0xC3]
    BE3 --> BE4[Addr 0x103: 0xD4 LSB]
    ST -->|Little-Endian| LE[Addr 0x100: 0xD4 LSB]
    LE --> LE2[Addr 0x101: 0xC3]
    LE2 --> LE3[Addr 0x102: 0xB2]
    LE3 --> LE4[Addr 0x103: 0xA1 MSB]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Big-Endian and Little-Endian byte ordering with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer:
**Big-Endian** stores the **most significant byte (MSB)** of a word at the **lowest memory address**, while **Little-Endian** stores the **least significant byte (LSB)** at the **lowest memory address**.

**Example:** Consider storing the 32-bit value `0x12345678` at address `0x100`:

| Address | Big-Endian Byte | Little-Endian Byte |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|
| 0x100 | **0x12** (MSB) | **0x78** (LSB) |
| 0x101 | 0x34 | 0x56 |
| 0x102 | 0x56 | 0x34 |
| 0x103 | 0x78 (LSB) | 0x12 (MSB) |

MIPS-32 typically uses **Big-Endian**, while x86 and most modern ARM use **Little-Endian**. The numeric value of the word is the same when read back via `lw`. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain how a 32-bit constant is loaded into a MIPS register using `lui` and `ori`. Why is this two-instruction sequence needed?**

#### Model Answer:
A MIPS instruction is **32 bits wide**, leaving no room to encode a 32-bit constant directly in a single instruction. The `lui` (Load Upper Immediate) instruction places a 16-bit immediate into the **upper 16 bits** of a register and zeroes the lower 16 bits. A second instruction, `ori` (OR Immediate), inserts the desired value into the **lower 16 bits** using bitwise OR.

**Example — To load `0xCAFEBABE`:**
```mips
lui  $t0, 0xCAFE       # $t0 = 0xCAFE0000
ori  $t0, $t0, 0xBABE  # $t0 = 0xCAFE0000 | 0x0000BABE = 0xCAFEBABE
```

This **two-step** approach efficiently packs a 32-bit constant into two 32-bit instructions. (3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option (a) (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the R-Type instruction format in detail. Draw the field diagram and describe each field.

**(b) [7 marks]** Encode the following MIPS assembly instructions into 32-bit machine code (hexadecimal):
  1. `add $s0, $s1, $s2`
  2. `sub $t0, $t3, $t5`
  3. `and $v0, $a0, $a1`

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **R-Type (Register-Type) format** is used for MIPS instructions that operate purely on registers (no memory access). All R-Type instructions are **32 bits wide** and contain **six fields**:

```
  31       26 25    21 20    16 15    11 10     6 5      0
 ┌──────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┬────────┐
 │   op     │   rs   │   rt   │   rd   │ shamt  │ funct  │
 │ (6 bits) │(5 bits)│(5 bits)│(5 bits)│(5 bits)│(6 bits)│
 └──────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┴────────┘
   000000     src1     src2     dest      0       code
```

**Field Descriptions:**

| Field | Width | Description |
|-------|-------|-------------|
| `op` | 6 | Operation code. **Always `000000`** for R-Type. |
| `rs` | 5 | First source register number. |
| `rt` | 5 | Second source register number. |
| `rd` | 5 | Destination register (result). |
| `shamt` | 5 | Shift amount (for shift instructions; 0 for arithmetic/logic). |
| `funct` | 6 | Function code — selects the exact operation (e.g., `add`=100000, `sub`=100010). |

The `op` field identifies the format; the `funct` field distinguishes among all R-Type operations. Common `funct` codes: `add`=32, `addu`=33, `sub`=34, `and`=36, `or`=37, `slt`=42, `sll`=0, `srl`=2. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Register Numbers Used:**
`$s0`=16, `$s1`=17, `$s2`=18, `$t0`=8, `$t3`=11, `$t5`=13, `$v0`=2, `$a0`=4, `$a1`=5

---

**Instruction 1: `add $s0, $s1, $s2`**

Operation: `add`, so `funct` = `100000`; `op` = `000000`; `shamt` = `00000`.

Field mapping: `rd = $s0 = 16`, `rs = $s1 = 17`, `rt = $s2 = 18`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary:} \quad & 000000 \; 10001 \; 10010 \; 10000 \; 00000 \; 100000 \\
& 0000\;0010\;0011\;0010\;1000\;0000\;0010\;0000 \\
\text{Hex:} \quad & 0x02328020
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 2: `sub $t0, $t3, $t5`**

Operation: `sub`, so `funct` = `100010`; `op` = `000000`; `shamt` = `00000`.

Field mapping: `rd = $t0 = 8`, `rs = $t3 = 11`, `rt = $t5 = 13`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary:} \quad & 000000 \; 01011 \; 01101 \; 01000 \; 00000 \; 100010 \\
& 0000\;0010\;1011\;0110\;1010\;0000\;1000\;1000 \;(\text{recheck}) \\
& \text{Grouping correctly: } 000000\;01011\;01101\;01000\;00000\;100010 \\
& \Rightarrow 0000\;0010\;1011\;0110\;1000\;0000\;1000\;1000 \\
\text{Hex:} \quad & 0x02B64088
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Instruction 3: `and $v0, $a0, $a1`**

Operation: `and`, so `funct` = `100100`; `op` = `000000`; `shamt` = `00000`.

Field mapping: `rd = $v0 = 2`, `rs = $a0 = 4`, `rt = $a1 = 5`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Binary:} \quad & 000000 \; 00100 \; 00101 \; 00010 \; 00000 \; 100100 \\
& 0000\;0010\;0001\;0100\;0100\;0000\;0010\;0100 \\
\text{Hex:} \quad & 0x00844024
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Option (b) (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the memory addressing modes in MIPS. Discuss word alignment with an example.

**(b) [7 marks]** Decode the following 32-bit MIPS machine language instructions and write the corresponding assembly instructions:
  1. `0x00A63820`
  2. `0x00E55822`
  3. `0x012A482A`

#### Model Solution for (a):

**MIPS Memory Addressing Modes:**

MIPS uses a single primary addressing mode for memory access: **Base Register + Signed Offset** (also called **register indirect with displacement**).

The **Effective Address (EA)** is computed as:

$$
\text{EA} = R[\text{rs}] + \text{sign\_extend}(\text{offset})
$$

where `offset` is a **16-bit signed immediate** (range: −32768 to +32767), sign-extended to 32 bits before the addition.

**Word Alignment:**
A MIPS **word is 4 bytes (32 bits)**. Word addresses must be aligned to **4-byte boundaries**, i.e., the address must be a multiple of 4 (0, 4, 8, 12, …). This is called **natural alignment**.

**Example:** Consider memory with the following byte addresses:
- Address `0x0000` (word-aligned): valid for `lw $t0, 0($s0)`
- Address `0x0002` (misaligned): would cause an **address error exception**
- Address `0x0003` (misaligned): would cause an **address error exception**

The 16-bit offset allows access to ±32 KB of data from any base register. To reach further, the global pointer `$gp` or the stack pointer `$sp` is used, or the offset is split using `lui` + add. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Decode each 32-bit hex instruction using the R-Type format:**

---

**Instruction 1: `0x00A63820`**

Convert to binary:
$$
0x00A63820 = 0000\;0000\;1010\;0110\;0011\;1000\;0010\;0000
$$

Slice the fields:
$$
\underbrace{000000}_{op} \; \underbrace{00101}_{rs} \; \underbrace{00011}_{rt} \; \underbrace{00111}_{rd} \; \underbrace{00000}_{shamt} \; \underbrace{100000}_{funct}
$$

Convert fields to decimal:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{op} &= 0 \Rightarrow \text{R-Type} \\
\text{rs} &= 5 \Rightarrow \$a1 \\
\text{rt} &= 3 \Rightarrow \$v1 \\
\text{rd} &= 7 \Rightarrow \$a3 \\
\text{funct} &= 100000_2 = 32 \Rightarrow \text{ADD}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assembly:** `add $a3, $a1, $v1` (i.e., `$a3 ← $a1 + $v1`)

---

**Instruction 2: `0x00E55822`**

Convert to binary:
$$
0x00E55822 = 0000\;0000\;1110\;0101\;0101\;1000\;0010\;0010
$$

Slice the fields:
$$
\underbrace{000000}_{op} \; \underbrace{00111}_{rs} \; \underbrace{00101}_{rt} \; \underbrace{01011}_{rd} \; \underbrace{00000}_{shamt} \; \underbrace{100010}_{funct}
$$

Convert fields to decimal:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{rs} &= 7 \Rightarrow \$a3 \\
\text{rt} &= 5 \Rightarrow \$a1 \\
\text{rd} &= 11 \Rightarrow \$t3 \\
\text{funct} &= 100010_2 = 34 \Rightarrow \text{SUB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assembly:** `sub $t3, $a3, $a1` (i.e., `$t3 ← $a3 − $a1`)

---

**Instruction 3: `0x012A482A`**

Convert to binary:
$$
0x012A482A = 0000\;0001\;0010\;1010\;0100\;1000\;0010\;1010
$$

Slice the fields:
$$
\underbrace{000000}_{op} \; \underbrace{01001}_{rs} \; \underbrace{01010}_{rt} \; \underbrace{01001}_{rd} \; \underbrace{00000}_{shamt} \; \underbrace(101010)_{funct}
$$

Convert fields to decimal:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{rs} &= 9 \Rightarrow \$t1 \\
\text{rt} &= 10 \Rightarrow \$t2 \\
\text{rd} &= 9 \Rightarrow \$t1 \\
\text{funct} &= 101010_2 = 42 \Rightarrow \text{SLT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assembly:** `slt $t1, $t1, $t2` (i.e., `$t1 ← ($t1 < $t2) ? 1 : 0`, signed comparison)

**(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students confuse the order of `rd` and `rs` in the syntax. Remember: `add rd, rs, rt` → **rs is FIRST source, rt is SECOND source, rd is DESTINATION**. Mis-mapping these inverts the arithmetic.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** When converting hex to binary, students **frequently miscount the bit positions** (e.g., placing `rt` in the `rd` field). Always verify: `op` is bits 31–26, `rs` is bits 25–21, `rt` is 20–16, `rd` is 15–11, `shamt` is 10–6, `funct` is 5–0.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to convert decimal register numbers to **5-bit binary** (with leading zeros). For example, register 5 = `00101` (5 bits), not `101`.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing `lui`'s effect — it places the immediate in the **upper 16 bits**, **not** the lower 16 bits. The lower 16 bits are zeroed.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Treating `lb` and `lbu` as identical. `lb` sign-extends; `lbu` zero-extends. This single-bit difference can cause a 200+ error in numeric values.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Exam**

- ✅ A MIPS **word = 4 bytes = 32 bits**; word addresses must be **multiples of 4**.
- ✅ MIPS has **32 general-purpose registers**, each **32 bits wide**, named `$0` to `$31`.
- ✅ `$zero` (`$0`) is **hardwired to 0** and read-only.
- ✅ MIPS uses **Load/Store architecture**: only `lw`, `sw`, `lh`, `sh`, `lb`, `sb`, `lhu`, `lbu` access memory.
- ✅ The **Effective Address (EA)** for loads/stores is: `EA = R[rs] + sign_extend(offset)`.
- ✅ **Big-Endian**: MSB at lowest address; **Little-Endian**: LSB at lowest address. Both give the **same** logical value.
- ✅ A 32-bit constant cannot fit in one instruction. Use `lui $t, upper16` followed by `ori $t, $t, lower16`.
- ✅ **R-Type format** is **six fields** totaling 32 bits: `op(6) | rs(5) | rt(5) | rd(5) | shamt(5) | funct(6)`.
- ✅ For **all R-Type instructions**, the `op` field is **always `000000`**; the `funct` field distinguishes the operation.
- ✅ Syntax: `add rd, rs, rt` → **`rd` is the destination, `rs` and `rt` are sources** — order matters!
- ✅ Key `funct` codes to memorize: `add`=`100000`, `sub`=`100010`, `and`=`100100`, `or`=`100101`, `slt`=`101010`, `sll`=`000000`, `srl`=`000010`.
- ✅ To encode an R-Type: write all 6 fields in binary, concatenate left-to-right (`op rs rt rd shamt funct`), then group into 4-bit nibbles for hex.
- ✅ To decode: convert hex to 32-bit binary, slice into the 6 fields, convert each field to decimal, look up registers and `funct`.
- ✅ Memory size in MIPS-32 = **2³² bytes = 4 GB**.
- ✅ The 16-bit signed offset in load/store gives a range of **−32,768 to +32,767** bytes (≈ ±32 KB) from the base register.
- ✅ `lb` **sign-extends** the byte to 32 bits; `lbu` **zero-extends** — be careful with values > 0x7F.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I-Type
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** I-Type

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: I-Type Instructions — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **MIPS (Microprocessor without Interlocked Pipelined Stages) Instruction Set Architecture**, an **I-Type instruction** (Immediate-type instruction) is a class of **fixed 32-bit machine instructions** whose encoding format contains a **6-bit opcode**, a **5-bit source register operand ($rs$)**, a **5-bit target register operand ($rt$)**, and a **16-bit immediate constant field**.

The I-type format is used for three primary classes of operations in MIPS:
1. **Arithmetic/Logical operations with immediates** (e.g., `addi`, `andi`, `ori`, `slti`)
2. **Data transfer (load/store) instructions** (e.g., `lw`, `sw`, `lh`, `lbu`)
3. **Conditional branch instructions** (e.g., `beq`, `bne`)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** I-type instructions form the backbone of constant manipulation and memory-access operations. Unlike R-type (Register-type) instructions that operate on two register operands, I-type instructions embed the second operand *directly inside the instruction bits*, eliminating the need to load constants from memory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post-it Note" on a Form

Imagine you are filling out a government form. The form has fixed boxes:
- **Box 1:** Form Type (e.g., Tax Form) → analogous to **opcode (6 bits)**
- **Box 2:** Your Name → analogous to **$rs$ (5 bits)** — source register
- **Box 3:** Spouse's Name → analogous to **$rt$ (5 bits)** — destination register
- **Box 4:** A small writable note area where you scribble an extra number → analogous to **immediate field (16 bits)**

The I-type is exactly this: a 32-bit "form" with a built-in small note area. Instead of looking up the constant from a register, the constant *travels with the instruction itself*, like a Post-it Note stuck onto the instruction. This makes I-type instructions **self-contained** for small constants, but the trade-off is that the constant is limited to **16 bits in width**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Instruction word size:** **32 bits = 4 bytes**
> - **Opcode field width:** **6 bits** → can encode $2^6 = 64$ distinct primary opcodes
> - **Register field width:** **5 bits** → can address $2^5 = 32$ general-purpose registers (`$0` through `$31`)
> - **Immediate field width:** **16 bits** → range $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$ = **−32768 to +32767**
> - **General-purpose registers in MIPS:** **32** (numbered `$0` to `$31`)

> [!NOTE]
> **$0 register is hardwired to zero** — this is a crucial MIPS design feature exploited in I-type instructions to synthesize operations like `move` (e.g., `addi $t0, $0, 100`) or to clear a register.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2: I-Type — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 32-Bit I-Type Encoding Layout

Every I-type instruction follows this precise bit-level layout:

| Field Name | Bits (MSB → LSB) | Width | Purpose |
|------------|------------------|-------|---------|
| `opcode`   | bits [31:26]     | 6 bits | Specifies the operation (e.g., `001000` for `addi`) |
| `rs`       | bits [25:21]     | 5 bits | First source register number |
| `rt`       | bits [20:16]     | 5 bits | Destination register (for ALU ops) or base register (for loads) |
| `immediate` | bits [15:0]    | 16 bits | Signed 2's complement constant |

The bit positions are fixed across **all** I-type instructions; only the opcode and the semantic interpretation of fields change.

## 2.2 Why 16 Bits? — The Design Trade-off

- A 16-bit field can encode integers from **−32,768 to +32,767**.
- This range covers approximately **99% of constants** used in real programs (loop counters, small offsets, ASCII characters, small array indices).
- The remaining constants (large addresses, large numbers) are constructed using a **two-instruction sequence**:
  1. `lui $rt, upper_16_bits` — loads upper 16 bits into the upper half of a register.
  2. `ori $rt, $rt, lower_16_bits` — ORs the lower 16 bits into position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** MIPS uses a **load-store architecture** — only `lw`/`sw` (and variants) access memory. All other operations are register-to-register. This clean separation is what makes the fixed 32-bit instruction format viable.

## 2.3 Sign Extension — The Critical Mechanism

The 16-bit immediate is **signed 2's complement**. When it must be used in a 32-bit ALU operation, the CPU performs **sign extension**:

$$
\text{Extended 32-bit value} = \begin{cases} \text{immediate} & \text{if immediate} \geq 0 \\ \text{immediate} + 2^{32} & \text{if immediate} < 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Mechanically**, sign extension replicates the **most significant bit (MSB)** of the 16-bit value across the upper 16 positions of the 32-bit word.

- If `immediate[15] = 0` (positive): upper 16 bits become `0000 0000 0000 0000`.
- If `immediate[15] = 1` (negative): upper 16 bits become `1111 1111 1111 1111`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign extension vs Zero extension:**
> - **Sign extension** is used for **arithmetic immediates** (e.g., `addi`, `lw`, `beq`) where negative offsets are meaningful.
> - **Zero extension** is used for **logical immediates** (e.g., `andi`, `ori`, `xori`) where the immediate is treated as an unsigned bit-pattern.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|---------------|
| Immediate value range | $-2^{15} \leq \text{imm} \leq 2^{15} - 1$ | **−32768** to **+32767** |
| Zero-extended unsigned range | $0 \leq \text{imm} \leq 2^{16} - 1$ | **0** to **65535** |
| Sign extension (positive imm) | Upper 16 bits = `0x0000` | MSB of imm = 0 |
| Sign extension (negative imm) | Upper 16 bits = `0xFFFF` | MSB of imm = 1 |
| Branch target address | $\text{PC}_{\text{new}} = (\text{PC}+4) + (\text{imm} \times 4)$ | PC-relative, word-aligned |
| `lui` + `ori` 32-bit constant | $L = \text{upper}_{16} \cdot 2^{16} + \text{lower}_{16}$ | Two-instruction sequence |
| Memory address (load/store) | $\text{EA} = \text{Reg[rs]} + \text{imm}$ | Effective Address formula |
| LW base+offset | `lw $rt, offset($rs)` → EA = $R[rs] + \text{sign\_ext(imm)}$ | Word offset |
| SW base+offset | `sw $rt, offset($rs)` → EA = $R[rs] + \text{sign\_ext(imm)}$ | Store 32 bits |
| Total instruction size | $6 + 5 + 5 + 16 = 32$ bits | Fixed format |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

I-type format underpins **nearly every program** ever written:
- **Embedded systems** rely on small constants (sensor thresholds, pin addresses) — exactly the I-type sweet spot.
- **Compiler code generation** uses I-type for the vast majority of arithmetic and memory operations.
- **Branch instructions** use the I-type immediate as a *relative offset* — making position-independent code possible and reducing code size (no need for absolute 32-bit addresses in branches).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 2: I-Type — Step-by-Step Decoding, Encoding, and Address Computation

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Decoding Machine Code to Assembly

**Problem:** Decode the following 32-bit I-type machine instruction into MIPS assembly.

$$
\text{Machine code (hex):} \quad \texttt{0x21280005}
$$

### Step 1 — Convert Hex to 32-bit Binary

$$
\texttt{0x21280005} = \underbrace{0010\,0001}_{\texttt{0x21}}\;\underbrace{0010\,1000}_{\texttt{0x28}}\;\underbrace{0000\,0000}_{\texttt{0x00}}\;\underbrace{0000\,0101}_{\texttt{0x05}}
$$

So the 32-bit binary is:

$$
\texttt{0010\;0001\;0010\;1000\;0000\;0000\;0000\;0101}
$$

### Step 2 — Slice the Fields

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{opcode} &= \text{bits}[31:26] = \texttt{001000}_{2} = 8_{10} \\
\text{rs} &= \text{bits}[25:21] = \texttt{00101}_{2} = 5_{10} \\
\text{rt} &= \text{bits}[20:16] = \texttt{01000}_{2} = 8_{10} \\
\text{immediate} &= \text{bits}[15:0] = \texttt{0000\;0000\;0000\;0101}_{2} = 5_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Interpret the Opcode

Opcode `001000` corresponds to the MIPS instruction **`addi`** (Add Immediate, signed).

### Step 4 — Map Registers

| Register Number | MIPS Name |
|-----------------|-----------|
| 5  | `$a1` (first argument register) |
| 8  | `$t0` (temporary register) |

### Step 5 — Form the Assembly Instruction

$$
\boxed{\texttt{addi \$t0, \$a1, 5}}
$$

**Semantic meaning:** $R[\$t0] \leftarrow R[\$a1] + 5$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** The sign bit of `0x0005` is `0`, so sign extension yields `0x00000005` — correct positive value.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Encoding Assembly to Machine Code

**Problem:** Encode the following MIPS assembly instruction into 32-bit machine code.

$$
\texttt{lw \$t1, 32(\$s2)}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify Opcode

`lw` (Load Word) → opcode = `100011` (decimal 35, hex `0x23`).

### Step 2 — Identify Registers

| Operand | Register | Number |
|---------|----------|--------|
| `$t1` (destination) | `$rt` | 9 |
| `$s2` (base) | `$rs` | 18 |

### Step 3 — Identify Immediate

Offset = 32 in decimal. Convert to 16-bit binary (positive, so sign bit = 0):

$$
32_{10} = 0000\;0000\;0010\;0000_{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Concatenate the Fields

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\texttt{opcode} &&: \texttt{100011} \\
&\texttt{rs=18}   &&: \texttt{10010} \\
&\texttt{rt=9}    &&: \texttt{01001} \\
&\texttt{imm=32}  &&: \texttt{0000\;0000\;0010\;0000}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenated (MSB → LSB):

$$
\texttt{100011\;10010\;01001\;0000\;0000\;0010\;0000}
$$

### Step 5 — Group into Bytes for Hex

$$
\underbrace{1000\,1110}_{\texttt{0x8E}}\;\underbrace{0100\,1000}_{\texttt{0x48}}\;\underbrace{0000\,0000}_{\texttt{0x00}}\;\underbrace{0010\,0000}_{\texttt{0x20}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\texttt{0x8E480020}}
$$

### Step 6 — Verify Effective Address Formula

$$
\text{EA} = R[\$s2] + \text{sign\_ext}(32) = R[\$s2] + 32
$$

This matches the intended memory access. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Branch Target Calculation

**Problem:** A `beq $t0, $t1, label` instruction is located at memory address `0x00400020`. The `immediate` field encodes the value `−4`. Compute the branch target address.

### Step 1 — Recall the Branch Address Formula

$$
\text{Target Address} = (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{immediate} \times 4)
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC} &= \texttt{0x00400020} \\
\text{PC} + 4 &= \texttt{0x00400024} \\
\text{immediate} &= -4 \\
\text{immediate} \times 4 &= -16
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Final Address

$$
\text{Target} = \texttt{0x00400024} + (-16) = \texttt{0x00400014}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multiply by 4?** Because MIPS instructions are **word-aligned** (4 bytes each). The offset in the instruction is given in *words*, not bytes. This is a standard MIPS optimization that doubles the effective branch range to **±128 KB**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Sign Extension of a Negative Immediate

**Problem:** The 16-bit immediate field of an `addi` instruction is `0xFFFC`. What is the 32-bit sign-extended value (in decimal)?

### Step 1 — Identify Sign Bit

MSB of `0xFFFC` = `1` → number is negative.

### Step 2 — Convert 16-bit to Decimal (Signed)

$$
0xFFFC = -(2^{16} - 0xFFFC) = -(65536 - 65532) = -4
$$

### Step 3 — Sign-Extend to 32 bits

$$
\text{Result}_{32} = \texttt{0xFFFFFFFC}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify

$$
\texttt{0xFFFFFFFC}_{32} = -4_{10} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5: Constructing a 32-bit Constant

**Problem:** Load the constant `0x12345678` into `$t0`.

### Step 1 — Split into Upper and Lower 16-bit Halves

$$
\texttt{0x12345678} = \underbrace{\texttt{0x1234}}_{\text{upper}} \cdot 2^{16} + \underbrace{\texttt{0x5678}}_{\text{lower}}
$$

### Step 2 — Use `lui` (Load Upper Immediate)

`lui` is an **I-type instruction** (opcode `001111`) that places its 16-bit immediate into the upper 16 bits of `$rt` and zeros the lower 16 bits.

$$
\texttt{lui \$t0, 0x1234} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R[\$t0] = \texttt{0x12340000}
$$

### Step 3 — Use `ori` (OR Immediate) to Insert Lower Bits

`ori` is an **I-type instruction** (opcode `001101`) that ORs the zero-extended 16-bit immediate into `$rt`.

$$
\texttt{ori \$t0, \$t0, 0x5678} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R[\$t0] = \texttt{0x12345678}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `ori` and not `addi`?** Because `addi` would sign-extend `0x5678` (positive, so OK here), but for constants whose lower 16 bits have MSB = 1, `addi` would corrupt the result. `ori` is **safe for all 16-bit bit-patterns**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 2: I-Type — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 I-Type 32-Bit Encoding Layout

The following block diagram illustrates the exact bit-level partitioning of an I-type instruction word.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["31-26: opcode (6 bits)"] --> B["25-21: rs (5 bits)"]
    B --> C["20-16: rt (5 bits)"]
    C --> D["15-0: immediate (16 bits)"]
```

| Slice      | Field Name   | Width  | Role in Arithmetic Immediate | Role in Load/Store | Role in Branch |
|------------|--------------|--------|------------------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 31 to 26   | `opcode`     | 6 bits | Identifies operation          | Identifies operation| Identifies operation |
| 25 to 21   | `rs`         | 5 bits | First source register         | Base address register | Compare register A |
| 20 to 16   | `rt`         | 5 bits | Destination register          | Source (for store) or destination (for load) | Compare register B |
| 15 to 0    | `immediate`  | 16 bits| Signed constant (sign-ext.)   | Signed byte offset  | Signed word offset (×4) |

## 4.2 Functional Execution Flow of an I-Type Instruction

The diagram below maps the data path and control flow when the CPU fetches, decodes, and executes a typical I-type instruction such as `addi`.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Instruction Fetch: Read 32-bit word from PC"] --> B["Decode: Extract opcode, rs, rt, immediate"]
    B --> C{"Opcode determines I-type category"}
    C -->|Arithmetic Immediate| D["Read R rs from Register File"]
    D --> E["Sign-extend 16-bit immediate to 32 bits"]
    E --> F["ALU performs op: R rs + sign-ext immediate"]
    F --> G["Write result into R rt"]
    C -->|Load or Store| H["Read R rs from Register File"]
    H --> I["Sign-extend immediate to 32 bits"]
    I --> J["ALU computes Effective Address = R rs + immediate"]
    J --> K{"Load or Store?"}
    K -->|Load| L["Read memory at EA into R rt"]
    K -->|Store| M["Write R rt into memory at EA"]
    C -->|Branch| N["Read R rs and R rt from Register File"]
    N --> O["Sign-extend immediate and shift left by 2"]
    O --> P["Compare R rs and R rt"]
    P --> Q{"Are they equal or not equal?"}
    Q -->|Yes branch condition| R["Update PC to PC+4 + sign-ext immediate times 4"]
    Q -->|No branch condition| S["PC increments normally to PC+4"]
```

## 4.3 Sign-Extension Functional Unit

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["16-bit immediate (bit 15 is sign)"] --> B{"Sign bit = 0?"}
    B -->|Yes Positive| C["Upper 16 bits set to 0x0000"]
    B -->|No Negative| D["Upper 16 bits set to 0xFFFF"]
    C --> E["32-bit sign-extended value ready for ALU"]
    D --> E
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardware Note:** The sign-extension unit is a simple combinational circuit: the upper 16 output lines are tied directly to the sign bit (bit 15) of the input. This adds essentially **zero propagation delay**, making it a critical enabler for high-clock-frequency pipelined CPUs.

## 4.4 Categorical Map of Common MIPS I-Type Instructions

```mermaid
graph TD
    IType["I-Type Instructions (32 bits)"] --> Cat1["Arithmetic/Logical Immediate"]
    IType --> Cat2["Data Transfer (Load/Store)"]
    IType --> Cat3["Conditional Branch"]
    IType --> Cat4["Special: lui"]

    Cat1 --> A1["addi opcode 001000"]
    Cat1 --> A2["addiu opcode 001001"]
    Cat1 --> A3["andi opcode 001100"]
    Cat1 --> A4["ori opcode 001101"]
    Cat1 --> A5["xori opcode 001110"]
    Cat1 --> A6["slti opcode 001010"]
    Cat1 --> A7["sltiu opcode 001011"]

    Cat2 --> B1["lw opcode 100011"]
    Cat2 --> B2["sw opcode 101011"]
    Cat2 --> B3["lh opcode 100001"]
    Cat2 --> B4["lhu opcode 100101"]
    Cat2 --> B5["lb opcode 100000"]
    Cat2 --> B6["sb opcode 101000"]

    Cat3 --> C1["beq opcode 000100"]
    Cat3 --> C2["bne opcode 000101"]

    Cat4 --> D1["lui opcode 001111"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 2: I-Type — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: What is meant by an I-type instruction in the MIPS instruction set? Mention the size and function of each of its fields.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **I-type (Immediate-type) instruction** in MIPS is a fixed-length 32-bit machine instruction that includes a 16-bit constant (immediate value) embedded directly within the instruction itself, alongside register operands.

The four fields are:

1. **Opcode (6 bits, bits 31–26):** Specifies the operation to be performed (e.g., `addi`, `lw`, `beq`).
2. **rs (5 bits, bits 25–21):** The first source register number.
3. **rt (5 bits, bits 20–16):** The second source register (in branches) or destination register (in ALU/lw operations) or source data register (in stores).
4. **Immediate (16 bits, bits 15–0):** A signed 2's complement constant that is sign-extended to 32 bits before use in the ALU.

These fields occupy a total of $6 + 5 + 5 + 16 = 32$ bits, matching the MIPS fixed instruction size.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the term "sign extension" in the context of I-type instructions. Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Sign extension** is the process of expanding a 16-bit signed immediate value to a full 32 bits before it is used by the 32-bit ALU. The most significant bit (bit 15) of the immediate is replicated across all upper 16 bit positions.

**It is necessary because:**
1. The MIPS ALU operates on **32-bit operands** — a 16-bit value cannot be fed directly without increasing its width.
2. The immediate is a **signed** quantity; simply zero-padding it would incorrectly convert negative numbers (e.g., −1 = `0xFFFF`) into large positive numbers (`0x0000FFFF` = 65535).
3. Correct sign extension preserves the **arithmetic value** of the constant, ensuring correct results for subtraction, comparison, and offset-based addressing.

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Between A and B)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Encode the following MIPS assembly instructions into their 32-bit hexadecimal machine code. Show the bit slicing clearly. (7 marks)

1. `addi $t2, $s0, -7`
2. `sw $t3, 64($sp)`

**(b)** A `beq $t4, $t5, LOOP` instruction is located at address `0x00400040`. The 16-bit immediate field in its machine code is `0x0010`. Calculate the target address of the branch. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Step 1 — Register Number Lookup:**

| Register | Number | Binary |
|----------|--------|--------|
| `$t2` | 10 | `01010` |
| `$s0` | 16 | `10000` |
| `$t3` | 11 | `01011` |
| `$sp` | 29 | `11101` |

**Step 2 — Opcode Lookup:**

- `addi` → opcode = `001000` (decimal 8)
- `sw` → opcode = `101011` (decimal 43)

**Step 3 — Instruction 1: `addi $t2, $s0, -7`**

Immediate value = −7. Represent −7 in 16-bit 2's complement:

$$
-7 = 2^{16} - 7 = 65536 - 7 = 65529 = \texttt{0xFFF9}
$$

In binary: `1111 1111 1111 1001`.

Bit slicing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{opcode} &= \texttt{001000} \\
\text{rs=16} &= \texttt{10000} \\
\text{rt=10} &= \texttt{01010} \\
\text{imm=-7} &= \texttt{1111\;1111\;1111\;1001}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenated 32-bit word:

$$
\texttt{001000\;10000\;01010\;1111\;1111\;1111\;1001}
$$

Group into 4-bit nibbles:

$$
\underbrace{0010}_{\texttt{0x2}}\;\underbrace{0010}_{\texttt{0x2}}\;\underbrace{0000}_{\texttt{0x0}}\;\underbrace{1010}_{\texttt{0xA}}\;\underbrace{1111}_{\texttt{0xF}}\;\underbrace{1111}_{\texttt{0xF}}\;\underbrace{1111}_{\texttt{0xF}}\;\underbrace{1001}_{\texttt{0x9}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\texttt{0x220AFFF9}}
$$

**Step 4 — Instruction 2: `sw $t3, 64($sp)`**

Immediate value = 64. In 16-bit binary:

$$
64 = \texttt{0000\;0000\;0100\;0000}
$$

Bit slicing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{opcode} &= \texttt{101011} \\
\text{rs=29} &= \texttt{11101} \\
\text{rt=11} &= \texttt{01011} \\
\text{imm=64} &= \texttt{0000\;0000\;0100\;0000}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenated:

$$
\texttt{101011\;11101\;01011\;0000\;0000\;0100\;0000}
$$

Group into nibbles:

$$
\underbrace{1010}_{\texttt{0xA}}\;\underbrace{1111}_{\texttt{0xF}}\;\underbrace{0101}_{\texttt{0x5}}\;\underbrace{0110}_{\texttt{0x6}}\;\underbrace{0000}_{\texttt{0x0}}\;\underbrace{0000}_{\texttt{0x0}}\;\underbrace{0100}_{\texttt{0x4}}\;\underbrace{0000}_{\texttt{0x0}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\texttt{0xAF560040}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

The **branch target address** formula in MIPS is:

$$
\text{Target} = (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{immediate}_{16} \times 4)
$$

**Given:**
- PC = `0x00400040`
- immediate (hex) = `0x0010`

**Step 1 — Convert immediate to decimal and multiply by 4:**

$$
0x0010 = 16_{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad 16 \times 4 = 64
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PC + 4:**

$$
\text{PC} + 4 = \texttt{0x00400040} + 4 = \texttt{0x00400044}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the offset:**

$$
\text{Target} = \texttt{0x00400044} + 64 = \texttt{0x00400044} + \texttt{0x40} = \texttt{0x00400084}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Target Address} = \texttt{0x00400084}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Since the offset is positive and the multiplier is 4, we are moving **forward** in memory. The target address `0x00400084` is indeed 64 bytes (16 words) ahead of `0x00400044`, confirming the calculation. ✓

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Alternative Choice)]**

**(a)** Decode the following 32-bit I-type machine instructions into MIPS assembly. Show the field extraction. (7 marks)

1. `0x8D280020`
2. `0x2129000A`

**(b)** Explain with a suitable example how a 32-bit constant is loaded into a register using only I-type instructions. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Solution:**

**Instruction 1: `0x8D280020`**

Step 1 — Convert to 32-bit binary:

$$
\texttt{0x8D280020} = \texttt{1000\;1101\;0010\;1000\;0000\;0000\;0010\;0000}
$$

Step 2 — Slice the fields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{opcode} &= \text{bits}[31:26] = \texttt{100011}_{2} = 35_{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \texttt{lw} \\
\text{rs} &= \text{bits}[25:21] = \texttt{01001}_{2} = 9_{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \$t1 \\
\text{rt} &= \text{bits}[20:16] = \texttt{01000}_{2} = 8_{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \$t0 \\
\text{immediate} &= \text{bits}[15:0] = \texttt{0000\;0000\;0010\;0000}_{2} = 32_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Assembly:

$$
\boxed{\texttt{lw \$t0, 32(\$t1)}}
$$

**Meaning:** Load the 32-bit word at memory address $R[\$t1] + 32$ into `$t0`.

---

**Instruction 2: `0x2129000A`**

Step 1 — Convert to 32-bit binary:

$$
\texttt{0x2129000A} = \texttt{0010\;0001\;0010\;1001\;0000\;0000\;0000\;1010}
$$

Step 2 — Slice the fields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{opcode} &= \texttt{001000}_{2} = 8_{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \texttt{addi} \\
\text{rs} &= \texttt{01001}_{2} = 9_{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \$t1 \\
\text{rt} &= \texttt{01000}_{2} = 8_{10} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \$t0 \quad \text{(Note: 01000 was previously \$t0; let's recheck)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recheck:** `0010 1001` = `0x29`. Let me re-decode `rt`: bits[20:16] = `01000` = 8 → **`$t0`**. But wait — for `addi`, the destination is `rt`. So destination is `$t0`. Let me re-verify the original bit pattern: `00100001 00101000 00000000 00001010` → yes, rt = `01000` = 8 = `$t0`.

Actually, re-reading my hex: `0x2129000A`. Let me re-slice carefully:
- bits[31:26] = `001000` = `addi` ✓
- bits[25:21] = `10010` = 18 = `$s2` ✓
- bits[20:16] = `01000` = 8 = `$t0` ✓
- bits[15:0] = `0x000A` = 10 ✓

$$
\boxed{\texttt{addi \$t0, \$s2, 10}}
$$

**Meaning:** $R[\$t0] \leftarrow R[\$s2] + 10$

---

**Part (b) — Solution:**

A 32-bit constant cannot fit in a single 16-bit immediate field. MIPS uses a two-instruction sequence of I-type instructions:

**Step 1 — `lui` (Load Upper Immediate):** Places the upper 16 bits into bits [31:16] of the destination register and clears bits [15:0] to zero.

**Step 2 — `ori` (OR Immediate):** Zero-extends its 16-bit immediate and ORs it into the lower 16 bits of the destination register.

**Example:** Load the constant `0xABCD1234` into `$t0`.

Step 1 — Split the constant:

$$
\texttt{0xABCD1234} = \underbrace{\texttt{0xABCD}}_{\text{upper}} \cdot 2^{16} + \underbrace{\texttt{0x1234}}_{\text{lower}}
$$

Step 2 — Issue the two I-type instructions:

$$
\texttt{lui \$t0, 0xABCD} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R[\$t0] = \texttt{0xABCD0000}
$$

$$
\texttt{ori \$t0, \$t0, 0x1234} \quad\Rightarrow\quad R[\$t0] = \texttt{0xABCD1234}
$$

**Why `ori` (not `addi`)?** Because if the lower 16 bits had MSB = 1 (e.g., `0x8000`), `addi` would sign-extend it to `0xFFFF8000` and add it, producing `0xABCC8000` (wrong). `ori` zero-extends and bitwise-ORs, guaranteeing the exact bit pattern is preserved.

$$
\boxed{R[\$t0] = \texttt{0xABCD1234}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Wrong field sizes:** Students often write `rs = 6 bits` or `immediate = 5 bits`. The exact widths (6, 5, 5, 16) **must be memorized**.
> 2. **Bit-order reversal:** When converting to hex, the 32-bit binary is grouped as **bits 31–28, 27–24, 23–20, 19–16, 15–12, 11–8, 7–4, 3–0** — preserving the original MSB-to-LSB order. Do **not** reverse the order.
> 3. **Forgetting sign extension in branches:** The branch offset is **multiplied by 4** because it is a *word* offset. Skipping this multiplication loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Using `addi` instead of `ori` for 32-bit constant construction:** Causes errors for negative lower 16 bits.
> 5. **Misinterpreting `$rt`:** In **branches**, `$rt` is a *compare* register, not a destination. In **stores**, `$rt` is the *source* data. The role of `rt` changes with opcode.
> 6. **Sign extension of 16-bit to 32-bit:** Examiners explicitly look for `0x0000` prefix (positive) or `0xFFFF` prefix (negative). Skipping this step loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ I-type is a **fixed 32-bit** format with **6 + 5 + 5 + 16 = 32** bits, partitioned as **`opcode | rs | rt | immediate`**.
- ✅ The **opcode (6 bits)** uniquely identifies the operation within the I-type family.
- ✅ The **immediate field is 16 bits, signed 2's complement** — range **−32768 to +32767**.
- ✅ **Sign extension** is mandatory for arithmetic and address-offset operations; **zero extension** is used for logical immediates (`andi`, `ori`, `xori`).
- ✅ **Register fields are 5 bits** → 32 registers total. `$0` is hardwired to **zero**.
- ✅ **Load/Store instructions** use I-type with effective address: $\text{EA} = R[\text{rs}] + \text{sign\_ext(imm)}$.
- ✅ **Branch instructions** use PC-relative addressing: $\text{Target} = (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{imm} \times 4)$ — note the **×4 multiplier**.
- ✅ **Branch range:** Approximately $\pm 2^{15} \times 4 = \pm 128\,\text{KB}$ from the branch instruction.
- ✅ **`lui` + `ori`** is the standard two-instruction sequence for loading any 32-bit constant.
- ✅ In I-type, `rt` plays different roles based on opcode: **destination** (arithmetic/lw), **source** (sw), or **second comparator** (branches).
- ✅ Common I-type opcodes to memorize: `addi` (001000), `lw` (100011), `sw` (101011), `beq` (000100), `bne` (000101), `lui` (001111), `ori` (001101).
- ✅ The 16-bit immediate limitation is a deliberate MIPS design choice — favoring **simplicity and speed** over **constant range**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: J-Type Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** J-Type Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the **MIPS (Microprocessor without Interlocked Pipelined Stages)** instruction set architecture — which forms a foundational reference model for the KTU **PERUT418 – Computer Organisation and Architecture** syllabus — every machine instruction is encoded as a **fixed 32-bit word**. The bits are partitioned into fields whose meaning depends on the **instruction class (format)**. The three classical MIPS formats are:

| Format | Primary Use | Key Fields |
|---|---|---|
| **R-Type** | Register-to-register ALU operations | `opcode (6) | rs (5) | rt (5) | rd (5) | shamt (5) | funct (6)` |
| **I-Type** | Immediate ALU ops, loads/stores, branches | `opcode (6) | rs (5) | rt (5) | immediate (16)` |
| **J-Type** | Unconditional jumps (control transfer) | `opcode (6) | address (26)` |

A **J-Type (Jump-Type) instruction** is a control-transfer instruction whose 32-bit encoding is split into two fields only:
- A **6-bit `opcode`** that uniquely identifies the operation (e.g., `000010` for `j`, `000011` for `jal`).
- A **26-bit `target address`** field that encodes the destination of the jump.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** J-Type instructions are the principal mechanism in MIPS for implementing **unconditional control transfer** to an arbitrary 32-bit address. The 26-bit field is **not** the full target address; it is combined with the upper bits of the **Program Counter (PC)** at execution time using a method known as **pseudo-direct addressing**.

The two canonical J-Type instructions are:
- **`j target`** — Jump. Load `PC ← target_address`.
- **`jal target`** — Jump And Link. Load `PC ← target_address` and simultaneously save the return address (`PC + 4`) into register `$ra` (i.e., `$31`). This is the standard call primitive used to invoke subroutines/procedures.

> [!NOTE]
> Note that the **R-type** instructions `jr rs` (Jump Register) and `jalr rs, rd` (Jump And Link Register) also perform control transfer, but they are *encoded* using the R-format (funct field distinguishes them). The "J-Type" classification is strictly reserved for the **opcode-driven** `j` and `jal` encodings.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "GPS Bookmark" View

Imagine you are reading a **3,000-page engineering textbook** (this represents the entire 4 GB address space of a 32-bit MIPS processor). Each page is a 32-bit **instruction word**.

- An **R-type** instruction is like turning to the *next page* — a tiny, local step governed by arithmetic.
- An **I-type branch** (`beq`, `bne`) is like skipping forward or backward a small number of pages using a relative offset written in the margin — useful for nearby destinations like the end of a loop.
- A **J-type instruction** is a **hardcover bookmark** 🔖. It lets you *fly* to a faraway chapter, but the bookmark is small (only 26 pages of space), so the textbook binder *automatically assumes* you want to stay in the **same quarter of the book (same 256 MB region)**. If you try to jump out of that quarter, the binder silently clips you back inside it.

The **opcode (6 bits)** tells the textbook "this is a bookmark" (vs. a margin note or page turn). The **26-bit target** is the page number *within the current quarter*, and the hardware concatenates the quarter identifier from your current location (the upper 4 bits of the PC) to reconstruct the full address.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Encoding Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard MIPS J-Type Encoding Metrics (Mandatory for KTU Board Exams):**
> - **Instruction word size:** 32 bits = **4 bytes**
> - **Opcode field width:** **6 bits** → $2^6 = 64$ distinct opcodes
> - **Address field width:** **26 bits**
> - **Left-shift amount:** **2 bits** (because word addresses are byte addresses ÷ 4)
> - **Effective word-address range (after shift):** 28 bits → $2^{28} = 268{,}435{,}456$ words = **256 MB**
> - **Bits inherited from PC+4:** **Upper 4 bits** of the 32-bit address
> - **Bits always appended:** Lower **2 bits = 00** (word alignment guarantee)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Walk-Through — What Happens When a `j` Executes

The execution of a J-Type instruction is performed in the following strictly ordered steps by the **MIPS hardware datapath**:

1. **Instruction Fetch (IF):** The PC supplies the address of the current 32-bit instruction word to instruction memory. After a successful fetch, the PC is unconditionally incremented by 4 (to `PC + 4`) so it points to the next sequential instruction.
2. **Instruction Decode / Register Fetch (ID):** The 6-bit opcode `000010` (`j`) or `000011` (`jal`) is decoded. The 26-bit `target` field is extracted. For `jal`, register `$ra` (R31) is identified as the link register.
3. **Target Address Construction:** The hardware assembles the 32-bit jump destination using **pseudo-direct addressing** (see §2.2).
4. **PC Update:** On the next clock edge, the PC is overwritten with the constructed 32-bit target. The previously latched `PC + 4` value is captured into `$ra` (only for `jal`).
5. **Next Cycle:** The IF stage now fetches from the new PC — control flow has been transferred.

## 2.2 Pseudo-Direct Addressing — The Core 'How'

The reason a 26-bit field is sufficient to reach any 32-bit address lies in two architectural conventions:

**(a) Word Alignment:** MIPS instructions are always aligned to 4-byte boundaries. Hence the lowest **two bits** of any valid instruction address are always `00`. We can omit them from the encoding and **shift the 26-bit field left by 2**, giving us a 28-bit *word* address.

**(b) Region Locality:** Branches and most jumps stay within the same **256 MB region** of memory (i.e., the upper 4 bits of the PC do not change). The hardware therefore **reuses the upper 4 bits of `PC + 4`** to form the high-order part of the new PC.

The result is the **canonical pseudo-direct addressing formula** that appears in every MIPS datapath textbook and is a guaranteed KTU board-exam high-yield item.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Raw instruction width | $W = 32$ | bits | Fixed for all MIPS instructions |
| 2 | Opcode field | $W_{op} = 6$ | bits | Leftmost (MSB-side) field |
| 3 | Target field | $W_{tgt} = 26$ | bits | Right-side field for J-type |
| 4 | Effective word-address bits | $W_{tgt} + 2 = 28$ | bits | After left-shift by 2 |
| 5 | Region bits inherited from PC+4 | $4$ | bits | Upper 4 bits of $(PC+4)$ |
| 6 | Lower bits appended | $2$ | bits | Hardwired to `00` |
| 7 | **Jump Target Address** $\text{JTA}$ | $\text{JTA} = \{(PC+4)_{31..28},\; \text{instr}_{25..0},\; 2'b00\}$ | 32-bit | **Pseudo-direct addressing** |
| 8 | Reach (per region) | $2^{28} = 256\ \text{MB}$ | bytes | Maximum forward/backward jump span |
| 9 | `j` opcode | $\texttt{000010}_2 = 0x02$ | hex | Jump |
| 10 | `jal` opcode | $\texttt{000011}_2 = 0x03$ | hex | Jump and Link |
| 11 | `jal` link register | `$ra = \$31` | — | Saves $PC+4$ of the instruction *after* the `jal` |
| 12 | PC increment per fetch | $PC \leftarrow PC + 4$ | bytes | Standard MIPS byte step |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the notation $(PC+4)_{31..28}$ means "the upper four bits (bits 31, 30, 29, 28) of the 32-bit value $PC+4$." The braces `{, , }` denote **bitwise concatenation**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

J-Type pseudo-direct addressing reflects a deep engineering trade-off between **encoding density** and **addressing range**. The same principle appears in modern architectures:

- **ARM** uses 24-bit signed offsets for conditional branches, achieving branch reach of ±16 MB — a similar locality-aware compromise.
- **RISC-V** uses multi-bit opcode extensions (`JAL` uses a J-type variant) but still constrains the direct jump to a 1 MiB region, falling back to indirect jumps for long-range transfers.
- **x86** uses a 32-bit relative displacement for its `JMP rel32`, sidestepping the issue entirely by *always* using a 32-bit relative offset.

The KTU examiner expects students to recognize that MIPS's pseudo-direct addressing **minimizes hardware cost** (no 32-bit adder is needed for the jump — just a shifter and a few muxes) at the price of a **256 MB region limit** that programmers must respect when laying out code (or work around using `jr`/`jalr` for long-distance transfers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pseudo-Direct Target Address

We begin with the **32-bit instruction word** at the output of the instruction memory, call it $I$. We partition it:

$$
I = \underbrace{I_{31..26}}_{\text{opcode (6 bits)}}\ \Vert\ \underbrace{I_{25..0}}_{\text{target (26 bits)}}
$$

The encoding constraints of MIPS guarantee that every instruction address $A$ is a multiple of 4:

$$
A \equiv 0 \pmod{4}
$$

Therefore the two least-significant bits $A_1$ and $A_0$ are always $0$. We can **strip them off** and represent $A$ in *word units* using only 30 bits.

To convert the 26-bit field $I_{25..0}$ into a *word* address, we left-shift by 2:

$$
\text{word\_addr} = I_{25..0} \ll 2
$$

This produces a 28-bit value. To obtain a full 32-bit byte address, we need **4 more high-order bits** and the **2 implicit low-order zeros**. By the pseudo-direct convention, these high-order bits are taken from the upper nibble of the address of the instruction **immediately following** the jump (which is $PC + 4$, since the PC is already incremented during fetch).

$$
\text{Jump Target Address} = \big((PC+4)_{31..28}\big)\ \Vert\ \big(I_{25..0} \ll 2\big)
$$

Or, in fully bit-sliced form (showing every bit position):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{JTA}_{31..28} &= (PC+4)_{31..28} \\
\text{JTA}_{27..2}  &= I_{25..0} \quad (\text{the 26-bit field, occupying bits 27 down to 2}) \\
\text{JTA}_{1..0}   &= 00
\end{aligned}
$$

Total: $4 + 26 + 2 = 32$ bits. ✓

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** A 32-bit MIPS word at address `0x0040302C` is `0x08102040`. Identify the instruction, decode the 26-bit target field, and compute the effective jump destination. (Take the next-PC as `PC + 4`.)

**Step 1 — Identify the instruction.**
The instruction word is `0x08102040`. In binary:

$$
\texttt{0x08102040} = \underbrace{00001000}_{= 0x08}\ \underbrace{102040}_{\text{remainder}}
$$

The opcode is `000010` = **`j`** (jump). Therefore the instruction is a J-type `j` and the target field is the lower 26 bits.

**Step 2 — Extract the target field.**
`instr_index = 0x08102040 & 0x03FFFFFF` (mask out the 6-bit opcode).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{instr\_index} &= \texttt{0x08102040}\ \text{AND}\ \texttt{0x03FFFFFF} \\
&= \texttt{0x00102040}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here, `\text{AND}` denotes the bitwise logical AND operation, used to isolate specific bit fields of a binary word.)

In binary, the 26-bit field is:

$$
\texttt{0x00102040} = 0000\ 0000\ 0001\ 0000\ 0010\ 0000\ 0100\ 0000_2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $PC + 4$.**

$$
PC = \texttt{0x0040302C} \implies PC + 4 = \texttt{0x00403030}
$$

**Step 4 — Extract upper 4 bits of $PC + 4$.**

$$
(PC+4)_{31..28} = \texttt{0x0} \quad \text{(upper nibble of 0x00403030)}
$$

**Step 5 — Shift target left by 2 and concatenate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{shifted} &= \texttt{0x00102040} \ll 2 \\
              &= \texttt{0x00408100}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Assemble final 32-bit target.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{JTA} &= \big((PC+4)_{31..28}\big)\ \Vert\ (\text{target} \ll 2) \\
          &= \texttt{0x0}\ \Vert\ \texttt{00408100} \\
          &= \texttt{0x000408100}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result `0x000408100` is interpreted as a byte address. The CPU will fetch the next instruction from this address. The two least-significant bits `00` are guaranteed by the left-shift; this is why hardware never needs to verify alignment for `j` targets — it is structurally impossible to produce a misaligned address from a 26-bit field shifted left by 2.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Edge & Boundary Conditions

| Condition | Behavior | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Target field = `0x0000000` | Jump to start of current 256 MB region (`0x?0000000`) | Used to implement a region-level "reset jump" |
| Target field = `0x3FFFFFF` | Jump to `0x?FFFFFFC` (end of current 256 MB region) | Maximum forward reach within region |
| Jump goes outside current 256 MB region | Hardware **silently clips** to the same upper 4 bits | **Bug magnet** — must use `jr`/`jalr` for long-distance jumps |
| `jal` followed by `jr $ra` | Classic procedure call / return pattern | Foundation of MIPS calling convention |
| $PC$ crosses a 256 MB region boundary mid-jump | Target is **wrong** because region bits are stale | Avoid by linking large programs to stay in one region or use indirection |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 J-Type Instruction Encoding Layout

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the bit-level layout of a J-Type instruction and the flow of the **pseudo-direct target construction** inside the MIPS datapath.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Bit 31 ... Bit 26"] -->|opcode 6 bits| B["Opcode Decoder"]
    C["Bit 25 ... Bit 0"] -->|target 26 bits| D["Left-Shift by 2"]
    PC4["PC plus 4"] --> E["Upper 4 bits selector 31..28"]
    D --> F["Concatenator"]
    E --> F
    Z["Two zero bits 00"] --> F
    F --> G["32-bit Jump Target Address JTA"]
    B --> H["Control Unit"]
    H --> I["PC MUX selects JTA"]
    G --> I
    I --> PCout["New PC value"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The figure below shows the *architectural role* of a J-Type instruction in the MIPS pipeline: how the fetch, decode, and execute stages cooperate to redirect control flow.

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1["Stage IF: Instruction Fetch from PC"] --> F2["Increment PC by 4"]
    F2 --> D1["Stage ID: Opcode equals 000010 j or 000011 jal"]
    D1 --> D2["Extract 26-bit target field"]
    D2 --> D3["Shift target left by 2 to form 28-bit word address"]
    D3 --> D4["Take upper 4 bits of PC plus 4"]
    D4 --> D5["Concatenate upper4 plus shifted plus 00"]
    D5 --> E1["Stage EX: Drive PC MUX to select Jump Target"]
    E1 --> F1
    D1 -->|jal only| L1["Write PC plus 4 into register ra 31"]
```

## 4.3 Comparison: J-Type vs. I-Type Branch vs. R-Type Jump Register

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Encoding Comparison"
    JT["J-Type j / jal: opcode 6 bits, target 26 bits"]
    IT["I-Type beq / bne: opcode 6, rs 5, rt 5, signed offset 16 bits"]
    RT["R-Type jr / jalr: opcode 6, rs 5, rt 5, rd 5, shamt 5, funct 6"]
    end
    JT --> RJ["Target Range: any 32-bit address within current 256 MB region"]
    IT --> RJ2["Target Range: PC plus 4 plus sign-extended offset times 4, approx plus or minus 128 KB"]
    RT --> RJ3["Target Range: any 32-bit address held in a register, fully unrestricted"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each box labelled with `opcode` and field widths represents the *encoding format* of that instruction class. The arrows show the effective reach of control transfer. Note how J-Type occupies a middle ground — larger reach than I-Type branches, but constrained to a 256 MB region unless combined with `jr`/`jalr`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the format of a J-Type instruction in MIPS. How many bits are allocated for the opcode and the address field, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A J-Type instruction in MIPS is a 32-bit word divided into two fields: a **6-bit opcode** and a **26-bit address** field.

- The **6-bit opcode** identifies the operation (e.g., `j` = `000010`, `jal` = `000011`). Six bits allow $2^6 = 64$ distinct opcodes, sufficient for the MIPS instruction set.
- The **26-bit address** field encodes the jump destination in a *pseudo-direct* fashion: the field is left-shifted by 2 (because all MIPS instructions are 4-byte aligned, the lowest 2 bits are always zero and need not be stored) and the upper 4 bits of the target are inherited from the upper 4 bits of $PC+4$.

This gives an effective reach of $2^{28} = 256\ \text{MB}$ within the current region, while keeping the instruction word a compact 32 bits. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the role of the `$ra` register in a `jal` instruction? How is its value computed?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `jal` (Jump And Link) instruction performs two actions:
1. It transfers control to the address computed by pseudo-direct addressing.
2. It **saves the return address** in the dedicated link register `$ra` (register `$31`).

The value stored in `$ra` is the address of the **instruction immediately following the `jal`**, which is $PC + 4$ at the time the `jal` is in the IF/ID pipeline register (i.e., after the PC has been incremented by 4). The procedure being called later executes `jr $ra` to return to this saved address, thereby resuming the caller's flow. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *[7 marks]* With a neat diagram, describe the **format of a J-Type instruction** in MIPS and explain the **pseudo-direct addressing** scheme used to compute the effective target address. Clearly show how the **upper 4 bits**, the **26-bit field**, and the **lower 2 bits** combine to form the 32-bit destination. **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(b)** *[7 marks]* A J-Type instruction `j LOOP` is stored at memory address `0x00500020`. The 32-bit machine code of the instruction is `0x08100010`. Determine the effective address to which control is transferred. Show every step of the calculation. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — Format and Pseudo-Direct Addressing (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Bit-level format (2 marks)**

A J-Type instruction is laid out as:

| Bits 31–26 | Bits 25–0 |
|---|---|
| `opcode` (6 bits) | `target` (26 bits) |

The opcode `000010` corresponds to `j`; opcode `000011` corresponds to `jal`.

**Step 2 — Address construction formula (2 marks)**

The 32-bit Jump Target Address (JTA) is formed by concatenating three pieces:

$$
\text{JTA} = \big((PC+4)_{31..28}\big)\ \Vert\ \big(I_{25..0} \ll 2\big)
$$

**Step 3 — Why each part is used (2 marks)**

- **Upper 4 bits from $PC+4$:** Most jumps stay in the same 256 MB region, so we *reuse* the upper 4 bits of the current PC to avoid wasting instruction bits on a 32-bit absolute address.
- **26-bit field shifted left by 2:** Word alignment means the low 2 bits of any valid PC are zero. Omitting them and shifting left by 2 turns the 26-bit field into a 28-bit word-addressed value.
- **Implicit `00` at LSB:** Hardware appends two zero bits, ensuring the target is always word-aligned.

**Step 4 — Pipeline datapath role (1 mark)**

In the MIPS multi-cycle/pipelined datapath, the constructed 32-bit JTA drives the PC MUX, replacing the $PC+4$ incrementer output as the next PC value. For `jal`, the datapath additionally writes $PC+4$ into `$ra` (register 31) in the same cycle.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Worked Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- PC of the `j LOOP` instruction: $\texttt{0x00500020}$
- 32-bit machine code: $\texttt{0x08100010}$

**Step 1 — Verify opcode (1 mark)**

$$
\texttt{0x08100010} \gg 26 = \texttt{0x02} = \texttt{000010}_2 = j
$$

Opcode confirms it is a J-type `j` instruction. ✓

**Step 2 — Extract the 26-bit target field (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{25..0} &= \texttt{0x08100010}\ \text{AND}\ \texttt{0x03FFFFFF} \\
         &= \texttt{0x00100010}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here, `\text{AND}` denotes the bitwise AND operation used to mask out the upper 6 opcode bits.)

**Step 3 — Compute $PC + 4$ (1 mark)**

$$
PC + 4 = \texttt{0x00500020} + 4 = \texttt{0x00500024}
$$

**Step 4 — Upper 4 bits of $PC+4$ (1 mark)**

$$
(PC+4)_{31..28} = \texttt{0x0}
$$

**Step 5 — Shift target left by 2 (1 mark)**

$$
I_{25..0} \ll 2 = \texttt{0x00100010} \times 4 = \texttt{0x00400040}
$$

**Step 6 — Concatenate to form JTA (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{JTA} &= \texttt{0x0}\ \Vert\ \texttt{0x00400040} \\
          &= \texttt{0x000400040}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** The processor transfers control to byte address `0x000400040`, and the instruction at that address is fetched next. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for J-Type Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the left-shift by 2.** Many students compute the target directly as the 26-bit field padded with zeros, producing an address that is **¼ of the correct value**. The shift by 2 is mandatory because MIPS addresses instructions in *bytes*, not words.
> 2. **Confusing the upper 4 bits.** Students often take the upper 4 bits of the **PC of the jump itself** instead of **PC + 4**. The hardware uses $PC+4$ because the PC has already been incremented by the time the jump executes. Off-by-one region errors follow.
> 3. **Masking the opcode incorrectly.** Use `0x03FFFFFF` (26 low bits), not `0x3FFFFFFF` (30 bits) — getting the mask wrong shifts your final answer catastrophically.
> 4. **Forgetting that `jal` saves `PC + 4`, not `PC`.** The return address is the instruction *after* the `jal`, which is what `$ra` must hold for `jr $ra` to work correctly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *[7 marks]* Compare and contrast **J-Type (`j`)**, **I-Type branch (`beq`)**, and **R-Type jump-register (`jr`)** instructions in MIPS with respect to (i) instruction format, (ii) target address computation, (iii) range of jump, and (iv) typical use case. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(b)** *[7 marks]* A subroutine `PRINT` begins at memory address `0x10020000`. The main program is at `0x10010040` and contains the instruction `jal PRINT` at address `0x10010050`. Show the encoding of the `jal PRINT` instruction. What value is stored in `$ra` immediately after the `jal` executes? When `PRINT` finishes and executes `jr $ra`, where does control return? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Comparison (7 marks)

| Aspect | J-Type `j target` | I-Type `beq rs, rt, offset` | R-Type `jr rs` |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) **Format** | `opcode (6)` + `target (26)` | `opcode (6)` + `rs (5)` + `rt (5)` + `offset (16)` | `opcode (6)` + `rs (5)` + `rt (5)` + `rd (5)` + `shamt (5)` + `funct (6)` |
| (ii) **Target computation** | $\{(PC+4)_{31..28}, \text{target} \ll 2\}$ (pseudo-direct) | $(PC+4) + (\text{offset} \ll 2)$ (PC-relative) | Contents of register `$rs` directly |
| (iii) **Range** | 256 MB within current region | $\pm 2^{15} \times 4 = \pm 128\ \text{KB}$ (signed) | **Full 32-bit** — anywhere in address space |
| (iv) **Use case** | Long unconditional jumps within a region, e.g., skipping past large data blocks | Short conditional branches (loops, if-else) | Returning from subroutines (`jr $ra`), computed/dynamic jumps (switch tables) |

*(Distribute 1–2 marks per row × 4 rows = 7–8 marks; trim/expand as needed.)*

**Key insight (1 mark):** The three instruction types represent a hierarchy — R-type jump register offers the most flexibility (no range limit), J-type offers moderate reach with a small encoding, and I-type branch offers the smallest reach but supports *conditional* logic. MIPS uses each for the role it is best suited to.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Encoding `jal PRINT` (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify opcode (1 mark)**
`jal` opcode = `000011` = `0x03` in the upper 6 bits.

**Step 2 — Compute target field (2 marks)**
We need the 26-bit field that, when concatenated with $(PC+4)_{31..28}$ and a `00` suffix, gives the byte address `0x10020000`.

The 32-bit target is `0x10020000`. Strip the lower 2 bits (already `00`), strip the upper 4 bits (which will come from PC+4):

$$
\begin{aligned}
(PC+4)_{31..28} &= (\texttt{0x10010050} + 4)_{31..28} = \texttt{0x1}_{31..28} \\
\text{lower 26 bits of target shifted right by 2} &= \texttt{0x10020000}_{25..2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert `0x10020000` to binary, then extract bits 25..2:

$$
\texttt{0x10020000}_2 = 0001\ 0000\ 0000\ 0010\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000
$$

Bits 25..2 (counting from the right, bit 0 is the rightmost):

$$
\texttt{0x10020000} \gg 2 = \texttt{0x00400800}
$$

So the 26-bit field to encode is `0x00400800`.

**Step 3 — Assemble the 32-bit instruction (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{jal PRINT} &= (\texttt{opcode} \ll 26)\ \text{OR}\ \text{target field} \\
                  &= \texttt{0x03} \ll 26\ \text{OR}\ \texttt{0x00400800} \\
                  &= \texttt{0x0C100800}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here, `\text{OR}` denotes the bitwise OR operation used to merge non-overlapping fields of the instruction word.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Decode `0x0C100800` → upper 6 bits `000011` = `jal` ✓, lower 26 bits `0x00400800`. Shift left by 2: `0x01002000`. Concatenate with upper 4 bits of PC+4 = `0x1`: `0x10020000` ✓ matches `PRINT`'s start address.

**Step 4 — Value stored in `$ra` and return address (2 marks)**

- After the `jal` executes, the value written into `$ra` (R31) is $PC + 4$ of the `jal` itself:

$$
\$ra = \texttt{0x10010050} + 4 = \texttt{0x10010054}
$$

- When `PRINT` finishes and executes `jr $ra`, the CPU loads `PC ← 0x10010054`, which is the instruction immediately after the `jal PRINT` call site in the main program. Execution of the main program resumes seamlessly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **J-Type format:** 6-bit `opcode` + 26-bit `target` = 32 bits.
- ✅ **Only two J-Type opcodes exist in classic MIPS:** `j` (`000010`) and `jal` (`000011`).
- ✅ **Pseudo-direct addressing formula:**
  $$\text{JTA} = \big((PC+4)_{31..28}\big)\ \Vert\ \big(I_{25..0} \ll 2\big)$$
- ✅ **Why left-shift by 2:** MIPS instructions are word-aligned (4 bytes), so the low 2 bits of any address are always `00` and need not be encoded.
- ✅ **Why only 4 region bits:** A 26-bit field covers $2^{28}$ word addresses = 256 MB. Assuming locality, reusing the PC's upper 4 bits extends reach to 32 bits without enlarging the encoding.
- ✅ **Reach of a `j`:** Anywhere within the **current 256 MB region**. Cross-region jumps **must** use `jr`/`jalr`.
- ✅ **`jal` saves `PC + 4` in `$ra` (R31).** It is the *return address* — the instruction immediately after the call site.
- ✅ **`jr` and `jalr` are R-Type, not J-Type.** They reach the full 32-bit space but require the target to be loaded into a register first.
- ✅ **Address computation uses `PC + 4`, not `PC`.** This is because the PC is incremented during the fetch stage, *before* the jump target is computed in EX.
- ✅ **Two implicit zero LSBs** of JTA make the target **structurally aligned** — no runtime alignment check is needed.
- ✅ **Typical exam traps:** forgetting the ×4 shift, using PC instead of PC+4, masking the opcode with the wrong constant, and confusing `$ra` storage convention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interpreting Machine Language Code; MIPS Addressing Modes - Register Only
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interpreting Machine Language Code; MIPS Addressing Modes - Register Only

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Interpreting Machine Language Code — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> *Machine Language Code* is the **lowest-level representation of a computer program**, expressed entirely in **binary (0s and 1s)**. Each instruction in the MIPS ISA is encoded as a **fixed 32-bit word**. *Interpreting* this code means **decoding the 32-bit binary pattern** back into its human-readable assembly form by extracting the fields (opcode, registers, function code, etc.) that uniquely identify the operation.

**KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> "Interpreting machine language is the process of mapping a 32-bit binary instruction word to its corresponding MIPS assembly mnemonic, register operands, and operation class, by parsing the instruction format (R, I, or J)."

**Key Properties of MIPS Machine Code:**
- **Fixed length**: Every MIPS instruction is exactly **32 bits (4 bytes)** wide.
- **Word-aligned**: Instructions start at addresses that are multiples of **4**.
- **Three instruction formats**: R-format, I-format, J-format.
- **Big-endian byte ordering** (KTU standard textbook reference: Patterson & Hennessy).
- **32 general-purpose registers**, each referenced by a **5-bit address** (since $2^5 = 32$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For R-type instructions, the canonical 32-bit layout is:
> `opcode(6) | rs(5) | rt(5) | rd(5) | shamt(5) | funct(6)`
> This sums to $6+5+5+5+5+6 = \mathbf{32}$ bits — a frequently asked short-answer question.

---

### 1.2 MIPS Addressing Modes — Register Only

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:**
> *Register Addressing Mode* is the addressing mode in which **all operands of the instruction reside in CPU registers**, not in memory. The instruction explicitly names the registers holding the source values and the register receiving the result. No memory access (no load/store) occurs.

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> "In *Register-only addressing*, both source operands and the destination operand are specified as register names (e.g., `$t0, $t1, $t2`). The ALU operation is performed entirely within the register file; memory is not accessed."

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy 1 — The Recipe Card (Machine Code Interpretation):**
