Imagine a **recipe card with 32 checkboxes** (the 32 bits). The card is divided into labelled sections: "What kind of recipe?" (opcode, 6 boxes), "Who is the head chef?" (rs, 5 boxes), "Who is the assistant?" (rt, 5 boxes), "Who plates the dish?" (rd, 5 boxes), "How fine to chop?" (shamt, 5 boxes), and "Exact cooking method?" (funct, 6 boxes). To interpret the card, you **read each section** and translate the checkbox pattern back to words. This is exactly how the CPU's decoder interprets machine code.

**Analogy 2 — The Whiteboard (Register-Only Mode):**
Picture a **whiteboard with 32 labelled slots** (registers `$0` to `$31`). To do a calculation, the professor (CPU) **reads two values from the whiteboard**, hands them to a calculator (ALU), and **writes the result back to one slot**. Nothing is fetched from the cupboard (memory). The whiteboard itself is *fast, on-chip, and tiny* — exactly like a register file. This is the essence of register-only addressing.

**Analogy 3 — Direct Phone Call vs Letter:**
- *Register mode* = **Direct face-to-face chat** (instant, in-CPU, no delays).
- *Immediate/Memory modes* (covered later) = **Writing a letter and posting it** (slower, off-chip).

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighted)

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction length** | **32 bits = 4 bytes** | Fixed for all MIPS instructions |
| **Number of general-purpose registers** | **32** ($0 to $31) | Requires 5 bits to address |
| **Number of bits to address a register** | **5 bits** | $\log_2(32) = 5$ |
| **Number of bits in opcode** | **6 bits** | $\log_2(64) = 6$ |
| **Number of bits in funct field** | **6 bits** | Identifies R-type sub-operation |
| **R-type opcode** | **000000 (binary) = 0 (decimal)** | All R-type instructions share this |
| **Register `$zero` value** | **Always 0** | Hardwired to ground |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this 2-line fact for KTU:**
> - R-type opcode = `000000` (6 bits of zeros)
> - `$zero` = `$0` always holds the value **0**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MIPS R-Type Instruction Format — Field-by-Field Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **R-Type Format (MIPS-32, 32 bits total):**
>
> | Bits 31–26 | Bits 25–21 | Bits 20–16 | Bits 15–11 | Bits 10–6 | Bits 5–0 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | **opcode** (6) | **rs** (5) | **rt** (5) | **rd** (5) | **shamt** (5) | **funct** (6) |

**Field Semantics:**

- **`opcode` (6 bits)**: Operation class. For **all R-type** instructions, this field is `000000`. The CPU uses this to recognise the instruction as a register-register ALU operation.
- **`rs` (5 bits)**: First **source register** number. Holds the first operand fed to the ALU.
- **`rt` (5 bits)**: Second **source register** number. Holds the second operand fed to the ALU.
- **`rd` (5 bits)**: **Destination register** number. Where the ALU's output is written.
- **`shamt` (5 bits)**: **Shift amount** — used only for shift instructions (`sll`, `srl`, `sra`); set to `00000` for arithmetic/logical ops like `add`, `sub`, `and`, `or`.
- **`funct` (6 bits)**: **Function code** — identifies the *exact* operation within the R-type class. Examples:
  - `100000` = `add`
  - `100010` = `sub`
  - `100100` = `and`
  - `100101` = `or`
  - `101010` = `slt`
  - `000000` = `sll`
  - `000010` = `srl`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two levels (opcode + funct)?**
> MIPS designers wanted opcode `000000` to mean "this is an R-type instruction" — the *funct* field then disambiguates *which* R-type. This is **micro-coding elegance** and is a 3-mark KTU favourite.

---

### 2.2 Why Register-Only Mode? — Engineering Motivation

1. **Speed**: Registers are inside the CPU, accessed in **1 clock cycle**. Memory takes **100+ cycles** if it misses cache.
2. **Determinism**: Register access time is **constant** (no cache misses).
3. **Compactness**: A 5-bit field can name 1 of 32 registers — encoding is dense.
4. **Compiler-friendly**: Modern compilers (GCC, llvm) heavily optimise for register reuse.

**Real-world utility:** Every modern CPU (Intel x86, ARM, RISC-V, Apple M-series) has a register file. The MIPS register-only mode is the conceptual ancestor of these.

---

### 2.3 MIPS Register Naming Convention (High-Yield for KTU)

| Name | Number | Usage |
|---|---|---|
| `$zero` / `$0` | 0 | Constant 0 (read-only) |
| `$at` | 1 | Assembler temporary |
| `$v0–$v1` | 2–3 | Function return values |
| `$a0–$a3` | 4–7 | Function arguments |
| `$t0–$t9` | 8–15, 24–25 | Caller-saved temporaries |
| `$s0–$s7` | 16–23 | Callee-saved |
| `$t8–$t9` | 24–25 | More temporaries |
| `$gp` | 28 | Global pointer |
| `$sp` | 29 | Stack pointer |
| `$fp` | 30 | Frame pointer |
| `$ra` | 31 | Return address |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU commonly tests**: mapping register *names* to *numbers* (e.g., `$t2` = ?). The trick: `$t0`–`$t9` map to 8, 9, 10, …, 15, 24, 25. So `$t2` = **register 10**, `$t9` = **register 25**.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA BOX — Commit to Memory**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total instruction length | $L = 32$ bits | Fixed for MIPS |
| 2 | Bits to address $N$ registers | $b = \log_2 N$ | For $N=32 \Rightarrow b=5$ |
| 3 | Bits in opcode | $\log_2(\text{number of formats/ops})$ | R-type uses 6 |
| 4 | R-type field widths | $6+5+5+5+5+6 = 32$ | Always sums to 32 |
| 5 | Field value of `$zero` | 0 | Read-only zero source |
| 6 | Register `$tn` number | $8 + n$ for $n=0..7$; $24 + (n-8)$ for $n=8,9$ | KTU trick |
| 7 | Register `$sn` number | $16 + n$ for $n=0..7$ | Saves |
| 8 | Machine code → integer value | $\sum_{i=0}^{31} b_i \cdot 2^i$ | Standard binary |
| 9 | R-type opcode | `000000` (decimal 0) | All R-type |
| 10 | `add` funct | `100000` (decimal 32) | Most-tested |
| 11 | `sub` funct | `100010` (decimal 34) | |
| 12 | `and` funct | `100100` (decimal 36) | |
| 13 | `or` funct | `100101` (decimal 37) | |
| 14 | `slt` funct | `101010` (decimal 42) | |
| 15 | Number of R-type ops | $2^6 = 64$ possible funct codes | |
| 16 | Register-file read ports | 2 (source) + 1 (write) | In single-cycle datapath |

> [!TIP]
> **Conversion Tip (No `|` in tables):** When expressing absolute value in formulas, write $\vert x \vert$ using `\vert` — never the bare `|` character, as it breaks markdown tables.

---

### 2.5 Why 5 Bits for a Register Address — The Logic

$$N_{\text{registers}} = 2^{b} \Rightarrow 32 = 2^{b} \Rightarrow b = \log_2 32 = 5 \text{ bits}$$

This is why the `rs`, `rt`, `rd` fields are each exactly 5 bits wide in R-format. KTU loves this 1-line question: *"Why is the rs field 5 bits wide?"*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — Decoding a 32-bit Machine Code to Assembly (The KTU Classic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — Decode the following MIPS machine instruction:**
> `000000 01001 01010 01000 00000 100000`

#### Step 1 — Identify the Format (Read opcode = first 6 bits)

$$\text{opcode} = 000000_2 = 0_{10}$$

Since opcode is `000000`, this is an **R-type** instruction. Therefore the format is:

$$\text{opcode}(6) \mid \text{rs}(5) \mid \text{rt}(5) \mid \text{rd}(5) \mid \text{shamt}(5) \mid \text{funct}(6)$$

#### Step 2 — Slice the 32-bit word into the 6 fields

| Field | Bits | Binary | Decimal |
|---|---|---|---|
| `opcode` | 31–26 | `000000` | 0 |
| `rs` | 25–21 | `01001` | 9 |
| `rt` | 20–16 | `01010` | 10 |
| `rd` | 15–11 | `01000` | 8 |
| `shamt` | 10–6 | `00000` | 0 |
| `funct` | 5–0 | `100000` | 32 |

**Conversion logic (binary to decimal for `rs` = `01001`):**
$$0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0 = 0 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 = 9$$

#### Step 3 — Convert register numbers to MIPS names

- `rs = 9` → register `$t1`
- `rt = 10` → register `$t2`
- `rd = 8` → register `$t0`

**Conversion logic (number to name):**
- Registers 8–15 are `$t0`–`$t7`. So 9 = `$t1`, 10 = `$t2`, 8 = `$t0`.

#### Step 4 — Identify the operation from `funct`

| funct (binary) | funct (decimal) | Operation |
|---|---|---|
| `100000` | 32 | `add` |
| `100010` | 34 | `sub` |
| `100100` | 36 | `and` |
| `100101` | 37 | `or` |
| `101010` | 42 | `slt` |

Our funct = `100000` = 32 → **`add`**.

#### Step 5 — Assemble the assembly instruction

$$\text{MIPS assembly: } \texttt{add \$t0, \$t1, \$t2}$$

**Meaning:** R[`$t0`] ← R[`$t1`] + R[`$t2`]

> [!TIP]
> **Order rule (MIPS):** The first register after the mnemonic is the *destination*, then the two sources. Mnemonic format: `op rd, rs, rt`.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Encoding `sub $s3, $s1, $s0` to Machine Code

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — Encode the following into 32-bit binary:**
> `sub $s3, $s1, $s0`

#### Step 1 — Identify register numbers

- `$s3` → 16 + 3 = **19**
- `$s1` → 16 + 1 = **17**
- `$s0` → 16 + 0 = **16**

**Logic:** `$s0`–`$s7` map to registers 16–23. So `$sn` = 16 + n.

#### Step 2 — Identify opcode and funct

- Opcode (R-type) = `000000`
- `sub` funct = `100010`

#### Step 3 — Map to fields

- `rd` (destination) = 19 (since `$s3` is destination)
- `rs` (source 1) = 17 (since `$s1` is first source)
- `rt` (source 2) = 16 (since `$s0` is second source)
- `shamt` = `00000` (not a shift)

#### Step 4 — Convert each decimal to 5-bit / 6-bit binary

$$\begin{aligned}
19_{10} &= 10011_2 \\
17_{10} &= 10001_2 \\
16_{10} &= 10000_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic for 19:**
$$19 = 16 + 2 + 1 = 2^4 + 2^1 + 2^0 = 10011_2$$

#### Step 5 — Concatenate fields

$$\underbrace{000000}_{\text{opcode}} \; \underbrace{10001}_{\text{rs}=17} \; \underbrace{10000}_{\text{rt}=16} \; \underbrace{10011}_{\text{rd}=19} \; \underbrace{00000}_{\text{shamt}} \; \underbrace{100010}_{\text{funct}}$$

**Final 32-bit machine code:**
$$\mathbf{00000010001100001001100000100010}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Validation:** Group as `0000 0010 0011 0000 1001 1000 0010 0010` (4-bit nibbles) → hex `0x02309822`. This is the canonical hex form often given in textbooks.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — `and $t4, $t0, $t1` — Full Round-Trip

**Step 1 — Register numbers:**
- `$t4` = 8 + 4 = **12**
- `$t0` = **8**
- `$t1` = **9**

**Step 2 — Fields:**
- opcode = `000000`
- `rs` = 9 (source 1: `$t1`)
- `rt` = 8 (source 2: `$t0`)
- `rd` = 12 (destination: `$t4`)
- `shamt` = `00000`
- funct = `100100` (for `and`)

**Step 3 — Convert to binary:**

$$\begin{aligned}
12_{10} &= 8 + 4 = 01100_2 \\
9_{10} &= 01001_2 \\
8_{10} &= 01000_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Concatenate:**

$$\mathbf{000000 \; 01001 \; 01000 \; 01100 \; 00000 \; 100100}$$

**Final:**
$$\mathbf{00000001001010000110000000100100}$$

**Verification in hex:** `0128C024` (8 hex digits = 32 bits ✓)

---

### 3.4 Example 4 — Decoding `and $t0, $t1, $t2` (reverse of Example 1)

Input: `000000 01001 01010 01000 00000 100100`

| Field | Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| opcode | `000000` | R-type |
| rs | 9 | `$t1` |
| rt | 10 | `$t2` |
| rd | 8 | `$t0` |
| shamt | 0 | not a shift |
| funct | 36 = `100100` | `and` |

**Output:** `and $t0, $t1, $t2`

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — All Common R-Type Instructions

| Mnemonic | Operation | funct (binary) | funct (dec) | shamt used? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `add` | R[rd] = R[rs] + R[rt] | `100000` | 32 | No (0) |
| `addu` | R[rd] = R[rs] + R[rt] (unsigned) | `100001` | 33 | No |
| `sub` | R[rd] = R[rs] − R[rt] | `100010` | 34 | No |
| `subu` | R[rd] = R[rs] − R[rt] (unsigned) | `100011` | 35 | No |
| `and` | R[rd] = R[rs] AND R[rt] | `100100` | 36 | No |
| `or` | R[rd] = R[rs] OR R[rt] | `100101` | 37 | No |
| `xor` | R[rd] = R[rs] XOR R[rt] | `100110` | 38 | No |
| `nor` | R[rd] = NOT(R[rs] OR R[rt]) | `100111` | 39 | No |
| `slt` | R[rd] = (R[rs] < R[rt]) ? 1 : 0 | `101010` | 42 | No |
| `sltu` | Unsigned version of `slt` | `101011` | 43 | No |
| `sll` | R[rd] = R[rt] << shamt | `000000` | 0 | **Yes** |
| `srl` | R[rd] = R[rt] >> shamt (logical) | `000010` | 2 | **Yes** |
| `sra` | R[rd] = R[rt] >> shamt (arithmetic) | `000011` | 3 | **Yes** |
| `jr` | PC = R[rs] | `001000` | 8 | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: For shift instructions (`sll`, `srl`, `sra`), the source is in **`rt`**, not `rs`! And the **`shamt`** field carries the shift count. The `rs` field is unused (set to `00000`).

---

### 3.6 Endianness — How the 32 Bits Are Stored in Memory

A MIPS word is 4 bytes. In **big-endian**, the most significant byte is stored at the lowest memory address.

**Example: `0x02309822` (the `sub $s3, $s1, $s0` instruction)**

| Address | Byte 0 | Byte 1 | Byte 2 | Byte 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0x1000 | 0x02 | 0x30 | 0x98 | 0x22 |
| | (bits 31–24) | (bits 23–16) | (bits 15–8) | (bits 7–0) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam tip:** A common 3-mark question: *"Given address 0x1000 contains 0x02, 0x1001 contains 0x30, …, identify the instruction."* — This tests big-endian parsing.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — R-Type Instruction Decoding Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[32-bit Instruction Word in IR] --> B[Opcode Decoder 6 bits]
    B -->|opcode equals 000000| C[R-Type Path]
    C --> D[Extract rs 5 bits]
    C --> E[Extract rt 5 bits]
    C --> F[Extract rd 5 bits]
    C --> G[Extract shamt 5 bits]
    C --> H[Extract funct 6 bits]
    D --> I[Register File Read Port 1]
    E --> J[Register File Read Port 2]
    I --> K[ALU Input A]
    J --> L[ALU Input B]
    H --> M[ALU Control Unit]
    M --> N[ALU Operation Select]
    N --> K
    N --> L
    K --> O[ALU Result]
    L --> O
    O --> P[Write to Register rd]
    F --> P
    P --> Q[Updated Register File]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The 32-bit instruction is first split by the **opcode decoder**; for R-type, it fans out into five field extractors. The two source registers (`rs`, `rt`) are read from the register file and fed to the ALU. The **funct** field drives the **ALU control** to choose the operation (add/sub/and/or/etc.). The result is written back to `rd`.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — MIPS Register File (32 × 32-bit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph RegFile["MIPS Register File 32 x 32 bits"]
        R0["$0 zero always 0"]
        R1["$1 at"]
        R2["$2 v0"]
        R3["$3 v1"]
        R8["$8 t0"]
        R9["$9 t1"]
        R10["$10 t2"]
        R16["$16 s0"]
        R31["$31 ra"]
    end
    RA["Read Reg 1: rs 5 bits"] --> R0
    RA --> R1
    RA --> R2
    RA --> R3
    RA --> R8
    RA --> R9
    RA --> R10
    RA --> R16
    RA --> R31
    RB["Read Reg 2: rt 5 bits"] --> R0
    RB --> R1
    RB --> R2
    RB --> R3
    RB --> R8
    RB --> R9
    RB --> R10
    RB --> R16
    RB --> R31
    R0 -.->|out A| OA["Output Bus A 32 bits"]
    R1 -.->|out A| OA
    R31 -.->|out A| OA
    R0 -.->|out B| OB["Output Bus B 32 bits"]
    R31 -.->|out B| OB
    WW["Write Reg: rd 5 bits + Write Enable + Data In 32 bits"] --> R0
    WW --> R1
    WW --> R31
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — End-to-End Decoding of R-Type

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Memory Address PC] --> FETCH[Instruction Fetch Unit]
    FETCH -->|32-bit word| IR[Instruction Register]
    IR --> DEC[Decoder]
    DEC -->|opcode 000000| RTYPE[R-Type Handler]
    RTYPE --> REG[Register Read: rs and rt]
    REG --> ALU[ALU Operation chosen by funct]
    ALU -->|result| WB[Write Back to rd]
    WB --> RF[Updated Register File]
    style M fill:#E8F4FD
    style FETCH fill:#D6EAF8
    style IR fill:#FCF3CF
    style DEC fill:#FAD7A0
    style RTYPE fill:#F5B7B1
    style REG fill:#D5F5E3
    style ALU fill:#D2B4DE
    style WB fill:#A9DFBF
    style RF fill:#82E0AA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Explanation of the architecture flow:**
> 1. **PC** (Program Counter) holds the address of the current instruction.
> 2. **Instruction Fetch** reads 4 bytes from memory at that address.
> 3. The 32-bit word is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
> 4. The **Decoder** examines the first 6 bits; if they are `000000`, it dispatches to the **R-Type Handler**.
> 5. The handler reads the two source registers (`rs`, `rt`) from the **Register File**.
> 6. The **ALU** performs the operation dictated by the `funct` field.
> 7. The result is **written back** to the destination register `rd`.
> 8. The cycle repeats for the next instruction.

---

### 4.4 Decision Tree — Identifying an Instruction from Machine Code

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Read 32-bit word] --> Q1{Opcode bits 31-26 equals 000000?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R[R-Type]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Opcode is 001xxx or 100xxx etc?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| I[I-Type: lw sw addi beq]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Opcode is 000010 or 000011?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| J[J-Type: j jal]
    Q3 -->|No| ERR[Undefined / Illegal]
    R --> F{Check funct bits 5-0}
    F -->|100000| A1[add]
    F -->|100010| A2[sub]
    F -->|100100| A3[and]
    F -->|100101| A4[or]
    F -->|101010| A5[slt]
    F -->|000000| A6[sll]
    F -->|000010| A7[srl]
    F -->|001000| A8[jr]
    style S fill:#E8F4FD
    style R fill:#FAD7A0
    style I fill:#A9DFBF
    style J fill:#D2B4DE
    style ERR fill:#F1948A
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2-Mark & 3-Mark)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. [3 Marks]** *What is meant by interpreting machine language code? Why is MIPS machine code always 32 bits long?*

**Model Answer:**
Interpreting machine language code means **decoding a 32-bit binary instruction word** into its corresponding human-readable MIPS assembly form by parsing the instruction format (R, I, or J) and extracting the fields (opcode, rs, rt, rd, shamt, funct).

MIPS is a **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** architecture. It uses a **fixed 32-bit instruction length** to:
- Simplify instruction fetch and decoding (one fetch per instruction).
- Align instructions on 4-byte boundaries for easy PC increment.
- Provide enough bits to encode up to 3 registers (5 bits each) plus opcode and funct fields.

The choice of 32 bits is a tradeoff: long enough to encode the R-type fields, short enough to keep instruction memory efficient.

---

**Q2. [3 Marks]** *List the fields of the MIPS R-type instruction format along with their bit widths. State the purpose of each field.*

**Model Answer:**

| Field | Width | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `opcode` | 6 bits | Operation class; `000000` for all R-type |
| `rs` | 5 bits | First source register number |
| `rt` | 5 bits | Second source register number |
| `rd` | 5 bits | Destination register number |
| `shamt` | 5 bits | Shift amount (used only for shift instructions) |
| `funct` | 6 bits | Function code specifying the exact R-type operation |

Total = 6 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 6 = **32 bits**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14-Mark Module Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Internal Choice Pattern]**
> *Answer the following with sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks.*

---

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** *Consider the MIPS assembly instruction: `add $t0, $s2, $s1`. Encode this instruction into its 32-bit binary machine code. Show all intermediate steps clearly.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify register numbers:**
- `$t0` → register **8**
- `$s2` → register 16 + 2 = **18**
- `$s1` → register 16 + 1 = **17**

**Step 2 — Identify opcode and funct:**
- R-type opcode = `000000`
- `add` funct = `100000`

**Step 3 — Map to R-type fields (destination first, then sources):**

| Field | Value (dec) | Value (bin) |
|---|---|---|
| `opcode` | 0 | `000000` |
| `rs` | 18 | `10010` |
| `rt` | 17 | `10001` |
| `rd` | 8 | `01000` |
| `shamt` | 0 | `00000` |
| `funct` | 32 | `100000` |

**Step 4 — Verification of binary conversions:**
- $18 = 16 + 2 = 2^4 + 2^1 = \mathbf{10010_2}$ ✓
- $17 = 16 + 1 = 2^4 + 2^0 = \mathbf{10001_2}$ ✓
- $8 = 2^3 = \mathbf{01000_2}$ ✓

**Step 5 — Concatenation (final 32-bit machine code):**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{000000 \; 10010 \; 10001 \; 01000 \; 00000 \; 100000}}$$

In hexadecimal: `0x02514020` *(8 hex digits = 32 bits ✓)*.

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** *Decode the following 32-bit MIPS machine code into its assembly instruction. Show the parsing of each field. The code is given in hex as `0x02324020`.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert hex to binary** (each hex digit = 4 bits):

| Hex | 0 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 4 | 0 | 2 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bin | 0000 | 0010 | 0011 | 0010 | 0100 | 0000 | 0010 | 0000 |

Full 32-bit binary:
$$\mathbf{0000\;0010\;0011\;0010\;0100\;0000\;0010\;0000}$$

**Step 2 — Slice into R-type fields:**

| Field | Bits | Binary | Decimal |
|---|---|---|---|
| `opcode` | 31–26 | `000000` | 0 |
| `rs` | 25–21 | `10001` | 17 |
| `rt` | 20–16 | `10001` | 17 |
| `rd` | 15–11 | `01000` | 8 |
| `shamt` | 10–6 | `00000` | 0 |
| `funct` | 5–0 | `100000` | 32 |

**Step 3 — Identify registers:**
- `rs = 17` → `$s1`
- `rt = 17` → `$s1`  *(same as rs — squaring!)*
- `rd = 8` → `$t0`

**Step 4 — Identify operation from funct:**
- `funct = 100000_2 = 32` → **`add`**

**Step 5 — Final assembly:**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{add \; \$t0, \; \$s1, \; \$s1}}$$

**Meaning:** `R[$t0] = R[$s1] + R[$s1] = 2 × R[$s1]` (this is a common MIPS trick to multiply by 2 without using `mul`!).

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the MIPS register-only addressing mode with a suitable example. How many bits are required to address the 32 registers, and why?*

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** In the *register-only addressing mode*, all operands (sources and destination) of an instruction are located in **CPU registers**. The instruction names the registers using 5-bit fields. No memory reference is made for operands; the ALU operates directly on the values read from the register file.

**Example:** `add $t0, $t1, $t2`
- Sources: `$t1` and `$t2` (read from register file).
- Destination: `$t0` (written back to register file).
- Operation: `R[$t0] ← R[$t1] + R[$t2]`.

**Bits for register address:**
$$N = 2^b \Rightarrow 32 = 2^b \Rightarrow b = \log_2 32 = \mathbf{5 \text{ bits}}$$

**Justification:** 5 bits can encode $2^5 = 32$ distinct register numbers, sufficient to address all 32 general-purpose registers (`$0` to `$31`).

**Advantages of register-only mode:**
1. Fast access (registers are inside the CPU).
2. Deterministic timing (no cache misses).
3. Compiler can optimise register reuse efficiently.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** *Consider the instruction: `or $s4, $s2, $s3`. Convert it to its 32-bit binary machine code. Also, list the binary representations of the registers involved.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Register numbers:**
- `$s4` → 16 + 4 = **20**
- `$s2` → 16 + 2 = **18**
- `$s3` → 16 + 3 = **19**

**Step 2 — Opcode and funct:**
- R-type opcode = `000000`
- `or` funct = `100101`

**Step 3 — Field mapping:**

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| `opcode` | 0 |
| `rs` | 18 (first source `$s2`) |
| `rt` | 19 (second source `$s3`) |
| `rd` | 20 (destination `$s4`) |
| `shamt` | 0 |
| `funct` | 37 |

**Step 4 — Binary conversions:**
- $20 = 16+4 = 2^4+2^2 = \mathbf{10100_2}$
- $18 = 16+2 = 2^4+2^1 = \mathbf{10010_2}$
- $19 = 16+2+1 = 2^4+2^1+2^0 = \mathbf{10011_2}$

**Step 5 — Final 32-bit machine code:**

$$\boxed{\mathbf{000000 \; 10010 \; 10011 \; 10100 \; 00000 \; 100101}}$$

Hex equivalent: `0x0253A025`.

**Operation performed:** `R[$s4] = R[$s2] OR R[$s3]` (bitwise OR of two 32-bit values).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Operand order error (Most common — costs 1–2 marks):** MIPS syntax is `op rd, rs, rt` — destination is FIRST. Students often write `add $t1, $t2, $t0` thinking it means `t1 = t1 + t2`. The fields `rs` and `rt` correspond to the *second and third* operands, not first and second.
>
> 2. **Confusing `rs`/`rt` for shift instructions:** For `sll`, the source is in `rt`, NOT `rs`. Marking `rs` instead of `rt` loses 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Wrong register number for `$t8`, `$t9`:** Many students think `$t8` = 16. Wrong! `$t8` = 24, `$t9` = 25.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the `00000` opcode for R-type:** Some students omit the leading zeros in the binary string. Always write all 32 bits explicitly.
>
> 5. **Sign extension vs zero extension confusion (for I-type):** Not applicable to register-only R-type, but a common confusion in I-type questions.
>
> 6. **Hex-to-binary mistakes:** A common error is to reverse nibble order. Always verify: 8 hex digits = 32 bits exactly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2: Machine Code & Register-Only Mode**

- ✅ MIPS instructions are **fixed 32 bits** long.
- ✅ R-type format: `opcode(6) | rs(5) | rt(5) | rd(5) | shamt(5) | funct(6)`.
- ✅ **All R-type** instructions have opcode = `000000`.
- ✅ **`funct` field** distinguishes R-type operations: `add`=32, `sub`=34, `and`=36, `or`=37, `slt`=42.
- ✅ **Register-only mode** = operands in CPU registers only; **no memory access**.
- ✅ MIPS has **32 general-purpose registers**; addressing requires **5 bits** since $\log_2 32 = 5$.
- ✅ Register aliases: `$t0`–`$t7` = 8–15; `$t8`, `$t9` = 24, 25; `$s0`–`$s7` = 16–23; `$zero`/`$0` = 0.
- ✅ MIPS assembly syntax: `op rd, rs, rt` — **destination first**.
- ✅ For shift instructions, source is in `rt`; `shamt` carries shift count.
- ✅ To **decode**: split the 32-bit word into 6 fields, convert each to decimal, look up register names, look up funct code.
- ✅ To **encode**: map register names to numbers, fill fields, concatenate 6+5+5+5+5+6 bits.
- ✅ Big-endian byte ordering: most significant byte at lowest address.
- ✅ Hex shorthand: group 32 bits into 8 hex digits (4 bits each).
- ✅ ALU performs the operation; result is **written back to `rd`** in the same clock cycle (single-cycle datapath).
- ✅ Funct code examples to memorize: `add`=100000, `sub`=100010, `and`=100100, `or`=100101, `slt`=101010.
- ✅ Common trick: `add $t0, $s1, $s1` = multiply by 2 (no `mul` needed for shift-by-one).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Funct Codes:**
> **"A-S-A-O-S"** → **A**dd(100000) – **S**ub(100010) – **A**nd(100100) – **O**r(100101) – **S**lt(101010).
> The binary pattern **starts with `100`** for arithmetic/logical and **ends with `010`** for `slt`. Mnemonic: "slt ends in 010 = 'Smaller is True → 10'".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Immediate
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Immediate

# IMMEDIATE ADDRESSING MODE — A KTU 2024 Scheme Comprehensive Study Module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This module falls under *Module 2: Addressing Modes & Instruction Formats* of the B.Tech Computer Organisation and Architecture course (PERUT418). The concept of the *Immediate* addressing mode is a **CO2 / CO3 aligned topic** — *Understand addressing modes and their application in instruction design.*

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> The **Immediate Addressing Mode** is a CPU addressing technique in which the **operand (data) is explicitly embedded within the instruction itself**, rather than being fetched from a memory location or a register. The instruction format contains an *opcode* followed by a *constant value* (the immediate operand) that the processor uses directly without any additional memory reference.

Mathematically, an instruction in immediate mode can be represented as:

$$
\text{Instruction Word} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{\text{Operation Field}} \,\Vert\, \underbrace{I}_{\text{Immediate Operand (Constant)}}
$$

Where $I$ is a fixed-width signed or unsigned integer constant embedded in the instruction stream.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sticky Note" Instruction:**
> Imagine you are a chef (CPU) and your manager hands you a recipe card (the instruction). 
> - In **Direct Addressing**, the recipe says: *"Go to shelf 7 and use whatever you find there."* (You must go fetch from memory.)
> - In **Immediate Addressing**, the recipe card itself says: *"Use 5 grams of salt"* — the value **5 grams** is *printed directly on the card*. You do not need to look anywhere else; the data is *literally part of the instruction*.
> 
> **This is exactly how immediate mode works** — the constant travels with the instruction like a sticky note attached to it.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Architectural Metrics

The following table summarizes the standard architectural parameters associated with immediate operands in typical KTU-referenced processor designs (e.g., 8086, MIPS, ARM):

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Immediate field width (MIPS) | **16** | bits |
| Immediate field width (8086) | **8 or 16** | bits |
| Sign-extension behavior | **Mandatory** | — |
| Memory access cycles required | **0 (Zero)** | cycles |
| Fetch cycle contribution | **1** | cycle (same as instruction) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact:** Immediate mode requires **the fewest memory access cycles** of *any* addressing mode, because the data is co-located with the instruction in the instruction cache / main memory. The CPU only performs a single fetch.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — "How the CPU Executes an Immediate Instruction"

The execution of an immediate-mode instruction follows a precise 3-stage micro-sequence:

- **Stage 1 — Instruction Fetch (IF):**
  The Program Counter (PC) supplies the address of the next instruction to memory. The *entire instruction word* (opcode + immediate constant) is loaded into the Instruction Register (IR) in a single memory access. This is a key efficiency benefit.

- **Stage 2 — Decode & Operand Extraction (ID):**
  The Control Unit (CU) decodes the opcode. Simultaneously, the immediate field is extracted from the instruction word and routed to the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) input bus or the General Purpose Register (GPR) write-back path.

- **Stage 3 — Execute (EX):**
  The ALU performs the requested operation (e.g., ADD, SUB, AND, OR) using the extracted immediate value as the source operand. The result is written to the destination register (specified in the instruction). **No memory operand fetch is issued.**

### 2.2 The Core "Why" — Architectural Justification

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the Immediate mode matter?**
> 1. **Speed:** It eliminates the *Effective Address (EA) calculation* stage entirely. There is no address arithmetic, no Memory Data Register (MDR) read, and no Address Register (AR) update.
> 2. **Determinism:** The value is known at *compile time*, enabling constant folding by the assembler/compiler.
> 3. **Code Density for Constants:** Loading small constants is more compact than allocating a memory word and using direct addressing.

### 2.3 Sign Extension — The Critical Subtletly

> [!WARNING]
> **Most Common KTU Board Mistake:** Students frequently forget that immediate values are **sign-extended** to match the destination register width before execution.

For an *n*-bit immediate $I$ loaded into an *N*-bit register (where $N > n$, typically $N = 32$ and $n = 16$):

$$
I_{\text{extended}} = 
\begin{cases}
I & \text{if } I \geq 0 \text{ (MSB of } I = 0) \\
I + 2^N & \text{if } I < 0 \text{ (MSB of } I = 1, \text{ two's complement wrap)}
\end{cases}
$$

Alternatively, in bit-replication form:

$$
I_{\text{extended}}[N-1 \colon 0] = \underbrace{I[n-1]}_{N-n \text{ times replicated}} \,\Vert\, I[n-1 \colon 0]
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Relation | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $EA$ | Effective Address | $EA = \text{PC} + \text{offset}$ (not used in immediate) | bytes |
| $I$ | Immediate operand (raw) | $I \in [-2^{n-1}, 2^{n}-1]$ | integer |
| $n$ | Immediate field width | **16 (MIPS)** / 8 or **16 (8086)** | bits |
| $I_{\text{ext}}$ | Sign-extended immediate | $I_{\text{ext}} = I \cdot \text{sext}$ | integer |
| $T_{\text{exec}}$ | Execution time per instruction | $T_{\text{exec}} = T_{\text{fetch}} + T_{\text{decode}} + T_{\text{ALU}}$ | ns |
| $T_{\text{mem}}$ | Memory access time | $T_{\text{mem}} = 0$ for immediate mode | ns |
| $\text{Throughput}$ | Instructions per cycle | $\text{IPC} = 1$ (single-cycle MIPS) | instr/cycle |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems Firmware:** Writing to hardware configuration registers (e.g., setting a GPIO pin high requires loading `1` into a specific bit — immediate mode is ideal).
- **Compiler Optimization:** Constant propagation and literal pool generation frequently emit immediate-mode instructions for small integer constants.
- **Cryptographic Accelerators:** XOR operations with embedded key bytes rely on immediate operands.
- **Operating System Kernels:** Setting up page table entries, interrupt masks, and segment selectors all use immediate values.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Sign Extension of an Immediate Value

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** You are given a 16-bit immediate $I = \texttt{0x8F2A}$ to be loaded into a 32-bit register. Show the full sign-extension process step by step.

**Given:**

$$
I = \texttt{0x8F2A} = 1000\,1111\,0010\,1010_2, \quad n = 16, \quad N = 32
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Most Significant Bit (MSB) of the immediate:**

$$
I_{15} = 1 \quad (\text{the leftmost bit of } \texttt{0x8F2A})
$$

Since $I_{15} = 1$, the value is **negative** in two's complement representation.

**Step 2 — Compute the numerical value of $I$ in 16-bit two's complement:**

$$
I_{16} = -2^{15} + \sum_{k=0}^{14} b_k \cdot 2^k
$$

$$
I_{16} = -32768 + (0 \cdot 2^{14} + 0 \cdot 2^{13} + 0 \cdot 2^{12} + 1 \cdot 2^{11} + 1 \cdot 2^{10} + 1 \cdot 2^9 + 1 \cdot 2^8 + 0 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 1 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0)
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the sum term-by-term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2048 + 1024 + 512 + 256 + 32 + 8 + 2 \\
&= 2048 + 1024 = 3072 \\
&+ 512 = 3584 \\
&+ 256 = 3840 \\
&+ 32 = 3872 \\
&+ 8 = 3880 \\
&+ 2 = 3882
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine to get the signed 16-bit value:**

$$
I_{16} = -32768 + 3882 = -28886
$$

**Step 5 — Perform sign extension to 32 bits:**

The MSB ($I_{15} = 1$) is replicated to fill the upper 16 bits of the 32-bit register:

$$
I_{32} = \underbrace{1111\,1111\,1111\,1111}_{16 \text{ copies of MSB}} \,1000\,1111\,0010\,1010
$$

**Step 6 — Verify equivalence in 32-bit two's complement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{32} &= -2^{31} + 2^{30} + 2^{29} + 2^{28} + 2^{27} + 2^{26} + 2^{25} + 2^{24} + 2^{23} + 2^{22} + 2^{21} + 2^{20} \\
&\quad + 2^{19} + 2^{18} + 2^{17} + 2^{16} + 3882
\end{aligned}
$$

The upper 16 bits sum to:

$$
\sum_{k=16}^{31} 2^k = 2^{32} - 2^{16} = 4294967296 - 65536 = 4294901760
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_{32} = -4294967296 + 4294901760 + 3882 = -28886 \;\;\checkmark
$$

The sign extension preserves the numerical value, as required.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Addressing Modes (KTU Favorite Question)

| Addressing Mode | Effective Address Calculation | Memory Refs | Speed | Example (8086) | Example (MIPS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Immediate** | $\text{None}$ | **0** | **Fastest** | `MOV AX, 1234H` | `ADDI \$t0, \$t1, 100` |
| Register | $\text{None}$ | 0 | Fastest | `MOV AX, BX` | `ADD \$t0, \$t1, \$t2` |
| Direct | $EA = \text{address field}$ | 1 | Slow | `MOV AX, [1234H]` | `LW \$t0, 0(\$t1)` (approx.) |
| Indirect | $EA = (R)$ | 1 | Slow | `MOV AX, [BX]` | — |
| Register Indirect | $EA = (R)$ | 1 | Moderate | `MOV AX, [SI]` | `LW \$t0, 0(\$t1)` |
| Displacement | $EA = (R) + \text{offset}$ | 1 | Moderate | `MOV AX, [BX+SI+10H]` | `LW \$t0, 100(\$t1)` |
| Indexed | $EA = (R) + \text{index reg}$ | 1 | Moderate | `MOV AX, [SI+10H]` | — |
| Relative | $EA = (PC) + \text{offset}$ | 1 (for branch) | Moderate | `JNC rel8` | `BEQ \$t0, \$t1, label` |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU Boards:** Immediate mode is the *only* non-register mode that **does not require an Effective Address (EA) calculation**. This is why it scores the highest marks in "Compare addressing modes" questions.

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CPU Data Path for Immediate Mode Execution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Main Memory"] -- "Fetch Instruction" --> B["Instruction Register"]
    B -- "Opcode field" --> C["Control Unit"]
    B -- "Immediate field 16 bits" --> D["Sign Extend Unit"]
    D -- "32 bit extended value" --> E["ALU Input B"]
    F["Register File Read Port 1"] -- "Operand A" --> G["ALU Input A"]
    E --> H["ALU Core"]
    G --> H
    C -- "ALU control signals" --> H
    H -- "Result" --> I["Register File Write Port"]
    I -- "Write back" --> J["Destination Register"]
    C -- "RF write enable" --> I
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow Explanation:**

1. The **Main Memory** holds the instruction. Only **one fetch** is required because the immediate is co-located with the opcode.
2. The **Instruction Register (IR)** captures the full instruction word. The *Control Unit* decodes the opcode, while the *Sign-Extend Unit* processes the immediate field.
3. The **Sign-Extend Unit** expands a 16-bit immediate to 32 bits by replicating the MSB.
4. The **ALU** receives the extended immediate on Input B and a register operand on Input A. It produces the result.
5. The **Register File** writes the result back to the destination register specified by the instruction.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Instruction Format for Immediate Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph instr["32-bit MIPS Instruction Word"]
        direction LR
        op["Opcode 6 bits"] --> rs["rs 5 bits"] --> rt["rt 5 bits"] --> imm["Immediate 16 bits"]
    end
    style instr fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
```

**Field-by-Field Functional Mapping:**

- **Opcode (6 bits):** Specifies the operation (e.g., `001000` for ADDI in MIPS).
- **rs (5 bits):** Source register number.
- **rt (5 bits):** Destination register number.
- **Immediate (16 bits):** The constant operand. After sign extension, it becomes a 32-bit value used by the ALU.

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Execution Cycle Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Issue Instruction"] --> B{"Addressing Mode?"}
    B -- "Immediate" --> C["Single Fetch from Memory"]
    B -- "Direct / Indirect" --> D["Instruction Fetch"]
    D --> E["Effective Address Calc"]
    E --> F["Operand Fetch from Memory"]
    C --> G["Decode + Execute in 1 cycle"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Write Back to Register"]
    H --> I["End"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The Immediate mode branch skips the entire *Effective Address Calculation* and *Operand Memory Fetch* stages, completing the instruction in **one fewer micro-step** than direct or indirect modes.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

Define the **Immediate Addressing Mode**. How does it differ from **Register Addressing Mode**? Give one example of each from the 8086 instruction set.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Immediate Addressing Mode** is one in which the source operand is a *constant* value that is *part of the instruction itself*. The processor does not need to access any memory location or register to obtain the operand — it is directly available in the instruction stream.
>
> **Register Addressing Mode**, on the other hand, specifies a *CPU register* as the source operand. The operand is the *contents* of the named register, not a literal.
>
> **Key Difference:** In immediate mode, the operand is a *literal constant* embedded in the instruction; in register mode, the operand is a *register reference* whose contents can change at runtime.
>
> **Example (Immediate):** `MOV AX, 1234H` — loads the constant `1234H` into AX.
> **Example (Register):** `MOV AX, BX` — copies the *contents* of register BX into AX.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

What is the role of **sign extension** when loading an immediate value into a register? Show the result of sign-extending `0xFA80` (16-bit) to 32 bits.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Role of Sign Extension:** When an immediate operand is smaller in width than the destination register, the upper bits must be filled. Sign extension preserves the *sign* (positive/negative) of the value by replicating the Most Significant Bit (MSB) of the immediate into all upper bit positions.
>
> **For `0xFA80` (binary: `1111 1010 1000 0000`):**
> - MSB = 1 (the value is negative).
> - Sign-extended to 32 bits: `1111 1111 1111 1111 1111 1010 1000 0000` = `0xFFFAFA80`.
>
> This preserves the numerical value $-1408$ in 32-bit two's complement.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are given an internal choice between two full questions (Question A OR Question B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Immediate Addressing Mode** in detail. Describe its instruction format with a suitable diagram. List **two advantages** and **two limitations** of this mode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 16-bit immediate `0xA47C` is sign-extended and added to register `$t1` containing the value `0x00000018`. The instruction used is `ADDI $t2, $t1, 0xA47C`. Compute the final 32-bit result stored in `$t2`. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The Immediate Addressing Mode is an addressing technique in which the operand (a constant) is embedded within the instruction itself, rather than being fetched from a memory location or register.

**Instruction Format (2 Marks):**

$$
\text{Instruction} = \underbrace{\text{Opcode}}_{k \text{ bits}} \,\Vert\, \underbrace{I}_{n \text{ bits (immediate)}}
$$

For MIPS R-type/I-type hybrid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Instruction Word (32 bits)} &= \text{Opcode} \mid \text{Rs} \mid \text{Rt} \mid \text{Immediate} \\
&= 6 \text{ bits} \mid 5 \text{ bits} \mid 5 \text{ bits} \mid 16 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Diagram (1 Mark):**

(See the Mermaid instruction-format diagram in Section 4.2 above.)

**Advantages (2 Marks):**
1. **Fastest execution** — No memory reference for the operand; only a single instruction fetch is required.
2. **No effective address calculation** — Saves ALU cycles and simplifies the control unit.
3. **Compact code for small constants** — More memory-efficient than allocating a memory word.

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
1. **Limited operand size** — The immediate field width (e.g., 16 bits) restricts the range of representable constants.
2. **Not suitable for runtime-variable data** — The value is fixed at compile time; cannot reference dynamic values.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Identify the 16-bit immediate and the register value (1 Mark):**

$$
I_{16} = \texttt{0xA47C}, \quad R[\$t1] = \texttt{0x00000018}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert `0xA47C` to binary (1 Mark):**

$$
\texttt{0xA47C} = 1010\,0100\,0111\,1100_2
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the sign (1 Mark):**

The MSB of `0xA47C` is `1` (since `A = 1010` in binary). Therefore, the value is **negative** in 16-bit two's complement.

**Step 4 — Compute the signed numerical value (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{16} &= -2^{15} + (0 \cdot 2^{14} + 1 \cdot 2^{13} + 0 \cdot 2^{12} + 0 \cdot 2^{11} + 1 \cdot 2^{10} + 0 \cdot 2^9 + 0 \cdot 2^8 + 0 \cdot 2^7 + 1 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 1 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0) \\
&= -32768 + (8192 + 1024 + 64 + 32 + 16 + 8 + 4) \\
&= -32768 + 9340 \\
&= -23428
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sign-extend to 32 bits (1 Mark):**

$$
I_{32} = \texttt{0xFFFFA47C} = 1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1010\,0100\,0111\,1100
$$

**Step 6 — Perform the ADDI operation (2 Marks):**

$$
R[\$t2] = R[\$t1] + I_{32} = \texttt{0x00000018} + \texttt{0xFFFFA47C}
$$

Performing 32-bit addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\texttt{0x00000018} &= 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001\,1000 \\
\texttt{0xFFFFA47C} &= 1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1010\,0100\,0111\,1100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
R[\$t2] = \underbrace{\texttt{0xFFFFA494}}_{\text{final result}}
$$

In decimal:

$$
24 + (-23428) = -23404
$$

**Final Answer:** `$t2 = 0xFFFFA494` (decimal: $-23404$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Compare the **Immediate**, **Direct**, and **Register Indirect** addressing modes in terms of (i) number of memory references, (ii) effective address calculation, (iii) execution speed, and (iv) typical use case. Use a tabular format. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write the sequence of MIPS instructions to compute `Y = (A + 5) - B` where `A`, `B`, and `Y` are memory locations. Use **immediate addressing** wherever possible. Show the data path for one of the immediate instructions. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Criterion | Immediate | Direct | Register Indirect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory references for operand | **0** | 1 | 1 |
| Effective address calculation | **None** | $EA = \text{Address field}$ | $EA = (R)$ |
| Execution speed | **Fastest** | Slow | Moderate |
| Typical use case | Loading constants | Accessing globals | Pointer dereferencing, arrays |
| Operand location | In the instruction | Memory at fixed address | Memory at address held in register |
| Example | `ADDI \$t0, \$0, 100` | `LW \$t0, 5000` | `LW \$t0, 0(\$t1)` |

**Explanation (2 Marks):**

- **Immediate mode** is the fastest because it eliminates the need for any memory access to retrieve the operand. The constant is co-located with the instruction.
- **Direct mode** requires one memory access to read the operand from a fixed memory address specified in the instruction. It is slower but allows referencing global/static variables.
- **Register Indirect mode** uses the contents of a register as the effective address. This is widely used for pointer-based data structures, array traversal, and stack operations.

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**MIPS Instruction Sequence (5 Marks):**

Assuming `A` is at address `100`, `B` is at address `104`, and `Y` is at address `108`:

```text
LW   $t0, 100($zero)    # Load A from memory into $t0
ADDI $t1, $t0, 5         # $t1 = A + 5  (IMMEDIATE addressing)
LW   $t2, 104($zero)    # Load B from memory into $t2
SUB  $t3, $t1, $t2      # $t3 = (A+5) - B
SW   $t3, 108($zero)    # Store result in Y
```

**Data Path Diagram for `ADDI $t1, $t0, 5` (2 Marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M["Instruction Memory"] -- "Fetch: ADDI instr" --> IR["Instruction Register"]
    IR -- "Opcode" --> CU["Control Unit"]
    IR -- "Immediate 5" --> SX["Sign Extend Unit"]
    IR -- "rt field" --> RFW["RF Write Select"]
    SX -- "32b 5" --> ALUB["ALU Input B"]
    RF1["Register File Port 1"] -- "$t0 contents" --> ALUA["ALU Input A"]
    ALUA --> ALUC["ALU: ADD"]
    ALUB --> ALUC
    CU -- "ALU op = ADD" --> ALUC
    ALUC -- "Result $t1" --> RFW
    RFW -- "Write to $t1" --> RF2["Register File"]
    CU -- "RegWrite=1" --> RF2
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students often **omit the sign-extension step** in data path diagrams — this is a **1-mark deduction** in the KTU 2024 scheme. Always show the Sign-Extend block explicitly.
> - Do **not** confuse `ADDI` (add immediate) with `ADD` (add register). `ADDI` takes a constant; `ADD` takes two register operands.
> - In MIPS, register `$zero` is hard-wired to `0`, so `100($zero)` is equivalent to direct addressing at address 100.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Immediate Mode = Constant embedded in the instruction.** Zero memory references for the operand.
- ✅ **Fastest addressing mode** in terms of operand access (no EA calculation).
- ✅ **Typical immediate field width:** 16 bits (MIPS) or 8/16 bits (8086).
- ✅ **Sign extension is mandatory** when loading into a wider register — replicate the MSB.
- ✅ **MIPS example:** `ADDI \$rt, \$rs, imm` performs `\$rt = \$rs + sign\_extend(imm)`.
- ✅ **8086 example:** `MOV AX, 1234H` loads the constant `1234H` into AX.
- ✅ **Limitation:** Operand size is limited by the immediate field width. Large constants require `LUI + ORI` (MIPS) or multiple instructions.
- ✅ **Use case dominance:** Loading small integer constants, setting hardware registers, computing addresses with fixed offsets.
- ✅ **Compare with Register mode:** Register mode reads *contents* of a CPU register; Immediate mode provides a *literal constant* directly.
- ✅ **KTU exam-favorite phrasing:** *"Distinguish between Immediate and Direct addressing modes."* — Always highlight that immediate mode has *no memory operand fetch*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Base
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Base

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of "Base" (Radix)

In the context of **Computer Organisation and Architecture (COA)**, the term **Base** (also called **Radix**) of a number system is defined as the total number of unique digits (including the digit zero) that a positional numeral system uses to represent all possible numerical values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The **base** $b$ of a number system is the number of unique symbols (digits) used in that system, and it determines the weight of each digit position as an integer power of $b$. For any number represented as $(d_n d_{n-1} \dots d_1 d_0 . d_{-1} \dots d_{-m})_b$, its value is computed using positional weights of $b$.

Mathematically, if a number $N$ is expressed in base $b$, it is written as:

$$N = (d_{n-1} d_{n-2} \dots d_1 d_0 . d_{-1} d_{-2} \dots d_{-m})_b$$

where each digit $d_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, b-1\}$.

### 1.2 Common Bases Used in Computer Architecture

| Base | Name | Digits Used | Common Use in COA |
|------|------|-------------|-------------------|
| **2** | Binary | 0, 1 | Internal CPU/ALU operations |
| **8** | Octal | 0–7 | Compact representation of binary |
| **10** | Decimal | 0–9 | Human-readable / Input-Output |
| **16** | Hexadecimal | 0–9, A–F | Memory addresses, machine code |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Fact for KTU Exams:** In any base-$b$ system, the largest digit allowed is $(b-1)$, and the digit positions carry weights $b^0, b^1, b^2, \dots$ moving from right to left (after the radix point: $b^{-1}, b^{-2}, \dots$).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **base** like the **"language grammar" of counting**:

- Imagine counting marbles using only your **two hands' fingers** → you naturally form the **binary (base-2)** system because you run out of new symbols after 2.
- If you count with all **10 fingers**, you form the **decimal (base-10)** system.
- If a clock face replaces 10 with the letter **A**, then 10, then **B**, then **C**, then **D**, then **E**, then **F** — you have **hexadecimal (base-16)**, which fits perfectly with the CPU's preference for grouping **4 binary bits** ($2^4 = 16$).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** "Base = Number of unique symbols available before you 'roll over' to the next position." Just like the odometer of a car in base-10 rolls from 9 to 10, a base-2 system rolls from 1 to 10.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Positional Number System Theory

In a positional (weighted) number system, the value of a number depends on:
1. **The digits** used in each position.
2. **The position** (place value) of each digit.
3. **The base** $b$ of the system.

The generalized expansion formula for any number in base $b$ is:

$$N_b = \sum_{i=-m}^{n-1} d_i \cdot b^i$$

where:
- $d_i$ = digit at position $i$
- $b^i$ = positional weight
- $n$ = number of integer digits
- $m$ = number of fractional digits

### 2.2 Why Different Bases Matter in Computer Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Computers are fundamentally built from **transistors** that operate as **two-state switches (ON/OFF)** — this physical constraint forces base-2 (binary) as the native language of the machine. However, humans need shorter, human-friendly notations, so engineers use **octal** and **hexadecimal** as **shorthand groupings** of binary bits.

**Why these specific groupings?**
- **Octal:** 1 octal digit = **3 binary bits** ($2^3 = 8$)
- **Hexadecimal:** 1 hex digit = **4 binary bits** ($2^4 = 16$)

This is why base-8 and base-16 are "natural" companions of base-2 in COA.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Conversion Type | Method / Formula | Example |
|----------------|------------------|---------|
| Any Base → Decimal | $\sum d_i \cdot b^i$ | $(1011)_2 = 8+0+2+1 = 11$ |
| Decimal → Any Base (Integer) | Repeated Division by $b$ | $25 \div 2 = 12$ R **1** |
| Decimal → Any Base (Fraction) | Repeated Multiplication by $b$ | $0.625 \times 2 = 1.25$ |
| Binary → Octal | Group bits in **3s** from radix point | $101\,110 \to 56$ |
| Octal → Binary | Expand each octal digit to **3 bits** | $(7)_8 \to 111$ |
| Binary → Hexadecimal | Group bits in **4s** from radix point | $1010\,1100 \to AC$ |
| Hexadecimal → Binary | Expand each hex digit to **4 bits** | $(F)_{16} \to 1111$ |
| Octal → Hexadecimal | Binary → Hex (via grouping 4s) | Use binary intermediary |
| Hexadecimal → Octal | Binary → Octal (via grouping 3s) | Use binary intermediary |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - All digits must satisfy: $0 \le d_i \le (b-1)$
> - The fractional conversion **may not terminate** in some bases — keep track of precision.
> - For hex digits: A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Memory Addressing:** Modern 32-bit and 64-bit CPUs use **hexadecimal** to display memory addresses (e.g., `0x7FFE3A2C`) because each hex digit represents exactly 4 bits.
- **Network Engineering:** IPv6 addresses are written in **hexadecimal** (`2001:0db8::`) for compactness.
- **Assembly Language:** All opcodes, registers, and addresses in machine code are presented in hex.
- **Embedded Systems:** Octal is used in Unix file permissions (`chmod 755`).
- **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal):** Used in digital clocks, calculators, and financial systems where decimal accuracy is critical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Method 1: Convert Any Base to Decimal (Direct Expansion)

**Procedure:** Expand the number using positional weights.

**Example:** Convert $(1A4)_{16}$ to decimal.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(1A4)_{16} &= 1 \times 16^2 + A \times 16^1 + 4 \times 16^0 \\
&= 1 \times 256 + 10 \times 16 + 4 \times 1 \\
&= 256 + 160 + 4 \\
&= 420_{10}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Each digit is multiplied by its positional weight ($b^i$), then summed. Letter digits (A–F) must first be converted to their decimal equivalents.

---

### 3.2 Method 2: Convert Decimal Integer to Any Base (Repeated Division)

**Procedure:** Repeatedly divide by the target base; collect remainders in **reverse order**.

**Example:** Convert $(173)_{10}$ to binary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
173 \div 2 &= 86 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
86 \div 2 &= 43 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{0} \\
43 \div 2 &= 21 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
21 \div 2 &= 10 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
10 \div 2 &= 5 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{0} \\
5 \div 2 &= 2 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
2 \div 2 &= 1 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{0} \\
1 \div 2 &= 0 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading remainders **bottom to top**: $(10101101)_2$

**Verification:** $128 + 0 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 1 = 173$ ✓

---

### 3.3 Method 3: Convert Decimal Fraction to Any Base (Repeated Multiplication)

**Procedure:** Repeatedly multiply the fractional part by the base; record the integer part each time.

**Example:** Convert $(0.625)_{10}$ to binary.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.625 \times 2 &= 1.250 \quad \rightarrow \text{integer } = \mathbf{1}, \text{ fraction} = 0.250 \\
0.250 \times 2 &= 0.500 \quad \rightarrow \text{integer } = \mathbf{0}, \text{ fraction} = 0.500 \\
0.500 \times 2 &= 1.000 \quad \rightarrow \text{integer } = \mathbf{1}, \text{ fraction} = 0.000
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading top to bottom: $(0.101)_2$

**Verification:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.101)_2 &= 1 \times 2^{-1} + 0 \times 2^{-2} + 1 \times 2^{-3} \\
&= 0.5 + 0 + 0.125 = 0.625_{10} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Method 4: Binary ↔ Octal (3-bit Grouping)

**Example:** Convert $(101110011.1011)_2$ to octal.

**Step 1:** Group bits in **triplets** from the radix point (both directions), padding with zeros if needed.

$$101\,110\,011\,.\,101\,100$$

**Step 2:** Convert each triplet using $2^2, 2^1, 2^0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
101 &= 4+0+1 = 5 \\
110 &= 4+2+0 = 6 \\
011 &= 0+2+1 = 3 \\
101 &= 4+0+1 = 5 \\
100 &= 4+0+0 = 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $(563.54)_8$

---

### 3.5 Method 5: Binary ↔ Hexadecimal (4-bit Grouping)

**Example:** Convert $(1101011110.101110)_2$ to hexadecimal.

**Step 1:** Pad to groups of 4 (from radix point):

$$0011\,0101\,1110\,.\,1011\,1000$$

**Step 2:** Convert each group:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0011 &= 0+0+2+1 = 3 \\
0101 &= 0+4+0+1 = 5 \\
1110 &= 8+4+2+0 = 14 = \mathbf{E} \\
1011 &= 8+0+2+1 = 11 = \mathbf{B} \\
1000 &= 8+0+0+0 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $(35E.B8)_{16}$

---

### 3.6 Conversion Analysis Matrix

| From → To | Direct Method? | Recommended Path | Difficulty |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|-----------|
| Decimal ↔ Binary | ✅ Yes | Direct expansion / division | Easy |
| Decimal ↔ Octal | ✅ Yes | Direct expansion / division | Easy |
| Decimal ↔ Hex | ✅ Yes | Direct expansion / division | Easy |
| Binary ↔ Octal | ✅ Yes | 3-bit grouping | Easy |
| Binary ↔ Hex | ✅ Yes | 4-bit grouping | Easy |
| Octal ↔ Hex | ❌ Indirect | Octal → Binary → Hex | Medium |
| Any-Base to Any-Base | ❌ Indirect | Convert via Decimal or Binary | Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why go through Decimal or Binary for indirect conversions?**
> Because the positional weight formula $b^i$ only directly applies to the base being expanded into base-10. There is no direct "octal-to-hex" weight system — you must go through a common reference (decimal or binary is preferred since binary is the CPU's native language).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Conversion Flow Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete conversion strategy between the four primary number systems used in Computer Organisation:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Decimal Base 10] -->|Repeated Division by b| B[Target Base b]
    B -->|Positional Expansion Σ d_i·b^i| A
    A -->|Divide by 2 repeatedly| C[Binary Base 2]
    C -->|Group 3 bits| D[Octal Base 8]
    C -->|Group 4 bits| E[Hexadecimal Base 16]
    D -->|Expand each digit to 3 bits| C
    E -->|Expand each digit to 4 bits| C
    D -->|Binary bridge| E
    E -->|Binary bridge| D
    C -->|Σ d_i·2^i| A
    D -->|Σ d_i·8^i| A
    E -->|Σ d_i·16^i| A
```

### 4.2 Hex Digit ↔ Binary Reference Chart (Architect's Quick-Look)

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEX0[0]:::nodeA --> BIN0000[0000]
    HEX1[1]:::nodeA --> BIN0001[0001]
    HEX2[2]:::nodeA --> BIN0010[0010]
    HEX3[3]:::nodeA --> BIN0011[0011]
    HEX4[4]:::nodeA --> BIN0100[0100]
    HEX5[5]:::nodeA --> BIN0101[0101]
    HEX6[6]:::nodeA --> BIN0110[0110]
    HEX7[7]:::nodeA --> BIN0111[0111]
    HEX8[8]:::nodeA --> BIN1000[1000]
    HEX9[9]:::nodeA --> BIN1001[1001]
    HEXA[A=10]:::nodeB --> BIN1010[1010]
    HEXB[B=11]:::nodeB --> BIN1011[1011]
    HEXC[C=12]:::nodeB --> BIN1100[1100]
    HEXD[D=13]:::nodeB --> BIN1101[1101]
    HEXE[E=14]:::nodeB --> BIN1110[1110]
    HEXF[F=15]:::nodeB --> BIN1111[1111]

    classDef nodeA fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1976D2,color:#000
    classDef nodeB fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Repeated Division Algorithm — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Decimal N]) --> init[Set i = 0]
    init --> check{N > 0?}
    check -- No --> reverse[Reverse Collect Remainders]
    check -- Yes --> div[N ÷ b = Quotient Q, Remainder R]
    div --> store[Store R in array position i]
    store --> assign[N ← Q]
    assign --> inc[i ← i + 1]
    inc --> check
    reverse --> end([Output: Number in base b])

    style start fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32
    style end fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828
    style check fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Interpretation:** Each cycle of the loop "carves off" the lowest-order digit of the number in the new base. The **first remainder** is the **least significant digit (LSD)**, and the **last remainder** is the **most significant digit (MSD)** — this is why we read the remainders in **reverse order**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the term "Base" (Radix) of a number system. Why is base-2 the natural number system for digital computers?

**Model Answer:**

The **base** (or **radix**) of a number system is the total number of unique digit symbols available to represent all numbers in that system. It defines the positional weight of each digit as a power of the base.

Base-2 (binary) is the natural choice for digital computers because:
1. Digital circuits are built from transistors that have **two stable states**: ON (1) and OFF (0).
2. Binary signals are highly **noise-resistant** — only a clear distinction between two voltage levels is required.
3. Binary maps directly to **Boolean logic** (TRUE/FALSE), enabling logic gates and arithmetic circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Allocation:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for justification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
State any three differences between the binary, octal, and hexadecimal number systems.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Binary | Octal | Hexadecimal |
|----------|--------|-------|-------------|
| Base | 2 | 8 | 16 |
| Digits used | 0, 1 | 0–7 | 0–9, A–F |
| Grouping of bits | Native (1 bit) | 3 bits per digit | 4 bits per digit |
| Common Use | Internal CPU | Unix permissions | Memory addresses |

Any three of the above are accepted.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Convert the decimal number **(245.375)₁₀** into its equivalent **binary** number. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert integer part 245 to binary (Repeated Division)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
245 \div 2 &= 122 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
122 \div 2 &= 61 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{0} \\
61 \div 2 &= 30 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
30 \div 2 &= 15 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{0} \\
15 \div 2 &= 7 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
7 \div 2 &= 3 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
3 \div 2 &= 1 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
1 \div 2 &= 0 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading bottom to top: $(245)_{10} = (11110101)_2$

**Step 2: Convert fractional part 0.375 to binary (Repeated Multiplication)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.375 \times 2 &= 0.750 \quad \rightarrow \mathbf{0} \\
0.750 \times 2 &= 1.500 \quad \rightarrow \mathbf{1} \\
0.500 \times 2 &= 1.000 \quad \rightarrow \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading top to bottom: $(0.375)_{10} = (0.011)_2$

**Step 3: Combine both parts.**

$$(245.375)_{10} = (11110101.011)_2$$

**Verification:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
(11110101.011)_2 &= 128+64+32+16+0+4+0+1 + 0+0.25+0.125 \\
&= 245 + 0.375 = 245.375_{10} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b)** Convert the binary number **(101101.1101)₂** into its equivalent **octal** and **hexadecimal** representations. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part 1: Binary → Octal (3-bit grouping)**

Group bits in triplets from radix point:
$$101\,101\,.\,110\,100$$

Convert each group:
- $101 = 4+0+1 = 5$
- $101 = 4+0+1 = 5$
- $110 = 4+2+0 = 6$
- $100 = 4+0+0 = 4$

$$\boxed{(101101.1101)_2 = (55.64)_8}$$

**Part 2: Binary → Hexadecimal (4-bit grouping)**

Group bits in quadruplets from radix point (pad with zeros):
$$0010\,1101\,.\,1101$$

Convert each group:
- $0010 = 0+0+2+0 = 2$
- $1101 = 8+4+0+1 = 13 = \mathbf{D}$
- $1101 = 8+4+0+1 = 13 = \mathbf{D}$

$$\boxed{(101101.1101)_2 = (2D.D)_{16}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a suitable example, explain the **"Divide-by-Base" method** for converting a decimal integer into any other base. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
The divide-by-base method (also called **repeated division method**) works on the principle that the **remainder** at each division step gives the **next digit** (in least-significant-first order) of the number in the new base.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Divide the decimal number by the target base $b$.
2. Record the **remainder** (this is the next LSD).
3. Replace the number with the **quotient**.
4. Repeat until quotient becomes 0.
5. Read the remainders in **reverse order** (bottom-up) to get the final answer.

**Worked Example:** Convert $(89)_{10}$ to base 4.

$$
\begin{aligned}
89 \div 4 &= 22 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
22 \div 4 &= 5 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{2} \\
5 \div 4 &= 1 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1} \\
1 \div 4 &= 0 \text{ remainder } \mathbf{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading bottom-up: $(89)_{10} = (1121)_4$

**Verification:**
$$1 \times 4^3 + 1 \times 4^2 + 2 \times 4^1 + 1 \times 4^0 = 64+16+8+1 = 89 \quad \checkmark$$

**Why it works:** Since $N = q \cdot b + r$, the first remainder $r$ is the digit in the units place ($b^0$), and the quotient $q$ is what we divide next to get the next-higher digit.

---

**(b)** Convert the octal number **(327.5)₈** into hexadecimal. Show every intermediate step. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Octal → Binary (3-bit expansion of each digit)**

Expand each octal digit into 3 binary bits:
- $3 = 011$
- $2 = 010$
- $7 = 111$
- $5 = 101$

$$\boxed{(327.5)_8 = (011\,010\,111\,.\,101)_2}$$

Or simplified (remove leading zero): $(11010111.101)_2$

**Step 2: Binary → Hexadecimal (4-bit grouping from radix point)**

Pad to groups of 4:
$$1101\,0111\,.\,1010$$

Convert each group:
- $1101 = 8+4+0+1 = 13 = \mathbf{D}$
- $0111 = 0+4+2+1 = 7$
- $1010 = 8+0+2+0 = 10 = \mathbf{A}$

$$\boxed{(327.5)_8 = (D7.A)_{16}}$$

**Verification via decimal bridge:**
- $(327.5)_8 = 3(64)+2(8)+7+5(0.125) = 192+16+7+0.625 = 215.625_{10}$
- $(D7.A)_{16} = 13(16)+7+10(0.0625) = 208+7+0.625 = 215.625_{10}$ ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Reversing digit order** in the divide-by-base method: Students often read remainders top-down instead of **bottom-up**. The *first* remainder is the **LSD**, the *last* remainder is the **MSD**.
>
> 2. **Improper padding in grouping:** When grouping binary bits into 3s or 4s, students forget to add **leading/trailing zeros** to complete the last group. Padding goes outward from the radix point.
>
> 3. **Invalid digits in a given base:** A digit must always satisfy $d_i < b$. Writing $(2A2)_{16}$ is fine, but $(2A2)_{8}$ is **invalid** because 'A' is not an octal digit.
>
> 4. **Hex letter confusion:** Students sometimes write `13` instead of `D`, or forget that $A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15$.
>
> 5. **No verification step:** Examiners award 1 mark for verification. Always re-check your answer by expanding it back into decimal.
>
> 6. **Fractional precision:** For terminating decimals in one base, the same number may be **non-terminating** in another base. State "approximate" when necessary.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Base (Radix)** = total number of unique digits in a number system.
- ✅ In base-$b$: allowed digits are $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, b-1\}$; positional weights are powers of $b$.
- ✅ **Decimal (base-10)** is human-oriented; **Binary (base-2)** is computer-oriented.
- ✅ **Octal** groups binary in 3s; **Hexadecimal** groups binary in 4s.
- ✅ **Convert to decimal:** Use the positional expansion formula $N = \sum d_i \cdot b^i$.
- ✅ **Convert from decimal (integer):** Use **repeated division by $b$**, read remainders in reverse.
- ✅ **Convert from decimal (fraction):** Use **repeated multiplication by $b$**, read integers in order.
- ✅ **Binary ↔ Octal:** Group/ungroup in 3 bits; pad with zeros at the edges.
- ✅ **Binary ↔ Hex:** Group/ungroup in 4 bits; pad with zeros at the edges.
- ✅ **Octal ↔ Hex:** Use **binary as the bridge** (always!).
- ✅ Hex letters: A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.
- ✅ Always **verify** by converting your final answer back to decimal.
- ✅ Engineering uses: Memory addressing (hex), Unix permissions (octal), BCD (financial/clock systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PC-Relative
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** PC-Relative

# PC-Relative Addressing — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> *PC-Relative Addressing* is a mode of addressing in which the operand's effective memory address is computed by adding a signed displacement (offset) contained within the instruction to the current contents of the **Program Counter (PC)**. It is fundamentally used to implement branch and jump instructions that must remain *position-independent* in memory.

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In a **PC-Relative addressing** scheme, the CPU's Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) performs an address calculation during the **Execute (EX)** stage of the instruction pipeline. The Effective Address (EA) is evaluated as:

$$\text{EA} = \text{PC}_{\text{updated}} + \text{Offset}$$

Where:
- **PC_updated** = Value of the Program Counter after it has been incremented to point to the *next sequential instruction* (typically `PC + 2` for 16-bit ISAs and `PC + 4` for 32-bit MIPS/RISC-V ISAs).
- **Offset** = A signed, typically two's-complement binary displacement embedded in the instruction word itself (e.g., 8-bit, 16-bit, or 19-bit depending on architecture).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Library Index Card System"**
>
> Imagine you are reading a book and you reach a footnote that says: *"Go back 4 pages and read paragraph 3."* You don't need the absolute page number of that paragraph. You only need a **relative reference from where you are now**. PC-Relative addressing works identically: instead of embedding a large absolute memory address (which would require many bits and would break if the program were loaded at a different address), the instruction embeds a *small signed distance* measured from the current instruction. The CPU just says: *"Jump 10 instructions ahead"* or *"Jump 5 instructions behind."*

## 1.3 Why PC-Relative Addressing is Critical

| Property | Engineering Benefit |
|---|---|
| **Position-Independent Code** | The same program can be loaded at **any memory address** without modification (critical for shared libraries, position-independent executables, PIC). |
| **Reduced Instruction Size** | Branch instructions only need a small offset field (e.g., **8-bit** or **16-bit**) instead of a full **32-bit absolute address**, saving memory bandwidth. |
| **Hardware Simplicity** | The adder used for PC incrementation can be **reused** for EA computation — saving silicon area. |
| **Faster Pipelining** | Branch target can be computed early in EX stage since the offset is already decoded from the instruction register. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step by Step

> [!NOTE]
> **The Lifecycle of a PC-Relative Branch Instruction:**
>
> 1. **IF (Instruction Fetch) Stage:** PC holds the address of the current branch instruction. Instruction is fetched from memory at address `PC`.
> 2. **ID (Instruction Decode) Stage:** PC is incremented to `PC + N` (where `N` = instruction word length in bytes). The offset field is sign-extended to match the register width. The branch condition (for conditional branches) is evaluated.
> 3. **EX (Execute) Stage:** The ALU computes `EA = (PC + N) + sign_extended_offset`.
> 4. **MEM (Memory Access) Stage:** For unconditional branches, the PC register is simply overwritten with `EA`. For conditional branches, the PC is updated *only if* the condition is true; otherwise execution falls through to `PC + N` (next instruction).
> 5. **WB (Write Back):** No register write-back occurs for branch instructions.

## 2.2 Key Architectural Distinctions

### 2.2.1 MIPS Convention (32-bit architecture)
In MIPS, every instruction is **4 bytes** (32 bits). Therefore, before adding the offset, the PC is first incremented by 4. Then the **16-bit signed offset** is added:

$$\text{EA}_{\text{MIPS}} = (\text{PC} + 4) + \text{sign\_extend}(\text{offset}_{16\text{-bit}})$$

The branch range in MIPS is therefore: **±2¹⁵ instructions = ±128 KB** from the branch instruction.

### 2.2.2 RISC-V Convention
RISC-V uses a **12-bit signed offset** for conditional branches (`BEQ`, `BNE`, etc.) and a **20-bit offset** for unconditional jumps (`JAL`). The increment is by **2 bytes** (for compressed) or **4 bytes** (for standard 32-bit).

### 2.2.3 8051 Microcontroller Convention
In the 8051 family, conditional jumps like `SJMP` (Short Jump) and `JC` (Jump if Carry) use an **8-bit signed relative offset**, giving a branch range of **−128 to +127 bytes** from the next instruction.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in every KTU board exam on this topic.**

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Effective Address | $\text{EA} = \text{PC}_{\text{next}} + \text{Offset}$ | Core equation |
| PC Update | $\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + N$ | $N$ = instruction size in bytes |
| Branch Range (Forward) | $+ (2^{k-1} - 1) \times N$ | $k$ = offset bits |
| Branch Range (Backward) | $- (2^{k-1}) \times N$ | Two's complement minimum |
| Total Branch Span | $2^k \times N$ bytes | Full reach |
| Sign Extension | Replicate MSB of offset to fill upper bits | Critical step |

**Standard KTU Architecture Test Values (Memorize):**

| Architecture | Instruction Size $N$ | Offset Bits $k$ | Branch Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| MIPS-32 | **4 bytes** | **16 bits** | ±128 KB |
| RISC-V (Conditional) | **4 bytes** | **12 bits** | ±8 KB |
| RISC-V (JAL) | **4 bytes** | **20 bits** | ±1 MB |
| 8051 (SJMP) | **1–3 bytes** | **8 bits** | **−128 to +127 bytes** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never use the vertical bar `|` for absolute value in KTU answer sheets — KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous notation. Always write $\vert \text{EA} \vert$ explicitly or use "magnitude of EA".

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compilers:** GCC's `-fPIC` (Position Independent Code) flag explicitly generates PC-relative addressing for shared library code on Linux.
- **Bootloaders:** U-Boot and BIOS ROMs use PC-relative jumps because absolute addresses are unknown until runtime.
- **Operating Systems:** The Linux kernel's position-independent relocations rely on PC-relative arithmetic.
- **Embedded Systems:** 8051 firmware uses PC-relative jumps extensively due to the small 8-bit offset efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example #1 — MIPS-32 PC-Relative Branch (KTU Standard Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> A MIPS-32 program is executing at memory location `0x00400020`. The current instruction is a conditional branch with a 16-bit signed offset field equal to `0xFFFA` (in two's complement). Calculate the Effective Address (EA) of the branch target.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the instruction size for MIPS-32.**
All MIPS instructions are 32 bits = **4 bytes**. Therefore, the PC must be incremented by 4 before adding the offset.

$$\text{PC}_{\text{current}} = 0x00400020$$
$$\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 4 = 0x00400020 + 0x00000004 = 0x00400024$$

**Step 2: Interpret the 16-bit signed offset.**

The raw offset value is `0xFFFA`. To find its signed decimal equivalent, we apply two's complement analysis:

$$\text{Since the MSB of } 0x\text{FFFA is 1, the number is negative.}$$
$$\text{Compute the magnitude: } \text{Invert bits and add 1.}$$
$$\sim 0x\text{FFFA} = 0x0005$$
$$0x0005 + 0x0001 = 0x0006$$
$$\text{Therefore, signed value} = -6$$

Verification via formula for 16-bit two's complement:
$$\text{Offset}_{\text{signed}} = 0x\text{FFFA} - 0x10000 = 65530 - 65536 = -6$$

**Step 3: Sign-extend the 16-bit offset to 32 bits.**

The MSB of `0xFFFA` is `1`, so the upper 16 bits are filled with `1`s:

$$\text{Sign-extended offset} = 0x\text{FFFF}FFFA$$

**Step 4: Compute the Effective Address (EA).**

$$\text{EA} = \text{PC}_{\text{next}} + \text{Sign-extended offset}$$
$$\text{EA} = 0x00400024 + 0x\text{FFFF}FFFA$$

Performing the addition:
$$0x00400024 + 0xFFFF\text{FFFA}$$

Let's add component-wise (handling the carry carefully):
$$\begin{aligned}
0x00400024 &= 0000\ 0000\ 0100\ 0000\ 0000\ 0000\ 0010\ 0100 \\
0xFFFF\text{FFFA} &= 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1111\ 1010 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Adding the least significant 32 bits column-wise, the result wraps around modulo $2^{32}$:

$$\text{EA} = 0x0040001\text{E}$$

**Step 5: Verify the answer in decimal.**

$$0x0040001\text{E} = (4 \times 16^7) + (0 \times 16^6) + \ldots + (1 \times 16^1) + (14 \times 16^0)$$
$$= 67108864 + 30 = 67108894$$

Checking: $0x00400024 - 6 = 4194340 - 6 = 4194334 = 0x0040001\text{E}$ ✓

**Final Answer:** The branch target is at `0x0040001E`, which is **6 instructions backward** from the next sequential instruction.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example #2 — 8051 PC-Relative Jump Range Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:**
> In an 8051 microcontroller, the instruction `SJMP rel` is located at address `0x0100` in code memory. The 8-bit relative offset byte is `0x7F`. Calculate the jump target address.

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1: Identify the instruction size.**

The 8051 `SJMP` instruction is **2 bytes** (opcode byte + relative offset byte).

**Step 2: Update the PC.**

After the CPU fetches the 2-byte instruction, the PC advances to:

$$\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = 0x0100 + 2 = 0x0102$$

**Step 3: Interpret the 8-bit signed offset.**

$$\text{Offset} = 0x7\text{F} = 127_{10} \quad (\text{MSB} = 0, \text{so positive})$$

**Step 4: Sign-extend and compute EA.**

$$\text{EA} = 0x0102 + 0x007\text{F} = 0x0181$$

**Final Answer:** Jump target is at `0x0181` (127 bytes forward from `0x0102`).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Analysis
