**Final Value of Accumulator:**
$$\boxed{AC = 0700_8}$$

**Memory References Count:**
- Reference 1: Fetching instruction from $100_8$
- Reference 2: Fetching operand from $250_8$
$$\boxed{N_{ref} = 2}$$

**(Award 7 marks: 3 for AC value, 2 for trace, 2 for reference count)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **🔴 Indirect Bit Confusion:** Many students wrongly assume I=1 means "indirect" without checking the actual bit value in the instruction word. Always write the binary and show the I bit explicitly.
>
> 2. **🔴 Forgetting PC Increment:** Omitting $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ during $T_1$ loses 1 mark. PC ALWAYS increments after instruction fetch (except for branch/jump).
>
> 3. **🔴 Missing Data Register Step:** Writing directly $AC \leftarrow M[AR]$ (skipping $DR \leftarrow M[AR]$) loses 2 marks. The DR step is **mandatory** in single-bus architecture.
>
> 4. **🔴 Cycle Count Error:** Confusing cycles (5 or 6) with memory references (2 or 3). KTU may ask either; mark yourself accordingly.
>
> 5. **🔴 Memory Trace Mistakes:** In indirect mode problems, students often load the wrong final value (using the pointer instead of the operand). Always re-verify which address was last loaded into AR.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **LOAD = Memory-to-Register transfer** → $AC \leftarrow M[AR]$ in RTL.
- [x] **Opcode `001`** identifies LOAD as a Memory Reference Instruction.
- [x] **Indirect bit (I) bit 12** decides direct (I=0) vs. indirect (I=1) addressing.
- [x] **Direct LOAD** takes **5 cycles / 2 memory references**.
- [x] **Indirect LOAD** takes **6 cycles / 3 memory references**.
- [x] **Data Register (DR)** is a mandatory buffer to avoid bus contention.
- [x] **PC auto-increments** during $T_1$ ($PC \leftarrow PC + 1$).
- [x] **Effective Address (EA)** in direct mode = address field; in indirect mode = content at address field.
- [x] **Execution Time Formula:** $T_{exec} = N_{cycles} \times T_{MC}$.
- [x] **Memory Architecture:** 4096 × 16 bits, 12-bit address, 16-bit data, 16-bit instruction.
- [x] **LOAD vs. STORE:** LOAD is memory READ; STORE is memory WRITE — opposite data flows.
- [x] **Fetch Phase is Universal:** Every instruction (LOAD, STORE, ADD, etc.) uses the same $T_0$–$T_1$ micro-operations.
- [x] **Industry Equivalents:** ARM `LDR`, x86 `MOV`, RISC-V `lw` — all are LOAD descendants.
- [x] **Performance Bottleneck:** LOAD is the most memory-intensive operation; cache hierarchies exist to accelerate it.
- [x] **Exam Tip:** Always write the **complete RTL table** — KTU awards marks for every micro-operation row, not just the final $AC \leftarrow M[AR]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Basic MIPS Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** A Basic MIPS Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**A Basic MIPS Implementation** refers to the construction of a complete, single-cycle (or simplified) **datapath** and **control unit** capable of executing a *core subset* of the MIPS (Microprocessor without Interlocked Pipelined Stages) instruction set. The core subset typically includes three instruction formats:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> *A Basic MIPS Implementation covers the design of a unified datapath that can execute the three core MIPS instruction classes — **R-type** (register-register arithmetic/logical), **Load/Store** (data transfer with memory), and **Branch** (program control flow) — within a single clock cycle, along with the associated hardwired control logic that drives the datapath.*

The three instruction classes are:

1. **R-type (Arithmetic/Logical):** `add`, `sub`, `and`, `or`, `slt`
2. **Memory Access (Load/Store):** `lw`, `sw`
3. **Control Flow (Branch):** `beq`

The instruction formats share the field layout below (each 32 bits):

| Field Size (bits) | 6 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R-type | `opcode` | `rs` | `rt` | `rd` | `shamt` | `funct` |
| Load/Store | `opcode` | `rs` | `rt` | *immediate (16)* | | |
| Branch | `opcode` | `rs` | `rt` | *immediate (16)* | | |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Factory Assembly Line

Imagine a **car factory** where a single worker is responsible for one complete car from start to finish before moving to the next. This is exactly the **single-cycle MIPS implementation**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** The worker has a fixed set of tools (Register File, ALU, Memory, Sign-Extender). For each car (= each instruction), the worker picks the car body from the warehouse (Instruction Memory), follows a checklist from the supervisor (**Control Unit**), assembles it using tools, and parks it in the correct lot (Register File or Data Memory). Each car takes the same fixed amount of time (= **one clock cycle**), regardless of complexity. The supervisor (Control Unit) only enables the right tools based on the work order (= **opcode**).

The **datapath** is the workshop floor with all tools, and the **control unit** is the supervisor that decides *which* tools to use and *when* for each instruction.

## 1.3 Key Functional Units (The Building Blocks)

| Unit | Standard Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Program Counter (PC)** | PC register | Holds address of current instruction; increments by **4** per instruction |
| **Instruction Memory** | IM | Given an address, returns the 32-bit instruction |
| **Register File (RF)** | RF | Two read ports, one write port; 32 × 32-bit registers |
| **ALU** | Arithmetic Logic Unit | Performs +, −, AND, OR, SLT; **zero** output flag |
| **Data Memory** | DM | Read/Write 32-bit words; addressed by byte address |
| **Sign-Extend Unit** | SX | Extends 16-bit immediate to 32 bits (replicates MSB) |
| **Shift-Left-2 (SLL2)** | SL2 | Shifts a 26-bit address left by 2 (for branch/jump target) |
| **Add 4 unit** | Adder | Computes `PC + 4` for next sequential instruction |
| **Branch Add unit** | Adder | Computes `PC+4 + (SignExtend<<2)` for branch target |
| **Multiplexors (MUX)** | 2-to-1, 3-to-1 | Route one of N data inputs to output (sel lines = control) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The MIPS **byte addressing** convention means every word is 4 bytes, hence **PC is incremented by 4** every cycle. All memory addresses used inside the datapath are **byte addresses**, but the PC, register indices, and word offsets are word-based.

## 1.4 Standard MIPS Performance Metric

The execution time for a program in this single-cycle design is governed by:

$$
T_{program} = (\text{Number of Instructions}) \times (\text{Clock Cycle Time})
$$

Where the **Clock Cycle Time** must be **at least as long as the longest instruction's propagation delay** through the datapath. In a single-cycle design, this is the load word (`lw`) instruction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Datapath Construction Methodology

A MIPS datapath is built **incrementally**, one instruction class at a time, then unified:

> [!NOTE]
> **Build Order (Follow this in KTU answers!):**
> 1. Start with the **instruction fetch (common to all)** — PC, IM, PC+4 Adder.
> 2. Add **R-type datapath** — RF, ALU, registers read/written.
> 3. Extend for **Load/Store** — DM, Sign-Extend, second ALU input MUX.
> 4. Extend for **Branch** — branch adder, ALU Zero, PC-source MUX.
> 5. Overlay **Control Unit** to drive all MUXes and enable signals.

### Step 1: Instruction Fetch (IF) — Common Sub-Datapath

Every instruction begins with the same fetch sequence:

$$
\text{PC} \rightarrow \text{Instruction Memory (Read)} \rightarrow \text{Instruction} \;\&\; \text{PC}+4
$$

Components:
- **PC register** (loaded at end of each cycle with either `PC+4` or branch target).
- **Instruction Memory**: combinational read (address in, instruction out).
- **Adder** that computes `PC + 4` using a **constant 4** (hardwired in many implementations).
- Output: 32-bit instruction is delivered to the next stage.

### Step 2: R-Type Datapath (Decoding + Execute + Write-Back)

For an R-type instruction (e.g., `add $rd, $rs, $rt`):

- `rs` and `rt` fields → **Read register 1** and **Read register 2** ports of Register File.
- `rd` field → **Write register** port of Register File.
- `Read data 1` and `Read data 2` enter the **ALU**.
- ALU performs operation determined by `ALU control` (derived from `opcode` + `funct`).
- ALU result → **Write data** input of Register File.
- **RegWrite** = **1** (enabled).

### Step 3: Load/Store Datapath Extension

For Load (`lw $rt, offset($rs)`) and Store (`sw $rt, offset($rs)`):

- The **16-bit immediate** is **sign-extended** to 32 bits by the **Sign-Extend unit**.
- A **2-to-1 MUX** selects the ALU's second operand:
  - Select = 0 → Read data 2 (for R-type)
  - Select = 1 → Sign-extended immediate (for lw/sw)
- ALU computes `Read data 1 + SignExtend(immediate)`.
- For `lw`: ALU result is the **memory address** → sent to Data Memory, which returns the data on `Read data` line → written back to `rt` (RegWrite = 1).
- For `sw`: `Read data 2` (i.e., the data to store) goes to the **Data Memory Write Data** port. **RegWrite = 0**, **MemWrite = 1**.

### Step 4: Branch Datapath Extension

For Branch (`beq $rs, $rt, offset`):

- The 16-bit offset is **sign-extended** and **shifted left by 2** (× 4 for word addressing).
- A second **Adder** computes the branch target: `Branch Target = (PC+4) + (SignExtend(offset) << 2)`.
- The ALU subtracts (`rs - rt`); if equal, the **ALU Zero** output is **1**.
- A **2-to-1 MUX** at the PC input chooses:
  - Select = 0 → `PC+4` (default sequential)
  - Select = 1 → Branch target (when Zero = 1)

## 2.2 The Control Unit

The control unit takes the **6-bit opcode** as input and produces a set of **control signals** that drive every MUX, enable line, and memory operation in the datapath.

### Control Signals Required

| Signal | Effect When 0 | Effect When 1 | Used By |
|---|---|---|---|
| `RegDst` | Write register = `rt` (5 bits) | Write register = `rd` (5 bits) | R-type MUX |
| `ALUSrc` | ALU 2nd input = Read data 2 | ALU 2nd input = Sign-extended immediate | ALU MUX |
| `MemtoReg` | Write data = ALU result | Write data = Data Memory read data | WB MUX |
| `RegWrite` | No write | Write enabled | Register File |
| `MemRead` | — | Data Memory read | Data Memory |
| `MemWrite` | — | Data Memory write | Data Memory |
| `Branch` | PC source = PC+4 | PC source = branch target (if Zero) | PC MUX |
| `ALUOp (2 bits)` | See ALU Control truth table | — | ALU Control unit |
| `Jump` | PC source = PC+4 or branch | PC source = jump target | PC MUX |

### The ALU Control Unit

The ALU control is a *separate* combinational block that takes `ALUOp` (from main control) and the 6-bit `funct` field (from R-type instructions) and produces a 4-bit `ALU operation` code.

> [!NOTE]
> **ALUOp Encoding:**
> - `00` → Load/Store → ALU must compute **add** (for address)
> - `01` → Branch (`beq`) → ALU must compute **subtract** (to set Zero)
> - `10` → R-type → ALU operation determined by `funct` field
> - `11` → (reserved / unused in basic implementation)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clock Cycle Time (single-cycle) | $T_c \geq \max(T_{lw}, T_{sw}, T_{R}, T_{beq})$ | seconds |
| 2 | Next PC (no branch) | $\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{PC} + 4$ | byte address |
| 3 | Branch Target | $\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = (\text{PC}+4) + (\text{SignExt}_{16 \to 32}(\text{offset}) \times 4)$ | byte address |
| 4 | Jump Target (extended) | $\text{PC}_{\text{jump}} = \{(\text{PC}+4)_{31..28}, \text{Inst}_{25..0}, 2'b00\}$ | byte address |
| 5 | Sign Extension | $\text{SignExt}(b_{15}b_{14}...b_0) = b_{15}\text{ repeated 16 times} \;\vert\; b_{15}...b_0$ | 32-bit signed |
| 6 | CPI (single-cycle) | $\text{CPI} = 1$ | instructions/cycle |
| 7 | Execution Time | $T_{\text{exec}} = \frac{N \times T_c}{\text{(for single-cycle)}}$ | seconds |
| 8 | ALU Zero flag (beq) | $\text{Zero} = 1 \iff (A - B) = 0 \iff A = B$ | boolean |
| 9 | Register file size | $32 \text{ registers} \times 32 \text{ bits} = 128 \text{ bytes} = \mathbf{1 \, KB}$ | memory |
| 10 | Word size | $\mathbf{32 \text{ bits}} = \mathbf{4 \text{ bytes}}$ | aligned to 4 |
| 11 | Sign-bit position | MSB of 16-bit immediate is **bit 15** | boolean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the branch target formula, the **shift-left-2 is done BEFORE adding to PC+4**, not after. Also, the immediate is a **byte offset** that is **sign-extended to 32 bits FIRST**, then shifted. The order matters in the exam.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The single-cycle MIPS datapath is the **conceptual ancestor of every modern CPU**:
- **ARM Cortex-M** microcontrollers use a similar (though more complex) unified datapath.
- **RISC-V**, the open-source ISA dominating modern designs, follows nearly identical datapath principles.
- Understanding the MIPS implementation gives engineers a **first-principles foundation** for FPGA-based processor design, ASIC tape-outs, and instruction-set extensions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — The Branch Target Address

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is a KTU 14-mark favorite. Memorize the steps.**

**Given:** A branch instruction `beq $rs, $rt, offset` located at address `PC` in memory.

**To Find:** The effective byte address of the next instruction **if the branch is taken**.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} &= \text{PC}_{\text{next sequential}} + \text{branch displacement} \\[4pt]
\text{PC}_{\text{next sequential}} &= \text{PC} + 4 \\[4pt]
\text{branch displacement (in bytes)} &= \text{offset} \times 4
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} &= (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{offset} \times 4) \\[4pt]
&= (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{offset} \ll 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

In terms of fields of the branch instruction, the 16-bit `offset` field is **sign-extended** before the shift:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = (\text{PC} + 4) + \big(\,(\text{SignExt}_{16\to 32}(\text{Inst}_{15..0})) \ll 2\,\big)
$$

The `<< 2` is required because MIPS instructions are **word-aligned (4 bytes)**, and the offset stored in the instruction is the number of **words** to jump, not bytes.

**Numerical Example:**
- `PC = 0x00400010`
- `offset field = 0xFFFB` (signed 16-bit = `−5`)
- After sign-extend (32-bit) = `0xFFFFFFFB` (= `−5`)
- After `<< 2` = `0xFFFFFFEC` (= `−20`)
- $\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = (0x00400014) + (0xFFFFFFEC) = 0x00400000$

**Verification:** The target is 5 instructions earlier (each 4 bytes = 20 bytes), which matches the `−5` word offset. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation — Sign Extension (16 → 32 bits)

A 16-bit immediate $I = b_{15}b_{14} \ldots b_0$ is sign-extended to 32 bits:

$$
\text{SignExt}(I) = \underbrace{b_{15} b_{15} \ldots b_{15}}_{16 \text{ copies}} \;\vert\; b_{15} b_{14} \ldots b_0
$$

**Example:** $I = 0x8FA0$ (binary: `1000 1111 1010 0000`, MSB = 1)
- $\text{SignExt}(I) = 0xFF8F8FA0$ (all upper 16 bits replicate the MSB `1`)

**Example:** $I = 0x1234$ (binary: `0001 0010 0011 0100`, MSB = 0)
- $\text{SignExt}(I) = 0x00001234$ (upper 16 bits are all `0`)

## 3.3 Derivation — ALU Zero Flag for `beq`

The `beq` instruction compares registers `$rs` and `$rt`. The ALU performs:

$$
\text{ALU output} = A - B = R[\text{rs}] - R[\text{rt}]
$$

The **Zero output** of the ALU is:

$$
\text{Zero} = 1 \iff \text{ALU output} = 0 \iff R[\text{rs}] - R[\text{rt}] = 0 \iff R[\text{rs}] = R[\text{rt}]
$$

The **Branch AND gate** then produces the final PC select:

$$
\text{PCSrc} = \text{Branch} \,\text{AND}\, \text{Zero}
$$

So the next PC is:

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} & \text{if } \text{PCSrc} = 1 \\
\text{PC}+4 & \text{if } \text{PCSrc} = 0
\end{cases}
$$

## 3.4 KTU Control Signal Truth Table (ANALYTICAL MATRIX)

This is the **single most important table** for KTU exams on this topic.

| Instruction | `RegDst` | `ALUSrc` | `MemtoReg` | `RegWrite` | `MemRead` | `MemWrite` | `Branch` | `ALUOp1` | `ALUOp0` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `R-type` | **1** | **0** | **0** | **1** | **0** | **0** | **0** | **1** | **0** |
| `lw`     | **0** | **1** | **1** | **1** | **1** | **0** | **0** | **0** | **0** |
| `sw`     | **X** | **1** | **X** | **0** | **0** | **1** | **0** | **0** | **0** |
| `beq`    | **X** | **0** | **X** | **0** | **0** | **0** | **1** | **0** | **1** |

(`X` = don't care; encoded in implementation as 0)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoding the opcode into control signals** (6-bit opcode → control bits) is typically done with a **PLA / ROM / Hardwired logic** and is a high-value 7-mark sub-question in KTU.

## 3.5 ALU Control Truth Table

| `ALUOp` | `Funct` field | `ALU Operation` | Symbolic |
|---|---|---|---|
| 00 | XXXXXX | 0010 | **Add** |
| 01 | XXXXXX | 0110 | **Subtract** |
| 10 | 100000 (add) | 0010 | Add |
| 10 | 100010 (sub) | 0110 | Subtract |
| 10 | 100100 (and) | 0000 | AND |
| 10 | 100101 (or)  | 0001 | OR |
| 10 | 101010 (slt) | 0111 | Set-on-Less-Than |
| 11 | XXXXXX | (undefined) | — |

The 4-bit ALU operation codes: `0010` = Add, `0110` = Sub, `0000` = AND, `0001` = OR, `0111` = SLT, `1100` = NOR (not in basic subset).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete Single-Cycle MIPS Datapath (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the full datapath with control signals for all three instruction types.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PC[PC Register] -->|address| IM[Instruction Memory]
    IM -->|Instruction 32b| InstBus[Instruction Bus 31:0]
    PC --> A1[Adder +4]
    A1 --> PCp4[PC_plus_4]

    InstBus -->|opcode 6b| CU[Main Control Unit]
    InstBus -->|rs 5b| RFR1[Reg File Read Reg 1]
    InstBus -->|rt 5b| RFR2[Reg File Read Reg 2]
    InstBus -->|rd 5b| RD_MUX{{MUX RegDst}}
    InstBus -->|rt 5b| RT_MUX{{MUX RegDst}}
    RD_MUX --> WReg[Write Register]
    RT_MUX --> WReg

    RFR1 --> RD1[Read Data 1]
    RFR2 --> RD2[Read Data 2]

    InstBus -->|imm 16b| SX[Sign Extend 16 to 32]
    SX --> SIGNIMM[Sign Ext Immediate]

    RD2 --> ALUMUX{{MUX ALUSrc}}
    SIGNIMM --> ALUMUX
    ALUMUX --> ALUB[ALU B Input]

    RD1 --> ALUA[ALU A Input]
    ALUA --> ALU_main[ALU]
    ALUB --> ALU_main
    CU -->|ALUOp 2b| ALUCtl[ALU Control]
    InstBus -->|funct 6b| ALUCtl
    ALUCtl -->|ALU Ctl 4b| ALU_main

    ALU_main --> ALURes[ALU Result]
    ALU_main --> Zero[Zero Flag]

    ALURes --> DM_Addr[Data Memory Address]
    RD2 --> DM_WD[Data Memory Write Data]

    DM_Addr --> DM[Data Memory]
    DM_WD --> DM
    CU -->|MemRead| DM
    CU -->|MemWrite| DM
    DM --> DM_RD[Data Memory Read Data]

    ALURes --> WBMUX{{MUX MemtoReg}}
    DM_RD --> WBMUX
    WBMUX --> WData[Write Data]
    WData --> RF_W[Register File Write]
    WReg --> RF_W
    CU -->|RegWrite| RF_W
    RD1 --> RF_W
    RD2 --> RF_W

    PCp4 --> PCMUX{{MUX PCSrc}}
    Zero --> AND_Branch[AND with Branch Signal]
    CU -->|Branch| AND_Branch
    AND_Branch --> PCMUX
    SX --> SL2[Shift Left 2]
    SL2 --> BRADD[Branch Adder]
    PCp4 --> BRADD
    BRADD --> PCMUX
    PCMUX --> PC
```

## 4.2 ALU Control Unit (Internal Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Op[ALUOp 2b] --> ALUCtlLogic[Hardwired Combinational Logic]
    F[funct 6b] --> ALUCtlLogic
    ALUCtlLogic --> Out[ALU Operation 4b]
    Out --> ALU[ALU Unit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to design the ALU control, write the truth table (Section 3.5) and then either:
> - **Method A:** Express each output bit as a **sum-of-products** Boolean expression, or
> - **Method B:** Describe a **PLA / ROM** implementation with input/output mapping.

## 4.3 Control Signal Flow (Per Instruction Class)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R_TYPE [R Type Instruction]
        R1[RegDst = 1] --> R2[ALU Src = 0]
        R2 --> R3[MemtoReg = 0]
        R3 --> R4[RegWrite = 1]
        R4 --> R5[ALUOp = 10]
    end
    subgraph LW_TYPE [Load Word]
        L1[RegDst = 0] --> L2[ALU Src = 1]
        L2 --> L3[MemtoReg = 1]
        L3 --> L4[RegWrite = 1]
        L4 --> L5[MemRead = 1]
        L5 --> L6[ALUOp = 00]
    end
    subgraph SW_TYPE [Store Word]
        S1[ALU Src = 1] --> S2[RegWrite = 0]
        S2 --> S3[MemWrite = 1]
        S3 --> S4[ALUOp = 00]
    end
    subgraph BEQ_TYPE [Branch Equal]
        B1[ALU Src = 0] --> B2[RegWrite = 0]
        B2 --> B3[Branch = 1]
        B3 --> B4[ALUOp = 01]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**List the main functional units of the single-cycle MIPS datapath and state the role of the Sign-Extend unit.**

**Model Answer:**

The main functional units of the single-cycle MIPS datapath are:

1. **Program Counter (PC)** — holds the address of the current instruction.
2. **Instruction Memory** — supplies the 32-bit instruction at a given address.
3. **Register File (32 × 32-bit)** — provides two read operands and one write-back destination.
4. **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** — executes arithmetic, logical, and comparison operations.
5. **Data Memory** — performs load and store operations.
6. **Sign-Extend unit** — converts a 16-bit immediate to a 32-bit signed value.
7. **Shift-Left-2 unit** — multiplies a 26-bit or extended offset by 4 (for branch/jump targets).
8. **Adders** — one computes `PC+4`; another computes the branch target address.
9. **Multiplexors** — route between alternative data sources under control signal direction.

**Role of Sign-Extend unit:**
The Sign-Extend unit takes the 16-bit immediate field of Load/Store/Branch instructions and extends it to a 32-bit value by **replicating the sign bit (bit 15) into the upper 16 bits**. This preserves the arithmetic sign of the immediate so that it can be correctly used in address calculations and ALU operations. *(3 marks)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain the purpose of the ALU Control unit. Why can't the Main Control unit directly produce the 4-bit ALU operation code?**

**Model Answer:**

**Purpose of ALU Control:**
The ALU Control is a small auxiliary combinational logic block that converts a 2-bit `ALUOp` field (from the Main Control) together with the 6-bit `funct` field (from R-type instructions) into the 4-bit `ALU operation` code that the ALU actually uses.

**Why a separate ALU Control?**
- For Load/Store, the ALU must perform **addition** (address computation), so `ALUOp = 00` fixes it to ADD regardless of any `funct`.
- For Branch (`beq`), the ALU must perform **subtraction** (to test equality), so `ALUOp = 01` fixes it to SUB.
- For R-type, the actual operation depends on the `funct` field (add, sub, and, or, slt), so `ALUOp = 10` lets the `funct` field select the operation.
- The Main Control does not "know" the `funct` field's meaning, so an **intermediate decoder** (ALU Control) is required. This separation keeps the Main Control simpler and **decouples the opcode decoding from the R-type function decoding**. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **single-cycle MIPS datapath** for executing R-type, Load, and Store instructions. Clearly label all datapath elements, MUXes, and the relevant control signals. **(7 marks)**

**b)** For the following MIPS code segment, determine the values of all control signals (`RegDst`, `ALUSrc`, `MemtoReg`, `RegWrite`, `MemRead`, `MemWrite`, `Branch`, `ALUOp1`, `ALUOp0`) for **each** instruction, and write the corresponding 32-bit machine code in hexadecimal. Assume the assembler assigns `\$s0 = 16, \$s1 = 17, \$t0 = 8, \$t1 = 9`. **(7 marks)**

```mips
add  $t0, $s0, $s1
lw   $t1, 4($s0)
sw   $s1, 0($t0)
beq  $t0, $s1, exit
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**a)** Design the **ALU Control unit** for the basic MIPS implementation. Provide the **truth table**, explain the **Boolean equations** for each output bit, and describe how it can be implemented using a PLA. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A branch instruction `beq $t0, $t1, Label` is located at byte address `0x00400020`. The 16-bit offset field in the instruction is `0x000C`. Compute the **branch target address** in hexadecimal, showing every step. Also explain what happens if `R[$t0] ≠ R[$t1]`. **(7 marks)**

---

## Model Solutions

### Question A — Part (a) Model Answer

The unified single-cycle datapath consists of the following stages:

**Stage 1 — Instruction Fetch (common to all):**
- `PC` is fed as the address to **Instruction Memory**.
- An **Adder** computes `PC + 4` for the next sequential instruction.
- Output: 32-bit instruction and `PC+4`.

**Stage 2 — Instruction Decode & Register Read:**
- The 6-bit `opcode` is sent to the **Main Control Unit**.
- The two 5-bit register fields `rs` and `rt` are sent to the **Read Register 1** and **Read Register 2** ports of the **Register File**.
- For R-type, the `rd` field is also read but used only at write-back.

**Stage 3 — Execution / Effective Address Calculation:**
- The **ALU** operates on the two read data values.
- The second ALU input is selected by a **2-to-1 MUX (ALUSrc)**:
  - For R-type: `Read Data 2`.
  - For lw/sw: `SignExtend(imm16)`.
- The ALU performs:
  - R-type: operation per `funct` (add, sub, etc.).
  - lw/sw: addition of base + offset.
- ALU result is the **arithmetic output** OR the **memory address** (for lw/sw).

**Stage 4 — Memory Access / Write-Back Preparation:**
- For `lw`: ALU result is sent to **Data Memory** as address; memory returns data on Read Data port.
- For `sw`: Read Data 2 is sent to Data Memory as Write Data; ALU result is discarded.
- A **2-to-1 MUX (MemtoReg)** selects what gets written back to the register file:
  - For R-type: ALU result.
  - For lw: Data Memory read data.
  - For sw: (don't care — no write).

**Stage 5 — Write-Back:**
- The **RegDst MUX** selects the write register:
  - For R-type: `rd`.
  - For lw: `rt`.
- `RegWrite = 1` only for R-type and lw.
- The selected data is clocked into the destination register at the end of the cycle.

**Key control signals** (values for each instruction as per Section 3.4 truth table):

| Signal | R-type | lw | sw |
|---|---|---|---|
| RegDst | 1 | 0 | X |
| ALUSrc | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| MemtoReg | 0 | 1 | X |
| RegWrite | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| MemRead | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| MemWrite | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| Branch | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| ALUOp | 10 | 00 | 00 |

*(7 marks — full block diagram carries 4 marks, signal mapping carries 3 marks)*

---

### Question A — Part (b) Model Answer

**Step 1: Identify opcodes and funct codes**

- `add` → opcode = `000000`, funct = `100000`
- `lw`   → opcode = `100011` (I-type format)
- `sw`   → opcode = `101011` (I-type format)
- `beq`  → opcode = `000100` (I-type format)

**Step 2: Encode each instruction (binary → hex)**

**(i) `add $t0, $s0, $s1` — R-type format**

$$
000000 \;|\; 10000 \;|\; 10001 \;|\; 01000 \;|\; 00000 \;|\; 100000
$$

- opcode: `000000`
- rs = `$s0` = 16 → `10000`
- rt = `$s1` = 17 → `10001`
- rd = `$t0` = 8 → `01000`
- shamt = 0 → `00000`
- funct = `100000`

Binary: `000000 10000 10001 01000 00000 100000`  
Grouped: `0000 0010 0001 0010 1000 0000 0010 0000`  
**Hex: `0x02108020`**

**Control signals:** `RegDst=1, ALUSrc=0, MemtoReg=0, RegWrite=1, MemRead=0, MemWrite=0, Branch=0, ALUOp=10`

**(ii) `lw $t1, 4($s0)` — I-type format**

$$
100011 \;|\; 10000 \;|\; 01001 \;|\; 0000000000000100
$$

- opcode: `100011`
- rs = `$s0` = 16 → `10000`
- rt = `$t1` = 9 → `01001`
- immediate = 4 → `0000000000000100`

Binary: `1000 1110 0000 1001 0000 0000 0000 0100`  
**Hex: `0x8E090004`**

**Control signals:** `RegDst=0, ALUSrc=1, MemtoReg=1, RegWrite=1, MemRead=1, MemWrite=0, Branch=0, ALUOp=00`

**(iii) `sw $s1, 0($t0)` — I-type format**

$$
101011 \;|\; 01000 \;|\; 10001 \;|\; 0000000000000000
$$

- opcode: `101011`
- rs = `$t0` = 8 → `01000`
- rt = `$s1` = 17 → `10001`
- immediate = 0 → `0000000000000000`

Binary: `1010 1101 0001 0001 0000 0000 0000 0000`  
**Hex: `0xAD110000`**

**Control signals:** `RegDst=X, ALUSrc=1, MemtoReg=X, RegWrite=0, MemRead=0, MemWrite=1, Branch=0, ALUOp=00`

**(iv) `beq $t0, $s1, exit` — I-type format**

$$
000100 \;|\; 01000 \;|\; 10001 \;|\; \text{(16-bit offset, depends on `exit` location)}
$$

- opcode: `000100`
- rs = `$t0` = 8 → `01000`
- rt = `$s1` = 17 → `10001`
- offset = 16-bit label offset (cannot be resolved without link info).

**Control signals:** `RegDst=X, ALUSrc=0, MemtoReg=X, RegWrite=0, MemRead=0, MemWrite=0, Branch=1, ALUOp=01`

*(7 marks — 1.5 marks per instruction encoding + 0.5 marks per control signal set)*

---

### Question B — Part (a) Model Answer

**ALU Control Unit Design:**

The ALU Control takes `ALUOp` (2 bits) and `funct` (6 bits) as inputs and produces a 4-bit ALU operation code.

**Step 1 — Truth Table (see Section 3.5)**

**Step 2 — Boolean Equations (example for ALUOp[2] bit):**

Let $A = \text{ALUOp}_1$, $B = \text{ALUOp}_0$, and $F_5 F_4 F_3 F_2 F_1 F_0 = \text{funct field}$.

For the **ADD** output bit of the ALU control (call it `Operation[2]`):

$$
\text{Operation}[2] = (\overline{A} \cdot \overline{B}) \;+\; (A \cdot \overline{B} \cdot F_5 \cdot \overline{F_4} \cdot \overline{F_3} \cdot \overline{F_2} \cdot \overline{F_1} \cdot \overline{F_0})
$$

**Interpretation:** ADD is selected either when `ALUOp = 00` (load/store always need add) OR when `ALUOp = 10` AND `funct = 100000` (R-type add).

Similar equations exist for SUB, AND, OR, SLT.

**Step 3 — PLA Implementation:**

A Programmable Logic Array implements the Boolean equations using an **AND plane** (for product terms) and an **OR plane** (for sums). The 8 inputs (`ALUOp[1:0]`, `funct[5:0]`) feed the AND plane; the 4 outputs feed the OR plane.

- **Inputs:** 8 lines (and their complements — 16 into AND plane)
- **Product terms:** ~10 distinct terms
- **Outputs:** 4 ALU operation bits

**PLA Diagram (textual):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[ALUOp1] --> AND_PLANE[AND Plane]
    I2[ALUOp0] --> AND_PLANE
    I3[funct 5..0] --> AND_PLANE
    AND_PLANE --> OR_PLANE[OR Plane]
    OR_PLANE --> O1[Op3]
    OR_PLANE --> O2[Op2]
    OR_PLANE --> O3[Op1]
    OR_PLANE --> O4[Op0]
```

*(7 marks — 2 marks truth table, 3 marks Boolean equations, 2 marks PLA description)*

---

### Question B — Part (b) Model Answer

**Given:**
- PC (current instruction address) = `0x00400020`
- 16-bit offset field = `0x000C`

**Step 1: Sign-extend the 16-bit offset to 32 bits.**

Since bit 15 of `0x000C` is `0` (positive number), sign extension yields `0x0000000C` (decimal = 12 words).

**Step 2: Shift-left by 2 (multiply by 4) to convert word offset to byte offset.**

$$
0x0000000C \times 4 = 0x00000030
$$

(Decimal: 12 words × 4 bytes/word = 48 bytes)

**Step 3: Compute PC+4.**

$$
0x00400020 + 0x4 = 0x00400024
$$

**Step 4: Add to get branch target.**

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{target}} = 0x00400024 + 0x00000030 = 0x00400054
$$

**Step 5: Verification.**

`PC_target − (PC+4) = 0x00400054 − 0x00400024 = 0x30 = 48 bytes` = 12 words forward. ✓

**Final Answer: Branch target = `0x00400054`**

**What if R[$t0] ≠ R[$t1]?**
- The ALU performs subtraction: `R[$t0] − R[$t1]`, producing a **non-zero** result.
- The **Zero output** of the ALU = **0**.
- The **PCSrc AND gate** output = `Branch AND Zero` = `1 AND 0` = **0**.
- The PC source MUX selects `PC+4` (the default sequential flow).
- The branch is **NOT taken**, and execution continues to the instruction at `PC+4 = 0x00400024`.

*(7 marks — 4 marks calculation, 1 mark target, 2 marks branch-not-taken explanation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `<< 2` shift** in branch target calculation. The offset is a **word** count, not a byte count.
> 2. **Confusing sign extension with zero extension.** MIPS uses **sign extension** (always). Forgetting to replicate the MSB loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Swapping `rs` and `rt` in branch encoding.** `beq $rs, $rt, offset` — the order matters.
> 4. **Mixing up MemtoReg and RegDst.** MemtoReg selects **data** source; RegDst selects **register** destination. These are different MUXes.
> 5. **Omitting the `Branch AND Zero` gate.** Just `Branch` alone is not sufficient — it must be **ANDed with ALU Zero** to form PCSrc.
> 6. **Forgetting the `Add 4` to PC in branch target.** Some students compute `PC + (offset << 2)`, omitting the `+ 4`. The branch target is `PC+4` (not `PC`) plus the byte-offset displacement.
> 7. **Writing the entire 14-mark answer as prose.** Always use **tables, signal diagrams, and explicit step-by-step** format. KTU examiners reward structured answers.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Single-cycle MIPS** executes **exactly one instruction per clock cycle**. CPI = 1.
- ✅ **PC is incremented by 4** every cycle (MIPS is word-addressed with 32-bit / 4-byte words).
- ✅ The **datapath** = physical data-routing hardware (MUXes, ALU, Register File, Memories).
- ✅ The **control unit** = hardwired logic that sets MUX selects and memory enables based on the **opcode**.
- ✅ Three instruction classes supported: **R-type, lw, sw, beq** (basic subset).
- ✅ **Sign-Extend** replicates bit 15 of the 16-bit immediate into the upper 16 bits.
- ✅ **Branch target** = `(PC+4) + (SignExt(offset) << 2)` — the `<< 2` converts words to bytes.
- ✅ **ALU Zero** flag is the key for branch decision: `PCSrc = Branch AND Zero`.
- ✅ **ALU Control** decodes 2-bit `ALUOp` + 6-bit `funct` → 4-bit ALU operation. This is a *separate* combinational block.
- ✅ **Memorize the control signal truth table** (Section 3.4) — it is a guaranteed 5+ mark question in KTU.
- ✅ **R-type → ALUOp = 10**, **lw/sw → ALUOp = 00**, **beq → ALUOp = 01**.
- ✅ **RegDst** selects the **destination register** (`rd` for R-type, `rt` for lw).
- ✅ **MemtoReg** selects the **write-back data** (ALU result for R-type, memory data for lw).
- ✅ **Single-cycle limitation:** Clock period is bounded by the **slowest instruction** (typically `lw`), wasting time on faster instructions. (This motivates pipelining in Module 4.)
- ✅ **MUXes are the "decision points"** — every MUX in the datapath is controlled by a signal from the control unit.
- ✅ The **ALUOp encoding** is the bridge between the Main Control and the ALU Control. Without it, the Main Control would need 4 bits directly (more outputs to encode).
- ✅ **Branch is "PC-relative"** — it is relative to `PC+4`, not the current PC (this is a very common KTU pitfall).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building a Datapath
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Building a Datapath

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Computer Organisation and Architecture*, the **Datapath** is formally defined as the collection of functional units (such as the Arithmetic Logic Unit, registers, memories, and buses) along with the data paths connecting them, that perform the actual processing of data and the transfer of operands, addresses, and results during instruction execution. It represents the *hardware infrastructure* over which the Control Unit (which is *not* part of the datapath itself) orchestrates the execution of machine instructions.

Mathematically, a datapath operation can be abstracted as a state-transition function:

$$
\text{State}_{\text{next}} = f(\text{State}_{\text{current}}, \text{Instruction}_{\text{decoded}})
$$

where $f$ is the combined transformation performed by the functional units (ALU, Registers, Memory) under the supervision of control signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PERUT418 Module 3**
> The datapath is treated as the *spinal cord* of the CPU. KTU 2024 emphasizes the **single-cycle datapath** first, then extends it to the **multi-cycle datapath**, and finally compares it with **pipelined datapath** in higher modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition: The Factory Assembly Line**
> Imagine a **car manufacturing assembly line**. The *stationary machines* (robotic arms, paint sprayers, welding units) are the **functional units** — ALU, register file, memory. The *conveyor belts* that move the car chassis between these machines are the **buses**. The *factory supervisor* shouting instructions ("weld now!", "paint now!") is the **Control Unit**, and the entire floor — machines plus belts — is the **datapath**.

So, when we say *"building a datapath"*, we are essentially *engineering the factory floor* that takes a raw instruction from memory, processes its operands, and writes back the result — all coordinated by carefully timed control signals.

### 1.3 Why Datapath Design Matters

- **Performance**: Determines the **Clock Cycles Per Instruction (CPI)** and ultimately the **execution time** of programs.
- **Hardware Cost**: More parallel datapath elements (e.g., dual-port register file) cost more silicon area but reduce CPI.
- **Energy Efficiency**: A lean datapath reduces dynamic power consumption.

The three classic metrics — *latency, throughput, and cost* — are tightly bound to datapath architecture, and KTU questions frequently test these trade-offs.

---

### 1.4 The Five Classic Functional Blocks

Every KTU-style datapath (single-cycle, multi-cycle, or pipelined) is built from these five canonical blocks:

| # | Functional Block | Role |
|---|------------------|------|
| 1 | **Instruction Memory (IM)** | Stores the program; outputs the current instruction. |
| 2 | **Register File (RF)** | 32 × 32-bit registers in MIPS; provides read/write operand access. |
| 3 | **ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit)** | Performs arithmetic (+, −, ×, ÷) and logic (AND, OR, etc.). |
| 4 | **Data Memory (DM)** | Read/Write memory for `lw` and `sw`. |
| 5 | **Program Counter (PC)** | Holds the address of the next instruction. |

Auxiliary elements that glue these blocks together include **multiplexers (MUX)**, **sign-extenders**, and **adder units**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip**: A common exam trick is to ask *which unit is NOT part of the datapath*. The answer is always the **Control Unit** itself — it generates signals *to* the datapath but is logically separate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Single-Cycle MIPS Datapath

The KTU 2024 syllabus uses the **MIPS instruction set architecture** as the canonical reference. The datapath is constructed incrementally by tracing which functional units each *instruction class* needs.

The three core instruction classes are:

- **R-type (Register)**: `add $t1, $t2, $t3`  → Arithmetic on registers.
- **Load/Store (I-type)**: `lw $t1, 4($t2)`, `sw $t1, 4($t2)` → Memory access via register + offset.
- **Branch (I-type)**: `beq $t1, $t2, Label` → Conditional PC update.

### 2.2 Operational Steps for Building the Datapath

We construct the datapath *bottom-up*, ensuring every instruction class can execute correctly:

**Step 1 — Instruction Fetch (Universal for all instructions)**
- Send `PC` to Instruction Memory.
- Read out the 32-bit instruction.
- Increment PC by 4 (since MIPS instructions are 4 bytes aligned).

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{PC}_{\text{current}} + 4
$$

**Step 2 — Instruction Decode & Register Read**
- Split the 32-bit instruction into fields (`opcode`, `rs`, `rt`, `rd`, `shamt`, `funct`).
- Use `rs` and `rt` as addresses to the **Register File** to read two operands.

**Step 3 — Execution / Effective Address Calculation**
- For **R-type**: Send the two operands to the **ALU**, with `ALUControl` set to the operation encoded in the `funct` field. The ALU output becomes the result.
- For **lw/sw**: Send one operand + sign-extended offset to the ALU to compute the *effective memory address*.
- For **beq**: ALU subtracts the two operands and sets the **Zero flag** if equal.

**Step 4 — Memory Access or Write-back**
- For **R-type**: ALU result is written back to the register file at address `rd`.
- For **lw**: Data Memory is read at the effective address, and the loaded value is written to `rt`.
- For **sw**: The data from `rt` is written to Data Memory.
- For **beq**: PC is updated to `PC + 4 + (\text{sign-extended offset} \times 4)$ if the ALU Zero flag is `1`.

**Step 5 — MUX insertion for path sharing**
- A multiplexer after the ALU selects whether the **ALU result** or the **Data Memory read data** should be written back to the register file. This MUX is driven by the `MemtoReg` control signal.
- Another MUX on the second ALU input selects between the second register operand (`Read data 2`) and the sign-extended 16-bit immediate.
- A MUX on the PC selects between `PC + 4` and the **branch target address**.

### 2.3 The 7 Main Control Signals (KTU must-know)

These signals are generated by the **Main Control Unit** (opcode-driven) and the **ALU Control Unit** (funct-driven):

| Signal | Meaning | When Asserted |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| `RegDst` | Destination register select | `1` → `rd` (R-type); `0` → `rt` (lw) |
| `ALUSrc` | Second ALU operand source | `1` → sign-extended immediate; `0` → Read data 2 |
| `MemRead` | Read from Data Memory | `1` for `lw` |
| `MemWrite` | Write to Data Memory | `1` for `sw` |
| `MemtoReg` | Write-back data select | `1` → Memory data; `0` → ALU result |
| `RegWrite` | Write to Register File | `1` for R-type, `lw` |
| `Branch` | Enable branch target | `1` for `beq` (combined with ALU Zero) |

Additional ALU control lines (`ALUOp1`, `ALUOp0`) feed into the **ALU Decoder** which outputs a 4-bit `ALUOperation` code.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-grade essentials**. Memorize the relations, not just the symbols.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
|---|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}$ | **CPU execution time**. $T_{\text{CPU}}$ is total program time; $N_{\text{inst}}$ is dynamic instruction count; CPI is cycles per instruction; $T_{\text{clock}}$ is clock period. |
| 2 | $T_{\text{clock}} = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{clock}}}$ | Clock period in seconds; $f_{\text{clock}}$ in **Hz**. Typical KTU values: $100\,\text{MHz}$, $1\,\text{GHz}$. |
| 3 | $\text{CPI}_{\text{single-cycle}} = 1$ | In a single-cycle datapath, **every instruction takes exactly 1 clock cycle**, but the cycle must be as long as the slowest instruction. |
| 4 | $T_{\text{cycle}} \geq \max(T_{\text{R-type}},\ T_{\text{lw}},\ T_{\text{sw}},\ T_{\text{beq}})$ | Cycle time equals the *worst-case path delay* — the longest of the instruction classes. |
| 5 | $\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = \text{PC} + 4$ | Standard sequential PC update for **word-aligned** MIPS instructions. |
| 6 | $\text{PC}_{\text{branch}} = (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{sign-extended immediate} \times 4)$ | Branch target address (byte-addressed, **word offset** multiplied by 4). |
| 7 | $\text{Bytes}_{\text{inst}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times 4$ | Total instruction bytes in a MIPS program. |
| 8 | $\text{Memory size} = 2^n\,\text{bytes}$ | If the address bus is $n$ bits wide, max addressable memory is $2^n$ bytes. |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Single-cycle datapath** is *conceptually clean* and ideal for teaching — used in the textbook by **Patterson & Hennessy** (which KTU follows).
- **Real processors (Intel, ARM)** use **pipelined multi-cycle datapaths** with CPI close to 1, sometimes *less than 1* (superscalar).
- Datapath timing analysis is the foundation for **VLSI floor-planning** and **FPGA implementation** of soft-cores.

---

### 2.6 Analytical Comparison: Single-Cycle vs. Multi-Cycle

| Parameter | Single-Cycle Datapath | Multi-Cycle Datapath |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| CPI | **1** | $\geq 1$ (varies per instruction class) |
| Cycle Time | Longest instruction | Shortest functional unit |
| Register File | 2 reads + 1 write simultaneously | 1 read or 1 write per cycle |
| Memory | Separate I-cache & D-cache | Unified or split |
| Hardware Reuse | Low | High |
| Control | Combinational | FSM-based micro-sequencer |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly states that students must be able to *draw and explain both single-cycle and multi-cycle datapaths*. Expect a direct 7-mark diagram question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Clock Period Calculation for Single-Cycle Datapath

> **Problem (KTU-style)**: A single-cycle MIPS datapath has the following unit delays (in nanoseconds): *Instruction Memory* = 200 ns, *Register File read* = 100 ns, *ALU* = 200 ns, *Data Memory* = 200 ns, *Register File write* = 100 ns, *MUX* = 30 ns, *Sign-extend* = 20 ns, *PC + 4 adder* = 50 ns. Compute the **minimum clock period** for each instruction class.

#### 3.1.1 Path Analysis for R-type (`add`)

For an R-type instruction, the datapath traversal is:

$$
\text{PC} \rightarrow \text{Instr.Mem} \rightarrow \text{Reg.File read} \rightarrow \text{ALU} \rightarrow \text{MUX (MemtoReg)} \rightarrow \text{Reg.File write}
$$

Step-by-step summation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{R-type}} &= T_{\text{Instr.Mem}} + T_{\text{RF.read}} + T_{\text{ALU}} + T_{\text{MUX}} + T_{\text{RF.write}} \\
&= 200 + 100 + 200 + 30 + 100 \\
&= 630\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.2 Path Analysis for Load Word (`lw`)

For `lw`, the path is:

$$
\text{PC} \rightarrow \text{Instr.Mem} \rightarrow \text{RF.read} \rightarrow \text{ALU} \rightarrow \text{Data Memory} \rightarrow \text{MUX} \rightarrow \text{RF.write}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{lw}} &= T_{\text{Instr.Mem}} + T_{\text{RF.read}} + T_{\text{ALU}} + T_{\text{DM.read}} + T_{\text{MUX}} + T_{\text{RF.write}} \\
&= 200 + 100 + 200 + 200 + 30 + 100 \\
&= 830\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.3 Path Analysis for Store Word (`sw`)

`sw` writes memory but **does NOT write back** to register file:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{sw}} &= T_{\text{Instr.Mem}} + T_{\text{RF.read}} + T_{\text{ALU}} + T_{\text{DM.write}} \\
&= 200 + 100 + 200 + 200 \\
&= 700\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note: Data Memory write and ALU may happen in parallel — but for a *conservative* single-cycle design, KTU treats them as sequential.

#### 3.1.4 Path Analysis for Branch (`beq`)

`beq` computes the branch target and updates PC conditionally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{beq}} &= \max\!\left(T_{\text{PC+4 adder}},\, T_{\text{Instr.Mem}} + T_{\text{RF.read}} + T_{\text{ALU}}\right) + T_{\text{MUX (PC)}} \\
&= \max(50,\ 200+100+200) + 30 \\
&= 500 + 30 \\
&= 530\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Here we conservatively assume the branch-target adder runs in parallel with the instruction-decode ALU.)

#### 3.1.5 Final Clock Period

$$
T_{\text{clock}} = \max(T_{\text{R-type}},\ T_{\text{lw}},\ T_{\text{sw}},\ T_{\text{beq}}) = \max(630,\, 830,\, 700,\, 530) = 830\ \text{ns}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_{\text{clock,max}} = \frac{1}{830 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.204\,\text{MHz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation**: The `lw` instruction dominates because it uses *every* functional unit in series. This is exactly why KTU 2024 emphasizes moving to a **multi-cycle datapath** — splitting `lw` into 5 cycles reduces the average CPI dramatically.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Branch Target Address Calculation

> **Problem**: Given a `beq $t1, $t2, 12` instruction located at memory address `0x0040\_0020` (byte address). If the branch is taken, what is the new value of the PC?

In MIPS, the 16-bit immediate `12` is *word-offset*, not byte-offset.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{next (sequential)}} &= 0x00400020 + 4 = 0x00400024 \\
\text{PC}_{\text{branch}} &= (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{immediate} \times 4) \\
&= 0x00400024 + (12 \times 4) \\
&= 0x00400024 + 48 \\
&= 0x00400024 + 0x30 \\
&= 0x00400054
\end{aligned}
$$

If the immediate were negative (say `−4`):

$$
\text{PC}_{\text{branch}} = 0x00400024 + (-4 \times 4) = 0x00400024 - 16 = 0x00400014
$$

This relies on **sign extension** of the 16-bit immediate to 32 bits before the left-shift by 2.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Signal Values per Instruction Class

This table is a *guaranteed KTU exam favorite*. Memorize it:

| Instruction | `RegDst` | `ALUSrc` | `MemReg` | `RegWrite` | `MemRead` | `MemWrite` | `Branch` | `ALUOp1` | `ALUOp0` |
|-------------|----------|----------|----------|------------|-----------|------------|----------|----------|----------|
| **R-type** (`add`) | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **lw** | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **sw** | X | 1 | X | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **beq** | X | 0 | X | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 |

(`MemReg` is shorthand for `MemtoReg`; `X` denotes *don't care*.)

The 2-bit `ALUOp` then feeds the **ALU Control Decoder** to produce the 4-bit `ALUOperation`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ALUOp = 00} &\Rightarrow \text{ALUOperation = 0010 (ADD)} \quad \text{used by lw, sw} \\
\text{ALUOp = 01} &\Rightarrow \text{ALUOperation = 0110 (SUB)} \quad \text{used by beq} \\
\text{ALUOp = 10} &\Rightarrow \text{ALUOperation from funct field} \quad \text{used by R-type}
\end{aligned}
$$

For example, `add` has `funct = 100000`, which decodes to `ALUOperation = 0010 (ADD)`. For `sub`, `funct = 100010` → `ALUOperation = 0110 (SUB)`.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Performance Comparison: Single vs. Multi-Cycle

> **Problem**: A benchmark executes 100 instructions with the following class distribution: 40% R-type, 30% `lw`, 15% `sw`, 15% `beq`. Compute average CPI and total execution time for a single-cycle datapath. (Assume $T_{\text{clock}} = 830$ ns as derived above.)

For a single-cycle datapath, **CPI = 1** for every instruction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average CPI} &= 1 \\
\text{Total time} &= 100 \times 1 \times 830\ \text{ns} = 83\,000\ \text{ns} = 83\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, suppose we move to a **5-stage multi-cycle** datapath with the following per-instruction cycles: R-type = 4, `lw` = 5, `sw` = 4, `beq` = 3, and $T_{\text{clock}} = 200$ ns.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average CPI} &= 0.40(4) + 0.30(5) + 0.15(4) + 0.15(3) \\
&= 1.60 + 1.50 + 0.60 + 0.45 \\
&= 4.15 \\
\text{Total time} &= 100 \times 4.15 \times 200\ \text{ns} = 83\,000\ \text{ns} = 83\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight**: The total time is *the same* in this specific scenario. But the multi-cycle design allows a **faster clock** (because each cycle is shorter) and **smaller hardware reuse**. KTU questions often ask students to compute the **break-even clock frequency** at which multi-cycle becomes faster.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Cycle MIPS Datapath — Top-Level Block Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below captures the **flow of data** between functional units, the locations of all major **multiplexers**, and the datapath elements that are *reused across instruction classes*. This is the diagram KTU examiners expect a student to draw (by hand or tool) for full marks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[PC Register] --> PCMux{Mux: PC Source}
    PCMux --> IMem[Instruction Memory]
    PCMux --> Adder1[Adder: PC + 4]

    IMem --> InstrBits[32-bit Instruction]
    InstrBits --> CU[Main Control Unit]
    InstrBits --> RegFile[Register File]
    InstrBits --> SignExt[Sign Extender]

    RegFile --> RdA[Read Data 1]
    RegFile --> RdB[Read Data 2]
    SignExt --> Imm32[32-bit Immediate]

    RdA --> ALUMux1{Mux: ALU Input B}
    RdB --> ALUMux1
    Imm32 --> ALUMux1
    ALUMux1 --> ALUinB[ALU B-Input]

    RdA --> ALUinA[ALU A-Input]
    ALUinA --> ALU[ALU]
    ALUinB --> ALU
    ALU --> ALUResult[ALU Result]
    ALU --> ZeroFlag{Zero Flag}

    Adder1 --> BranchAdder[Branch Target Adder]
    Imm32 --> BranchAdder
    BranchAdder --> BranchMux{Mux: PC Source}
    BranchMux --> PCMux

    ALUResult --> DMemAddr[Data Memory Address]
    DMemAddr --> DMem[Data Memory]
    RdB --> DMemWriteData[Data Write Data]
    DMemWriteData --> DMem
    DMem --> DMemReadData[Data Read Data]

    DMemReadData --> WB_Mux{Mux: MemtoReg}
    ALUResult --> WB_Mux
    WB_Mux --> WB_Data[Write-Back Data]
    WB_Data --> RegFile

    InstrBits --> RDstMux{Mux: RegDst}
    RegFile --> RDstMux
    RDstMux --> RegFile

    Adder1 --> PCMux
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students**
> - The **Main Control Unit** (block labelled `CU`) drives the dashed control signals to every MUX and to `RegFile`, `DMem`, etc. (These control lines are *not* drawn here to keep the diagram readable, but in KTU diagrams they MUST appear.)
> - The **two adders** — `PC + 4` and the **branch target adder** — run in parallel during instruction fetch.
> - The **register file** has *two read ports* (so an R-type can read both `rs` and `rt` simultaneously) and *one write port* (the write happens in the last third of the cycle).

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Datapath for an R-type Instruction (Focused View)

The following diagram is a *focused*, annotated view that KTU students often reproduce in the exam to explain R-type execution specifically:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC_R[PC] --> IMem_R[Instruction Memory]
    IMem_R --> Dec[Decode Fields: rs, rt, rd, funct]
    Dec --> RF_R[Register File]
    RF_R -- Read Data 1 --> A_R[Operand A]
    RF_R -- Read Data 2 --> B_R[Operand B]
    A_R --> ALUsrc_R{Mux: ALUSrc}
    B_R --> ALUsrc_R
    ALUsrc_R -- 0 selected --> ALU_R[ALU]
    A_R --> ALU_R
    ALU_R --> Res_R[ALU Result]
    Res_R --> WB_R{Mux: MemtoReg}
    WB_R -- 0 selected --> WBD_R[Write-Back Data]
    WBD_R --> RF_R
    Dec -- rd address --> Dest_R{Dest: RegDst Mux}
    RF_R --> Dest_R
    Dest_R -- 1 selected --> RF_R
```

**Path Walk-through** for `add $t1, $t2, $t3`:
1. `PC` → `IMem` outputs the instruction.
2. Decode extracts `rs = $t2`, `rt = $t3`, `rd = $t1`, `funct = 100000` (ADD).
3. Register File reads `$t2` and `$t3` in parallel.
4. The `ALUSrc` MUX passes `Read Data 2` (since `ALUSrc = 0`).
5. ALU computes `$t2 + $t3` because `ALUControl` is decoded as `ADD`.
6. `MemtoReg` MUX passes the ALU result.
7. Register File writes the result into `$t1` (because `RegDst = 1` selects `rd`).

---

### 4.3 Datapath Cycle Timing — Multi-Cycle FSM View (Optional Extension)

> The single-cycle datapath is *one huge combinational path*. The multi-cycle design breaks this into **5 sequential stages**: IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB. KTU 2024 expects students to be able to *redraw the single-cycle diagram into a multi-cycle one with shared busses*.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> S0_IF
    S0_IF : S0 Instruction Fetch
    S0_IF --> S1_ID
    S1_ID : S1 Instruction Decode / Register Read
    S1_ID --> S2_EX_R
    S1_ID --> S2_EX_LW
    S1_ID --> S2_EX_BEQ
    S2_EX_R : S2 ALU Execute R-type
    S2_EX_LW : S2 ALU Effective Address for lw/sw
    S2_EX_BEQ : S2 ALU Compare for beq
    S2_EX_R --> S4_WB
    S2_EX_LW --> S3_MEM
    S2_EX_BEQ --> S0_IF
    S3_MEM : S3 Memory Access (lw or sw)
    S3_MEM --> S4_WB
    S3_MEM --> S0_IF
    S4_WB : S4 Write-Back to Register File
    S4_WB --> S0_IF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: The arrows from `S2_EX_BEQ → S0_IF` and `S3_MEM → S0_IF` mean that `beq` and `sw` complete in **fewer than 5 cycles**, reducing their effective CPI. Make sure your diagram clearly distinguishes these *early exits* — this is a common 7-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, 3 Marks]**
**Define the term "datapath" as used in computer architecture. List any four functional units of a single-cycle MIPS datapath.**

**Model Answer:**
The **datapath** is the collection of functional units — such as the ALU, registers, memory units, and the buses interconnecting them — that perform data processing and data transfer operations during the execution of a machine instruction, under the direction of the control unit.

The four functional units of a single-cycle MIPS datapath are:
1. **Instruction Memory** (stores the program)
2. **Register File** (holds the 32 general-purpose registers)
3. **ALU** (performs arithmetic and logic operations)
4. **Data Memory** (stores data accessed by `lw` and `sw`)
*(PC, adder units, sign-extender, and MUXes are also valid candidates.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 Marks]**
**What is the function of the `MemtoReg` control signal? In which instructions is it asserted?**

**Model Answer:**
The **`MemtoReg`** control signal selects the data to be written back to the register file at the end of an instruction's execution. Specifically:

- When `MemtoReg = 0`, the **ALU result** is written back.
- When `MemtoReg = 1`, the **data read from Data Memory** is written back.

It is **asserted (= 1)** for the **`lw` (load word)** instruction, where data fetched from memory must be stored into a register. It is **de-asserted (= 0)** for R-type instructions (`add`, `sub`, `and`, `or`, etc.), where the ALU output is the write-back value.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** between two questions of 14 marks each. Both are provided below.

---

#### **Question A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the complete **single-cycle MIPS datapath** for executing the R-type instruction `add $t1, $t2, $t3`. Label every functional unit, multiplexer, and the relevant control signals.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same datapath, derive the **longest combinational path delay** (clock period) given the unit delays below:

| Unit | Delay |
|------|-------|
| Instruction Memory | 200 ns |
| Register File (read or write) | 100 ns |
| ALU | 200 ns |
| Data Memory | 200 ns |
| Multiplexer | 30 ns |
| Sign-extender | 20 ns |
| PC + 4 adder | 50 ns |

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

The complete datapath diagram is provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes (the Mermaid flowchart). For the exam, students should redraw it on paper with the following mandatory labels:

- **5 functional units**: Instruction Memory, Register File, ALU, Data Memory, PC.
- **3 adders**: PC + 4, branch target adder, and the ALU itself.
- **4 multiplexers**:
  - `RegDst` MUX (selects `rd` vs `rt` for write-back register).
  - `ALUSrc` MUX (selects register operand vs sign-extended immediate).
  - `MemtoReg` MUX (selects ALU result vs memory read data).
  - `PC Source` MUX (selects sequential `PC + 4` vs branch target).
- **Control signals**: `RegDst`, `ALUSrc`, `MemtoReg`, `RegWrite`, `MemRead`, `MemWrite`, `Branch`, plus `ALUOp[1:0]`.

For the `add` instruction, the active control signals are: `RegDst = 1`, `ALUSrc = 0`, `MemtoReg = 0`, `RegWrite = 1`, `MemRead = 0`, `MemWrite = 0`, `Branch = 0`, `ALUOp = 10`. The ALU Control decoder then drives `ALUOperation = 0010` (ADD).

**Mark Distribution Hint**: 2 marks for identifying functional units, 2 marks for correct MUX positions, 2 marks for correctly labelling control signals, 1 mark for a clean diagram.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

For an **R-type instruction** like `add`, the datapath traversal is:

$$
\text{PC} \rightarrow \text{Instr.Mem} \rightarrow \text{RF.read} \rightarrow \text{ALU} \rightarrow \text{MUX (MemtoReg)} \rightarrow \text{RF.write}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{add}} &= T_{\text{Instr.Mem}} + T_{\text{RF.read}} + T_{\text{ALU}} + T_{\text{MUX}} + T_{\text{RF.write}} \\
&= 200 + 100 + 200 + 30 + 100 \\
&= 630\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, in a single-cycle datapath the **clock period must accommodate the slowest instruction**. As computed in Section 3.1.2, the `lw` instruction is the slowest:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{lw}} &= T_{\text{Instr.Mem}} + T_{\text{RF.read}} + T_{\text{ALU}} + T_{\text{DM.read}} + T_{\text{MUX}} + T_{\text{RF.write}} \\
&= 200 + 100 + 200 + 200 + 30 + 100 \\
&= 830\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{clock}} = 830\ \text{ns}, \quad f_{\text{clock,max}} \approx 1.204\,\text{MHz}}
$$

The `add` instruction itself completes in 630 ns, but the CPU must idle for the remaining 200 ns — this is the fundamental inefficiency of the single-cycle design.

---

#### **Question B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model, 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of **sign extension** and the **ALU Control Unit** in the single-cycle MIPS datapath. How does the 2-bit `ALUOp` field interact with the 6-bit `funct` field to produce the final 4-bit `ALUOperation`?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **branch target address** for the following instruction sequence. Show all intermediate calculations in hexadecimal.

```
0x00400010:  beq $t1, $t2, 16
0x00400014:  add $t3, $t4, $t5
0x00400018:  sw   $t6, 4($t7)
```

Assume `$t1 ≠ $t2`. What is the value of the PC after this instruction completes?

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Sign Extension (3 of 7 marks):**
The 16-bit immediate field of an I-type MIPS instruction (used in `lw`, `sw`, `beq`) is a *signed* two's-complement number. To use it in 32-bit ALU operations (e.g., adding to a register), it must be widened to 32 bits. **Sign extension** replicates the most-significant bit (the sign bit) of the 16-bit value into the upper 16 bits, preserving the numerical value:

$$
\text{SignExtend}(1111\,1111\,1111\,1100)_{16\text{-bit}} = 1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1111\,1100_{32\text{-bit}} = -4_{10}
$$

Without sign extension, the value would be misinterpreted as a large positive number.

**ALU Control Unit (4 of 7 marks):**
The ALU is a *reconfigurable* arithmetic unit. It performs one of many operations (ADD, SUB, AND, OR, SLT, ...) based on its 4-bit `ALUOperation` input. The **ALU Control Unit** is a small combinational decoder that generates this 4-bit code using two inputs:
1. The 2-bit `ALUOp` from the Main Control.
2. The 6-bit `funct` field from R-type instructions.

The truth table is:

| `ALUOp1` | `ALUOp0` | Instruction Class | `funct` (R-type) | `ALUOperation` |
|----------|----------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| 0 | 0 | `lw`, `sw` | X | `0010` (ADD) |
| 0 | 1 | `beq` | X | `0110` (SUB) |
| 1 | 0 | R-type | `100000` (add) | `0010` (ADD) |
| 1 | 0 | R-type | `100010` (sub) | `0110` (SUB) |
| 1 | 0 | R-type | `100100` (and) | `0000` (AND) |
| 1 | 0 | R-type | `100101` (or)  | `0001` (OR)  |
| 1 | 0 | R-type | `101010` (slt) | `0111` (SLT) |

For non-R-type instructions, the `funct` field is *don't care* (X) because `ALUOp = 00 or 01` already uniquely determines the operation.

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

The instruction `beq $t1, $t2, 16` is at byte address `0x00400010`. MIPS uses *word-aligned* addressing and *word-offset* branches.

**Step 1 — Sequential PC update:**
$$
\text{PC} + 4 = 0x00400010 + 0x4 = 0x00400014
$$

**Step 2 — Sign-extend the immediate:**
The immediate value `16` in 16-bit two's-complement is:
$$
0000\,0000\,0001\,0000_{2}
$$
Sign-extended to 32 bits (still positive, so upper bits remain 0):
$$
0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0001\,0000_{2} = 0x00000010
$$

**Step 3 — Left-shift by 2 (word offset to byte offset):**
$$
\text{Target offset (bytes)} = 16 \times 4 = 64_{10} = 0x40
$$

**Step 4 — Compute branch target:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PC}_{\text{branch}} &= (\text{PC} + 4) + \text{offset} \\
&= 0x00400014 + 0x40 \\
&= 0x00400054
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Branch decision:**
The ALU performs `Read Data 1 − Read Data 2` (i.e., `$t1 − $t2`). Since `$t1 ≠ $t2`, the ALU's **Zero flag = 0**. Therefore, the PC MUX selects the **sequential** path, *not* the branch target.

**Final PC value**:
$$
\boxed{\text{PC}_{\text{next}} = 0x00400014}
$$

The CPU continues with the `add` instruction at `0x00400014`.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls for KTU 2024 Datapath Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `MemtoReg` MUX**: When drawing the datapath, students often omit this MUX and wire the ALU result directly to the register file write port. This loses 1–2 marks because `lw` cannot function without it.
> 2. **Confusing `RegDst` and `MemtoReg`**: These are *different* signals. `RegDst` selects the **destination register number**, while `MemtoReg` selects the **data to write**. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Missing the `ALUSrc` MUX**: This MUX is essential for I-type instructions. Without it, the ALU cannot receive the sign-extended immediate.
> 4. **Drawing the Control Unit as part of the datapath**: Strictly, the Control Unit is a *separate* block that *drives* the datapath. Drawing it inside the datapath loses a mark.
> 5. **Wrong clock period for single-cycle**: Students often compute the path for *one* instruction and forget to take the `max` over all instruction classes. The single-cycle clock period is determined by the **slowest** instruction.
> 6. **Branch target calculation**: Forgetting to **left-shift the immediate by 2** (word-to-byte conversion) is the #1 mistake in `beq` problems.
> 7. **Sign extension vs. zero extension**: For unsigned operations, zero extension is correct, but MIPS `lw`/`sw`/`beq` use *sign extension* because the immediate is signed.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Building a Datapath"**
>
> ✅ **Datapath Definition**: Functional units + buses + MUXes that move and transform data — *not* including the Control Unit.
>
> ✅ **Five canonical blocks**: Instruction Memory, Register File, ALU, Data Memory, PC.
>
> ✅ **Three instruction classes**: R-type (register-register), Load/Store (memory access), Branch (PC update).
>
> ✅ **Seven control signals** (memorize the table): `RegDst`, `ALUSrc`, `MemtoReg`, `RegWrite`, `MemRead`, `MemWrite`, `Branch`.
>
> ✅ **ALU Control**: 2-bit `ALUOp` + 6-bit `funct` → 4-bit `ALUOperation`.
>
> ✅ **Sign extension**: Replicates the MSB of a 16-bit immediate into the upper 16 bits to preserve sign in 32-bit arithmetic.
>
> ✅ **Branch target formula**: $\text{PC}_{\text{branch}} = (\text{PC} + 4) + (\text{immediate} \times 4)$.
>
> ✅ **Single-cycle rule**: $\text{CPI} = 1$, but $T_{\text{clock}}$ = longest instruction path. **Slowest instruction dictates the clock.**
>
> ✅ **Multi-cycle rule**: $\text{CPI} > 1$ but $T_{\text{clock}}$ is much shorter; hardware is reused across cycles.
>
> ✅ **Performance formula**: $T_{\text{CPU}} = N_{\text{inst}} \times \text{CPI} \times T_{\text{clock}}$ — know it cold.
>
> ✅ **KTU favourite mistakes to avoid**: (a) forgetting the `MemtoReg` MUX, (b) wrong branch target shift, (c) including the Control Unit in the datapath, (d) forgetting to take the maximum path delay.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Implementation Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Implementation Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Control Implementation Scheme** of a Central Processing Unit (CPU) refers to the systematic hardware/software methodology used to generate the precise sequence of **micro-operations** and **control signals** required to execute every machine instruction in the Instruction Set Architecture (ISA). It is the "brain of the brain" — the logic that orchestrates fetching, decoding, and executing instructions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the control implementation scheme is realized using one of two dominant paradigms:

- **Hardwired Control Unit (HCU)** — control logic is permanently encoded in fixed combinational and sequential digital circuits (gates, decoders, flip-flops).
- **Microprogrammed Control Unit (MCU)** — control logic is stored as binary words (microinstructions) in a dedicated read-only/rewritable memory called **Control Memory (CM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Students must understand the trade-offs between **speed vs. flexibility**, the structure of a **control word**, **microinstruction formats (horizontal vs. vertical)**, **microprogram sequencing**, and the classic **Wilkes Control scheme**. Direct questions on the design of a control word, microinstruction sequencing logic, and comparison between HCU vs. MCU are high-weightage in ESE.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **conductor leading a 100-piece orchestra**. The sheet of music in front of the conductor is exactly the **microprogram**. Each beat the conductor points to corresponds to a **microinstruction**, and each gesture (raise bow, tap drum, cue trumpet) is a **control signal** that activates a particular instrument (a hardware resource like the ALU, register, or memory bus).

- A **Hardwired Conductor** memorizes every note by muscle memory — extremely fast, but if the orchestra adds a new instrument, the conductor must be **completely retrained** (inflexible, hard to modify).
- A **Microprogrammed Conductor** simply **reads the next instruction from the sheet music** — slightly slower because reading takes time, but the sheet can be **rewritten** to play any new composition (flexible, easy to modify).

This metaphor is precisely how the modern CPU control unit is engineered.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key terminology that KTU examiners expect verbatim:
> - **Control Word (CW):** A binary code whose bits represent the control signals for one clock cycle / one micro-operation.
> - **Control Memory (CM):** A ROM or RAM (writable control store) that stores all microprograms.
> - **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction to be fetched.
> - **Control Data Register (CDR):** Holds the currently fetched microinstruction.
> - **Microinstruction:** One word in control memory that specifies one set of simultaneous micro-operations.

### 1.4 The Master Clock and Timing Foundation

All control signals are synchronized to a **master clock** that generates timing signals $T_0, T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_{n-1}$. Each instruction execution is divided into a fixed sequence of clock cycles, and at every cycle, a unique control word is activated.

$$f_{clock} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} \quad \text{(typical modern CPUs: 2.0 GHz – 5.0 GHz)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** In a hardwired scheme, the number of distinct timing signals equals the maximum number of micro-operations that can be done in one instruction cycle. In a microprogrammed scheme, each microinstruction may take 1 or more clock cycles depending on the microinstruction format chosen.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hardwired Control Unit (HCU) — Operational Decomposition

The hardwired control unit is constructed from three fundamental building blocks:

1. **Instruction Decoder** — decodes the opcode portion of the IR.
2. **Timing Generator (Sequencer)** — produces the periodic timing signals $T_i$.
3. **Control Logic Matrix (Combinational Network)** — a fixed Boolean network of AND/OR/NOT gates that combines decoded opcode, timing signals, flags, and status bits to produce control outputs.

The Boolean expression for a generic control signal $C_i$ is:

$$C_i = f(\text{Opcode}, \text{Timing}, \text{Flags})$$

More precisely, for an $n$-bit opcode with $k$ timing signals and $m$ flag inputs:

$$C_j = \sum_{i=0}^{2^n-1} (\text{Decode}_i) \cdot \sum_{l=0}^{k-1} T_l \cdot \prod_{p=0}^{m-1} F_p$$

#### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic of HCU Operation

- **Step 1:** The instruction word is loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
- **Step 2:** The opcode field is fed into a **3-to-8 or 4-to-16 decoder** (for $n$-bit opcode, $2^n$ output lines).
- **Step 3:** The timing generator produces ordered clocked pulses $T_0, T_1, \ldots, T_n$.
- **Step 4:** For every required control signal, a Boolean expression is pre-derived using a control sequence table (truth table), and that expression is **physically hardwired** using AND-OR-NOT gates.
- **Step 5:** At any given clock edge, the active timing signal AND the active decoded opcode AND the current flags produce the required control output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HCU is Fast:** The control signals are produced by pure combinational logic propagation. There is **no memory read** involved. The propagation delay is only a few gate delays (typically **3–5 ns**), making HCU the choice for RISC pipelines and high-frequency processors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HCU is Rigid:** Any change in the instruction set requires a **complete redesign and re-fabrication** of the combinational logic. As the instruction set grows, the number of gates grows **exponentially** (often called *control logic explosion*).

### 2.2 Microprogrammed Control Unit (MCU) — Operational Decomposition

The MCU replaces the hardwired AND-OR matrix with a **control memory** holding the microprogram. The architecture consists of:

- **Control Memory (CM):** Stores all microinstructions, organized as a 2D array of width $w$ (word size) and depth $N$ (number of microinstructions).
- **Control Address Register (CAR):** Holds the address of the next microinstruction.
- **Control Data Register (CDR):** Latches the microinstruction just read from CM.
- **Sequencer / Next Address Logic:** Determines the next CAR value using branch conditions.
- **External Inputs:** Opcode from IR, condition flags (Carry, Zero, Sign), and feedback from CDR.

#### 2.2.1 Microinstruction Cycle (Fetch-Execute Loop)

1. $CAR \rightarrow CM$: Address sent to control memory.
2. $CM \rightarrow CDR$: Microinstruction read.
3. $CDR \rightarrow$ Control signal generators.
4. $CDR_{next-address-field} \rightarrow$ Next Address Logic $\rightarrow$ $CAR$ (updated for next cycle).

#### 2.2.2 Microinstruction Formats — Horizontal vs. Vertical

| Feature | **Horizontal Microinstruction** | **Vertical Microinstruction** |
|---|---|---|
| Width | Very wide (50–100+ bits) | Narrow (16–32 bits typically) |
| Encoding | **Unencoded / direct** — one bit per control signal | **Encoded / fielded** — groups of bits decoded |
| Parallelism | High (many signals at once) | Low (one micro-op per field at a time) |
| Speed | Faster (no decoding of fields) | Slower (requires field decoders) |
| CM size | Large | Small |
| Use case | CISC, fast control | RISC, compact control |

### 2.3 The Control Word (CW) and Control Memory Sizing

For a basic single-bus CPU with the following resource signals:
- ALU: 5 control bits (op-select + carry-in)
- Shifter: 1 bit
- Register selection: Read = 2 bits, Write = 2 bits
- Memory: Read = 1 bit, Write = 1 bit
- PC control: 2 bits (increment, load, hold)
- Sequencing: 2 bits (next, branch, return-from-subroutine)

Total **control word width** $w = 5 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 16$ bits.

For an ISA with $M$ instructions, average $k$ micro-ops per instruction, plus handler microcode:

$$N_{microinstructions} \approx M \times k + \text{overhead (handlers, exceptions)}$$

Control memory size in bits:

$$S_{CM} = N \times w \quad \text{bits}$$

$$S_{CM} = \frac{N \times w}{8} \quad \text{bytes} = \frac{N \times w}{8 \times 1024} \quad \text{KB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** For an instruction set with 16 instructions, average 8 micro-ops per instruction, control word width 16 bits:
> $S_{CM} = (16 \times 8) \times 16 = 2048 \text{ bits} = 256 \text{ bytes}$.

### 2.4 Microprogram Sequencing Strategies

The Next Address Logic (NAL) determines how the next microinstruction is selected. Common strategies:

- **Sequential (NEXT):** $CAR \leftarrow CAR + 1$
- **Conditional Branch (BRANCH):** If condition true, $CAR \leftarrow$ target address from CDR field; else $CAR \leftarrow CAR + 1$.
- **Mapping (Dispatch):** $CAR \leftarrow$ Opcode-to-address map (the *mapping memory* converts opcode to entry address).
- **Subroutine Call/Return:** Push current CAR + 1 onto a stack; on return, pop.
- **Loop Control:** Use a counter register for iterative microcode.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — KTU exams test these formulas directly.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clock frequency | $f = 1/T$ | Hz |
| 2 | Control word width (unencoded/horizontal) | $w = \sum (\text{bits per resource})$ | bits |
| 3 | Control memory size | $S = N \times w$ | bits |
| 4 | Control memory size | $S_{bytes} = N \times w / 8$ | bytes |
| 5 | Encoded field width | $k = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | bits, for $n$ mutually exclusive signals |
| 6 | Encoding savings | $\text{Saving} = n - \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ | bits per field |
| 7 | Microinstruction cycle time | $T_{\mu} = T_{CM-read} + T_{decode} + T_{exec}$ | ns |
| 8 | Total instruction time | $T_{inst} = k \times T_{\mu}$ | ns, $k$ = number of $\mu$-ops |
| 9 | Number of distinct microprograms | $=$ number of machine instructions + handlers | count |
| 10 | HCU propagation delay | $T_{HCU} = 3 \text{ to } 5$ gate delays | ns |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** Modern Intel/AMD processors (x86) use a hybrid called *micro-op fusion* — the hardwired front-end decodes complex CISC x86 instructions into simpler RISC-like *micro-ops* stored in a micro-op cache, then a fast MCU-like scheduler executes them out-of-order. This blends the benefits of both schemes.

### 2.6 Engineering Trade-off Table

| Parameter | Hardwired CU | Microprogrammed CU |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | **Very fast** (a few ns) | Slower (memory access + decode) |
| Flexibility | Rigid, hard to modify | **Easily modified** (rewrite CM) |
| Cost of design change | High (re-fabrication) | Low (reprogram ROM) |
| Hardware complexity | Increases exponentially with ISA | Scales linearly with $N \times w$ |
| Diagnostic ease | Difficult | Easy (can dump microcode) |
| Typical use | RISC pipelines, fixed ISA | CISC, firmware-upgradeable control |
| Implementation tech | Gates, PLA, PAL | ROM, PLA, WCS (writable control store) |
