## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Control Word Width for a Generic Single-Bus CPU

**Problem Setup:** A single-bus CPU contains the following resources that require control signals:

- ALU: 8 operations (ADD, SUB, AND, OR, XOR, COM, INC, SHR) + 1 Carry-in = **4 bits (op-select) + 1 bit (Cin) = 5 bits**.
- 4 General Purpose Registers (R0–R3): Read select = **2 bits**, Write select = **2 bits** (one at a time is written).
- Memory: READ = **1 bit**, WRITE = **1 bit** (mutually exclusive).
- PC control: Increment, Hold, Load from bus = **2 bits**.
- AC (Accumulator) control: Load, Hold = **1 bit**.
- Sequencing: Next, Branch, Return = **2 bits**.

**Step-by-step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{ALU} &= \lceil \log_2 8 \rceil + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5 \text{ bits} \\
w_{Reg} &= 2 + 2 = 4 \text{ bits} \\
w_{Mem} &= 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ bits} \\
w_{PC} &= 2 \text{ bits} \\
w_{AC} &= 1 \text{ bit} \\
w_{Seq} &= 2 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total control word width:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{total} &= w_{ALU} + w_{Reg} + w_{Mem} + w_{PC} + w_{AC} + w_{Seq} \\
&= 5 + 4 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 2 \\
&= \mathbf{16 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual interpretation:** Each microinstruction in this CPU is 16 bits wide. One bit is allocated per mutually exclusive signal in the horizontal style, and $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ bits in the encoded (vertical) style. The choice changes the control memory size dramatically.

### 3.2 Derivation: Control Memory Capacity for an 8-Instruction ISA

**Problem:** A CPU has **8 instructions** (so opcode field is 3 bits), each instruction requires on average **5 micro-operations**, and we need an extra 16 micro-instructions for interrupt/exception handlers. Control word is 16 bits wide.

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{instr} &= 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ microinstructions} \\
N_{handlers} &= 16 \text{ microinstructions} \\
N_{total} &= 40 + 16 = 56 \text{ microinstructions} \\
w &= 16 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{CM} &= N_{total} \times w = 56 \times 16 = \mathbf{896 \text{ bits}} \\
S_{CM,bytes} &= \frac{896}{8} = \mathbf{112 \text{ bytes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The total number of microinstructions is the sum of micro-ops across all machine instructions plus the overhead for OS-level handlers. Multiplying by the control word width (in bits) gives the raw control memory size, which is then divided by 8 to convert bits to bytes.

### 3.3 Derivation: Hardwired Boolean Expression for a Control Signal

**Scenario:** We need to derive the Boolean expression for the control signal **LD_AC** (Load Accumulator) for the instruction **ADD R1, R2** in a CPU with timing signals $T_0, T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ and a 3-bit opcode where ADD has opcode $000$.

**Step-by-step analysis:**

The ADD micro-operations in a typical 4-cycle sequence are:

| Cycle | Micro-operation |
|---|---|
| $T_0$ | $MAR \leftarrow PC$ |
| $T_1$ | $MDR \leftarrow M[MAR]$, $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ |
| $T_2$ | $IR \leftarrow MDR$ |
| $T_3$ | $AC \leftarrow R1$, decode opcode |
| $T_4$ | $AC \leftarrow AC + R2$, **LD_AC = 1** |

The Boolean condition for LD_AC to be asserted is: opcode = ADD **AND** timing = $T_4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LD\_AC &= D_0 \cdot T_4 \\
\text{where } D_0 &= \overline{OP_2} \cdot \overline{OP_1} \cdot \overline{OP_0} \text{ (decoder output for opcode 000)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
LD\_AC = \overline{OP_2} \cdot \overline{OP_1} \cdot \overline{OP_0} \cdot T_4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** The decoder output $D_0$ goes HIGH only when the opcode is 000 (the ADD instruction). The timing signal $T_4$ is HIGH only during the 5th micro-cycle. The AND of these two conditions uniquely identifies the moment when the accumulator must be loaded. The final expression is directly implemented using one 3-input AND gate plus two NOT gates for $\overline{OP_2}$ and $\overline{OP_1}$ (or using a NAND-NAND equivalent for cheaper CMOS implementation).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Speed Comparison HCU vs MCU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Problem:** Compare instruction execution time between HCU and MCU.

Let:
- $T_g$ = gate propagation delay (HCU) = **4 ns**
- $T_{ROM}$ = control memory read time (MCU) = **25 ns**
- $T_{dec}$ = field decode time (MCU) = **5 ns**
- $k$ = number of micro-operations per instruction = **5**

**HCU execution time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{HCU} &= k \times T_g = 5 \times 4 = \mathbf{20 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MCU execution time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MCU} &= k \times (T_{ROM} + T_{dec}) = 5 \times (25 + 5) = 5 \times 30 = \mathbf{150 \text{ ns}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speed ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speed-up}_{HCU} &= \frac{T_{MCU}}{T_{HCU}} = \frac{150}{20} = 7.5\times
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** Hardwired control uses pure combinational logic per cycle, so its time is a small multiple of gate delay. Microprogrammed control reads from ROM every cycle and then decodes fields, adding significant latency. The ratio of ~7.5x is a representative real-world figure for older CISC designs; modern pipelined MCUs overlap the ROM read with execution to recover most of this gap.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Microprogram for a Single-Bus ADD Instruction

The following table shows the complete microprogram for **ADD R1, R2** (assumed opcode 010) in a 16-bit control word:

| μAddr | F1 (ALU) | F2 (RegRd) | F3 (RegWr) | F4 (MemRd) | F5 (MemWr) | F6 (PC) | F7 (AC) | F8 (Next) | CD (Next Addr) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 00 | — | — | — | — | — | INC | — | NEXT | 01 |
| 01 | — | — | — | READ | — | — | — | NEXT | 02 |
| 02 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | MAP(IR) | — |
| 03 | — | R1 | — | — | — | — | LOAD | NEXT | 04 |
| 04 | ADD | R2 | — | — | — | — | — | NEXT | 05 |
| 05 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | FETCH | 00 |

**Symbol decoding:** F1 = 4 bits, F2 = 2 bits, F3 = 2 bits, F4 = 1 bit, F5 = 1 bit, F6 = 2 bits, F7 = 1 bit, F8 = 2 bits (sequencing), CD = 2 bits (carry/next address). Total = 17 bits, here simplified to 16 by merging F3.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The MAP microinstruction at μAddr = 02 is the *opcode-to-address dispatch*. It uses the IR opcode as input to the **mapping memory** (a small ROM) that outputs the start address of the microprogram for that opcode. This is the modern equivalent of a *microprogram jump table*.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hardwired Control Unit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clock Generator] --> B[Timing Signal Generator T0 to Tn]
    IR[Instruction Register] --> C[Opcode Field 3 bits]
    C --> D[3 to 8 Instruction Decoder]
    D --> D0[D0: ADD]
    D --> D1[D1: SUB]
    D --> D2[D2: AND]
    D --> D3[D3: OR]
    D --> D4[D4: LDA]
    D --> D5[D5: STA]
    D --> D6[D6: JMP]
    D --> D7[D7: NOP]
    D0 --> E[AND OR NOT Logic Matrix]
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    D7 --> E
    B --> E
    FLG[Status Flags C Z S] --> E
    E --> F1[Control Signal: PC_INC]
    E --> F2[Control Signal: MEM_READ]
    E --> F3[Control Signal: IR_LOAD]
    E --> F4[Control Signal: ALU_OP]
    E --> F5[Control Signal: AC_LOAD]
    E --> F6[Control Signal: REG_WRITE]
    E --> F7[Control Signal: MEM_WRITE]
    E --> F8[Control Signal: PC_LOAD]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Microprogrammed Control Unit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CAR[Control Address Register CAR] --> CMA[Address Lines]
    CMA --> CM[(Control Memory ROM 2D array N x w)]
    CM --> CDR[Control Data Register CDR]
    CDR --> G1[Control Signal Generators]
    G1 --> O1[Output to ALU PC AC Memory Registers Bus]
    CDR --> NAL[Next Address Logic NAL]
    NAL --> CAR
    IR[Instruction Register Opcode] --> MAP[Mapping Memory ROM]
    MAP --> CAR
    CDR --> CDBR[Condition Branch Field]
    CDBR --> NAL
    FLG[Status Flags] --> NAL
    STK[Subroutine Stack] --> NAL
    CTR[Loop Counter] --> NAL
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Microinstruction Cycle (Fetch-Decode-Execute)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: CAR to CM Address Bus] --> P2[Step 2: ROM Read Cycle]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Microinstruction to CDR]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Field Decode if Vertical Format]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Control Signals Asserted to CPU Resources]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Next Address Logic Computes New CAR]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7: CAR Updated End of Clock Cycle]
    P7 --> P1
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Control Word Field Structure (Horizontal Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CW[16 bit Control Word] --> A[ALU 5 bits 0 to 4]
    CW --> B[Register Read 2 bits 5 to 6]
    CW --> C[Register Write 2 bits 7 to 8]
    CW --> D[Memory Read 1 bit 9]
    CW --> E[Memory Write 1 bit 10]
    CW --> F[PC Control 2 bits 11 to 12]
    CW --> G[AC Load 1 bit 13]
    CW --> H[Next Address Field 2 bits 14 to 15]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Microprogram Sequencing Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Current Microinstruction in CDR] --> Q{Decoded Branch Field}
    Q --> R1[Strategy 1: Sequential CAR plus 1]
    Q --> R2[Strategy 2: Conditional Branch to Target Address]
    Q --> R3[Strategy 3: Mapping from Opcode via Mapping ROM]
    Q --> R4[Strategy 4: Subroutine Call Push CAR Stack]
    Q --> R5[Strategy 5: Loop Decrement Counter Not Zero Loop]
    R1 --> Z[Update CAR]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    R5 --> Z
    Z --> W[Next Cycle Begin]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The block-level functional architecture above shows the *flow of control and data* in both hardwired and microprogrammed schemes. The mapping memory (in MCU) and the instruction decoder (in HCU) play analogous roles — both translate opcodes into the entry point of the control sequence for that instruction.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Differentiate between hardwired control and microprogrammed control. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Hardwired Control | Microprogrammed Control |
|---|---|
| Uses fixed combinational logic (gates, decoders) | Uses stored microinstructions in a ROM |
| **Faster** (no memory read delay) | **Slower** (ROM access per cycle) |
| Difficult to modify (re-fabrication needed) | Easy to modify (rewrite microprogram) |
| Complex for large ISAs | Scales gracefully for large ISAs |

**Advantages of Hardwired:** (i) High speed — only gate propagation delay. (ii) Lower power consumption per cycle.
**Advantages of Microprogrammed:** (i) Design flexibility and easy debugging. (ii) Easy to add new instructions by extending the microprogram.

**[3 Marks: 1 for definition, 1 for advantages of HCU, 1 for advantages of MCU]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**
**What is a control word? Explain horizontal and vertical microinstruction formats.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Control Word (CW)** is a binary code whose individual bits (or fields) control the activation of various hardware resources (ALU, registers, memory, bus) for one micro-operation cycle.

- **Horizontal Microinstruction:** Wide, unencoded format where **one bit per control signal**. Highly parallel, fast, but consumes more control memory.
- **Vertical Microinstruction:** Narrow, encoded format where bits are grouped into fields and decoded. Saves memory but adds decode delay and reduces parallelism.

Example: For 16 control signals, horizontal = **16 bits**, vertical encoded into 4 fields of 4 bits each = **16 bits total but with field decoding overhead**.

**[3 Marks: 1 for control word, 1 for horizontal, 1 for vertical]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the design of a hardwired control unit with a neat block diagram. Derive the Boolean expression for the control signal **LD_AC** (Load Accumulator) for the instruction **ADD R1, R2**, assuming a 3-bit opcode where ADD = 010 and timing signals $T_0$ to $T_5$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CPU has 32 instructions, each requiring on average 6 micro-operations. The control word is 20 bits wide. Calculate the control memory size in bytes. If we use a vertical (encoded) format where the 20 bits are split into 5 fields of 4 bits each, how much does the control memory size change?

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Hardwired Control Unit Design and Boolean Derivation [7 Marks]**

A hardwired control unit consists of:
1. **Instruction Decoder** — decodes the $n$-bit opcode into $2^n$ lines.
2. **Timing Generator** — produces sequential clocked timing signals.
3. **Control Logic Matrix** — AND-OR combinational logic producing control signals.

**Block diagram (textual):** Opcode $\rightarrow$ Decoder $\rightarrow$ 8 lines; Clock $\rightarrow$ Timing Generator $\rightarrow$ $T_0 \ldots T_5$; Outputs of decoder + timing generator + flags $\rightarrow$ AND-OR Matrix $\rightarrow$ Control Signals.

**Boolean Derivation for LD_AC during ADD:**

The ADD R1, R2 instruction executes over 6 cycles. The accumulator load occurs at $T_5$ (the cycle where the ALU sum is written back to AC). The opcode for ADD is 010, which activates decoder line $D_2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= OP_2 \cdot \overline{OP_1} \cdot OP_0 \\
LD\_AC &= D_2 \cdot T_5 \\
\Rightarrow LD\_AC &= OP_2 \cdot \overline{OP_1} \cdot OP_0 \cdot T_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hardware implementation:** Three NOT gates (for $\overline{OP_1}$, if needed) + one 4-input AND gate driving the LD_AC line. This signal, when HIGH, opens the AC's input buffer connecting it to the bus.

**[Marks: 2 for diagram description, 2 for identifying timing/opcode, 2 for Boolean expression, 1 for hardware]**

**(b) Control Memory Size Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Step 1:** Compute total number of microinstructions.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \text{Instructions} \times \text{micro-ops/instruction} \\
&= 32 \times 6 = 192 \text{ microinstructions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Add 20% overhead for interrupt handlers and dispatch logic.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= 192 \times 1.20 = 230.4 \approx 230 \text{ microinstructions}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Horizontal format — control word width = 20 bits.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{horiz} &= 230 \times 20 = 4600 \text{ bits} = \frac{4600}{8} = \mathbf{575 \text{ bytes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Vertical (encoded) format — same width (20 bits) here, but if encoded into 5 fields of 4 bits each, **the number of distinct micro-operations per cycle may be reduced** because only one signal per 4-bit field can be active. This means each "cycle" may require more microinstructions.

Assume vertical requires **1.4x** more microinstructions (to serialize operations that horizontal does in parallel):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{vert} &= 230 \times 1.4 = 322 \text{ microinstructions} \\
S_{vert} &= 322 \times 20 = 6440 \text{ bits} = \frac{6440}{8} = \mathbf{805 \text{ bytes}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Change in size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta S &= S_{vert} - S_{horiz} = 805 - 575 = \mathbf{+230 \text{ bytes (40% increase)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real interpretation:** Although the word width is identical, the vertical format **increases the total number of microinstructions** because it cannot express simultaneous operations. The student should note that encoding is preferred when the **width reduction** is significant (e.g., reducing 64 bits to 6 bits per field) so that the depth increase is offset by the width decrease.

**[Marks: 2 for step 1-2, 2 for step 3, 2 for step 4, 1 for conclusion]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **microprogrammed control unit**. Discuss the functions of **CAR**, **CDR**, and the **mapping memory**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the complete microprogram (control word sequence) for a single-bus CPU executing the instruction **LDA (Load Accumulator from memory address given by operand)**. Assume a 16-bit control word with the format specified below. List the control signals asserted at each micro-cycle.

| F1 (ALU) | F2 (RegRd) | F3 (RegWr) | F4 (MemRd) | F5 (MemWr) | F6 (PC) | F7 (AC) | F8 (Next) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Microprogrammed Control Unit Block Diagram and Components [7 Marks]**

**Block diagram description:** The MCU consists of:
- **Control Memory (CM):** A ROM of size $N \times w$ where $N$ is the number of microinstructions and $w$ is the control word width.
- **CAR (Control Address Register):** Holds the address of the microinstruction to be fetched. Initial value = 0.
- **CDR (Control Data Register):** Latches the microinstruction read from CM.
- **Next Address Logic (NAL):** Generates the next CAR value.
- **Mapping Memory:** A small ROM that maps the machine instruction opcode to the starting address of its microprogram.

**Functions:**
- **CAR:** Points to the next microinstruction; incremented sequentially or loaded from NAL output.
- **CDR:** Buffers the microinstruction read from CM, holds it stable for the entire cycle.
- **Mapping Memory:** Converts the $n$-bit opcode into a $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$-bit address pointing to the first microinstruction of the corresponding microprogram. Eliminates the need for a long sequential search.

**Operation sequence:** After fetch-decode of the machine instruction, the opcode bits address the mapping memory, whose output is loaded into CAR. The CM is then read at that address, and the microprogram executes. At the end of each microinstruction, NAL updates CAR. After execution, CAR is reset to 0 to begin the next instruction fetch.

**[Marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for CAR/CDR, 2 for mapping memory, 1 for operation]**

**(b) Microprogram for LDA Instruction [7 Marks]**

The LDA instruction loads the accumulator from a memory location whose address is the operand of the instruction. The microprogram on a single-bus CPU is:

| Cycle | μAddr | F1 (ALU) | F2 (RegRd) | F3 (RegWr) | F4 (MemRd) | F5 (MemWr) | F6 (PC) | F7 (AC) | F8 (Next) | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 00 | — | — | — | — | — | INC | — | NEXT | $PC \rightarrow MAR$, $PC \leftarrow PC+1$ |
| 2 | 01 | — | — | — | READ | — | — | — | NEXT | $MDR \leftarrow M[MAR]$ (fetch operand address) |
| 3 | 02 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | MAP | Dispatch to LDA microprogram start |
| 4 | 10 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | NEXT | $MAR \leftarrow MDR$ (operand addr to MAR) |
| 5 | 11 | — | — | — | READ | — | — | — | NEXT | $MDR \leftarrow M[MAR]$ (read data) |
| 6 | 12 | — | — | — | — | — | — | LOAD | NEXT | $AC \leftarrow MDR$ (load accumulator) |
| 7 | 13 | — | — | — | — | — | — | — | FETCH | Return to μAddr = 00 |

**Explanation of each cycle:**
- **Cycles 1–3:** Standard instruction fetch (shared with all instructions).
- **Cycle 4:** Transfer the operand address from MDR to MAR.
- **Cycle 5:** Read the actual data from memory.
- **Cycle 6:** Load the data into AC.
- **Cycle 7:** Return to fetch the next machine instruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The MAP instruction at cycle 3 is the *dispatch* mechanism. In modern designs, this is replaced by a *micro-op cache lookup* (Intel, AMD) for even faster decoding.

**[Marks: 1 for header, 4 for correct microprogram, 2 for explanation of MAP/FETCH]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing control word width with instruction word width.** The control word is **internal to the CPU** (typically 16–100 bits) and has nothing to do with the ISA word size. Many students erroneously equate them.
> 2. **Forgetting the mapping memory.** When asked to design an MCU, students often omit the mapping memory, leading to an incomplete design and loss of 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong Boolean expression for HCU.** The expression must include **BOTH** the decoded opcode line **AND** the timing signal. Many students write only one term, losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Misapplying $\lceil \log_2 n \rceil$.** Use it for *encoded* field width, NOT for the unencoded horizontal case.
> 5. **Ignoring the FETCH microinstruction.** Every microprogram must end with a FETCH (return to address 0) signal; omitting it makes the design non-functional.
> 6. **Mixing up the roles of CAR and CDR.** CAR = address pointer; CDR = data buffer. Conflating them is a common slip.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Control Implementation Scheme**

- ☐ Control unit generates control signals to orchestrate the data path during each micro-operation.
- ☐ **Hardwired Control** = fixed combinational logic (gates, PLAs); **fast but inflexible**.
- ☐ **Microprogrammed Control** = control logic stored in CM as microinstructions; **flexible but slower**.
- ☐ **Control Word (CW)** = binary code for one set of simultaneous control signals.
- ☐ **Control Memory (CM)** = ROM (or WCS) holding all microinstructions.
- ☐ **CAR** = Control Address Register; **CDR** = Control Data Register.
- ☐ **Mapping Memory** = converts opcode to microprogram start address.
- ☐ **Horizontal microinstruction** = wide, unencoded (1 bit per signal); **fast, large CM**.
- ☐ **Vertical microinstruction** = narrow, encoded (fields decoded); **slow, small CM**.
- ☐ Sequencing strategies: **NEXT, BRANCH, MAP, CALL/RETURN, LOOP**.
- ☐ **Encoded field width formula:** $k = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil$ for $n$ mutually exclusive signals.
- ☐ **CM size formula:** $S = N \times w$ bits; $S_{bytes} = N \times w / 8$ bytes.
- ☐ **Microinstruction cycle time (MCU):** $T_{\mu} = T_{ROM} + T_{decode} + T_{exec}$.
- ☐ **HCU Boolean form:** $C_j = f(\text{Decoded Opcode}, \text{Timing Signal}, \text{Flags})$.
- ☐ Modern CPUs (Intel, AMD) use a **hybrid**: hardwired front-end + micro-op cache + microprogrammed back-end.
- ☐ KTU-favorite: derive Boolean expressions for control signals and compute control memory size.
- ☐ KTU-favorite: design microprogram for ADD, LDA, STA, JMP, branch instructions.
- ☐ KTU-favorite: compare HCU vs MCU using speed, flexibility, cost, scalability metrics.

> **End of Notes — Control Implementation Scheme | Module 3 | PERUT418**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pipelining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipelining** is an implementation technique in computer architecture where multiple instructions are overlapped in execution. A pipeline is a set of data-processing elements (stages) connected in series, where the output of one stage is the input of the next. Each stage performs a specific operation, and multiple instructions can be in different stages of processing simultaneously, thereby improving instruction throughput without reducing the latency of a single instruction.

In the context of **KTU PERUT418 Module 3**, pipelining is studied as a fundamental technique to exploit **Instruction-Level Parallelism (ILP)**. The processor is divided into a sequence of stages, with each stage completing a part of an instruction in a single clock cycle. Pipelining does not reduce the time to complete one instruction; instead, it increases the throughput of instruction completion.

### Types of Pipelines (KTU Syllabus Mapping)
- **Instruction Pipeline**: Used in general-purpose CPUs to overlap fetch, decode, execute, memory access, and write-back phases of successive instructions (e.g., classic **5-stage RISC pipeline**).
- **Arithmetic (Floating-Point) Pipeline**: Used to perform complex arithmetic operations (multiply, divide, floating-point add) by breaking the operation into sub-operations that can be pipelined.
- **Processor Pipeline**: The broader concept of overlapping the work of different functional units of a processor.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Laundry Assembly Line**
> Imagine washing 4 loads of clothes using 3 machines: **Washer**, **Dryer**, and **Folder**.
>
> - **Without a Pipeline (Sequential):** You wash Load 1 (30 min), then dry it (30 min), then fold it (30 min) → 90 min. Then start Load 2. Total for 4 loads = **4 × 90 = 360 minutes**.
> - **With a Pipeline:** At time t=0, Load 1 enters Washer. At t=30, Load 1 moves to Dryer, and **Load 2 enters the Washer simultaneously**. At t=60, Load 1 is being Folded, Load 2 is in Dryer, and **Load 3 enters Washer**. This overlapping continues.
> - **Pipeline Time** = (3 stages) + (4–1) = **3 + 3 = 6 time slots of 30 min = 180 min**. Exactly **2× speedup**!

The clothes = **instructions**, the machines = **pipeline stages**, the slots = **clock cycles**. The key insight is that **all stages work in parallel** on different instructions, but a *single* instruction still passes through all stages sequentially.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics in Pipelining

The following standard metrics are universally used to evaluate any pipeline (board examiners expect these symbols verbatim):

- **n** = Total number of instructions (tasks) to be processed
- **k** = Number of stages in the pipeline (also called the **depth** of the pipeline)
- **Tp** = Clock cycle time (time per stage) of the pipelined processor
- **S** = **Speedup** of a pipeline over an equivalent non-pipelined system
- **η (Eta)** = **Efficiency** of the pipeline
- **τ (Tau)** = **Throughput** of the pipeline (instructions completed per unit time)
- **Pipeline Cycle Time (τp)** = Time taken by the slowest stage + latch (register) delay

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pipelining Works: The Principle of Overlapping

The core **"why"** behind pipelining is the **partitioning of a task** into independent sub-tasks (stages), each of which can be performed by a dedicated hardware unit. As long as there is a continuous flow of instructions entering the pipeline, all stages remain busy in every cycle. The performance gain is therefore derived from the **parallelism across instructions**, not from making any single instruction faster.

### Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. **Decomposition:** A sequential process (e.g., instruction execution) is broken into `k` independent stages.
2. **Allocation:** Each stage is assigned dedicated hardware (registers, ALU units, multiplexers).
3. **Synchronization:** A common clock drives the data movement between stages via **pipeline latches/registers** placed between stages.
4. **Overlap:** While stage `i` processes instruction `n+1`, stage `i+1` processes instruction `n`.
5. **Steady State:** After `k` cycles, the pipeline is full and completes **one instruction per cycle** (ideal case).

## 2.2 Design Rules for Balanced Pipelines

A pipeline is said to be **balanced** when every stage takes the same amount of time. For maximum efficiency:

- The **clock cycle time (Tp)** of a pipelined processor = **(time of the slowest stage) + (latch/buffer delay)**.
- If one stage is much slower than others, it becomes the **bottleneck**, and the clock must be slowed to match it, negating the speedup.
- Designers must **rebalance** stages by subdividing slow stages or combining fast ones.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Non-Pipelined Time (Tn) | $T_n = n \times k \times T_p$ | Time to execute `n` tasks sequentially through `k` stages | seconds |
| Pipelined Time (Tpipelined) | $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \times T_p$ | Time to execute `n` tasks through a `k`-stage pipeline | seconds |
| **Speedup (S)** | $S = \dfrac{T_n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$ | Ratio of non-pipelined to pipelined time | dimensionless |
| Maximum Speedup (Smax) | $S_{max} = k$ (as $n \to \infty$) | The theoretical limit of pipelining | dimensionless |
| **Efficiency (η)** | $\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$ | Fraction of time stages are useful | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Throughput (τ)** | $\tau = \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p}$ | Tasks completed per unit time | tasks/second |
| Maximum Throughput (τmax) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ (as $n \to \infty$) | One task per clock cycle | tasks/second |
| Stage Delay (unbalanced) | $T_p = \max(t_i) + t_{latch}$ | Slowest stage + latch overhead | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note for Markdown:** In all LaTeX expressions above, the absolute-value / division bar is rendered using `\dfrac{...}{...}` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Never write `|x|` inside a table cell.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Modern CPUs (Intel Core, AMD Ryzen, Apple M-series):** Use **14 to 20+ stage deep pipelines** to push clock frequencies above 5 GHz. ARM Cortex-A78 uses roughly a 13-stage pipeline.
- **GPUs:** Use massively deep pipelines with thousands of lanes — pipelining is the cornerstone of SIMD throughput.
- **DSPs (Digital Signal Processors):** Use **arithmetic pipelines** to sustain real-time FIR/FFT filter throughput.
- **FPGA & ASIC Design:** Pipeline stages are explicitly synthesized using registered boundaries between combinational logic blocks to meet timing closure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pipeline Speedup Formula

We will derive, from first principles, why $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$.

**Assumptions (Ideal Pipeline):**
- `k` perfectly balanced stages.
- No hazards, no stalls.
- Clock period = $T_p$ (uniform across all stages).

### Step 1 — Compute Non-Pipelined Time ($T_n$)

In a non-pipelined processor, each instruction must complete **all `k` stages** before the next instruction can begin.

$$
T_n = n \times (t_1 + t_2 + \dots + t_k)
$$

Because we assumed a balanced pipeline where each stage takes $T_p$:

$$
T_n = n \times k \times T_p
$$

**Logic:** There are `n` instructions, and each one consumes `k` clock cycles of $T_p$ each.

### Step 2 — Compute Pipelined Time ($T_{pipe}$)

In a pipelined processor, the **first instruction** still requires `k` clock cycles to traverse all stages (it cannot skip any).

$$
T_{first} = k \times T_p
$$

After the first instruction completes, the pipeline is "full." Now, **one new instruction finishes every additional clock cycle**, because all stages are busy working on different instructions in lockstep.

$$
T_{remaining} = (n - 1) \times T_p
$$

Therefore, the total pipelined time is:

$$
T_{pipe} = T_{first} + T_{remaining} = (k + n - 1) \times T_p
$$

**Logic:** We add the "fill time" of the pipeline (k cycles) and the "drain time" of the remaining instructions (n-1 cycles, one per cycle).

### Step 3 — Form the Speedup Ratio

By definition, Speedup is the ratio of sequential time to pipelined time:

$$
S = \dfrac{T_n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k \cdot T_p}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p}
$$

Cancel the common $T_p$ term:

$$
S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}
$$

**Result verified.** This is the canonical KTU board-exam formula.

### Step 4 — Take the Limit to Find Maximum Speedup

As the number of instructions $n \to \infty$ (very long-running program):

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $n$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{k}{\dfrac{k}{n} + 1 - \dfrac{1}{n}} = \dfrac{k}{0 + 1 - 0} = k
$$

**Interpretation:** The speedup can never exceed `k`, the number of stages. This is the **fundamental limit of pipelining**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Throughput

Throughput is the number of instructions completed per second. From the pipelined execution time:

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p
$$

$$
\tau = \dfrac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p}
$$

Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \tau = \lim_{n \to \infty} \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p} = \dfrac{1}{T_p}
$$

**Interpretation:** Once the pipeline is full, exactly one instruction finishes every clock cycle $T_p$. This is the **maximum sustainable throughput**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Solving a Balanced vs Unbalanced Pipeline

Consider a 4-stage pipeline with stage delays $t_1 = 30$ ns, $t_2 = 50$ ns, $t_3 = 40$ ns, $t_4 = 20$ ns, and a latch delay of $5$ ns. Compute the speedup for 100 tasks.

**Step 1 — Compute Balanced Clock Period.** A pipeline can only run as fast as its slowest stage.

$$
T_p = \max(t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4) + t_{latch} = 50 + 5 = 55 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Non-Pipelined Time.**

$$
T_n = n \times (t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4) = 100 \times (30 + 50 + 40 + 20) = 100 \times 140 = 14000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Pipelined Time.**

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \times T_p = (4 + 100 - 1) \times 55 = 103 \times 55 = 5665 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Speedup.**

$$
S = \dfrac{T_n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{14000}{5665} \approx 2.47
$$

**Step 5 — Compute Maximum Possible Speedup.**

$$
S_{max} = k = 4
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{2.47}{4} = 0.6175 = 61.75\%
$$

**Observation:** Because of the imbalance (stage 2 is 50 ns while stage 4 is 20 ns), the actual speedup (2.47) is much less than the theoretical maximum (4). This is why real-world pipeline designers strive to **rebalance stages**.

## 3.4 Pipeline Hazards — The Three Classes

Pipelining is never perfectly realized in practice because of **hazards**. A hazard is any condition that prevents the next instruction in the pipeline from executing in its designated clock cycle.

### (A) Structural Hazards (Resource Conflicts)
- **Definition:** Two instructions in the pipeline need the **same hardware resource** at the same time.
- **Example:** A single memory port being requested by both the instruction fetch (IF) of instruction $i+1$ and the data memory access (MEM) of instruction $i$.
- **Solution:** **Resource duplication** (e.g., separate instruction and data caches — *Harvard architecture*) or **pipeline stalling**.

### (B) Data Hazards (Data Dependency)
- **Definition:** An instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that is still in the pipeline.
- **Three Sub-types (using register `R2` as example):**
  - **RAW (Read After Write) — True Dependency:** `ADD R1, R2, R3` followed by `SUB R4, R1, R5`. The SUB needs R1 which is not yet written back. **This is the most common hazard.**
  - **WAR (Write After Read) — Anti-Dependency:** `ADD R1, R2, R3` followed by `SUB R3, R4, R5`. The SUB writes to R3 before ADD has read it. (Only a problem in pipelines with out-of-order completion.)
  - **WAW (Write After Write) — Output Dependency:** Two instructions both write to the same register, and the later one finishes first.
- **Solutions:** **Data forwarding (bypassing)**, **pipeline interlocks (stalls)**, and **compiler scheduling (instruction reordering)**.

### (C) Control Hazards (Branch Hazards)
- **Definition:** The pipeline does not know the outcome of a branch instruction until late in the pipeline. The instructions fetched after the branch may not be the right ones.
- **Example:** A `BEQ` (branch-if-equal) instruction — until it executes and resolves the condition, the processor does not know which instruction comes next.
- **Solutions:**
  - **Branch prediction** (static: predict-not-taken; dynamic: 1-bit or 2-bit saturating counters).
  - **Delayed branch** (compiler fills the delay slot with a useful independent instruction).
  - **Speculative execution** with rollback on misprediction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classic 5-Stage Instruction Pipeline (MIPS-style)

The diagram below maps the canonical **IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB** instruction pipeline used in KTU board questions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef stageStyle fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff,font-weight:bold
    classDef regStyle fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef ctrlStyle fill:#10b981,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    IF[IF INSTRUCTION FETCH]:::stageStyle
    ID[ID INSTRUCTION DECODE REGISTER FETCH]:::stageStyle
    EX[EX EXECUTE ADDRESS CALCULATION]:::stageStyle
    MEM[MEM MEMORY ACCESS]:::stageStyle
    WB[WB WRITE BACK]:::stageStyle

    IR1[PIPELINE REGISTER IF ID]:::regStyle
    IR2[PIPELINE REGISTER ID EX]:::regStyle
    IR3[PIPELINE REGISTER EX MEM]:::regStyle
    IR4[PIPELINE REGISTER MEM WB]:::regStyle

    INS((INSTRUCTION STREAM)) --> IF
    IF --> IR1 --> ID
    ID --> IR2 --> EX
    EX --> IR3 --> MEM
    MEM --> IR4 --> WB
    WB --> OUT((RESULT))
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read carefully:** Pipeline latches (IR1…IR4) sit *between* stages. They hold the intermediate data and control signals so that each stage can operate on a *different* instruction in the same clock cycle.

## 4.2 Pipeline Timing Diagram (4 Instructions Through 5 Stages)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Pipeline Execution Timing Diagram (4 Instructions I1 I2 I3 I4)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s

    section Instruction 1
    IF1      :a1, 0, 1
    ID1      :a2, 1, 1
    EX1      :a3, 2, 1
    MEM1     :a4, 3, 1
    WB1      :a5, 4, 1

    section Instruction 2
    IF2      :b1, 1, 1
    ID2      :b2, 2, 1
    EX2      :b3, 3, 1
    MEM2     :b4, 4, 1
    WB2      :b5, 5, 1

    section Instruction 3
    IF3      :c1, 2, 1
    ID3      :c2, 3, 1
    EX3      :c3, 4, 1
    MEM3     :c4, 5, 1
    WB3      :c5, 6, 1

    section Instruction 4
    IF4      :d1, 3, 1
    ID4      :d2, 4, 1
    EX4      :d3, 5, 1
    MEM4     :d4, 6, 1
    WB4      :d5, 7, 1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Insight:** Notice that **cycle 1** only finishes 1 instruction's first stage, but **cycle 4** (after 5-stage fill) finishes 1 instruction *and* starts the 5th. From cycle 5 onward, exactly **1 instruction completes per cycle**. Total time = 8 cycles for 4 instructions, matching $(k + n - 1) = 5 + 4 - 1 = 8$. The non-pipelined version would take $4 \times 5 = 20$ cycles.

## 4.3 Pipeline Hazard Resolution Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef hazardStyle fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef solveStyle fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef detectStyle fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    SH[STRUCTURAL HAZARD]:::hazardStyle
    DH[DATA HAZARD]:::hazardStyle
    CH[CONTROL HAZARD]:::hazardStyle

    HDU[HAZARD DETECTION UNIT]:::detectStyle
    FW[BYPASSING FORWARDING UNIT]:::solveStyle
    BPU[BRANCH PREDICTION UNIT]:::solveStyle
    STALL[INSERT PIPELINE BUBBLE STALL]:::solveStyle
    DC[DUPLICATE HARDWARE RESOURCE]:::solveStyle
    CS[COMPILER SCHEDULING]:::solveStyle

    SH --> HDU
    DH --> HDU
    CH --> HDU

    HDU --> FW
    HDU --> STALL
    HDU --> BPU
    HDU --> DC
    HDU --> CS
```

## 4.4 Arithmetic Pipeline Example: 4-Stage Floating-Point Multiplier

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef arithStyle fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef regStyle fill:#facc15,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    S1[S1 COMPARE EXPONENTS]:::arithStyle
    S2[S2 ADD EXPONENT TO BASE]:::arithStyle
    S3[S3 MULTIPLY MANTISSAS]:::arithStyle
    S4[S4 NORMALIZE RESULT]:::arithStyle

    R1[(LATCH 1)]:::regStyle
    R2[(LATCH 2)]:::regStyle
    R3[(LATCH 3)]:::regStyle

    IN((FLOAT INPUTS)) --> S1 --> R1 --> S2 --> R2 --> S3 --> R3 --> S4 --> OUT((NORMALIZED PRODUCT))
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a floating-point multiply, the mantissas and exponents are independent paths. Once balanced and pipelined, the IEEE 754 multiplier can achieve one multiply per cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

### **Q1. Define pipelining. What is the maximum speedup achievable in a `k`-stage pipeline?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Pipelining is a technique of decomposing a sequential process into `k` sub-processes called **stages**, where each stage is executed in a dedicated hardware segment and operates concurrently with the other stages on different instructions. The maximum speedup achievable in a `k`-stage pipeline is **`S_max = k`**, which is obtained as the number of instructions `n → ∞`. (1 mark: definition, 1 mark: formula, 1 mark: limit interpretation)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**

### **Q2. Differentiate between pipeline efficiency and throughput. Give their formulas.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Efficiency (η)** is the fraction of time the pipeline stages are doing useful work: $\eta = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$. (1.5 marks)
- **Throughput (τ)** is the number of instructions completed per unit time: $\tau = \dfrac{n}{(k + n - 1) \cdot T_p}$. (1.5 marks)
Efficiency is a ratio (0 to 1), while throughput is an absolute rate (instructions/second).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, End Semester Evaluation]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** A 4-stage pipeline has stage delays of 25 ns, 30 ns, 20 ns, and 35 ns, with a latch delay of 5 ns. Compute (i) the clock period of the pipeline, (ii) the speedup for executing 200 tasks, and (iii) the efficiency.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the three types of data hazards in an instruction pipeline with suitable examples. Briefly describe how **data forwarding** resolves them.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Define **structural hazard** and **control hazard**. With a neat diagram, explain the working of the **classic 5-stage RISC pipeline**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A non-pipelined processor takes 400 ns per instruction. Its pipelined version uses 5 stages with a clock period of 80 ns. Find the speedup, efficiency, and throughput for 500 instructions.

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-step Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Identify the slowest stage (bottleneck):
The delays are 25, 30, 20, 35 ns. The maximum is **35 ns**.

**Step 2:** Compute the clock period $T_p$ of the pipeline:
The clock period must accommodate the slowest stage plus the latch delay.

$$
T_p = \max(t_i) + t_{latch} = 35 + 5 = 40 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the non-pipelined execution time for `n = 200` tasks:
Each task requires all 4 stages to be performed.

$$
T_n = n \times (t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4) = 200 \times (25 + 30 + 20 + 35) = 200 \times 110 = 22000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the pipelined execution time:
With `k = 4` stages and `n = 200` instructions:

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \times T_p = (4 + 200 - 1) \times 40 = 203 \times 40 = 8120 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the speedup:

$$
S = \dfrac{T_n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{22000}{8120} \approx 2.71
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{2.71}{4} = 0.6775 \approx 67.75\%
$$

**Part (b) — Data Hazards Explained:**

The three data hazards in instruction pipelines are:

1. **RAW (Read After Write) — True Dependency:**
   The most common hazard. Instruction $i+1$ tries to read a source register before instruction $i$ has written its result.

   Example:

   | Cycle | I1: `ADD R1, R2, R3` | I2: `SUB R4, R1, R5` |
   |---|---|---|
   | 1 | IF |  |
   | 2 | ID | IF |
   | 3 | EX | ID ← **needs R1, not yet written** |
   | 4 | MEM | EX (stalled) |
   | 5 | WB | EX |

2. **WAR (Write After Read) — Anti-Dependency:**
   Instruction $i+1$ writes to a register that instruction $i$ is still about to read. Cannot occur in an in-order 5-stage pipeline but arises in **out-of-order** execution.

3. **WAW ( Write After Write) — Output Dependency:**
   Two instructions write to the same destination register. If they complete out of order, the final value will be wrong.

**Data Forwarding (Bypassing):**
A **hardware forwarding path** is added from the output of the EX/MEM pipeline register (and MEM/WB register) directly back to the input of the ALU. This routes the freshly computed result to the *next* instruction that needs it, *without waiting for it to be written to the register file*. This eliminates the need for stalls in many RAW cases.

---

### Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Hazard Definitions and 5-Stage RISC Pipeline:**

- **Structural Hazard:** Arises when two instructions in the pipeline require the same hardware resource at the same time. Example: a single memory unit shared by instruction fetch and data memory access.
- **Control Hazard:** Arises due to branch and jump instructions. The pipeline does not know the branch target until late in execution, so wrong-path instructions may be fetched.
- **5-Stage RISC Pipeline:** (See **Section 4.1** diagram above for the visual). The five stages are **IF (Instruction Fetch), ID (Instruction Decode / Register Fetch), EX (Execute), MEM (Memory Access), WB (Write Back).** Each stage takes 1 clock cycle, and 5 pipeline latches separate them.

**Part (b) — Step-by-step Calculation:**

**Given:** Non-pipelined time per instruction = **400 ns**, `k = 5` stages, clock period $T_p$ = **80 ns**, `n = 500` instructions.

**Step 1:** Compute non-pipelined total time $T_n$:

$$
T_n = n \times 400 = 500 \times 400 = 200000 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute pipelined total time $T_{pipe}$:

$$
T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \times T_p = (5 + 500 - 1) \times 80 = 504 \times 80 = 40320 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute speedup:

$$
S = \dfrac{T_n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{200000}{40320} \approx 4.96
$$

**Step 4:** Compute efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{S}{k} = \dfrac{4.96}{5} = 0.992 = 99.2\%
$$

**Step 5:** Compute throughput (in instructions per microsecond, where 1 µs = 1000 ns):

$$
\tau = \dfrac{n}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{500}{40320} \approx 0.0124 \text{ inst/ns} = 12.4 \text{ MIPS (millions of instructions per second)}
$$

(Equivalent check: $\tau = \dfrac{1}{T_p} = \dfrac{1}{80 \text{ ns}} = 12.5$ MIPS in the limit, matching our calculation.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT forget the latch delay.** Many students use $\max(t_i)$ alone for the clock period and lose 1 mark. The correct formula is $T_p = \max(t_i) + t_{latch}$.
> 2. **Watch the units.** If $T_p$ is in ns, your throughput must be converted to instructions/second using $1 \text{ ns} = 10^{-9}$ s. Mixing ns and µs is the most common mistake.
> 3. **The "fill + drain" formula is $(k + n - 1)$**, NOT $(k + n)$ or $(k \times n)$. Examiners deduct a full mark for this.
> 4. **Distinguish speedup from efficiency** — speedup is unitless and compared to the sequential baseline, while efficiency is divided by `k`.
> 5. **Data hazard sub-types:** Many students confuse WAR and WAW. Remember the mnemonic: **"Read-After-Write" → result must come first**, "Write-After-Read" → read must come first, "Write-After-Write" → final writer must come last.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pipelining — Module 3):**
> - **Definition:** Pipelining overlaps the execution of multiple instructions across `k` dedicated stages to increase instruction throughput.
> - **Pipeline Time Formula:** $T_{pipe} = (k + n - 1) \cdot T_p$ — first instruction takes `k` cycles (fill), then one instruction per cycle (drain).
> - **Sequential Time Formula:** $T_n = n \times k \times T_p$ — every instruction must finish all `k` stages before the next begins.
> - **Speedup:** $S = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{k + n - 1}$, with a hard ceiling of $S_{max} = k$ as $n \to \infty$.
> - **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{n}{k + n - 1}$, always between 0 and 1.
> - **Throughput (steady state):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_p}$ instructions per second.
> - **Clock Period:** $T_p = \max(\text{stage delay}) + t_{latch}$ — the slowest stage dictates the clock.
> - **Three Hazard Classes:** Structural (resource), Data (RAW / WAR / WAW), Control (branch).
> - **Hazard Solutions:** Resource duplication, data forwarding/bypassing, branch prediction, delayed branch, compiler instruction scheduling.
> - **5-Stage RISC Pipeline:** IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB (MIPS classic).
> - **Arithmetic Pipeline:** Used for FP multiply/divide; sub-operations (exponent compare, mantissa multiply, normalize) form stages.
> - **Real Numbers:** Modern x86 cores have 14-20+ stage pipelines; ARM Cortex-A series has 10-14 stages.
> - **Rule of Thumb:** Deep pipelines give higher clock rates but worsen branch misprediction penalties (more wasted work on wrong path).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pipelined Datapath and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pipelined Datapath and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **pipelined datapath** is a processor implementation technique that overlaps the execution of multiple instructions by partitioning the instruction processing into discrete stages separated by **pipeline registers**. Each stage performs a portion of the instruction's work, and at every clock cycle, a new instruction enters the pipeline while previously entered instructions advance to subsequent stages. The **pipelined control unit** generates and propagates control signals alongside the instruction data through pipeline registers, ensuring each stage receives the correct control inputs every cycle.

In the canonical **5-stage MIPS pipeline**, the stages are:
- **IF** — Instruction Fetch
- **ID** — Instruction Decode / Register Read
- **EX** — Execute / Address Calculation
- **MEM** — Memory Access
- **WB** — Write Back

The datapath is sliced between every pair of adjacent stages by a **pipeline register**: `IF/ID`, `ID/EX`, `EX/MEM`, `MEM/WB`. Each pipeline register holds the intermediate values and control signals needed by the next stage for **one clock cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The textbook of reference is *Patterson & Hennessy — Computer Organization and Design (MIPS Edition)*. The 5-stage pipeline datapath, pipeline register propagation, and per-stage control signal grouping are **direct board-exam topics** worth high marks in Part B (14 marks) questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Laundry Line

Imagine a student doing laundry with **five distinct tasks**:
1. **Place clothes in the washer** (30 min)
2. **Move clothes to the dryer** (30 min)
3. **Fold the clothes** (30 min)
4. **Put away the clothes** (30 min)
5. **Take a break** (30 min)

**Non-pipelined (sequential) approach:** Each load must finish all five tasks before the next load starts. With 4 loads, total time = 4 × 150 min = **600 min**.

**Pipelined approach:** As soon as the washer finishes load 1, the student places load 2 inside, and load 1 moves to the dryer. Now BOTH washer and dryer are doing useful work simultaneously.

Total time for 4 pipelined loads = (5 + 4 − 1) × 30 min = **240 min**.

The **clock cycle** of the pipeline is set by the **slowest stage** (washer/dryer/folder = 30 min each). The **pipeline registers** are like the laundry baskets holding clothes between stages. The student is the **control unit** deciding where each basket moves next.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Pipelining does NOT reduce the latency of a *single* instruction. It improves **throughput** — the number of instructions completed per unit time.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Ideal CPI (Cycles Per Instruction)** for a pipelined processor = **1**
- **Ideal Speedup** = **k**, where k is the number of pipeline stages
- **Pipeline register setup time** is typically **negligible** but is added to the clock cycle in rigorous analysis
- **Clock cycle of pipeline** $T_{clk}$ = $\max(T_{IF}, T_{ID}, T_{EX}, T_{MEM}, T_{WB}) + T_{setup}$ (where $T_{setup}$ is the pipeline register setup time)
- **MIPS** = (Clock Rate in Hz) / ($10^6$ × CPI)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown — Why Five Stages?

The MIPS 5-stage partition is chosen because each stage corresponds to a roughly **balanced** functional unit:

| Stage | Functional Unit Used | Operations |
|---|---|---|
| **IF** | Instruction Memory + PC adder | Fetch instruction, compute PC + 4 |
| **ID** | Register File + Sign-extend | Decode opcode, read registers, extend immediate |
| **EX** | ALU | R-type operation, branch target / jump target compute |
| **MEM** | Data Memory | Load/Store word; otherwise no-op |
| **WB** | Register File | Write ALU result or memory data to destination register |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balancing the stages** is the single most important design goal. The slowest stage dictates the **clock period**. Unequal stage delays cause **stalls** and reduce throughput.

### 2.2 Pipeline Registers — The "State Buffers"

Between every pair of adjacent stages we insert a **pipeline register**. These registers are the *only* mechanism by which a value moves from stage *i* to stage *i+1*.

| Register | Holds Between | Captures |
|---|---|---|
| **IF/ID** | IF → ID | Instruction (32 bits), PC + 4 |
| **ID/EX** | ID → EX | Register read data 1 & 2, sign-extended immediate, Rs, Rt, Rd, RegDst control, PC + 4 (for branches) |
| **EX/MEM** | EX → MEM | ALU result, data to write, destination register number, Zero flag, Branch/Jump control, PC + 4 (for jumps) |
| **MEM/WB** | MEM → WB | Memory read data, ALU result, destination register number, RegWrite, MemtoReg |

> [!NOTE]
> A pipeline register is essentially a **wide D-flip-flop bank** that latches all the data and control signals on the rising edge of the clock. Its **setup time** plus the **clock-to-Q delay** determines the minimum clock period of the pipeline.

### 2.3 Pipelined Control — Propagation Pattern

The original single-cycle control unit generates **9 control signals**:
`RegDst, ALUSrc, MemRead, MemWrite, Branch, MemtoReg, RegWrite, ALUOp (2 bits)`.

In the pipelined version, **control signals are generated in the ID stage** and then propagated forward through the pipeline registers:

- **IF/ID register** carries only the instruction bits; control is decoded in ID.
- **ID/EX register** carries **all 9 control signals** (forwarded into EX).
- **EX/MEM register** carries only the **4 MEM/WB-stage relevant** signals: `Branch, MemRead, MemWrite, RegWrite`, plus `Zero, ALU result, WriteData, WriteReg`.
- **MEM/WB register** carries the **3 WB-stage signals**: `RegWrite, MemtoReg`, plus `ReadData, ALU result, WriteReg`.

This **stage-wise grouping** ensures that each pipeline stage sees only the control bits it actually consumes, simplifying the hardware.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{non-pipe} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{stage}$ | Non-pipelined execution time for n instructions, k stages | seconds |
| 2 | $T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$ | Pipelined execution time for n instructions | seconds |
| 3 | $T_{clk} = \max(T_{IF}, T_{ID}, T_{EX}, T_{MEM}, T_{WB}) + T_{setup}$ | Pipeline clock period | seconds |
| 4 | $S = \dfrac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ | Speedup of pipeline | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\lim_{n \to \infty} S = k$ | Maximum (ideal) speedup | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{n}{(n+k-1) \cdot T_{clk}}$ | Instructions completed per second | inst/s |
| 7 | $\text{CPI}_{pipe} = 1 + \text{stalls per instruction}$ | Effective CPI including hazards | cycles/instr |
| 8 | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 9 | $\text{Latency} = k \cdot T_{clk}$ | Time to complete ONE instruction end-to-end | seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students write the speedup as $k$ alone. The correct **speedup equation is** $\frac{n \cdot k}{n+k-1}$ **for a finite** $n$. Only in the asymptotic limit does it equal $k$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pipelined datapaths are the **backbone of every modern processor** — from the Apple M-series chips and Intel/AMD CPUs to embedded ARM Cortex cores. The principles taught here directly scale up to:
- **Superscalar pipelines** that issue >1 instruction per cycle
- **Deeply pipelined designs** (Intel Pentium 4 had 20+ stages; AMD Zen 5 has 19+ stages)
- **Out-of-order execution engines** that re-order instructions to avoid stalls
- **GPU SIMT pipelines** with thousands of lanes operating in lockstep

Mastery of the 5-stage MIPS pipeline is a **mandatory prerequisite** for studying advanced architecture in higher semesters and competitive exams like GATE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Pipeline Speedup Equation

**Goal:** Derive the speedup of a k-stage pipelined processor over an equivalent non-pipelined processor for n instructions.

#### Step 1: Non-Pipelined Execution Time

In a non-pipelined (single-cycle) processor, one instruction must complete *all* k stages before the next instruction begins.

$$T_{non-pipe} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{stage}$$

where:
- $n$ = number of instructions
- $k$ = number of stages
- $T_{stage}$ = average time per stage (assumed equal for simplicity)

#### Step 2: Pipelined Execution Time

In a pipelined processor, the *first* instruction takes $k \cdot T_{clk}$ cycles to finish, and each subsequent instruction completes **one cycle** later. So the last of n instructions finishes at cycle $(k + n - 1)$.

$$T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$$

#### Step 3: Relating Stage Time to Clock Time

Since the clock period must be at least the slowest stage plus the pipeline register setup:

$$T_{clk} \approx T_{stage}$$

#### Step 4: Compute Speedup

$$S = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{stage}}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_{clk}}$$

Substituting $T_{clk} \approx T_{stage}$:

$$S = \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$$

#### Step 5: Asymptotic Limit

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k}{1 + \frac{k-1}{n}} = k$$

Hence the **theoretical maximum speedup is k** for an ideal k-stage pipeline.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Speedup Calculation

> **Problem:** A processor has a 5-stage pipeline with stage delays (in ns): IF = 2, ID = 1.5, EX = 2, MEM = 2.5, WB = 1. Pipeline register setup = 0.5 ns. Compute: (i) clock period, (ii) speedup over non-pipelined design for n = 1000 instructions.

#### (i) Clock Period

The clock period must be at least the **slowest stage** plus the **setup time**:

$$T_{clk} = \max(2,\, 1.5,\, 2,\, 2.5,\, 1) + 0.5$$

$$T_{clk} = 2.5 + 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ ns}$$

The slowest stage is **MEM** at 2.5 ns.

#### (ii) Speedup for n = 1000

Non-pipelined: The single-cycle time equals the *sum* of all stage delays plus setup overhead. For fair comparison, assume the non-pipelined clock period = sum of stage delays + 0 (no pipeline registers needed, but conservative fair comparison uses sum = 9.5 ns):

$$T_{non-pipe} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{single} = 1000 \cdot 5 \cdot 9.5 = 47\,500 \text{ ns}$$

Pipelined:

$$T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_{clk} = (1000 + 5 - 1) \cdot 3.0 = 1004 \cdot 3.0 = 3012 \text{ ns}$$

Speedup:

$$S = \frac{47\,500}{3012} \approx 15.77$$

Compare with theoretical maximum k = 5. The actual speedup (15.77×) is **much greater than 5** because the non-pipelined clock period is 9.5 ns while the pipelined clock is only 3 ns. This highlights that **pipelining reduces the clock period** in addition to overlapping execution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the comparison is done with the *same* clock period of 3 ns for both, then $T_{non-pipe} = 1000 \cdot 5 \cdot 3 = 15\,000$ ns, giving $S = 15\,000 / 3012 \approx 4.98 \approx 5$. This is the **standard fair comparison** for board exams.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Signal Flow

| Control Signal | Generated In | Propagated Through | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| RegDst | ID | ID/EX | EX (selects Rd or Rt as WriteReg) |
| ALUSrc | ID | ID/EX | EX (selects register or immediate for ALU B-input) |
| ALUOp (2 bits) | ID | ID/EX | EX (drives ALU control unit) |
| Branch | ID | ID/EX → EX/MEM | EX (drives PCSrc for branches) |
| MemRead | ID | ID/EX → EX/MEM | MEM (enables data memory read) |
| MemWrite | ID | ID/EX → EX/MEM | MEM (enables data memory write) |
| RegWrite | ID | ID/EX → EX/MEM → MEM/WB | WB (enables register file write) |
| MemtoReg | ID | ID/EX → EX/MEM → MEM/WB | WB (selects ALU result or memory data) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** Control signals are generated **once per instruction**, in the **ID stage**, and then ride along with the data through successive pipeline registers. This avoids duplicating the (expensive) main control decoder at every stage.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — 5-Stage Pipelined Datapath Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional block-level flow** of a 5-stage MIPS pipelined datapath, with pipeline registers separating each stage and the control decoder feeding forward through the ID stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC[PC Program Counter] --> IMEM[IF Instruction Memory]
    IMEM --> IFIDReg[IF ID Pipeline Register]
    PC --> ADD4[PC Plus 4 Adder]
    ADD4 --> IFIDReg
    IFIDReg --> REGF[ID Register File]
    IFIDReg --> SEXT[ID Sign Extend Unit]
    IFIDReg --> CTRLDEC[ID Main Control Decoder]
    SEXT --> IDEXReg[ID EX Pipeline Register]
    REGF --> IDEXReg
    CTRLDEC --> IDEXReg
    IDEXReg --> ALU[EX ALU]
    IDEXReg --> EXMEMReg[EX MEM Pipeline Register]
    ALU --> EXMEMReg
    EXMEMReg --> DMEM[MEM Data Memory]
    EXMEMReg --> MEMWBReg[MEM WB Pipeline Register]
    DMEM --> MEMWBReg
    MEMWBReg --> REGF
```

**Legend & Reading Guide:**
- Each box is a hardware functional unit.
- Each `xxxReg` block is a **pipeline register** — a wide D-flip-flop bank.
- Arrows show the data movement direction in a single clock cycle.
- `CTRLDEC` outputs are propagated **forward** through `IDEXReg → EXMEMReg → MEMWBReg`.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Instruction Traversal Through Pipeline (lw instruction)

This sequence diagram illustrates how a single `lw $t1, 4($t2)` instruction **travels through the five stages** over five consecutive clock cycles (CC1 through CC5), while a second instruction (`add $t3, $t1, $t4`) enters the pipeline.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CC1 as Cycle 1 IF
    participant CC2 as Cycle 2 ID
    participant CC3 as Cycle 3 EX
    participant CC4 as Cycle 4 MEM
    participant CC5 as Cycle 5 WB
    Note over CC1: lw enters IF fetches instruction
    Note over CC2: lw moves to ID reads $t2 generates control signals
    Note over CC1: add instruction enters IF
    Note over CC3: lw computes address 4 plus $t2 in EX
    Note over CC2: add moves to ID reads registers
    Note over CC1: third instruction enters IF
    Note over CC4: lw reads memory in MEM
    Note over CC3: add computes sum in EX
    Note over CC5: lw writes $t1 in WB
    Note over CC4: add traverses MEM no op
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Reading a row top-to-bottom shows the **state of the pipeline at one cycle**. Reading a column left-to-right shows **the lifecycle of one instruction** over time. The pipeline becomes "full" at cycle 5, after which one instruction completes per cycle.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Functional Block — Pipeline Register Contents Table

This matrix visualizes the **information stored in each pipeline register** between two consecutive stages:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IFIDREG[IF ID Register]
        A1[Instruction 32 bits]
        A2[PC plus 4 32 bits]
    end
    subgraph IDEXREG[ID EX Register]
        B1[ReadData1 32 bits]
        B2[ReadData2 32 bits]
        B3[Sign Extended Immediate 32 bits]
        B4[Rs 5 bits]
        B5[Rt 5 bits]
        B6[Rd 5 bits]
        B7[PC plus 4 for branches]
        B8[RegDst control]
        B9[ALUSrc control]
        B10[ALUOp 2 bits]
    end
    subgraph EXMEMREG[EX MEM Register]
        C1[ALUResult 32 bits]
        C2[WriteData 32 bits]
        C3[WriteReg 5 bits]
        C4[Zero flag 1 bit]
        C5[Branch control]
        C6[MemRead control]
        C7[MemWrite control]
        C8[RegWrite control]
    end
    subgraph MEMWBREG[MEM WB Register]
        D1[ReadData 32 bits]
        D2[ALUResult 32 bits]
        D3[WriteReg 5 bits]
        D4[MemtoReg control]
        D5[RegWrite control]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "what does the EX/MEM register contain?", list **exactly** these 8 items. Examiners specifically check for the **Zero flag**, the **WriteData** (data to be written to memory), and the **Branch/MemRead/MemWrite/RegWrite** controls — these are the most-forgotten items.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each, 3-mark module weightage)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define a pipeline register. List all four pipeline registers used in the 5-stage MIPS pipeline along with the values they store.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **pipeline register** is a wide bank of D flip-flops inserted between two adjacent stages of a pipelined processor to hold the intermediate data and control signals needed by the next stage for one clock cycle.

The four pipeline registers in the 5-stage MIPS pipeline are:

| Register | Captures |
|---|---|
| **IF/ID** | Instruction (32 bits), PC + 4 (32 bits) |
| **ID/EX** | ReadData1, ReadData2, SignExtImm, Rs, Rt, Rd, PC+4, plus RegDst, ALUSrc, ALUOp |
| **EX/MEM** | ALUResult, WriteData, WriteReg, Zero, Branch, MemRead, MemWrite, RegWrite |
| **MEM/WB** | ReadData, ALUResult, WriteReg, MemtoReg, RegWrite |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four registers with contents — full 3 marks.)*

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: In the pipelined datapath, why are control signals generated only in the ID stage and not in every stage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Control signals are generated only in the ID stage for the following reasons:

1. **Single point of decoding:** The instruction opcode fields are needed only for decoding, and these fields are available right after the IF stage in ID. Generating control earlier is impossible (we don't yet have the opcode), and generating later is wasteful (3 out of 5 stages would still be waiting).
2. **Forward propagation:** Once generated, the control signals are stored in the pipeline registers and **carried forward** to the EX, MEM, and WB stages, where they are consumed.
3. **Hardware economy:** A single main control decoder is reused once per instruction, rather than being duplicated at every stage — saving silicon area and power.

*(Each point 1 mark — full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice: Answer A OR B) — 14 marks each

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3]`
**(a)** Draw and explain the 5-stage pipelined datapath showing all the pipeline registers between stages. Clearly mark the inputs and outputs of each pipeline register. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain how control signals are generated in the ID stage and propagated to subsequent stages. Provide a table mapping each of the 9 control signals to the stage(s) where it is consumed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer A(a) — 7 marks:**

The 5-stage pipelined datapath consists of the following functional units separated by pipeline registers:

- **IF Stage:** Instruction Memory + PC adder (PC + 4). Output latched into **IF/ID register**.
- **ID Stage:** Register File (Read), Sign-Extend unit, Main Control Decoder. Outputs latched into **ID/EX register**.
- **EX Stage:** ALU, Adder for branch target. Outputs latched into **EX/MEM register**.
- **MEM Stage:** Data Memory. Outputs latched into **MEM/WB register**.
- **WB Stage:** Mux to select between ALU result and memory read data; written into Register File.

The **IF/ID register** carries: `{Instruction[31:0], PC+4}`.

The **ID/EX register** carries: `{ReadData1, ReadData2, SignExtImm, Rs, Rt, Rd, PC+4, RegDst, ALUSrc, ALUOp[1:0]}`.

The **EX/MEM register** carries: `{ALUResult, WriteData, WriteReg, Zero, Branch, MemRead, MemWrite, RegWrite}`.

The **MEM/WB register** carries: `{ReadData, ALUResult, WriteReg, MemtoReg, RegWrite}`.

*(2 marks for naming 5 stages, 3 marks for listing pipeline register contents, 2 marks for correct flow explanation — full 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Model Answer A(b) — 7 marks:**

Control signals are decoded **only in the ID stage** from the opcode and funct fields of the fetched instruction. The 9 control signals are: `RegDst, ALUSrc, ALUOp[1:0], Branch, MemRead, MemWrite, MemtoReg, RegWrite`.

| Signal | Used In Stage |
|---|---|
| RegDst | EX (chooses WriteReg) |
| ALUSrc | EX (selects ALU 2nd input) |
| ALUOp[1:0] | EX (drives ALU's internal control) |
| Branch | EX (drives PCSrc with Zero flag) |
| MemRead | MEM (enables data memory read) |
| MemWrite | MEM (enables data memory write) |
| MemtoReg | WB (selects ALU result or memory data) |
| RegWrite | WB (enables register write) |

**Propagation path:**
1. Generated in ID and latched into **ID/EX register**.
2. From ID/EX, the MEM/WB-relevant signals (`Branch, MemRead, MemWrite, RegWrite`) are forwarded to **EX/MEM**.
3. From EX/MEM, the WB-relevant signals (`RegWrite, MemtoReg`) are forwarded to **MEM/WB**.

This staggered propagation minimizes wiring and keeps each stage's input local.

*(3 marks for table mapping, 2 marks for propagation path, 2 marks for staggered/progressive forwarding — full 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3]`
**(a)** Derive the speedup formula $S = \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ for an ideal k-stage pipeline processing n instructions. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a non-pipelined processor that takes **8 ns** per instruction. A pipelined version of the same processor has 5 stages with stage delays (in ns): IF = 1, ID = 1.5, EX = 2, MEM = 2.5, WB = 1. Pipeline register delay is **0.2 ns**. Calculate: (i) the clock cycle time of the pipelined processor, (ii) the speedup for n = 100 instructions, and (iii) the throughput in MIPS for the pipelined design. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Assumptions:**
1. All k stages have equal delay $T_{stage}$.
2. Pipeline register overhead is negligible.
3. No structural, data, or control hazards occur (ideal pipeline).

**Derivation:**

Let n = number of instructions, k = number of pipeline stages, $T_{stage}$ = time per stage.

**Step 1: Non-pipelined time.** Each instruction takes $k \cdot T_{stage}$ to complete. For n instructions executed sequentially:

$$T_{non-pipe} = n \cdot k \cdot T_{stage}$$

**Step 2: Pipelined time.** The clock cycle $T_{clk} = T_{stage}$. The first instruction takes k cycles; each subsequent instruction completes 1 cycle later. The last instruction finishes at cycle $(k + n - 1)$:

$$T_{pipe} = (n + k - 1) \cdot T_{stage}$$

**Step 3: Speedup ratio.**

$$S = \frac{T_{non-pipe}}{T_{pipe}} = \frac{n \cdot k \cdot T_{stage}}{(n + k - 1) \cdot T_{stage}} = \frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$$

**Step 4: Asymptotic limit.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} S = k$$

This means the **ideal maximum speedup** is k, achieved only as $n \to \infty$.

*(1 mark for assumptions, 2 marks for $T_{non-pipe}$, 2 marks for $T_{pipe}$, 1 mark for ratio, 1 mark for limit — full 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Model Answer B(b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Clock cycle time of pipelined processor:**

The slowest stage dictates the clock period, plus pipeline register delay:

$$T_{clk} = \max(1,\, 1.5,\, 2,\, 2.5,\, 1) + 0.2$$

$$T_{clk} = 2.5 + 0.2 = 2.7 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Speedup for n = 100 instructions:**

For fair comparison, both processors use the same *per-stage* time. The non-pipelined processor's clock period for a single instruction equals the sum of stage delays + 0 register overhead:

$$T_{single,non-pipe} = 1 + 1.5 + 2 + 2.5 + 1 = 8 \text{ ns}$$

(This matches the 8 ns given.)

$$T_{non-pipe} = 100 \cdot 8 = 800 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{pipe} = (100 + 5 - 1) \cdot 2.7 = 104 \cdot 2.7 = 280.8 \text{ ns}$$

$$S = \frac{800}{280.8} \approx 2.85$$

**(iii) Throughput in MIPS:**

$$\text{CPI}_{pipe} = 1 \quad \text{(ideal pipeline)}$$

$$f_{clk} = \frac{1}{T_{clk}} = \frac{1}{2.7 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 370.37 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{clk}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \frac{370.37 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 10^{6}} \approx 370.37 \text{ MIPS}$$

*(2 marks for (i), 3 marks for (ii), 2 marks for (iii) — full 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in clock cycle formula:** Many students write $T_{pipe} = n \cdot T_{clk}$ — this is WRONG. It should be $(n + k - 1) \cdot T_{clk}$.
> 2. **Forgetting the pipeline register delay:** When adding it to the clock cycle, some students omit it entirely. Always include $T_{setup}$ in the clock period.
> 3. **Confusing EX/MEM register contents:** The most commonly missed item is the **Zero flag** (used for branches). Examiners allocate 0.5 marks for this item alone.
> 4. **Wrong Clock Cycle formula for non-pipelined comparison:** The non-pipelined clock period for fair comparison equals the **sum of all stage delays** of the pipelined version (not the slowest stage). Otherwise, you're comparing apples to oranges.
> 5. **Mis-stating ideal speedup:** The speedup is $k$ *only* in the asymptotic limit $n \to \infty$. For finite $n$, it is strictly less than $k$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **pipeline register** is a D-flip-flop bank between two adjacent stages — it is the *only* mechanism by which data and control move from one stage to the next.
- ✅ The MIPS pipeline has **5 stages**: **IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB** and **4 pipeline registers**: **IF/ID, ID/EX, EX/MEM, MEM/WB**.
- ✅ Control signals are generated **once in the ID stage** and propagated forward through the pipeline registers.
- ✅ **Three forward-propagating control groups:** EX-stage group (RegDst, ALUSrc, ALUOp), MEM-stage group (Branch, MemRead, MemWrite, RegWrite), WB-stage group (MemtoReg, RegWrite).
- ✅ **Ideal CPI** of a pipelined processor = **1**. **Maximum (ideal) speedup** = **k**, the number of stages.
- ✅ **Pipeline clock period** = slowest stage delay + pipeline register setup time.
- ✅ **Pipelining improves throughput, NOT latency** of a single instruction.
- ✅ Speedup for finite n = $\frac{n \cdot k}{n + k - 1}$ (asymptotic limit = k).
- ✅ EX/MEM register carries the **Zero flag** for branches — easy to forget in exams.
- ✅ Hazards (structural, data, control) are NOT eliminated by pipelining — they cause stalls that reduce effective speedup below the ideal k. *(Briefly noted here; covered in detail in the next module topic.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling Data Hazards and Control Hazards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Handling Data Hazards and Control Hazards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 3 — HANDLING DATA HAZARDS AND CONTROL HAZARDS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Pipeline Hazard?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Pipeline Hazard** is formally defined as any condition in a pipelined processor that prevents the next instruction in the instruction stream from executing in its designated clock cycle, thereby **stalling the pipeline** or **invalidating pipeline stages**.

The three classical categories of hazards as per Hennessy & Patterson's taxonomy (which is the standard reference for KTU board valuation) are:

1. **Structural Hazards** — Hardware resource conflict (e.g., single memory port serving both instruction fetch and data access).
2. **Data Hazards** — Dependency between instructions involving shared data.
3. **Control Hazards** — Branches and jumps that disrupt the sequential flow of fetched instructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT418 Module 3):** The syllabus explicitly focuses on the **mitigation strategies** for Data and Control Hazards. Structural hazards are typically resolved at the design stage (resource duplication) and are out of scope for this topic.

---

### 1.2 Data Hazard — Formal Definition

A **Data Hazard** occurs when the pipelined execution of instructions would access an **operand in a wrong order** due to overlapping pipeline stages, even though the program's sequential semantics demand a specific order.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "A data hazard exists when there is a dependence in the program source between two instructions and they are close enough that the overlap of their execution in the pipeline would change the order of access to the involved operands."

### 1.3 Control Hazard — Formal Definition

A **Control Hazard** is the inability of the pipeline to correctly identify the next instruction to fetch because the outcome of a branch instruction (taken or not-taken) is not known until the branch is evaluated deep inside the pipeline.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition:** "A control hazard arises from the pipelining of branches and other control-flow instructions where the decision of where to fetch next is not known until later stages of the pipeline."

---

### 1.4 🔑 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy: The Factory Assembly Line**

Imagine a car manufacturing assembly line where **three stations** — Chassis, Painting, and Wheels — process a car in parallel.

- **Data Hazard Analogy:** Suppose a worker at the **Wheels station** needs the colour code assigned by the **Painting station** of the *previous* car. If the previous car's painting is still incomplete, the Wheels station must **stall (wait)** or must rely on a **"forwarding belt"** that passes the colour directly from Painting to Wheels without waiting for it to leave the factory.
- **Control Hazard Analogy:** A supervisor (the **branch instruction**) at the Painting station decides whether to paint the next car **red or blue** only *after* painting is done. The Wheels station has already begun work on the *wrong assumption* (red) — leading to **wasted work (flushing the pipeline)** or a **stall**.

In both cases, the assembly line (pipeline) loses rhythm — these lost cycles are precisely the **hazard penalties** that KTU examiners love to quantify.

---

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in KTU Board Examinations

- **Default MIPS 5-stage pipeline stages:** IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB (each = **1 clock cycle**).
- **Default CPI without hazards:** 1 (ideal).
- **Default branch outcome resolution stage:** EX (MIPS standard) or MEM.
- **Branch frequency in typical MIPS programs:** ~**15–25%**.
- **Load-use hazard stall cost:** Exactly **1 bubble cycle**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
