### 2.1 Taxonomy of Data Hazards

The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to distinguish the three types rigorously. Consider two instructions `i` and `j` (where `i` comes before `j`):

#### 2.1.1 RAW — Read After Write (True Data Dependency)

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Type** | True dependence; program-order semantic |
| **Cause** | `j` reads a register that `i` is supposed to write |
| **Order Required** | `i` writes **before** `j` reads |
| **Pipeline Impact** | Causes **stalls** if not resolved by forwarding |
| **KTU Weight** | Most frequently tested |

**Example (MIPS):**
```text
ADD R1, R2, R3      ; i: writes R1
SUB R4, R1, R5      ; j: reads R1
```

---

#### 2.1.2 WAR — Write After Read (Anti-Dependency / Name Dependency)

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Name dependency; introduced by register reuse |
| **Cause** | `j` writes a register that `i` is supposed to read |
| **Order Required** | `i` reads **before** `j` writes |
| **Pipeline Impact** | Cannot occur in in-order issue, simple 5-stage MIPS |
| **KTU Weight** | Rarely tested in basic boards |

**Example:**
```text
ADD R1, R2, R3      ; i: reads R2
SUB R2, R4, R5      ; j: writes R2
```

---

#### 2.1.3 WAW — Write After Write (Output Dependency / Name Dependency)

| Attribute | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Type** | Name dependency; two instructions write same register |
| **Cause** | `i` writes register, `j` also writes register |
| **Order Required** | `i`'s write must complete **before** `j`'s write |
| **Pipeline Impact** | Cannot occur in simple scalar MIPS, appears in VLIW/Superscalar |

**Example:**
```text
ADD R1, R2, R3      ; i: writes R1
SUB R1, R4, R5      ; j: writes R1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Fact:** Only **RAW** hazards manifest in a classical 5-stage MIPS pipeline with in-order issue. WAR and WAW appear in **out-of-order execution** (which is beyond PERUT418 scope).

---

### 2.2 Data Hazard Resolution Techniques (KTU Favourite)

#### 2.2.1 Hardware Stalling (Pipeline Interlock / Bubbles)

A **hazard detection unit** in the ID stage detects RAW hazards and inserts a **bubble** (NOP) into the pipeline, freezing the PC and IF/ID register.

#### 2.2.2 Data Forwarding (Bypassing) — ⭐ KTU Most Tested

Forwarding routes the computed result **directly from a later pipeline stage back to an earlier stage** without waiting for the WB stage.

| Forwarding Path | Source | Destination | Hazard Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EX → EX** | ALU output of EX/MEM latch | ALU input of EX stage | 1-cycle gap dependency |
| **MEM → EX** | ALU output of MEM/WB latch | ALU input of EX stage | 2-cycle gap dependency |
| **MEM → MEM** | Data from MEM stage | Store data in EX | Store-to-Load dependency |
| **WB → ID** | WB data via register file write | Operand read in ID | Legacy; rarely used now |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question:** *"Can forwarding eliminate ALL data hazards?"* — **NO!** Forwarding cannot eliminate the **load-use hazard**, because the value to be forwarded is not yet available when it is needed (memory access happens in MEM stage, but EX stage needs it). This requires **1 stall cycle**.

---

### 2.3 Control Hazard Resolution Techniques (KTU Favourite)

#### 2.3.1 Stall until branch is resolved (Simplest)
- Insert **N** bubbles equal to the number of stages between IF and branch resolution.
- **Branch Penalty** = N clock cycles per branch.

#### 2.3.2 Predict Not-Taken (Static)
- Treat every branch as **not-taken**.
- Continue fetching sequentially.
- Flush only if branch is taken (1–2 cycle penalty).
- Achieves correct prediction ~60–70% of the time.

#### 2.3.3 Predict Taken (Static)
- Useful for **loop-back branches**.
- Loop branches are taken ~90% of the time.

#### 2.3.4 Delayed Branch (Compiler-Scheduled)
- The instruction **immediately following the branch** is the **delay slot**, which **always executes regardless of branch direction**.
- The compiler's job is to **fill** this slot with a useful instruction.

#### 2.3.5 Branch Target Buffer (BTB) — ⭐ KTU Advanced Topic
A small **cache** indexed by PC that stores:
- **Predicted PC** (branch target address)
- **Prediction bit** (taken/not-taken history)
- **Valid bit**

If PC hit → use predicted target. Misprediction → flush pipeline.

#### 2.3.6 Dynamic Branch Prediction — 2-Bit Saturating Counter
- Uses **2 bits** per branch to track history.
- State transitions: Strongly Taken → Weakly Taken → Weakly Not-Taken → Strongly Not-Taken.
- **Threshold-based**: only switch prediction after 2 consecutive mispredictions.

---

### 2.4 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Definition | Formula / Expression | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}}$ | Effective CPI with hazards | $\text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \sum (\text{Stall}_i \times \text{Freq}_i)$ | cycles/instruction |
| $\text{Speedup}_{\text{pipelined}}$ | Speedup vs. unpipelined | $\dfrac{\text{CPI}_{\text{unpipe}} \times T}{\text{CPI}_{\text{pipe}} \times T}$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{Speedup}_{\text{ideal}}$ | Ideal speedup (no hazards) | $\dfrac{n \times k}{n + k - 1} \approx k \text{ for large } n$ | dimensionless |
| $\text{BranchPenalty}$ | Stall cycles per branch | $\text{ResolutionStage} - \text{FetchStage} + \text{MispredictionCost}$ | clock cycles |
| $\text{EffectiveCPI}_{\text{branch}}$ | CPI contribution of branches | $f_{\text{branch}} \times P_{\text{branch}}$ | cycles/instruction |
| $\text{Amdahl}_{\text{hazard}}$ | Fractional stall cost | $1 + \text{stall\_cycles\_per\_instruction}$ | dimensionless |
| $f_{\text{branch}}$ | Branch frequency | $\dfrac{\text{Branch Instructions}}{\text{Total Instructions}}$ | $\in [0, 1]$ |
| $\text{FlushCycles}$ | Cycles lost on misprediction | $\text{BranchPenalty} - 1$ | clock cycles |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
> - **Ideal CPI** = **1** (after pipelining without hazards).
> - **Branch resolution in EX stage with no forwarding & no prediction** → Branch Penalty = **2 cycles** (IF + ID bubble for the branch).
> - **Load-use hazard** → ALWAYS incurs exactly **1 stall cycle** even with full forwarding.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These hazard handling techniques are **not academic abstractions**. They form the core of every commercial processor:

- **Forwarding:** Present in every Intel Core i-series, AMD Ryzen, and ARM Cortex-A cores.
- **BTB:** Modern Intel Golden Cove architecture uses a multi-level BTB with thousands of entries.
- **2-bit saturating counters:** Used in Pentium 4, AMD K8, ARM Cortex-A77, Apple M1.
- **Delayed branches:** Used in early MIPS R2000/R3000 RISC processors and still in some DSP architectures (TI TMS320).
- **Return Address Stack:** Found in all modern out-of-order processors (Intel, AMD, Apple) to predict `RET` instructions accurately.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: CPI with Data Hazard Stalls (Generic Formula)

Consider a benchmark with the following instruction mix (typical MIPS benchmark):

Let the total instruction count be $I$ instructions. Suppose there are $f_L$ fraction of **load instructions**, and a fraction $p_{LU}$ of those loads are immediately followed by an instruction that uses the loaded value (creating a **load-use hazard**).

**Step 1: Identify the stall source.**

Each load-use hazard causes exactly **1 bubble** in the pipeline.

**Step 2: Calculate total stall cycles per instruction.**

$$
\text{Stalls per instruction} = f_L \times p_{LU} \times 1
$$

**Step 3: Add to the base CPI.**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + f_L \times p_{LU}
$$

**Step 4: Convert to total execution cycles.**

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = I \times \text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} \times T_{\text{clk}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Branch Penalty with Static "Predict Not-Taken"

**Given:**
- Branch resolution occurs in **EX stage** (cycle 3).
- IF takes 1 cycle (cycle 1).
- Branch frequency $f_b = 0.20$.
- Branch taken probability $p_t = 0.60$.

**Step 1: Determine penalty per branch.**

When branch is taken, the pipeline has already fetched **2 instructions** (in IF and ID stages) that must be discarded. Therefore:

$$
\text{Penalty}_{\text{taken}} = 2 \text{ cycles}
$$

When branch is not-taken, the speculation was correct → penalty = **0 cycles**.

**Step 2: Average penalty per branch instruction.**

$$
\text{AvgPenalty} = p_t \times \text{Penalty}_{\text{taken}} + (1 - p_t) \times 0
$$

$$
\text{AvgPenalty} = 0.60 \times 2 + 0.40 \times 0 = 1.2 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3: Add to CPI.**

$$
\Delta\text{CPI} = f_b \times \text{AvgPenalty}
$$

$$
\Delta\text{CPI} = 0.20 \times 1.2 = 0.24 \text{ cycles/instruction}
$$

**Step 4: Total CPI.**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{total}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \Delta\text{CPI} = 1 + 0.24 = 1.24
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Forwarding Eliminates EX-EX Hazards But Not Load-Use

Consider the sequence:
```text
LW   R1, 0(R2)      ; IF ID EX MEM WB
ADD  R3, R1, R4     ;    IF ID EX  MEM WB
SUB  R5, R3, R6     ;       IF ID  EX  MEM WB
```

**Step 1: Track when value is available.**

- The `LW` reads memory at end of **MEM** stage (cycle 4).
- The `ADD` needs the value at the start of **EX** stage (cycle 4) — but value only available at end of cycle 4.

**Step 2: Forwarding CANNOT resolve this 0-cycle gap.**

Therefore the hazard detection unit must **stall** the `ADD` for **1 cycle**.

**Step 3: Resulting stall cost.**

$$
\text{LoadUseStallCost} = 1 \text{ cycle per load-use pair}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Speedup with and without Hazards

**Given:**
- 5-stage pipeline, $n = 100$ instructions.
- 1 stall per data hazard (assume 20 data hazards).
- 2-cycle branch penalty (assume 15 branches, 60% taken).

**Step 1: Ideal execution (no hazards).**

$$
T_{\text{ideal}} = (n + k - 1) \times T = (100 + 5 - 1) \times T = 104\,T
$$

**Step 2: Actual execution.**

$$
\text{DataStalls} = 20 \times 1 = 20 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
\text{BranchStalls} = 15 \times 0.60 \times 2 = 18 \text{ cycles}
$$

$$
T_{\text{actual}} = 104\,T + 20\,T + 18\,T = 142\,T
$$

**Step 3: Speedup vs. unpipelined.**

Unpipelined execution: $T_{\text{unpipe}} = n \times k \times T = 100 \times 5 \times T = 500\,T$.

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{actual}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{unpipe}}}{T_{\text{actual}}} = \dfrac{500\,T}{142\,T} \approx 3.52
$$

**Step 4: Compare to ideal speedup.**

$$
\text{Speedup}_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{500\,T}{104\,T} \approx 4.81
$$

**Step 5: Efficiency loss.**

$$
\text{Efficiency} = \dfrac{\text{Speedup}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Speedup}_{\text{ideal}}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3.52}{4.81} \times 100\% \approx 73.2\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This derivation is the most asked pattern in **Part B (14-mark) questions** under the topic of pipeline performance.

---

### 3.5 📊 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Hazard Handling Techniques

| Technique | Hardware Cost | Branch Penalty | Prediction Accuracy | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stall until resolved** | Low | High (N cycles) | 100% (no speculation) | Low |
| **Predict Not-Taken** | Very Low | Moderate (1–2 if taken) | ~60–70% | High |
| **Predict Taken** | Very Low | Moderate (1–2 if not-taken) | ~85–90% for loops | Medium |
| **Delayed Branch** | Zero (compiler) | 0 (slot filled usefully) | 100% if slot filled | High |
| **BTB (1-bit)** | Medium | Near-zero on hit | ~80–85% | Medium |
| **BTB (2-bit)** | Medium | Near-zero on hit | ~90–95% | High |
| **2-bit Saturating Counter** | Low | Near-zero on hit | ~93% | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: MIPS 5-Stage Pipeline with Forwarding Paths

```mermaid
graph TD
    IF1["IF: Instruction Fetch"] --> ID1["ID: Instruction Decode Register Read"]
    ID1 --> EX1["EX: Execute Address Calculate"]
    EX1 --> MEM1["MEM: Memory Access"]
    MEM1 --> WB1["WB: Write Back"]

    EX1 -.->|"EX/MEM Forwarding (ALU result)"| EX1
    MEM1 -.->|"MEM/WB Forwarding (Memory read or ALU)"| EX1
    MEM1 -.->|"MEM to MEM Forwarding (Store data)"| EX1
    WB1 -.->|"Register Write to File"| ID1

    HDU["Hazard Detection Unit ID Stage"] -->|"Stall Signal"| IF1
    HDU -->|"Insert Bubble"| EX1
```

**Description:** This block-level flow shows how the MIPS 5-stage pipeline (IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB) is augmented with three forwarding multiplexers that route data from later stages back to the EX stage ALU inputs, and a centralized **Hazard Detection Unit** that inserts stalls when forwarding is insufficient (e.g., load-use hazards).

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Control Flow for Branch Resolution (Predict Not-Taken)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["PC points to Branch Instruction"] --> Fetch["IF: Fetch Branch"]
    Fetch --> Decode["ID: Decode and Read Registers"]
    Decode --> Compare["EX: Compare Operands Evaluate Condition"]
    Compare --> Decision{"Branch Taken?"}
    Decision -->|"No - Correct Prediction"| Continue["Continue Fetching Sequential PC Flush Nothing"]
    Decision -->|"Yes - Misprediction"| Flush["Flush IF and ID Stages Insert 2 Bubbles Update PC to Target"]
    Flush --> Continue
```

**Description:** This flow illustrates the classic predict-not-taken policy. The pipeline assumes sequential flow until the EX stage confirms otherwise. On a misprediction, the IF and ID stage contents are invalidated (flushed).

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: 2-Bit Saturating Counter State Machine

```mermaid
graph LR
    ST["Strongly Taken T"] -->|Misprediction Not Taken| WT["Weakly Taken T"]
    WT -->|Correct Taken| WT
    WT -->|Misprediction Not Taken| WN["Weakly Not Taken N"]
    WN -->|Correct Not Taken| WN
    WN -->|Misprediction Taken| WT
    SN["Strongly Not Taken N"] -->|Misprediction Taken| WN
    SN -->|Correct Not Taken| SN
```

**Description:** Each state shows the current prediction (**T** = Taken, **N** = Not Taken). The counter requires **two consecutive mispredictions** before flipping its prediction, providing robustness against single-instance noise (KTU favourite for 14-mark questions).

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Delayed Branch Scheduling

```mermaid
graph TD
    BI["Branch Instruction"] --> DS["Delay Slot Instruction Always Executes"]
    DS --> Outcome{"Branch Outcome?"}
    Outcome -->|"Taken"| Target["Branch Target"]
    Outcome -->|"Not Taken"| Next["Sequential Next Instruction"]
```

**Description:** The delay slot is an instruction that executes regardless of branch direction. The compiler attempts three scheduling strategies: (1) from before the branch, (2) from the branch target, (3) from fall-through path. The first two strategies require **nullifying** semantics in some architectures to ensure correctness when branch is taken.

---

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Branch Target Buffer Lookup

```mermaid
graph TD
    PC["Current PC"] --> Lookup["BTB Lookup Indexed by PC"]
    Lookup --> Hit{"Hit?"}
    Hit -->|"Yes"| PredBit["Read Prediction Bit 2 Bit Counter"]
    PredBit --> Action{"Predict Taken?"}
    Action -->|"Yes"| TargetPC["Fetch from Stored Target PC"]
    Action -->|"No"| SeqPC["Fetch Sequential PC"]
    Hit -->|"No"| SeqPC
    SeqPC --> Verify["Verify in EX Stage"]
    Verify --> Mispred{"Misprediction?"}
    Mispred -->|"Yes"| Update["Update BTB with Correct Target Retrain Counter"]
```

**Description:** Modern processors consult the BTB in the IF stage. On a hit, the predicted PC is fed directly to the fetch unit, paralleling the cache lookup. On a misprediction, the BTB entry is updated.

---

### 4.6 Mermaid Diagram: Hazard Detection Unit (Combinational Logic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ID1["ID Stage Instruction 1"] -->|Reads Reg rs, rt| Match1{"rs1 matches ID2 or EX2 or MEM2 destination?"}
    Match1 -->|"Yes"| Stall["Assert Stall to IF and ID Insert Bubble in EX"]
    Match1 -->|"No"| Proceed["Proceed No Stall"]

    EX1["EX Stage Instruction 2"] -->|Result at ALU output| FWDEX["Forward to EX Stage EX/MEM Latch"]
    MEM2["MEM Stage Instruction 3"] -->|Result at MEM/WB Latch| FWDMEM["Forward to EX Stage MEM/WB Latch"]

    Stall --> FWDEX
    Stall --> FWDMEM
```

**Description:** The hazard detection unit is a combinational circuit in the ID stage that compares the source registers of the current instruction with the destination registers of instructions in the EX and MEM stages, asserting stalls or controlling forwarding muxes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define data hazard. List and briefly explain the three types of data dependencies that can occur between two consecutive instructions `i` and `j` in a pipelined processor.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **data hazard** is a condition in a pipelined processor where an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that is still in the pipeline, potentially causing incorrect data access.

**Three Types of Dependencies:**
1. **RAW (Read After Write) — True Dependency:** Instruction `j` tries to read a source register before instruction `i` has written it. Most common in pipelines. Example:
   ```text
   ADD R1, R2, R3     ; i writes R1
   SUB R4, R1, R5     ; j reads R1 before i writes
   ```
2. **WAR (Write After Read) — Anti-Dependency:** Instruction `j` writes to a register that instruction `i` is supposed to read. Cannot occur in simple in-order MIPS.
3. **WAW (Write After Write) — Output Dependency:** Both instructions write to the same register; wrong-order writes would corrupt the value.

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for types with example.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *What is a control hazard? Explain the "delayed branch" technique used to mitigate control hazards.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **control hazard** arises in pipelined processors when the instruction fetch unit cannot determine the next instruction to fetch because a branch instruction's outcome (taken or not-taken) is not yet resolved.

**Delayed Branch Technique:**
- The instruction slot immediately following the branch is called the **delay slot**.
- This delay slot instruction **always executes**, regardless of whether the branch is taken or not.
- The compiler is responsible for **filling** the delay slot with a useful instruction from:
  - An instruction from **before** the branch (with nullification if branch is taken), or
  - An instruction from the **branch target**, or
  - An instruction from the **fall-through path** (only safe if branch is not-taken).
- This achieves **zero branch penalty** when the slot can be filled usefully.

**(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for delayed branch explanation.)**

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Choice Note:** As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, students must answer ONE full question from this module (choice between two alternatives). Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question]*

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]** Consider the following MIPS instruction sequence:

```text
I1: LW   R1, 0(R2)
I2: ADD  R3, R1, R4
I3: SUB  R5, R3, R6
I4: OR   R7, R1, R8
I5: AND  R9, R1, R10
```

Assume a classic 5-stage MIPS pipeline (IF, ID, EX, MEM, WB) with **full forwarding**. Show the pipeline timing diagram. Identify all RAW hazards and explain why **only one stall cycle is needed**.

**Model Solution:**

**Pipeline Timing Diagram (Cycles 1–9):**

| Instruction | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| I1: LW | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — | — | — | — |
| I2: ADD | — | IF | ID | **stall** | EX | MEM | WB | — | — |
| I3: SUB | — | — | IF | **stall** | ID | EX | MEM | WB | — |
| I4: OR | — | — | — | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM | WB |
| I5: AND | — | — | — | — | — | IF | ID | EX | MEM |

**RAW Hazard Analysis:**
- **I1 → I2:** R1 needed by I2, produced by I1 in MEM stage (cycle 4). With forwarding, ideally available for I2's EX in cycle 4. **However, load data only available at end of MEM (cycle 4) — but I2's EX needs it at start of cycle 4.** This is the **load-use hazard** → **1 stall required**. Forwarding from MEM/WB is then used to forward to I2's EX in cycle 5.
- **I2 → I3:** R3 produced by I2 in EX (cycle 5), needed by I3 in EX (cycle 6). **EX → EX forwarding** resolves without stall.
- **I1 → I4:** R1 from I1 (MEM, cycle 4) forwarded via MEM/WB to I4's EX (cycle 7). No stall.
- **I1 → I5:** Similar to I1 → I4, forwarded from MEM/WB. No stall.

**Why exactly 1 stall?**
The load-use hazard is the **only case** where forwarding is insufficient because the load's data emerges at the **end** of MEM stage, but the dependent instruction requires it at the **start** of the EX stage of the same cycle. Inserting a 1-cycle bubble shifts the dependent instruction's EX to cycle 5, allowing the MEM/WB → EX forwarding path to supply R1 in time.

**(Award: 3 marks for correct timing diagram, 2 marks for hazard identification, 2 marks for forwarding explanation.)**

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **effective CPI** of a MIPS processor for the following benchmark statistics:
- Total instructions: 1000
- Base CPI: 1.0 (ideal pipelined)
- ALU instructions: 50% (no stalls)
- Load instructions: 30%
- Store instructions: 10%
- Branch instructions: 10%
- 25% of loads are immediately followed by a dependent instruction (load-use hazard)
- Branches are resolved in the EX stage
- Static prediction: "Predict Not-Taken"
- 60% of branches are actually taken

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Load-use stall contribution.**

$$
\text{Load-use stalls per instruction} = 0.30 \times 0.25 \times 1 = 0.075
$$

**Step 2: Branch penalty calculation.**

With "Predict Not-Taken" and resolution in EX, on a misprediction (branch actually taken), the pipeline must flush the 2 instructions fetched after the branch (in IF and ID stages). Penalty = **2 cycles**.

$$
\text{Branch stalls per branch instruction} = 0.60 \times 2 + 0.40 \times 0 = 1.2
$$

$$
\text{Branch stalls per instruction} = 0.10 \times 1.2 = 0.12
$$

**Step 3: Total effective CPI.**

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = 1.0 + 0.075 + 0.12 = 1.195 \text{ cycles/instruction}
$$

**Step 4: Total execution cycles.**

$$
T_{\text{exec}} = 1000 \times 1.195 = 1195 \text{ cycles}
$$

**(Award: 2 marks for stall calculation, 2 marks for branch penalty, 2 marks for total CPI, 1 mark for execution cycles.)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question]*

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Branch Target Buffer (BTB)** mechanism for handling control hazards. Discuss how a **2-bit saturating counter** improves prediction accuracy over a **1-bit predictor**.

**Model Solution:**

**BTB Structure (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
graph TD
    PCreg["Current PC"] --> Index["Index into BTB"]
    Index --> TagComp{"Tag Match?"}
    TagComp -->|"Yes - Hit"| ReadPred["Read 2-bit Prediction Counter"]
    ReadPred --> Mux{"Predict T or N?"}
    Mux -->|"T"| FetchT["Fetch from Stored Target Address"]
    Mux -->|"N"| FetchN["Fetch PC+4"]
    TagComp -->|"No - Miss"| FetchN
    FetchT --> Verify["Verify in EX/MEM Stage"]
    FetchN --> Verify
    Verify --> Update{"Correct?"}
    Update -->|"No"| Retrain["Update BTB Entry Update Counter"]
    Update -->|"Yes"| Continue["Continue"]
```

**BTB Mechanism:**
- The BTB is a small associative cache indexed by the branch PC.
- Each entry stores: **valid bit, tag, predicted target PC, prediction counter**.
- During IF, the PC is used to index the BTB in parallel with I-cache access.
- On a **hit**, the predicted target is used for the next fetch (taken prediction) or sequential PC is used (not-taken).
- On a **miss**, sequential fetch is assumed.
- The prediction is **verified** in EX stage. If wrong, the pipeline is **flushed** and the BTB entry is updated.

**1-bit Predictor Limitation:**
- A 1-bit predictor toggles on every misprediction.
- **Problem:** For a loop branch that is taken N-1 times and not-taken once, the predictor will be wrong on the first iteration (N-1 correct) **AND** on the loop exit (1 wrong). But it will then **stay wrong for the next loop's first iteration** as well, leading to 2 mispredictions per loop iteration cycle instead of 1.

**2-bit Saturating Counter:**
- States: **Strongly Taken (ST), Weakly Taken (WT), Weakly Not-Taken (WN), Strongly Not-Taken (SN)**.
- A single misprediction only moves the counter by one state — it does **not** flip the prediction immediately.
- This eliminates the "double-misprediction on loop boundary" problem.
- **Result:** 2-bit predictors achieve ~93% accuracy vs. ~85% for 1-bit predictors on typical SPEC benchmarks.

**(Award: 2 marks for BTB diagram, 2 marks for 1-bit limitation, 3 marks for 2-bit explanation with states.)**

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]** A 5-stage MIPS pipeline has the following code with branch instruction:

```text
LOOP:
    LW   R1, 0(R2)
    ADD  R1, R1, R3
    SUB  R4, R1, R5
    SW   R4, 0(R2)
    ADDI R2, R2, -4
    BNEZ R2, LOOP        ; Branch back to LOOP if R2 != 0
```

Assume branches are resolved in the **EX stage**. Calculate:
1. Number of stalls due to data hazards with **full forwarding**
2. Number of stalls due to control hazards assuming **"Predict Taken"** policy
3. Total cycles to execute 100 iterations of the loop (ignore first-time fetch latency for loop start)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Data Hazards per Iteration (with full forwarding).**

| Instruction Pair | Hazard Type | Stall? |
|---|---|---|
| LW → ADD (R1) | Load-use | **Yes, 1 stall** |
| ADD → SUB (R1) | RAW, EX→EX forwarding | No |
| SUB → SW (R4) | RAW, EX→EX forwarding (store) | No |
| ADDI → BNEZ (R2) | RAW, EX→EX forwarding | No |

**Data stalls per iteration = 1.**

**Step 2: Control Hazards per Iteration (Predict Taken).**

- The branch is **always taken** (loop branch).
- With "Predict Taken," the prediction is correct.
- **Branch penalty = 0 stalls per iteration.**

**Control stalls per iteration = 0.**

**Step 3: Total Cycles for 100 iterations.**

- Each iteration has 6 instructions.
- Without stalls: 6 cycles per iteration.
- With data stalls: 6 + 1 = **7 cycles per iteration**.
- For 100 iterations: $100 \times 7 = 700$ cycles.
- Add initial fill (5 cycles) and final drain (5 cycles): $700 + 5 + 5 = 710$ cycles.

**(Award: 2 marks for hazard table, 2 marks for branch analysis, 3 marks for total cycle calculation.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing WAR with RAW:** Many students write "WAR hazard" when they mean RAW. WAR is **NOT** a hazard in basic in-order MIPS — clarify this explicitly to score full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 1-cycle load-use stall:** Forwarding eliminates ALL RAW hazards **EXCEPT** load-use. The phrase "forwarding eliminates all data hazards" will lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong branch penalty:** For "Predict Not-Taken" with EX-stage resolution, the penalty is **2 cycles per taken branch**, NOT 1. The penalty equals the number of stages between IF and the resolution stage.
> 4. **Mixing up CPI formulas:** Don't add stalls as a percentage. The formula is: $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \text{Stalls per Instruction}$.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention that BTB uses PC indexing:** Students often describe BTB as "just a cache" without specifying the **indexing scheme** and the **parallel lookup with I-cache**.
> 6. **Ignoring initial pipeline fill/drain:** In timing diagrams, the first instruction takes **k cycles** to complete and the pipeline drains **k-1 cycles** after the last instruction. Always mention $(n + k - 1)$ in cycle calculations.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Pipeline Hazard = Any condition preventing the next instruction from executing in its designated clock cycle.**
- **Three hazard types:** Structural (hardware), Data (dependency), Control (branch).
- **Data Hazard Types:** RAW (true), WAR (anti), WAW (output). Only RAW occurs in in-order scalar MIPS.
- **Forwarding Paths:** EX→EX, MEM→EX, MEM→MEM, WB→ID.
- **Load-use hazard** is the ONLY hazard not solved by forwarding → requires **1 stall**.
- **Control Hazard Penalty** = (Resolution Stage Index) − (IF Stage Index).
- **Predict Not-Taken:** Penalty = 2 cycles × $p_t$ (probability of taken).
- **Predict Taken:** Best for loop-back branches.
- **Delayed Branch:** Compiler fills the delay slot to achieve **0 penalty**.
- **BTB** caches (PC → Target, Prediction) pairs, indexed by PC.
- **2-bit saturating counter** requires **2 consecutive mispredictions** before flipping prediction — eliminates the "loop boundary double-misprediction" issue of 1-bit predictors.
- **CPI formula:** $\text{CPI}_{\text{actual}} = 1 + f_L \cdot p_{LU} + f_b \cdot P_{\text{branch}}$.
- **Speedup with hazards:** Always less than the ideal $k$ (number of stages); the gap widens with higher branch frequency and higher data dependency density.
- **KTU Hotspot Topics:** Forwarding paths diagram, load-use hazard analysis, CPI calculation with hazards, BTB mechanism, 2-bit predictor state machine.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **MIPS architecture** and modern processor design (as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PERUT418 – Computer Organisation and Architecture*), an **Exception** is defined as an *unscheduled* event that occurs during the execution of a program that disrupts the normal sequential flow of instruction execution, causing the processor to transfer control to a dedicated system-level handler known as the **Exception Service Routine (ESR)** or **Exception Handler**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Exceptions are *unplanned* changes in control flow originating **from within the processor itself** (e.g., divide by zero, undefined opcode, system call). When the same mechanism is triggered by an **external hardware device**, it is traditionally called an **Interrupt**. In the unified MIPS philosophy, BOTH are handled through the *same hardware mechanism* and the term *exception* is used as the umbrella term.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are reading a textbook (your CPU executing instructions sequentially). Suddenly, three things can happen:
1. **You realize the page number is wrong** (a *program-detected exception* like an overflow or divide-by-zero).
2. **Someone taps your shoulder and asks a question** (an *external interrupt* like I/O completion).
3. **You intentionally press the "Help" button** to call the author of the book (a *system call / trap* — e.g., `syscall` in MIPS).

In all three cases, you:
- **Save your place** (the processor saves the **EPC – Exception Program Counter**).
- **Note down why this happened** (the processor writes to the **Cause** register).
- **Go deal with the event** (jump to the Exception Service Routine).
- **Eventually return** to your book (execute `eret` in MIPS to resume).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — "Synchronous" vs "Asynchronous":**
> - **Synchronous Exceptions** occur at the *same place every time* you run the program (e.g., divide by zero). They are caused by the *instruction itself*.
> - **Asynchronous Exceptions (Interrupts)** are caused by *external devices* and can occur *anytime* relative to the program.

### 1.3 The Five Standard Classes of Exceptions (Patterson & Hennessy Taxonomy)

| Class | Cause | Sync/Async | KTU Exam Buzzword |
|------|------|------------|-------------------|
| **Interrupt** | External I/O device | Async | "External interrupt" |
| **Trap (System Call)** | Intentional via `syscall` | Sync | "Software interrupt" |
| **Fault** | Recoverable error (e.g., page fault) | Sync | "Page fault" |
| **Abort** | Non-recoverable hardware failure | Sync | "Bus parity error" |
| **Arithmetic Exception** | Overflow, divide-by-zero | Sync | "Divide error" |

The **standard MIPS exception vector base address** is **`0x80000180`** (in unsegmented MIPS), and on reset, the processor jumps to **`0xBFC00000`**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants to Memorize for KTU:**
> - EPC register holds the address of the instruction that caused the exception.
> - **Cause register**: identifies the reason (exception code).
> - **Status register**: enables/disables interrupts and sets the privilege mode (Kernel vs User).
> - In MIPS, **EPC and Cause registers are CP0 (Coprocessor 0)** registers accessed via `mfc0` / `mtc0`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Exceptions — Detailed Breakdown

#### A. Asynchronous Exceptions (Interrupts)
- Generated by **external hardware** (keyboard, disk, network card).
- The processor can **disable** them by clearing the **IE bit** (Interrupt Enable) in the **Status** register.
- After completing the current instruction, the CPU checks the interrupt request line.
- **Maskable vs Non-Maskable**:
  - Maskable → can be disabled (most I/O interrupts).
  - Non-Maskable → cannot be disabled (power failure, memory parity).

#### B. Synchronous Exceptions (Traps, Faults, Aborts)
- **Traps**: Intentional (e.g., `syscall` in MIPS — used for OS services like I/O).
- **Faults**: Potentially recoverable (e.g., TLB miss, page fault). The faulting instruction is re-executed after the handler completes.
- **Aborts**: Catastrophic, non-recoverable. The program is terminated.

#### C. Arithmetic Exceptions
- Integer overflow → usually ignored in MIPS C-style semantics.
- Divide by zero → triggers exception with `Cause = 0xC` (12 decimal).
- Floating-point exceptions → IEEE 754 sticky flags or traps.

### 2.2 MIPS Hardware Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

When an exception occurs, the MIPS hardware performs **the following 5 atomic steps** in one clock cycle (on a non-pipelined simple implementation):

1. **Save the PC of the faulting instruction** into the **EPC** register.
   - *Why?* So that on return, the OS knows *which* instruction (or the *next* one) to re-execute.
2. **Save the reason** for the exception in the **Cause** register.
   - An N-bit code (N = log₂(number of exception types)).
3. **Save the current processor state** (Kernel/User mode, IE bit) in the **Status** register.
4. **Switch the processor to Kernel Mode** (privileged mode) and **disable further interrupts** so the OS handler is not itself interrupted.
5. **Set the PC to the exception vector address** (e.g., `0x80000180` for general exceptions, `0xBFC00000` for reset).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Value | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of exception types supported | $N$ | $N = 2^k$, where $k$ = bits in `Cause` |
| Exception Vector Address (MIPS) | `0x80000180` | Hex address in `kseg0` |
| Reset Vector Address (MIPS) | `0xBFC00000` | Hex address in `kseg1` (uncached) |
| EPC register width | **32 bits** (single word) | Holds the faulting PC |
| EPC + 4 adjustment | EPC = PC of faulting instr. + 4 | Done in Delay Slot handling |
| `mfc0 $t0, $14` | Move From Coprocessor 0 reg 14 (EPC) | MIPS assembly |
| `mtc0 $t0, $13` | Move To Coprocessor 0 reg 13 (Cause) | MIPS assembly |
| `eret` | Return from exception, restores PC from EPC | Resets EXL bit |
| Privileged Mode Bit in Status | `KSU` field (10 = User, 01 = Supervisor, 00 = Kernel) | 2 bits |
| Interrupt Enable Bit | `IE` bit in Status | 1 = enabled, 0 = disabled |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The same exception mechanism is the foundation of **Operating System process switching, virtual memory paging, device drivers, and system calls**. Every modern OS (Linux, Windows) boots through an exception handler at the reset vector, then handles page faults, divide errors, and I/O interrupts through the same EPC/Cause/Status infrastructure.

### 2.4 Pipeline Exceptions — Additional Complexity

In a **pipelined MIPS processor**, exceptions are harder because multiple instructions are in-flight simultaneously. The standard rule is:
- **Imprecise Exceptions:** Just save the PC of the *last completed* instruction. Simple but harder to debug.
- **Precise Exceptions (KTU standard):** Save the PC of the *earliest uncompleted* instruction that caused the exception. This requires **"in-order commit"** logic.

The **Killer Micro** problem (KleinOssoff / Patterson): a 5-stage pipeline that simply *flushes* the pipeline on every exception wastes up to 4 instruction slots of work. Solutions include:
- **Speculative execution with reorder buffer (ROB)** — used in modern out-of-order processors.
- **Restartable instructions** — instructions have side-effect-free early phases.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation: EPC Adjustment in Branch Delay Slots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favorite KTU question** — *Why does the OS need to inspect EPC+4 instead of just EPC?*

**Problem Setup:**
In MIPS, every branch instruction is followed by exactly **one** instruction that *always* executes — this is called the **Branch Delay Slot (BDS)**. If the branch instruction itself is the one that caused the exception, the EPC will contain the address of the *branch*, not the instruction we want to *re-execute* on return.

**Derivation:**

Let:
- $A$ = address of the branch instruction
- $A + 4$ = address of the delay slot instruction
- $A + 8$ = address of the instruction *after* the delay slot (the real branch target destination's sequential successor)

The hardware simply stores the address of the faulting instruction:

$$
EPC = A
$$

When the handler runs `eret`, it jumps back to address $A$. But executing the branch again will re-execute its delay slot at $A+4$ — which is correct *only if* the original program semantics intended that. The OS therefore must check a special bit (the **BD — Branch Delay** bit in the Cause register).

$$
\text{If } BD = 1 \Rightarrow EPC_{\text{effective}} = EPC + 4
$$

$$
\text{If } BD = 0 \Rightarrow EPC_{\text{effective}} = EPC
$$

**Worked example for KTU:** Given a program counter value of `0x00400030` and a branch delay slot, the OS code must perform:

$$
\text{NewPC} = \text{EPC} + (BD \;?\ 4 : 0)
$$

$$
\text{NewPC} = 0x00400030 + 4 = 0x00400034
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Exception Categories

| Exception Type | Sync? | User-Initiated? | Hardware or Software? | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | Async | No | Hardware | Power-on |
| External Interrupt | Async | No | Hardware (I/O device) | Keyboard keystroke |
| System Call (Trap) | Sync | **Yes** | Software (`syscall`) | Print integer |
| Arithmetic Overflow | Sync | No | Hardware | `INT_MAX + 1` |
| TLB Miss (Fault) | Sync | No | Hardware (MMU) | First access to a page |
| Bus Error (Abort) | Sync | No | Hardware | Unaligned address |
| Breakpoint | Sync | Yes | Software (`break`) | Debugger trap |

### 3.3 Cost of Exceptions — Derivation

Let the **average instruction latency** of a 5-stage pipelined MIPS processor be $L$ cycles. Suppose every $K$-th instruction triggers an exception, and each exception flushes the pipeline (i.e., 4 stages of work are lost).

$$
\text{Pipeline Flush Cost} = (S - 1) \cdot L
$$

where $S$ = number of pipeline stages (5 in our case).

$$
\text{Flush Cost} = (5 - 1) \cdot 1 = 4 \text{ cycles}
$$

The **CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) degradation** due to exceptions:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{total}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \frac{\text{Flush Cost}}{K}
$$

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{total}} = 1.0 + \frac{4}{K}
$$

> **Example:** If $K = 100$ (one exception per 100 instructions), the CPI rises to:

$$
\text{CPI}_{\text{total}} = 1.0 + \frac{4}{100} = 1.04
$$

a 4% throughput hit, which is acceptable. But for $K = 10$ (very exception-heavy workload), the cost balloons to $1.4$ — a **40% slowdown**, which is why *precise exception* hardware is critical in modern designs.

### 3.4 Status Register Bit Layout (MIPS CP0)

The MIPS `Status` register has the following critical fields (from MSB to LSB):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 31:} & \quad \text{BEV — Boot Exception Vector} \\
\text{Bits 30:28:} & \quad \text{— Reserved} \\
\text{Bit 22:} & \quad \text{BEV (alternate name in some encodings)} \\
\text{Bits 15:8:} & \quad \text{IM — Interrupt Mask (8 bits)} \\
\text{Bit 15:} & \quad \text{IE — Interrupt Enable} \\
\text{Bits 4:3:} & \quad \text{KSU — Kernel/Supervisor/User mode} \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad \text{EXL — Exception Level (cleared by eret)} \\
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad \text{— Reserved}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The `EXL` bit is set automatically when an exception occurs. This forces the processor into **Kernel Mode** and **disables further interrupts** until the OS executes `eret`, which clears `EXL`. This prevents the catastrophic case of an exception handler being interrupted by another exception before it has saved the EPC.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: MIPS Exception Handling Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instruction Execution in Pipeline] --> B{Exception Detected?}
    B -- No --> Z[Continue Normal Flow] --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Save Faulting PC to EPC]
    C --> D[Write Exception Code to Cause Register]
    D --> E[Save Old Status to Status Register]
    E --> F[Set EXL bit = 1 in Status]
    F --> G[Disable Interrupts IE = 0]
    G --> H[Switch to Kernel Mode KSU = 00]
    H --> I[Set PC to 0x80000180]
    I --> J[Begin Exception Service Routine]
    J --> K[OS Saves User Registers to Stack]
    K --> L[OS Examines Cause Register Code]
    L --> M{Which Exception?}
    M -- Syscall --> N[Service System Call]
    M -- Arithmetic --> O[Handle Divide by Zero / Overflow]
    M -- Interrupt --> P[Service I/O Device]
    M -- Fault --> Q[Handle Page Fault / TLB Miss]
    N --> R[Execute eret Instruction]
    O --> R
    P --> R
    Q --> R
    R --> S[Clear EXL Bit]
    S --> T[Restore PC from EPC]
    T --> U[Resume Faulting or Next Instruction]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: MIPS Coprocessor 0 (System Control Coprocessor)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[MIPS CPU Core] --> CP0[Coprocessor 0 System Control]
    CP0 --> EPC[EPC Register 32 bits]
    CP0 --> CR[Cause Register 32 bits]
    CP0 --> SR[Status Register 32 bits]
    CP0 --> TVEC[Vector Address Register]
    EPC --> OSP[OS Reads on Entry]
    CR --> OSR[OS Reads Exception Code]
    SR --> OSS[OS Saves and Restores Mode]
    TVEC --> PC[New PC loaded on Exception]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Exception Type Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Exception Event] --> B{Source of Event?}
    B -- External Hardware --> C[Asynchronous Interrupt]
    B -- Internal / Software --> D{Intentional?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Trap syscall]
    D -- No --> E2[Synchronous Exception]
    E2 --> F{Recoverable?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Fault e.g. Page Fault]
    F -- No --> H[Abort e.g. Bus Parity Error]
    C --> I[Vector 0x80000180]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> J[System Halt or Reboot]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Timeline: Pipeline Flush on Exception

```mermaid
gantt
    title Pipeline Stages During Exception Handling
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %s
    section Normal Pipeline
    IF Instruction Fetch    :a1, 0, 1
    ID Instruction Decode   :a2, 1, 1
    EX Execute              :a3, 2, 1
    MEM Memory Access       :a4, 3, 1
    WB Write Back           :a5, 4, 1
    section Exception Cycle
    Exception Detected at EX :crit, b1, 2, 1
    section Pipeline Flush
    Stages IF ID EX MEM WB all flushed :active, c1, 3, 4
    section Handler Entry
    PC = 0x80000180          :d1, 3, 1
    ESR Begins Execution     :d2, 4, 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** Notice that the **IF, ID, MEM, WB stages of the faulting instruction and subsequent instructions are all flushed** (the bubble). The `EX` stage of the faulting instruction is where the exception is *first detected*, so 4 cycles of work are lost.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a Trap, an Interrupt, and a Fault. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Type | Origin | Synchronous? | Recoverable? | Example |
|------|--------|--------------|--------------|---------|
| **Trap** | Intentional software | Yes | N/A (intentional) | MIPS `syscall` instruction |
| **Interrupt** | External hardware device | No (asynchronous) | Yes (handler returns) | Keyboard keypress |
| **Fault** | Internal hardware | Yes | Yes (re-execute faulting instruction) | TLB miss / Page fault |

(1 mark for correct definitions, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for highlighting sync vs async).

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of the EPC and Cause registers in the MIPS exception handling mechanism?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **EPC (Exception Program Counter):** A 32-bit CP0 register that **stores the memory address of the instruction that caused the exception**. The OS uses this to resume execution via the `eret` instruction. (1.5 marks)
- **Cause Register:** A 32-bit CP0 register that **identifies the reason for the exception** through an exception code field (e.g., `0x08` = syscall, `0x0C` = divide by zero, `0x00` = interrupt). It also contains the **IP (Interrupt Pending)** and **BD (Branch Delay)** bits. (1.5 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) Explain in detail the steps performed by the MIPS hardware when an exception occurs. Illustrate the role of EPC, Cause, and Status registers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When the MIPS processor detects an exception (either synchronous or asynchronous), the hardware performs the following **five atomic steps** in a single cycle:

1. **Save the faulting PC in EPC:** The address of the instruction that caused the exception is stored in the EPC register.
   $$ EPC = PC_{\text{faulting}} $$
2. **Save the reason in Cause:** An N-bit exception code is written into the Cause register, identifying the type of exception.
3. **Save the current state in Status:** The old value of the Status register is preserved (a "shadow copy") so it can be restored on return.
4. **Disable interrupts and enter Kernel Mode:** The `EXL` bit in Status is set to 1, the `IE` bit is cleared, and the `KSU` field is forced to `00` (Kernel Mode).
5. **Set PC to the exception vector:** The PC is loaded with `0x80000180` (or `0xBFC00000` if `BEV=1`).

**Role of registers:**

- **EPC (CP0 reg 14):** Holds the return address for `eret`.
- **Cause (CP0 reg 13):** Tells the OS *why* the exception occurred.
- **Status (CP0 reg 12):** Stores the IE bit, EXL bit, KSU mode bits, and Interrupt Mask (IM) field.

(7 marks: 2 marks for listing the 5 steps, 2 marks for register roles, 2 marks for explanation of mode transition, 1 mark for vector address).

---

**(b) Discuss the difference between precise and imprecise exceptions. Why are precise exceptions harder to implement in a pipelined processor? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Precise Exception:** The processor state (register file, memory) at the time of the exception looks *exactly* as if all instructions *before* the faulting one have completed, and *no* instruction *after* the faulting one has started. The EPC points to the *exact* faulting instruction, and it can be re-executed after the handler returns.

- **Imprecise Exception:** The processor state is *not* well-defined. Multiple instructions may be in flight, and the EPC may point to an instruction that is *not* the actual culprit. The OS cannot simply re-execute.

**Why precise exceptions are hard in a pipeline:**

1. **Multiple in-flight instructions:** In a 5-stage pipeline, up to 5 instructions execute simultaneously. Determining *which one* caused the exception is non-trivial.
2. **Out-of-order completion:** Instructions may complete in a *different order* than they were issued (especially in dynamic pipelines), so the "latest completed" PC is not necessarily the faulting one.
3. **Side effects:** Some instructions (e.g., store) have irreversible side effects on memory. The hardware must either **delay the side effect** until commit (using a **Reorder Buffer / ROB**), or **undo** the side effect on exception.
4. **Performance cost:** Maintaining precise exceptions typically requires:
   - An in-order commit stage (the "commit" or "graduation" stage).
   - A Reorder Buffer to hold pending results.
   - Precise state checkpoints.

Modern processors (Intel Core, AMD Zen, Apple M-series) use **ROB-based precise exception** mechanisms that are transparent to software. The classic example of an **imprecise exception design** is the CDC 6600, which was fast but extremely difficult to program for.

(7 marks: 2 marks for precise definition, 2 marks for imprecise definition, 3 marks for hardware challenges).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a) What are the different types of exceptions in a computer system? Explain the MIPS exception handling mechanism with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Exceptions (refer to Section 1.3 for full table):**
1. Reset
2. External Interrupt (Asynchronous)
3. Software Trap (e.g., `syscall`)
4. Arithmetic Exception (overflow, divide-by-zero)
5. TLB / Page Fault
6. Bus Error / Abort
7. Breakpoint / Debug exception

**MIPS Exception Handling Mechanism:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  User Program executing normally in User Mode            │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                         │ Exception occurs
                         ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Hardware: Save PC → EPC, code → Cause, mode → Status    │
│  Switch to Kernel Mode, disable interrupts               │
│  PC ← 0x80000180                                         │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                         ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Exception Service Routine (in Kernel)                   │
│  1. Save user registers (sp, ra, etc.)                   │
│  2. Examine Cause register                               │
│  3. Dispatch to specific handler                          │
│  4. Service the request (e.g., I/O, syscall)              │
│  5. Restore user registers                                │
│  6. Execute eret                                          │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                         ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  eret: PC ← EPC, Status restored, IE re-enabled          │
└────────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────┘
                         ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Resume faulting instruction in User Mode                 │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

(7 marks: 2 marks for listing types, 3 marks for mechanism explanation, 2 marks for diagram).

---

**(b) Explain the role of the Status register's EXL, IE, and KSU fields. Why is the EXL bit set automatically on exception? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **KSU field (Bits 4:3):** The *Kernel/Supervisor/User* mode selector.
  - `00` = Kernel Mode (privileged — can access CP0 registers).
  - `01` = Supervisor Mode.
  - `10` = User Mode (unprivileged).
  - On exception, KSU is *forced* to `00` (Kernel Mode) so the OS handler has full hardware access.

- **IE bit (Bit 15):** Interrupt Enable.
  - `1` = Interrupts enabled.
  - `0` = Interrupts disabled (masked).
  - On exception, IE is cleared to `0` to prevent nested interrupts from corrupting the handler.

- **EXL bit (Bit 1):** Exception Level.
  - `1` = Processor is currently *inside* an exception handler.
  - `0` = Normal execution.
  - On exception, EXL is set to `1`.
  - On `eret`, EXL is cleared to `0`.

**Why EXL is set automatically:**
1. **Prevents recursive exceptions:** If an exception occurs *during* the handler (e.g., a page fault while handling another page fault), the second exception would overwrite the EPC, losing the original return address. The EXL bit tells the hardware: *"We are already in a handler — do not take a new exception."* (3 marks)
2. **Forces Kernel Mode:** EXL=1 also forces the KSU bits to behave as if `00` (Kernel), regardless of their actual value, ensuring privileged execution. (2 marks)
3. **Allows nested `eret` detection:** Software can poll EXL to determine if it is currently in a handler context. (1 mark)

(7 marks: 2 marks for KSU+IE, 2 marks for EXL, 3 marks for the *why* part).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `Interrupt` and `Exception`:** In MIPS/Patterson terminology, *interrupt* is a *subclass* of exception. Students often write "interrupts and exceptions are different." In KTU 2024, the answer key expects the unified view: "Interrupts are asynchronous exceptions." (Lose 1-2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to mention `EXL` bit's role in preventing nested exceptions:** Many students describe Status register fields but fail to explain *why* EXL is critical. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 3. **Writing `0x80000000` instead of `0x80000180`:** The general exception vector in MIPS is `0x80000180` — the 0x80 offset accounts for the TLB refill handler at `0x80000000`. (Lose 0.5-1 mark)
>
> 4. **Not mentioning that exceptions *save* and `eret` *restores* the Status register:** Many students say "Status is set to Kernel Mode" but forget that the *old* value is preserved. (Lose 1 mark)
>
> 5. **Conflating precise and imprecise exceptions with vectored and non-vectored exceptions:** These are *different concepts*. Precise/imprecise is about *state consistency*; vectored is about *multiple vector addresses*. (Lose 1-2 marks)
>
> 6. **Pipelined exception diagrams missing the "bubble" cycles:** A pipeline flush causes 4 idle cycles. Forgetting to show this loses 1 mark in part (b) questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Cheat Sheet — Read this the night before the exam!**

- ✅ An **Exception** is *any* unplanned control flow change; **Interrupts** are async, **Traps** are sync+intentional, **Faults** are sync+recoverable, **Aborts** are sync+non-recoverable.
- ✅ MIPS exception vector is **`0x80000180`**; reset vector is **`0xBFC00000`**.
- ✅ **EPC** (CP0 reg 14) holds the faulting PC.
- ✅ **Cause** (CP0 reg 13) holds the exception code (e.g., `0x08`=syscall, `0x0C`=divide-by-zero).
- ✅ **Status** (CP0 reg 12) holds IE, EXL, KSU, IM bits.
- ✅ On exception: EPC ← PC, Cause ← code, Status ← old, EXL ← 1, IE ← 0, KSU ← 00, PC ← vector.
- ✅ On `eret`: PC ← EPC, EXL ← 0, IE ← 1 (restored), KSU ← old.
- ✅ **Precise exceptions** preserve the illusion of sequential execution; require ROB in modern CPUs.
- ✅ **Imprecise exceptions** are faster but hard to debug (CDC 6600 style).
- ✅ Branch delay slot handling: check the **BD bit** in Cause; if `BD=1`, add 4 to EPC.
- ✅ A 5-stage pipeline loses **4 cycles** of work on every exception flush.
- ✅ CPI penalty formula: $\text{CPI}_{\text{total}} = \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} + \dfrac{S-1}{K}$, where $S$ = stages, $K$ = instructions per exception.
- ✅ MIPS `mfc0 $rt, $rd` moves *from* CP0 to GPR; `mtc0 $rt, $rd` moves *to* CP0.
- ✅ **EXL bit prevents nested exceptions** — this is a high-value KTU answer point.
- ✅ The same mechanism handles **I/O, system calls, page faults, and divide errors** — KTU loves asking for a unified explanation.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Exceptions Notes**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, PERUT418, Outcome-Based Education (NEP 2020) standards.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
