## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part B questions in ESE carry 14 marks, with internal choice. Students answer **one out of two**. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each (e.g., (a) descriptive + (b) numerical). Both choices are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the seven key characteristics used to classify memory systems. With the help of a comparison table, classify **Registers, Cache, Main Memory (DRAM), and Hard Disk** based on these characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

**Seven Characteristics of Memory:**

1. **Location:** Whether the memory is *internal* to the CPU (registers, cache), *main* (DRAM on motherboard), or *external* (disk).
2. **Capacity:** The volume of data stored. Registers hold bytes to words; cache holds KB–MB; main memory holds GB; disks hold TB.
3. **Unit of Transfer:** The smallest chunk transferred per operation. Registers transfer *words*; caches transfer *blocks* (lines); DRAM transfers *pages*; disks transfer *sectors*.
4. **Access Method:** Sequential (tape), Direct (disk), Random (RAM), Associative (cache).
5. **Performance:** Access time, cycle time, transfer rate. Registers: ~1 ns. Cache: 1–10 ns. DRAM: 50–100 ns. Disk: 5–15 ms.
6. **Physical Type:** Semiconductor (registers/cache/DRAM), Magnetic (disk/tape), Optical (CD/DVD).
7. **Organisation / Volatility:** Volatile (RAM) vs Non-volatile (ROM, disk). Internal organisation (word-oriented vs bit-interleaved).

**Comparison Table:**

| Characteristic | Registers | Cache (SRAM) | Main Memory (DRAM) | Hard Disk |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Location | Internal CPU | On-chip | Motherboard | External |
| Capacity | 32–256 words | 32 KB – 32 MB | 4–32 GB | 1–20 TB |
| Unit of Transfer | Word | Block (64 B) | Page (4 KB) | Sector (512 B – 4 KB) |
| Access Method | Direct | Associative | Random | Direct |
| Access Time | < 1 ns | 1–10 ns | 50–100 ns | 5–15 ms |
| Volatile? | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| Physical Type | Semiconductor | Semiconductor | Semiconductor | Magnetic |
| Cost per bit | Very High | High | Medium | Very Low |

**(b) [7 Marks]** A two-level memory system has L1 access time $t_1 = 10$ ns and main memory access time $t_2 = 300$ ns. The hit ratio is **0.92**.

**(i)** Calculate the **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**.
**(ii)** What hit ratio is required to bring the AMAT down to **40 ns**?
**(iii)** Comment on the **significance** of locality in achieving this performance.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) AMAT calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= t_1 + (1 - h) \cdot t_2 \\
&= 10 + (1 - 0.92) \cdot 300 \\
&= 10 + 0.08 \cdot 300 \\
&= 10 + 24 \\
&= 34\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Required hit ratio for AMAT = 40 ns:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
40 &= 10 + (1 - h) \cdot 300 \\
30 &= (1 - h) \cdot 300 \\
1 - h &= 0.10 \\
h &= 0.90 = 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

A 90% hit ratio is *sufficient* — even our existing 92% exceeds the minimum requirement.

**(iii) Significance of Locality:**

- **Temporal Locality** ensures that recently accessed instructions and data (loop variables, function arguments) are likely to be reused soon, making cache hits frequent.
- **Spatial Locality** ensures that consecutive memory locations (array elements, instruction sequences) are accessed together, justifying the transfer of entire *blocks* (cache lines) rather than single words.
- Without locality, the cache would offer no benefit, and the average access time would always equal $t_2$. Locality is the *empirical foundation* of the entire memory hierarchy.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the **memory hierarchy** of a modern computer system. Discuss the **trade-off between speed, capacity, and cost** at each level.

**Model Answer:**

(See the Mermaid pyramid diagrams in SECTION 4 of these notes — students should redraw them on paper for the exam.)

**Explanation of Levels:**

1. **CPU Registers (L0):** On-chip, ~1 ns access, 32–256 words. Highest cost per bit. Used for operands of currently executing instructions.
2. **L1 Cache (SRAM):** 32–64 KB, ~1–2 ns. Holds most recently used instructions and data.
3. **L2/L3 Caches (SRAM):** MB scale, 5–20 ns. Shared across cores in modern CPUs.
4. **Main Memory (DRAM):** GB scale, 50–100 ns. Holds the *currently running* programs.
5. **Solid-State Drive (Flash):** Hundreds of GB, 50–500 µs. Persistent storage, no moving parts.
6. **Hard Disk Drive (Magnetic):** TB scale, 5–15 ms. Cheap archival storage.
7. **Optical/Tape Backup:** Highest capacity, lowest speed, used for offsite backup.

**Trade-off Discussion:**

| Property | Top of Pyramid (Registers) | Bottom of Pyramid (Tape) |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Fastest (~1 ns) | Slowest (seconds) |
| Capacity | Smallest | Largest |
| Cost/bit | Highest | Lowest |
| Proximity to CPU | Closest | Farthest |

This trade-off is **inevitable** because of physical laws: faster storage technologies use more silicon area per bit and consume more power. Designers therefore build a *pyramid* — only a tiny amount of fast memory, with progressively larger amounts of slower memory underneath.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A computer system has a **three-level memory hierarchy** with the following specifications:

| Level | Access Time | Hit Ratio |
|---|---|---|
| L1 (Cache) | 5 ns | 0.80 |
| L2 (Cache) | 50 ns | 0.90 (on L1 miss) |
| Main Memory | 500 ns | — |

**(i)** Compute the **overall Average Memory Access Time**.
**(ii)** What percentage of total CPU time is spent waiting for memory if the CPU executes **10⁹ instructions/sec** and each instruction requires, on average, **1.4 memory accesses**?

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Overall AMAT (three-level cascade):**

Hit ratios are conditional on the previous level missing. Apply the cascade formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= t_1 + (1 - h_1) \cdot t_2 + (1 - h_1)(1 - h_2) \cdot t_3 \\
&= 5 + (1 - 0.80)(50) + (1 - 0.80)(1 - 0.90)(500) \\
&= 5 + (0.20)(50) + (0.20)(0.10)(500) \\
&= 5 + 10 + 10 \\
&= 25\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Memory-stall percentage:**

**Step 1:** Total memory accesses per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accesses/sec} &= 10^9 \times 1.4 = 1.4 \times 10^9\ \text{accesses/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Total time spent waiting for memory per second.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory time/sec} &= 1.4 \times 10^9 \times 25\ \text{ns} \\
&= 1.4 \times 10^9 \times 25 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} \\
&= 1.4 \times 25\ \text{s} \\
&= 35\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — that exceeds 1 second, so let's recompute carefully:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory time/sec} &= 1.4 \times 10^9 \times 25 \times 10^{-9} \\
&= 1.4 \times 25 \times (10^9 \times 10^{-9}) \\
&= 35 \times 1 \\
&= 35\ \text{s? That's wrong — recheck.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction — Recheck unit arithmetic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
25\ \text{ns} &= 25 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} \\
1.4 \times 10^9 \times 25 \times 10^{-9} &= 1.4 \times 25 = 35
\end{aligned}
$$

The result is **35 seconds per 1 second of CPU time**, which is physically impossible. This means our AMAT is too large for the assumed CPU speed — indicating the system is **memory-bound**.

**Step 3:** Compute the ratio properly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Memory stalls per CPU second} &= 1.4 \times 10^9 \times 25 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{s} \\
&= 35\ \text{seconds per 1 CPU-second}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the CPU can only execute for **1 second of wall-clock time**, but memory waits would consume **35 seconds**, the system is **overwhelmed by memory stalls** — it would spend 35× longer waiting than executing. To make the problem realistic, either the CPU speed must be lower (e.g., $10^7$ instructions/sec) or the AMAT must be smaller.

**Conclusion / Interpretation:** The computed AMAT of 25 ns is mathematically correct, but the system is **memory-bound** at 10⁹ instructions/sec. In real engineering, this is exactly why modern CPUs use **prefetching, multi-level caches, and out-of-order execution** — to hide memory latency. The simple analytical model here captures the *lower bound* on stall time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** In Part B numericals, students frequently forget to convert nanoseconds → seconds before multiplying with instructions-per-second. *Always convert AMAT to seconds first.*
> 2. **Conditional vs unconditional hit ratio trap:** In multi-level problems, hit ratios are *conditional* on the previous level missing. Using them as unconditional global hit ratios is a **guaranteed 3-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Definition trap in Part A:** Writing "memory hierarchy is the arrangement of memories" without mentioning the **trade-off and locality** loses 1 mark. The full definition must include the *organising principle*.
> 4. **Diagram trap:** Drawing the pyramid without **labelling access times and capacities at each level** is treated as an incomplete diagram and loses 2 marks in Part B (a).
> 5. **Formula trap:** Writing the AMAT formula as $t_1 + t_2$ (without the $(1-h)$ term) is the **#1 most-common error** in KTU scripts. The CPU still pays $t_1$ even on a miss — never forget this.
<!-- SECTION_5B_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5C_START -->
## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second KTU Recap — read this three times before the exam.**

### ⭐ Core Definitions to Memorise Verbatim

- **Memory Hierarchy:** Stratified organisation of storage from fast/small/expensive (near CPU) to slow/large/cheap (far from CPU).
- **Locality:** Programs access a small subset of memory at any instant — temporal (re-use) + spatial (near-by).
- **Hit Ratio (h):** Probability that the accessed data is found at a given level.
- **AMAT:** Average time per memory access from the CPU's viewpoint.

### ⭐ The 5 Must-Know Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT (2-level)} &= t_1 + (1-h) \cdot t_2 \\
\text{AMAT (n-level)} &= t_{L1} + \sum_{i=2}^{n} (1 - h_{i-1}) \cdot t_{L_i} \\
\text{Miss Ratio} &= 1 - h \\
\text{Transfer Rate} &= N / (T_s + N \cdot T_c) \\
\text{Capacity Conversion} &= 1\ \text{KiB} = 2^{10}\ \text{bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### ⭐ The Seven Characteristics (Mnemonic: **L-C-U-A-P-T-O**)

**L**ocation, **C**apacity, **U**nit of transfer, **A**ccess method, **P**erformance, **T**ype (physical), **O**rganisation.

### ⭐ Comparison Shortcuts (for board-exam answers)

- Registers vs Cache vs Main Memory vs Disk — be able to draw a **comparison table in under 2 minutes**.
- SRAM vs DRAM: SRAM is faster, costlier, and used for cache; DRAM is denser, cheaper, and used for main memory.
- Volatile: RAM (yes), ROM/Flash/Disk (no).

### ⭐ Frequently Confused Pairs

- **Access Time vs Cycle Time** — Access time ≤ Cycle time. Cycle time includes a "recovery period."
- **Random Access vs Associative Access** — Random access uses an *address*; associative access uses a *tag* (content match).
- **Hit Ratio vs Miss Ratio** — Always complementary (sum = 1).
- **Capacity vs Unit of Transfer** — Capacity is the *total* storage; unit of transfer is the *per-operation* chunk.

### ⭐ Numerical-Heavy Topics to Practice

1. Two-level AMAT computation (given $h$, $t_1$, $t_2$ → find AMAT).
2. Inverse problem (given target AMAT → find required $h$).
3. Three-level AMAT with **conditional** hit ratios.
4. Hit ratio derivation from a memory trace (hits + misses at each level).
5. Memory-stall percentage calculation with proper unit conversion (ns → s).

### ⭐ Diagram to Practise Drawing in the Exam

- **Memory hierarchy pyramid** with all 5–7 levels labelled with capacity and access time.
- **Block diagram of a memory chip** (address bus, decoder, cell array, control buffer, data bus).

### ⭐ Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Trade-off Trilemma

> *"**F**ast, **L**arge, **C**heap — pick any **T**wo."* (Speed, Capacity, Cost — the F-L-C-T rule.)

---

> **END OF NOTES — MEMORY CHARACTERISTICS AND HIERARCHY (MODULE 4)**

> **Scheme:** KTU 2024 (NEP 2020 Aligned)

topics.title: Cache Memories
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cache Memories

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cache Memory** is a small, high-speed, volatile semiconductor memory (typically built using **SRAM** — Static RAM) that resides between the **CPU** and the **main memory (RAM/DRAM)**. Its primary purpose is to temporarily store a *subset* of the data and instructions most frequently accessed by the processor, thereby reducing the **average memory access time (AMAT)** and bridging the notorious **speed mismatch** (the *Memory Wall*) between ultra-fast CPU cycles and relatively slow main memory accesses.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, cache memory is treated as the **pivotal element of the Memory Hierarchy**, designed to exploit the **Principle of Locality of Reference** so that the program perceives a memory system that is *almost as fast as SRAM* and *almost as large and inexpensive as DRAM*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Office Desk vs. Filing Cabinet:**
> Imagine you are an accountant working at a desk.
> - **CPU** = You (the worker)
> - **Registers** = The calculator and pen in your hand (instant, very few)
> - **Cache Memory** = The small, neat stack of files you keep *on your desk* (small, fast to access)
> - **Main Memory (RAM)** = The filing cabinet in your office (larger, but takes longer to walk to)
> - **Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD)** = The massive archive room in the basement (huge, but very slow to retrieve from)
>
> When you need a document, you first glance at your desk (cache). If it's there — **HIT!** — you use it instantly. If not — **MISS!** — you walk to the cabinet (main memory), bring the document back, place a copy on your desk (so the next access is a hit), and return to work.
>
> **Cache memory does exactly this for the CPU.**

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Know KTU Glossary:**
> - **Hit** → Required data is found in the cache.
> - **Miss** → Required data is *not* in the cache; must be fetched from main memory.
> - **Hit Ratio (H)** → Fraction of memory accesses that result in a *hit*. Standard metric is **0 ≤ H ≤ 1**.
> - **Miss Ratio (M)** → `M = 1 − H`. Fraction of accesses that miss.
> - **Hit Time (T<sub>c</sub>)** → Time to access cache on a hit (typically **1–10 ns**).
> - **Miss Penalty (T<sub>p</sub>)** → Extra time to fetch a block from main memory on a miss.
> - **Block (Line)** → The fundamental unit of data transfer between cache and main memory.
> - **Tag** → A unique identifier stored with each block to identify which main-memory address it holds.
> - **Locality of Reference** → Programs tend to access a small, clustered region of memory repeatedly over short intervals.
>   - *Temporal Locality* → Recently accessed items will likely be accessed again soon.
>   - *Spatial Locality* → Items near a recently accessed address will likely be accessed soon.

## 1.4 The Memory Hierarchy (Visual Block)

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  REGISTER  →  L1 CACHE  →  L2 CACHE  →  L3 CACHE        │
│  (Fastest, Smallest, Costliest per bit)                  │
│              ↓                                            │
│           MAIN MEMORY (DRAM)                             │
│              ↓                                            │
│       SECONDARY STORAGE (SSD/HDD)                        │
│  (Slowest, Largest, Cheapest per bit)                    │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Cache Memory is Necessary — The Memory Wall

Modern processors operate at clock speeds of **3–5 GHz** (cycle time ≈ **0.2–0.33 ns**). Main memory (DRAM), however, has access times of **50–100 ns**. Without cache, the CPU would spend the vast majority of its time *stalled*, waiting for memory — a phenomenon called the **Memory Wall**. Cache reduces this gap by acting as a *high-speed buffer* holding the working set of the program.

## 2.2 Operational Principles — How Cache Works

The operation of a cache can be broken into a structured sequence:

1. **CPU issues a memory read request** for a particular address.
2. The **address is split** into three fields: **Tag**, **Index (Set/Line)**, and **Block Offset**.
3. The cache controller inspects the line(s) pointed to by the **index**.
4. The **Tag** of the line is compared with the incoming tag.
5. **If Tag matches** → *Cache HIT* → Data returned to CPU in **T<sub>c</sub>** time.
6. **If Tag does NOT match** → *Cache MISS* → The entire block containing the requested address is fetched from main memory, replacing an existing block per the **replacement policy**, and the requested word is delivered to the CPU. Total time ≈ **T<sub>c</sub> + Miss Penalty**.

## 2.3 Cache Mapping Techniques (Core KTU Module 4 Content)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 4 explicitly tests the **three mapping techniques** below. You must be able to draw and explain each, and solve numerical placement problems.

### 2.3.1 Direct Mapping
- Each main memory block maps to **exactly one** cache line.
- Mapping formula: `Cache Line Number = (Main Memory Block Number) mod (Number of Cache Lines)`
- **Pros:** Simple, fast hardware. **Cons:** High conflict miss rate (two popular blocks competing for the same line).

### 2.3.2 Fully Associative Mapping
- A main memory block can be placed in **any** cache line.
- **Pros:** Lowest conflict misses, most flexible. **Cons:** Requires parallel tag comparison (expensive hardware), slow for large caches.

### 2.3.3 Set-Associative Mapping (K-way)
- A compromise: cache is divided into **S sets**, each holding **K lines** (K = associativity).
- Mapping formula: `Set Number = (Main Memory Block Number) mod (S)`
- The incoming tag is compared *in parallel* against all K tags within the indexed set.
- **K = 1** → Direct Mapping, **K = Total Lines** → Fully Associative.

## 2.4 Replacement Algorithms (Used for Associative Caches)

When a miss occurs and all lines in a set are occupied, the cache must evict one. Common policies:
- **LRU (Least Recently Used)** — Evict the line unused for the longest time. Best practical performance, complex to implement for high associativity.
- **FIFO (First-In First-Out)** — Evict the oldest block. Simple but ignores usage pattern.
- **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** — Evict the line with the fewest accesses. Stable but slow to adapt.
- **Random** — Pick any line. Cheapest hardware; surprisingly competitive.

## 2.5 Write Policies

| Policy | Operation | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Write-Through** | Every write updates *both* cache and main memory. | Data always consistent in RAM. | High traffic on system bus, slow writes. |
| **Write-Back** | Write updates *only* cache; main memory is updated later (when the block is evicted), using a **dirty bit**. | Fast writes, low bus traffic. | Risk of data loss on power failure; complexity. |

A **Dirty Bit** (1 bit per line) tracks whether a line has been modified since being loaded.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical formula set** for solving KTU Cache Memory numerical problems. Memorize this table.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average Memory Access Time | $\text{AMAT} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p$ | seconds (or ns) |
| 2 | Alternate AMAT form | $\text{AMAT} = H \cdot T_c + (1 - H) \cdot (T_c + T_p)$ | equivalent expansion |
| 3 | Hit Ratio | $H = \dfrac{\text{Number of Hits}}{\text{Total CPU References}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \le H \le 1$ |
| 4 | Miss Ratio | $M = 1 - H$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Number of Cache Lines | $L = \dfrac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Block Size}}$ | lines |
| 6 | Index Bits (Direct) | $i = \log_2(L)$ | bits |
| 7 | Block Offset Bits | $b = \log_2(\text{Block Size in words/bytes})$ | bits |
| 8 | Tag Bits | $t = n - i - b$ | bits, where $n$ = address bits |
| 9 | Direct Mapping Line | $i = (\text{MM Block Number}) \bmod L$ | integer |
| 10 | Set-Associative Set | $s = (\text{MM Block Number}) \bmod S$ | $S$ = number of sets |
| 11 | Speedup | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_m}{T_c + (1-H)\cdot T_p}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| 12 | Effective Cycle Time | $t_{eff} = \text{AMAT} \times \text{CPU clock rate}$ | seconds |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cache memory is **ubiquitous** in modern engineering:
- **General-purpose CPUs** use multi-level caches (L1 ≈ 32–64 KB, L2 ≈ 256 KB – 1 MB, L3 ≈ 4–64 MB).
- **GPUs** use texture caches and L1/L2 caches for parallel workloads.
- **Embedded systems** (smartphones, IoT, automotive ECUs) rely on tightly coupled memories (TCM) and small caches for real-time guarantees.
- **DSP chips** use specialized cache hierarchies for signal processing pipelines.
- **Database engines** use buffer pools (software-level "caches") to keep hot pages in RAM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)

The CPU's effective memory access time is the weighted average of hit time and miss-time. Let us derive it rigorously.

Let:
- $T_c$ = Cache access time (on a hit, time to check tag and read data)
- $T_m$ = Main memory access time (time to read a block from RAM)
- $T_p$ = Miss Penalty = additional time beyond $T_c$ when a miss occurs. Often $T_p = T_m - T_c$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of derivation:** For every memory reference, the CPU *always* spends $T_c$ (to consult the cache). With probability $H$ it is done. With probability $(1-H)$, it must additionally pay $T_p$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Expanding: } \text{AMAT} &= T_c + (1-H)\cdot T_p \\
&= T_c + T_p - H\cdot T_p \\
&= H\cdot T_c + (1-H)\cdot(T_c + T_p) \quad \text{(alternate form)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** If $H = 1$ (all hits), $\text{AMAT} = T_c$ ✔. If $H = 0$ (all misses), $\text{AMAT} = T_c + T_p$ ✔.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1 — Address Bit Decomposition (Direct Mapped)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This exact problem type appears almost every semester in KTU exams.**

**Problem:** A 32-bit system has a **direct-mapped cache** of size **16 KB**, with a **block size of 4 words**. Word size = **32 bits = 4 bytes**. Compute: (i) Number of cache lines, (ii) Number of index bits, (iii) Number of block offset bits, (iv) Number of tag bits.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert all sizes into bytes (use a consistent byte addressable view).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cache Size} &= 16 \text{ KB} = 16 \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 2^{14} \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Block Size} &= 4 \text{ words} \times 4 \text{ bytes/word} = 16 \text{ bytes} = 2^4 \text{ bytes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute number of cache lines.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \frac{\text{Cache Size}}{\text{Block Size}} = \frac{2^{14}}{2^4} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ lines} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute block offset bits.** Offset must address every byte inside the 16-byte block.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \log_2(\text{Block Size in bytes}) = \log_2(2^4) = 4 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute index bits.** Index must select one of 1024 lines.

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= \log_2(L) = \log_2(2^{10}) = 10 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute tag bits.** Total address = 32 bits. Tag is the remainder.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 32 - i - b = 32 - 10 - 4 = 18 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Address Partition:**

| TAG (18 bits) | INDEX (10 bits) | BLOCK OFFSET (4 bits) |
|---|---|---|
| `TTTTTTTTTTTTTTTTTT` | `IIIIIIIIII` | `OOOO` |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2 — AMAT Calculation

**Problem:** A system has $T_c = 10$ ns, $T_m = 200$ ns, and hit ratio $H = 0.90$. Compute AMAT and effective speedup over a no-cache system.

**Step 1 — Compute Miss Penalty.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p &= T_m - T_c = 200 - 10 = 190 \text{ ns} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply AMAT formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p \\
&= 10 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot 190 \\
&= 10 + (0.10)(190) \\
&= 10 + 19 \\
&= 29 \text{ ns} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute speedup over a no-cache system.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{T_m}{\text{AMAT}} = \frac{200}{29} \approx 6.90 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even with 10% miss rate, the cache delivers a **~6.9× speedup**. This is why a 5% drop in H can halve performance in tight CPU pipelines.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3 — Set-Associative Placement

**Problem:** Consider a **2-way set-associative cache** with **4 sets**, each block holding **2 words** (word = 16 bits). Main memory is **128 words**. A CPU reference sequence arrives in this order: **0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 0, 1, …** Compute the number of hits and misses.

**Step 1 — Determine set index for each block (block size = 2 words, so block number = word/2).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Set} &= (\text{Block Number}) \bmod 4 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

| Word | Block # | Set # |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 |
| 3 | 1 | 1 |
| 4 | 2 | 2 |
| 5 | 2 | 2 |
| 6 | 3 | 3 |
| 7 | 3 | 3 |
| 8 | 4 | 0 |
| 0 (again) | 0 | 0 |

**Step 2 — Trace using LRU within each 2-way set.**

| Ref | Set 0 | Set 1 | Set 2 | Set 3 | Hit/Miss |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | [0] | – | – | – | **MISS** |
| 1 | [0,1] | – | – | – | **MISS** (different word in same set) |
| 2 | [0,1] | [2] | – | – | **MISS** |
| 3 | [0,1] | [2,3] | – | – | **MISS** |
| 4 | [0,1] | [2,3] | [4] | – | **MISS** |
| 5 | [0,1] | [2,3] | [4,5] | – | **MISS** |
| 6 | [0,1] | [2,3] | [4,5] | [6] | **MISS** |
| 7 | [0,1] | [2,3] | [4,5] | [6,7] | **MISS** |
| 8 | [8,1]* | [2,3] | [4,5] | [6,7] | **MISS** (LRU evicts 0) |
| 0 | [8,0]* | [2,3] | [4,5] | [6,7] | **MISS** |

*\* Block 8 evicts block 0 from Set 0 via LRU; re-reference of 0 is again a miss.*

**Final Result:** Hits = **0**, Misses = **10**, Hit Ratio = **0%**. (This is the classic *thrashing* scenario on small set-associative caches — a common KTU exam trap.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CPU REGISTERS - 1 ns - few bytes"] --> B["L1 CACHE - 2 ns - 32 to 64 KB"]
    B --> C["L2 CACHE - 5 ns - 256 KB to 1 MB"]

    D --> E["MAIN MEMORY DRAM - 50 to 100 ns - 4 to 32 GB"]

```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Direct Mapping Placement Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CPU issues address"] --> B["Split into TAG, INDEX, OFFSET"]
    B --> C["Use INDEX to select unique cache line"]
    C --> D{"Compare stored TAG with incoming TAG"}
    D -- "MATCH - HIT" --> E["Return data to CPU in T_c"]
    D -- "MISMATCH - MISS" --> F["Fetch block from Main Memory"]
    F --> G["Place block into selected line - overwrite old block"]
    G --> E

    style A fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#51cf66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#ff8787,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Three Mapping Techniques Compared

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DM["DIRECT MAPPED CACHE"]
        DM1["MM Block 0 -> Line 0"]
        DM2["MM Block 1 -> Line 1"]
        DM3["MM Block 4 -> Line 0 - overwrites block 0"]
        DM1 -.-> DM2
        DM2 -.-> DM3
    end

    subgraph FA["FULLY ASSOCIATIVE CACHE"]
        FA1["MM Block 0 -> ANY line"]
        FA2["MM Block 1 -> ANY line"]
        FA3["MM Block 4 -> ANY free line"]
    end

    subgraph SA["2-WAY SET ASSOCIATIVE - 4 sets"]
        SA1["MM Block 0 -> Set 0 - slot 1 or 2"]
        SA2["MM Block 4 -> Set 0 - other slot"]
        SA3["MM Block 1 -> Set 1"]
    end

    style DM fill:#ffe3e3,stroke:#c92a2a,stroke-width:1px
    style FA fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,stroke-width:1px
    style SA fill:#d0ebff,stroke:#1971c2,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.4 Mermaid — Write-Back vs Write-Through Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU executes STORE to address X"] --> B{"Write Policy?"}
    B -- "WRITE-THROUGH" --> C1["Update Cache Line for X"]
    C1 --> C2["Simultaneously write to Main Memory X"]
    C2 --> C3["Done - slow but consistent"]

    B -- "WRITE-BACK" --> D1["Update Cache Line for X"]
    D1 --> D2["Set DIRTY BIT = 1 for that line"]
    D2 --> D3["Main Memory updated later - when line is evicted"]
    D3 --> D4["Done - fast but RAM is stale until eviction"]

    style C3 fill:#51cf66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D4 fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define cache memory. Explain the concept of locality of reference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Cache memory is a small, high-speed SRAM-based memory located between the CPU and main memory. It stores a subset of the data and instructions most likely to be used by the CPU, reducing the average memory access time.

> **Locality of Reference (1.5 marks):** Programs exhibit two key locality patterns:
> 1. **Temporal Locality:** If a memory location is referenced now, it is likely to be referenced again in the near future (e.g., loop counters, frequently used variables).
> 2. **Spatial Locality:** If a memory location is referenced, nearby locations are likely to be referenced soon (e.g., sequential instruction execution, array traversal).
>
> Cache memory exploits both forms of locality to achieve high hit ratios.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Write-Through and Write-Back cache policies.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Write-Through | Write-Back |
|---|---|---|
| **Write Operation** | Updates both cache and main memory simultaneously. | Updates cache only; main memory is updated later when the block is evicted. |
| **Speed** | Slower (every write goes to slow RAM). | Faster (writes complete at cache speed). |
| **Bus Traffic** | High. | Low. |
| **Data Consistency** | Always consistent between cache and RAM. | RAM may be stale; requires a **dirty bit**. |
| **Risk** | None. | Data loss possible on power failure before write-back. |
| **Use Case** | Systems needing strong consistency, e.g., some database buffers. | General-purpose CPUs (most modern processors). |

---

## 📝 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module 4 Convention:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are given an **internal choice** between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### 🔷 **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the three cache mapping techniques: Direct, Associative, and Set-Associative. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Direct Mapping (2 marks)**
- Each main memory block maps to exactly **one specific cache line**.
- Formula: `Cache Line = (MM Block Number) mod (Number of Cache Lines)`.
- Address divided into **TAG | INDEX | OFFSET**.
- **Advantage:** Simple hardware, fast lookup.
- **Disadvantage:** High conflict misses (two popular blocks mapping to the same line cause thrashing).

**2. Fully Associative Mapping (2.5 marks)**
- A main memory block can be placed in **any** cache line.
- Address divided into **TAG | OFFSET** (no index).
- All tags are compared in parallel using associative hardware.
- **Advantage:** Most flexible; minimum conflict misses.
- **Disadvantage:** Expensive, complex, and power-hungry; slow for large caches due to parallel comparator.

**3. Set-Associative Mapping (2.5 marks)**
- Cache is divided into **S sets**, each holding **K lines** (K = associativity, e.g., 2-way, 4-way, 8-way).
- Formula: `Set = (MM Block Number) mod S`. Within the set, block can occupy any of the K ways.
- Address divided into **TAG | SET | OFFSET**.
- **Advantage:** Balances speed and flexibility; widely used in real CPUs (L1/L2 caches are typically 4-way to 16-way set-associative).
- **Disadvantage:** More complex than direct, slightly more expensive than direct, but vastly better miss rate.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A system uses a 2-way set-associative cache of 32 KB capacity. The cache has 64 sets, and the block size is 32 bytes. The main memory size is 16 MB and the system is byte-addressable. Compute:**
**(i) Number of bits in the TAG, SET, and OFFSET fields.**
**(ii) The format of the main memory address.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Cache size = 32 KB = $2^{15}$ bytes
- Number of sets = 64 = $2^6$
- Block size = 32 bytes = $2^5$ bytes
- Main memory = 16 MB → Address bits $n = \log_2(16 \times 2^{20}) = 24$ bits

**Step 1 — Compute OFFSET bits (block offset).**
Offset must address every byte inside the 32-byte block.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b &= \log_2(32) = 5 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute SET index bits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \log_2(64) = 6 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute TAG bits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 24 - 6 - 5 = 13 \text{ bits} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Address format.**

| TAG (13 bits) | SET (6 bits) | OFFSET (5 bits) |
|---|---|---|
| `TTTTTTTTTTTTT` | `SSSSSS` | `OOOOO` |

**Step 5 — Verification (1 mark).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bits} &= 13 + 6 + 5 = 24 \text{ bits} \quad \checkmark \\
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 🔷 **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A computer system has a cache with access time of 80 ns and main memory access time of 800 ns. If the hit ratio is 90%, calculate:**
**(i) Average Memory Access Time (AMAT).**
**(ii) The speedup factor achieved by the cache over a system without cache.**
**(iii) The new AMAT if the hit ratio is improved to 95%.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_c = 80$ ns, $T_m = 800$ ns, $H = 0.90$.

**Step 1 — Miss Penalty.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_p &= T_m - T_c = 800 - 80 = 720 \text{ ns} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — AMAT for H = 0.90 (i + ii).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT} &= T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p \\
&= 80 + (1 - 0.90) \cdot 720 \\
&= 80 + 0.10 \times 720 \\
&= 80 + 72 \\
&= 152 \text{ ns} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup over no-cache system:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Speedup} &= \frac{T_m}{\text{AMAT}} = \frac{800}{152} \approx 5.26 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — AMAT for H = 0.95 (iii).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{AMAT}_{95} &= 80 + (1 - 0.95) \cdot 720 \\
&= 80 + 0.05 \times 720 \\
&= 80 + 36 \\
&= 116 \text{ ns} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 5% increase in hit ratio reduces AMAT from **152 ns → 116 ns**, a **~24% improvement**. This demonstrates why KTU emphasizes hit ratio optimization.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the LRU and FIFO replacement algorithms with suitable examples. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. LRU — Least Recently Used (3.5 marks)**
- **Principle:** Replace the cache line that has not been accessed for the longest time.
- **Rationale:** Past usage predicts future usage (temporal locality).
- **Example:** If Set has 3 lines referenced in order `A, B, C, A, B, D, E` (in chronological order), the LRU at the time of replacement is `A` (least recently used after C was referenced).
- **Advantage:** Best practical hit rate for most workloads.
- **Disadvantage:** Hardware implementation is complex (needs to track access order — counters or stacks) and costly for high associativity (e.g., 16-way).

**2. FIFO — First-In First-Out (3.5 marks)**
- **Principle:** Replace the line that has been in the cache the longest, regardless of usage.
- **Rationale:** Simple queue-based eviction.
- **Example:** For the same reference stream, eviction order would be based on load time, not last-access time.
- **Advantage:** Very simple hardware — just a circular pointer/queue per set.
- **Disadvantage:** May evict a heavily used line simply because it was loaded long ago (Belady's anomaly — increasing cache size can increase misses in FIFO!).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Cache Memory Problems:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Cache sizes are often given in KB, but block sizes in *bytes* or *words*. Always normalize to **bytes** before computing bits.
> 2. **Address Bits Assumption:** KTU 2024 frequently assumes **byte-addressable** memory, giving address bits = $\log_2(\text{Memory in bytes})$. Students often mistakenly use word addressability.
> 3. **Word Size vs Block Size:** A "block of 4 words" does NOT mean 4 bits. Multiply words × word size (e.g., 4 words × 4 bytes/word = 16 bytes).
> 4. **AMAT Formula Variant:** KTU accepts **both** $T_c + (1-H)T_p$ and $H\cdot T_c + (1-H)(T_c + T_p)$ — use whichever the question implies. Read carefully whether $T_p$ is the *extra* penalty or the *total* miss time.
> 5. **Set-Associative Address Split:** Many students confuse "set index" with "line index". In K-way set-associative, address = TAG | **SET** | OFFSET (NOT TAG | LINE | OFFSET).
> 6. **Replacement Policy in Direct Mapping:** Since each block has a fixed home, **no replacement algorithm is needed** in direct-mapped caches. Writing LRU for direct mapping will cost marks.
> 7. **Write-Back + Dirty Bit:** Always mention the **dirty bit** explicitly when describing write-back. Examiners allocate marks for it.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cache Memories (Module 4):**

- **Definition:** Small, fast **SRAM** between CPU and DRAM; exploits **locality of reference**.
- **Two Localities:** **Temporal** (recent → reused soon) and **Spatial** (nearby → accessed soon).
- **Key Metrics:** Hit Ratio ($H$), Miss Ratio ($1-H$), Hit Time ($T_c$), Miss Penalty ($T_p$).
- **Golden Formula:** $\text{AMAT} = T_c + (1 - H) \cdot T_p$. Know the alternate form too.
- **Three Mappings:**
  - **Direct** → 1 block per home line. Fastest, most conflicts.
  - **Associative** → Any line. Most flexible, most expensive.
  - **Set-Associative (K-way)** → Practical compromise. **Most used in real CPUs.**
- **Address Format (Set-Associative):** `TAG | SET INDEX | BLOCK OFFSET`.
- **Bit Formulas:** $b = \log_2(\text{Block Size in bytes})$, $s = \log_2(\text{Number of Sets})$, $t = n - s - b$.
- **Replacement (Associative only):** **LRU** = best, **FIFO** = simplest (Belady's anomaly), **LFU** = frequency-based, **Random** = cheapest.
- **Write Policies:**
  - **Write-Through:** cache + RAM both updated. Slow, consistent.
  - **Write-Back:** cache only, with **dirty bit**; RAM updated on eviction. Fast, risk on crash.
- **Performance Insight:** A small drop in $H$ causes a *large* increase in AMAT. Even 5% miss-rate reduction can yield ~25% AMAT improvement.
- **Real-world Cache Hierarchy:** L1 (per-core, ~32 KB) → L2 (per-core, ~256 KB–1 MB) → L3 (shared, ~4–64 MB).
- **Exam Hot-Buttons:** Address-bit decomposition, AMAT calculation, mapping comparison, replacement tracing, write-policy comparison.
- **Don't Confuse:** *Block size* (data per transfer) vs *Cache size* (total capacity) vs *Number of lines* (capacity / block size).
- **Remember:** Direct-mapped caches need **no replacement algorithm**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Improving Cache Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Improving Cache Performance # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is "Improving Cache Performance"? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Improving Cache Performance* refers to a set of well-established architectural and compiler-level techniques designed to **reduce the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)** of a memory hierarchy. These techniques are traditionally classified by their effect on the three pillars of cache performance — **miss rate, miss penalty, and hit time** — and the bandwidth between cache and processor. In KTU Module-4 terms, the processor–memory gap (the *Memory Wall*) is bridged using **ten advanced optimizations** of the cache, originally systematized by Hennessy and Patterson. These are universally tested in Part-A and Part-B questions. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Office Filing Cabinet Imagine you are an accountant working in a tall office building: - **Cache (L1)** = The top drawer of your desk — small, extremely fast access, but limited storage. - **Cache (L2)** = A small filing cabinet beside you — bigger, slightly slower, but still close. - **Main Memory (RAM)** = The central archives in the basement — huge, but every trip downstairs takes **5 minutes**. - **Hit** = You find the file in your top drawer. Done. - **Miss** = Not in your drawer. Now you have to walk to the cabinet, and if not there, all the way to the basement. To *improve performance*, you can: - 🔹 **Buy a bigger drawer** (reduce miss rate). - 🔹 **Add another filing cabinet on the floor** (multi-level cache — reduce miss penalty). - 🔹 **Hire an intern to fetch files for you** (non-blocking/prefetch — hide latency). - 🔹 **Keep files alphabetically, not randomly** (associativity, write policies). - 🔹 **Pre-arrange tomorrow's files tonight** (compiler/hardware prefetch). Every cache optimization maps to one of these strategies. ## 1.3 The Performance Triangle > [!NOTE] > **The Three Levers of Cache Performance:** > 1. **Miss Rate** — *How often do we miss?* > 2. **Miss Penalty** — *How long does each miss take?* > 3. **Hit Time** — *How long does a hit take to service?* A fourth, modern lever — **Cache Bandwidth** — was added later to handle simultaneous instructions and data accesses (pipelined, multibanked caches). The fundamental equation that ties them all together is: $$ \text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + \text{Miss Rate} \times T_{\text{miss}} $$ Every optimization in this module is judged by how it moves one or more of these levers. # 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Ten Advanced Cache Optimizations (Standard KTU Framework) These are the **ten canonical techniques** as mandated by the KTU PERUT418 syllabus. Each is analyzed below under the lever it primarily affects. ### 🔻 Category 1: Reducing the Miss Rate #### (1) Larger Block Size - **Idea:** Increase the block/line size (e.g., 32 B → 64 B). - **Why it helps:** Exploits **spatial locality** — adjacent data is fetched together. - **Why it can hurt:** **Conflict and capacity misses** can rise, and **miss penalty grows** because more useless data is transferred. There exists an *optimal block size* (typically 16–64 bytes). - **Real-world use:** Most modern L1 caches use 64-byte lines (Intel/AMD). #### (2) Bigger Cache - **Idea:** Increase cache capacity. - **Effect:** Reduces **capacity misses** (but cannot help conflict misses if associativity is fixed). - **Drawback:** Higher **hit time** and increased cost/die area. #### (3) Higher Associativity - **Idea:** Allow a block to map into multiple sets (N-way set-associative). - **Effect:** Reduces **conflict misses**. - **Drawback:** Increases **hit time** and hardware complexity. The empirical *2:1 cache rule* states that an *N-way set-associative* cache of size *N* equals a *direct-mapped* cache of twice the size, in terms of miss rate. - **Sweet spot:** 2-way to 8-way for L1; 8-way to 16-way for L2. #### (4) Victim Cache - **Idea:** A small, fully-associative **auxiliary cache** placed between L1 and L2. Blocks evicted from L1 are placed in the victim cache; on a miss, L1 and victim cache are checked in parallel. - **Effect:** Drastically reduces **conflict misses** for direct-mapped L1 without increasing its hit time. #### (5) Pseudo-Associative (Column-Associative) Cache - **Idea:** Looks like a direct-mapped cache on a *hit*, but on a *miss*, checks a *second alternative location*. - **Effect:** Approaches the miss rate of 2-way with nearly the hit time of direct-mapped. - **Drawback:** Hit time is variable and pipelines become irregular. #### (6) Compiler Optimizations - **Idea:** Software-level techniques: - **Loop Interchange:** Reorder nested loops to access memory in sequential order. - **Loop Fusion:** Combine loops that access the same data. - **Blocking / Tiling:** Restructure loops to operate on cache-sized sub-matrices. - **Effect:** Improves **temporal and spatial locality**, reducing both **compulsory and capacity misses**. ### 🔻 Category 2: Reducing the Miss Penalty #### (7) Multi-Level Caches - **Idea:** Add an L2 (and sometimes L3) between L1 and main memory. - **Effect:** A miss in L1 is often a *hit* in L2, dramatically reducing the effective miss penalty. - **AMAT formula extension:** $$ \text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit, L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times T_{\text{hit, L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times T_{\text{mem}} $$ #### (8) Giving Read Priority over Writes on a Miss - **Idea:** When a write-back buffer exists, on a read miss, let the read bypass waiting writes. Reduces read miss penalty. #### (9) Early Restart and Critical Word First - **Critical Word First:** On a miss, the requested word is fetched **first**, then forwarded to the CPU before the rest of the block. The CPU can resume work earlier. - **Early Restart:** Fetch words in normal order, but as soon as the requested word arrives, restart the processor. ### 🔻 Category 3: Reducing the Hit Time #### (10) Small and Simple Caches - **Idea:** Keep L1 small and direct-mapped. The smaller the cache, the shorter the index decoding, hence lower hit time. - **Effect:** Lowers $T_{\text{hit}}$ drastically. > [!NOTE] > **Bonus technique (commonly tested): Way Prediction** > Predicts which "way" of a set-associative cache will hit, allowing a *single tag compare* in most cycles. Cuts hit time without losing associativity benefits. ### 🔻 Category 4: Increasing Cache Bandwidth - **Pipelined Caches:** Split cache access into multiple stages to increase throughput. - **Multibanked Caches:** Divide cache into independent banks that can service simultaneous requests (e.g., separate I-cache and D-cache, or 4 banks to support 2 reads + 2 writes/cycle). - **Non-Blocking (Lockup-Free) Caches:** Allow the cache to service further hits/misses while one miss is in progress, enabling *out-of-order* memory operations. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > All KTU numerical problems on this topic collapse to one of the following formulas. Memorize them! | # | Formula | Meaning | Unit | |---|---------|---------|------| | 1 | $\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times T_{\text{miss}}$ | Single-level cache | ns | | 2 | $\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit,L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}}\bigl(T_{\text{hit,L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \cdot T_{\text{mem}}\bigr)$ | Two-level cache | ns | | 3 | $\text{CPU Time} = \text{IC} \times \text{CPI}_{\text{base}} \times \text{Clock} + \text{Mem Stall Cycles}$ | Performance basis | cycles | | 4 | $\text{MemStall} = \text{IC} \times \text{Misses}_{\text{per\_instr}} \times T_{\text{miss}}$ | Stall cycles | cycles | | 5 | $2:1 \text{ Cache Rule}$ | N-way of size S $\approx$ Direct-mapped of size 2S | — | | 6 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{\text{AMAT}_{\text{old}}}{\text{AMAT}_{\text{new}}}$ | Comparison | unitless | | 7 | $\text{Total Miss Rate} = 1 - (1-\text{MR}_{\text{L1}})(1-\text{MR}_{\text{L2}})$ | Local vs global | fraction | | 8 | $\text{Bandwidth} = \dfrac{\text{Block Size}}{\text{Hit Time}}$ | Effective throughput | B/ns | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Optimization Used | |---|---| | **Intel i7/i9** | 3-level cache, way prediction, prefetch, multibanked | | **ARM Cortex-A** | Pseudo-associative, critical-word-first, NEON tiled loops | | **GPU (NVIDIA)** | Victim caches, read-priority writes, software prefetch | | **DSP / Embedded** | Loop tiling in compilers, lockup-free caches | | **Databases** | Buffer pools, blocking algorithms (compiler-style) | These optimizations are not academic — they are the **lifeline of every modern CPU shipped since 2005**. # 📒 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of AMAT for a Multi-Level Cache We begin with the single-level AMAT and extend it using conditional probability. ### Step 1 — Single-level formula $$ \text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times T_{\text{miss}} $$ **Logic:** A memory access either hits the cache (cost $T_{\text{hit}}$) or misses (probability MR) and then pays $T_{\text{miss}}$. ### Step 2 — Introduce a second level On an L1 miss, the access goes to L2. So the penalty of an L1 miss is no longer $T_{\text{mem}}$ but a *new AMAT* starting at L2: $$ T_{\text{miss,L1}} = T_{\text{hit,L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times T_{\text{mem}} $$ ### Step 3 — Substitute back $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT} &= T_{\text{hit,L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times T_{\text{miss,L1}} \\ &= T_{\text{hit,L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times \bigl(T_{\text{hit,L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times T_{\text{mem}}\bigr) \end{aligned} $$ This is the **standard KTU 14-mark formula**. It is fully derived from the principle of conditional expectation. ### Step 4 — Generalize to N levels $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT}_{N} &= T_{\text{hit,L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \cdot T_{\text{hit,L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}}\text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \cdot T_{\text{hit,L3}} + \ldots \\ &\quad + \prod_{i=1}^{N-1} \text{MR}_{\text{Li}} \cdot T_{\text{mem}} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 (Single-level AMAT) **Given:** - $T_{\text{hit}} = 1$ ns - Miss rate $\text{MR} = 5\% = 0.05$ - $T_{\text{miss}} = 50$ ns **Find:** AMAT. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT} &= T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times T_{\text{miss}} \\ &= 1 \text{ ns} + 0.05 \times 50 \text{ ns} \\ &= 1 \text{ ns} + 2.5 \text{ ns} \\ &= 3.5 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** AMAT = **3.5 ns**. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 (Two-level Cache) **Given:** - L1: $T_{\text{hit,L1}} = 1$ ns, $\text{MR}_{\text{L1}} = 8\%$ - L2: $T_{\text{hit,L2}} = 5$ ns, $\text{MR}_{\text{L2}} = 30\%$ - Main memory: $T_{\text{mem}} = 100$ ns **Find:** AMAT. **Solution:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT} &= T_{\text{hit,L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times \bigl(T_{\text{hit,L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times T_{\text{mem}}\bigr) \\ &= 1 + 0.08 \times (5 + 0.30 \times 100) \\ &= 1 + 0.08 \times (5 + 30) \\ &= 1 + 0.08 \times 35 \\ &= 1 + 2.8 \\ &= 3.8 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** AMAT = **3.8 ns**. Notice how L2 dramatically reduces the *effective* main-memory latency penalty from 8 ns (= 0.08 × 100) to 2.8 ns. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 (Speedup from Multi-level) **Compare**: - Cache A (single-level): $T_{\text{hit}} = 2$ ns, $\text{MR} = 5\%$, $T_{\text{miss}} = 100$ ns. - Cache B (two-level): $T_{\text{hit,L1}} = 2$ ns, $\text{MR}_{\text{L1}} = 5\%$, $T_{\text{hit,L2}} = 10$ ns, $\text{MR}_{\text{L2}} = 40\%$, $T_{\text{mem}} = 100$ ns. **Find:** Speedup of B over A. **Solution A (single-level):** $$ \text{AMAT}_A = 2 + 0.05 \times 100 = 2 + 5 = 7 \text{ ns} $$ **Solution B (two-level):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT}_B &= 2 + 0.05 \times (10 + 0.40 \times 100) \\ &= 2 + 0.05 \times (10 + 40) \\ &= 2 + 0.05 \times 50 \\ &= 2 + 2.5 = 4.5 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **Speedup:** $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{7}{4.5} \approx 1.56 $$ > **Result:** B is **1.56× faster** in AMAT. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 4 (Block Size Trade-off) A cache designer compares two block sizes for the same capacity of 32 KB: | Block size | Miss rate | Hit time | Miss penalty | |------------|-----------|----------|--------------| | 16 B | 4.0 % | 1 ns | 50 ns | | 64 B | 2.0 % | 1.5 ns | 70 ns | **Find:** Which block size is better? Compute AMAT for both. **AMAT for 16 B:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT}_{16} &= 1 + 0.04 \times 50 = 1 + 2 = 3 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ **AMAT for 64 B:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT}_{64} &= 1.5 + 0.02 \times 70 = 1.5 + 1.4 = 2.9 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** **64-byte blocks win** (2.9 ns < 3.0 ns). The 50 % miss-rate reduction outweighs the 50 % increase in miss penalty, *only* because hit time grew modestly. This is the trade-off examiners love to test. --- ## 3.6 Worked Numerical Problem 5 (Multi-banked / Bandwidth) **Given:** 4-bank L1 cache, each bank serves one access in 2 ns. The CPU issues 2 loads + 1 store per cycle. - Single-bank: serial → 3 × 2 = 6 ns - 4-bank: parallel → 1 × 2 = 2 ns **Effective bandwidth gain:** $$ \frac{6}{2} = 3 \times \text{ throughput} $$ > **Conclusion:** Multibanking is the *only* way to maintain bandwidth as issue width grows — a common modern-exam concept. # 📕 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Taxonomy of the Ten Cache Optimizations This diagram shows the canonical KTU classification tree, mapping every optimization to the lever it primarily moves. ```mermaid graph TD R["Improving Cache Performance
Reduce AMAT"] R --> A["1. Reduce Miss Rate"] R --> B["2. Reduce Miss Penalty"] R --> C["3. Reduce Hit Time"] R --> D["4. Increase Cache Bandwidth"] A --> A1["Larger Block Size"] A --> A2["Larger Cache Capacity"] A --> A3["Higher Associativity"] A --> A4["Victim Cache"] A --> A5["Pseudo-Associative Cache"] A --> A6["Compiler Optimizations"] B --> B1["Multi Level Caches L2 L3"] B --> B2["Read Priority over Writes"] B --> B3["Critical Word First"] B --> B4["Early Restart"] C --> C1["Small and Simple L1"] C --> C2["Way Prediction"] C --> C3["Trace Cache"] D --> D1["Pipelined Cache Access"] D --> D2["Multibanked Caches"] D --> D3["Non Blocking Lockup Free Cache"] ``` ## 4.2 AMAT Computation Flow (Single Level) This diagram walks through how a memory access resolves into hit or miss, and how AMAT is computed. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CPU issues memory address"] --> B["Lookup in L1 Cache"] B -->|Hit| C["Return data to CPU in T_hit"] B -->|Miss| D["Penalty T_miss
go to L2 or Main Memory"] D --> E["Block fetched from lower level"] E --> F["Data returned to CPU"] C --> G["AMAT = T_hit + MR x T_miss"] F --> G ``` ## 4.3 Multi-Level Cache Access Path ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CPU issues access"] --> B["L1 Cache Lookup"] B -->|Hit L1 92 percent| C["Return data
T_hit_L1 = 1 ns"] B -->|Miss L1 8 percent| D["L2 Cache Lookup"] D -->|Hit L2 70 percent| E["Return data
T_hit_L2 = 5 ns"] D -->|Miss L2 30 percent| F["Main Memory Access
T_mem = 100 ns"] E --> G["AMAT = T_hit_L1 + MR_L1 x T_hit_L2 + MR_L1 x MR_L2 x T_mem"] F --> G C --> G ``` ## 4.4 Victim-Cache Organization A victim cache sits *between* L1 and L2, catching recently evicted lines. This is a favourite diagram in KTU 14-mark questions. ```mermaid graph LR CPU["CPU Core"] -->|Read or Write| L1["L1 Cache
Direct Mapped
Fast, but conflict prone"] L1 -->|Evicted line| VC["Victim Cache
Fully Associative
Small 4 to 16 entries"] VC -->|On miss| L2["L2 Cache
Larger, Slower"] L2 --> MEM["Main Memory"] L1 -.->|Parallel lookup on miss| VC ``` ## 4.5 Multi-Banked Cache Architecture Used to provide parallel access ports to keep up with wide-issue superscalar processors. ```mermaid graph TB CPU["Wide Issue CPU
2 Loads + 1 Store"] --> CTRL["Bank Controller"] CTRL --> B0["Bank 0"] CTRL --> B1["Bank 1"] CTRL --> B2["Bank 2"] CTRL --> B3["Bank 3"] B0 --> TAG["Tag Array"] B1 --> TAG B2 --> TAG B3 --> TAG TAG --> RESULT["Result Multiplexer"] B0 --> RESULT B1 --> RESULT B2 --> RESULT B3 --> RESULT ``` ## 4.6 Critical Word First Transfer Shows how the requested word reaches the CPU *before* the rest of the block. ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant CPU participant Cache as L1 Cache participant Mem as Main Memory CPU ->> Cache: Request address 0x1004 Cache ->> Mem: Miss - request block 0x1000-0x103F Mem ->> CPU: Word at 0x1004 arrives FIRST Note over CPU: CPU resumes work early Mem ->> Cache: Words 0x1000, 0x1008 ... 0x103F Note over Cache: Block fully installed ``` # 📙 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) for a single-level cache. Why is it the central metric for evaluating cache optimizations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **AMAT** is the *expected* time per memory access, given hits and misses: > $$\text{AMAT} = T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times T_{\text{miss}}$$ > where $T_{\text{hit}}$ is the cache hit time, MR the miss rate, and $T_{\text{miss}}$ the miss penalty. > > It is central because it captures **all three** performance levers (miss rate, miss penalty, hit time) in a single time-based metric, directly translatable to CPU clock cycles and hence overall execution time. **(3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is a *victim cache*? How does it reduce conflict misses in a direct-mapped L1?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **victim cache** is a small, fully-associative auxiliary cache placed between L1 and L2. When a block is evicted from L1, it is installed in the victim cache. On an L1 miss, both L1 *and* the victim cache are searched in parallel. **(2 marks)** > > Because the victim cache is fully associative, blocks that would have been kicked out by a *direct-mapped* collision can be retained, eliminating many conflict misses without raising the L1 hit time. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > In AMAT numericals, students **commonly lose 4–5 marks** by: > 1. **Forgetting to add $T_{\text{hit}}$** before the miss component. > 2. **Mixing local and global miss rates** — a local L2 miss rate is *conditional* on reaching L2; the *global* L2 miss rate is $\text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times \text{MR}_{\text{L2}}$. > 3. **Wrong units** (ns vs cycles) — always convert and mention. > 4. **Skipping the speedup ratio** when "compare" is asked. > 5. **Writing only the final AMAT** without showing the substituted step — KTU demands the *step-by-step* substitution. --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **ten advanced cache optimization techniques** classified by their effect on **miss rate, miss penalty, and hit time**. For each, state the trade-off involved. **(b) [7 Marks]** A system has the following memory hierarchy: | Level | Hit Time | Miss Rate | |-------|----------|-----------| | L1 | 1 ns | 6 % | | L2 | 6 ns | 30 % (local) | | Main Memory | 100 ns | — | Compute the **AMAT**. If a *third-level* (L3) cache is added with $T_{\text{hit,L3}} = 20$ ns and $\text{MR}_{\text{L3}} = 50\%$, recompute the AMAT and find the **speedup**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] | Lever | Technique | Trade-off (1 line each) | |-------|-----------|-------------------------| | Miss Rate ↓ | Larger block size | More useless data transferred → larger miss penalty | | Miss Rate ↓ | Larger cache | Higher cost & higher hit time | | Miss Rate ↓ | Higher associativity | Slower hit time, more tag-comparison hardware | | Miss Rate ↓ | Victim cache | Extra tag-check on every L1 miss | | Miss Rate ↓ | Pseudo-associative cache | Variable hit time complicates pipelining | | Miss Rate ↓ | Compiler optimizations | Programming effort; not all code can be safely reordered | | Miss Penalty ↓ | Multi-level caches | Extra cost, coherence complexity | | Miss Penalty ↓ | Read priority over writes | Pending writes may stall longer | | Miss Penalty ↓ | Critical word first / Early restart | Complex memory controller | | Hit Time ↓ | Small & simple L1 | Higher miss rate | *(1 mark for correct classification into 3 categories, 5 marks for trade-off table, 1 mark for the small/simple trade-off completion.)* --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Step 1 — Original two-level AMAT:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT}_{2L} &= T_{\text{hit,L1}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L1}} \times \bigl(T_{\text{hit,L2}} + \text{MR}_{\text{L2}} \times T_{\text{mem}}\bigr) \\ &= 1 + 0.06 \times (6 + 0.30 \times 100) \\ &= 1 + 0.06 \times (6 + 30) \\ &= 1 + 0.06 \times 36 \\ &= 1 + 2.16 \\ &= 3.16 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ > **AMAT (2-level) = 3.16 ns** — *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for correct substitution)*. **Step 2 — Add L3:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{AMAT}_{3L} &= 1 + 0.06 \times \bigl(6 + 0.30 \times (20 + 0.50 \times 100)\bigr) \\ &= 1 + 0.06 \times \bigl(6 + 0.30 \times (20 + 50)\bigr) \\ &= 1 + 0.06 \times \bigl(6 + 0.30 \times 70\bigr) \\ &= 1 + 0.06 \times (6 + 21) \\ &= 1 + 0.06 \times 27 \\ &= 1 + 1.62 \\ &= 2.62 \text{ ns} \end{aligned} $$ > **AMAT (3-level) = 2.62 ns** — *(3 marks)*. **Step 3 — Speedup:** $$ \text{Speedup} = \frac{3.16}{2.62} \approx 1.206 $$ > **Speedup ≈ 1.21×** — *(1 mark)*. --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **direct-mapped, set-associative, and fully-associative** caches. With neat diagrams, show how a **victim cache** is organized and explain how it reduces conflict misses. **(b) [7 Marks]** A processor runs at 2 GHz with a base CPI of 1.0. The L1 cache has: - Miss rate = 5 % of all instructions - Miss penalty = 60 cycles (main memory access) Calculate the **actual CPI** with and without the cache. If a **second-level cache** is added that absorbs 90 % of L1 misses in 12 cycles, recompute the CPI. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **Comparison table (3 marks):** | Property | Direct-Mapped | N-way Set-Associative | Fully Associative | |----------|---------------|-----------------------|-------------------| | Mapping | 1 location | N locations | Anywhere | | Conflict misses | High | Reduced | None | | Hardware | Cheap | Moderate | Expensive (CAM) | | Hit time | Fastest | Slightly slower | Slowest | | Use case | L1 | L2 | TLB, Victim cache | **Victim cache diagram & explanation (4 marks):** See Section 4.4 diagram. > "The victim cache is a small (4–16 entry) fully-associative buffer between L1 and L2. Evicted L1 lines are stored here. On an L1 miss, both L1 and the victim cache are checked in parallel, recovering a fraction of conflict-missed lines at near-L1 speed." --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Given:** - Clock = 2 GHz → cycle time = 0.5 ns - Base CPI = 1.0 - Miss rate = 5 % = 0.05 - Miss penalty = 60 cycles **Step 1 — CPI without cache (all accesses go to memory):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CPI}_{\text{no cache}} &= 1.0 + 0.05 \times 60 = 1.0 + 3.0 = 4.0 \end{aligned} $$ > **CPI (no cache) = 4.0** — *(2 marks)*. **Step 2 — Wait, re-read:** The "no cache" condition here means *all* memory accesses go to main memory. With a cache, the misses are 5 %. So the difference is: - **Without cache** (hypothetical 100 % miss rate): CPI = 1 + 1.0 × 60 = **61.0** - **With L1 only**: CPI = 1 + 0.05 × 60 = **4.0** > **CPI (L1 only) = 4.0** — *(2 marks)*. **Step 3 — With L2 absorbing 90 % of L1 misses in 12 cycles:** Effective L2 miss penalty per L1 miss = 0.90 × 12 + 0.10 × 60 = 10.8 + 6 = 16.8 cycles. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{CPI}_{\text{L1+L2}} &= 1.0 + 0.05 \times 16.8 \\ &= 1.0 + 0.84 \\ &= 1.84 \end{aligned} $$ > **CPI (L1+L2) = 1.84** — *(3 marks: 1 for blending, 2 for final)*. **Speedup from adding L2 over L1-only:** $$ \frac{4.0}{1.84} \approx 2.17 \times $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module-4** > > - ✅ **AMAT** = $T_{\text{hit}} + \text{MR} \times T_{\text{miss}}$ — *always* add hit time first. > - ✅ **Two-level AMAT** — substitute L2-AMAT as the *miss penalty* of L1. > - ✅ **Ten optimizations**, three categories — know at least *two trade-offs per category*. > - ✅ **2:1 cache rule** — *N-way* of size *S* matches direct-mapped of size *2S* in miss rate. > - ✅ **Victim cache** — small, fully-associative, sits *between* L1 and L2. > - ✅ **Pseudo-associative** — direct-mapped *hit*, alternate-location *fallback* on miss. > - ✅ **Critical Word First** = requested word arrives *first*; rest of block follows. > - ✅ **Early Restart** = whole block fetched in order, processor resumes at requested word. > - ✅ **Multibanked caches** increase **bandwidth**, not necessarily reduce latency. > - ✅ **Non-blocking / lockup-free** caches overlap multiple misses → *hit-under-miss*. > - ✅ **Way Prediction** cuts hit time in set-associative caches. > - ✅ **Compiler optimizations** (loop interchange, fusion, blocking) reduce **miss rate** at *zero hardware cost*. > - ✅ **Local vs Global miss rate** — never mix them in a 14-mark AMAT numerical. > - ✅ Always show **substitution step-by-step** in KTU AMAT problems. > - ✅ **CPI = CPI_base + Misses_per_instruction × Miss_penalty** — the canonical performance equation.

topics.title: Virtual Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Virtual Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Module 4 — Virtual Memory: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Virtual Memory** is a memory management technique that provides an *illusion* of a very large, contiguous, private logical address space to every process, even though the process may physically reside only partially in main memory (RAM) and the rest on secondary storage (Hard Disk / SSD).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Virtual memory is a storage allocation scheme in which secondary memory can be addressed as though it were a part of the main memory. It addresses programs by virtual addresses, which are translated (mapped) into physical addresses by the Memory Management Unit (MMU)."*

---

## 🎯 Why Do We Need Virtual Memory?

In a multiprogramming system, many processes compete for a limited physical RAM. Without virtual memory:
- The entire program must reside in RAM to execute.
- A program larger than RAM cannot run.
- Processes can overwrite each other's memory (without protection).

Virtual Memory solves these problems:
- **Program Size Independence:** Programs larger than physical memory can execute.
- **Efficient Memory Use:** Only actively used "pages" need to be in RAM.
- **Process Isolation & Protection:** Each process sees its own private address space.
- **Simplified Programming:** Programmer can write code without worrying about physical memory sizes.

---

## 💡 Real-World Analogy: The "Infinite Desk" Illusion

Imagine you are a student writing a 500-page research thesis. Your desk only fits **50 pages** at a time.

- You keep your entire thesis in a **filing cabinet** (secondary memory / Hard Disk).
- On your desk (main memory / RAM), you keep only the **50 pages you are currently writing**.
- When you need a new page, you **swivel**, file away pages you no longer need, and bring new pages onto your desk.
- An index/register on your desk tells you where each page is in the cabinet.
- You *feel* as if your entire 500-page thesis is always in front of you, even though it isn't.

**This is precisely how Virtual Memory works:**
- 🗄️ **Filing Cabinet** = Hard Disk / Secondary Storage
- 🪑 **Desk** = Main Memory (RAM)
- 📄 **Pages** = Fixed-size blocks of the program
- 📇 **Index Register** = Page Table (maintained by the OS)

---

## 📐 Key Terminology (KTU Standard)

| Term | Meaning | Typical Size |
|------|---------|--------------|
| **Virtual Address (Logical Address)** | Address used by the CPU / process | **32-bit or 64-bit** |
| **Physical Address** | Real address in RAM | Determined by RAM size |
| **Page** | Fixed-size block of *logical* memory | **4 KB** (standard) |
| **Frame (Page Frame)** | Fixed-size slot in *physical* memory | Same as page size (**4 KB**) |
| **Page Table** | OS data structure mapping virtual → physical | Maintained per process |
| **MMU (Memory Management Unit)** | Hardware that performs address translation | In CPU |
| **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** | Hardware cache for recent translations | **64–1024 entries** |
| **Page Fault** | Exception when referenced page is NOT in RAM | Handled by OS |
| **Swap Space** | Reserved disk area for evicted pages | On HDD/SSD |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Demand Paging:** The most common implementation of Virtual Memory, in which pages are loaded into main memory **only when demanded** by the CPU (i.e., on first reference). If the page is not in memory, a **page fault** occurs and the OS loads it from disk.

---

## 🔁 The Virtual → Physical Translation (Conceptual Flow)

```
CPU generates Virtual Address (e.g., 0x3A2F)
        │
        ▼
┌──────────────────────┐
│  Page Number (p)     │ ──► Consult Page Table ──► Frame Number (f)
│  Offset (d)          │ ──────────────────────────► Offset (d) stays same
└──────────────────────┘
        │
        ▼
Physical Address = (Frame Number × Page Size) + Offset
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **offset** within a page (called the *displacement* or *d*) is unchanged during translation. Only the *page number* gets mapped to a *frame number* via the Page Table.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1️⃣ Address Translation Mechanism

Virtual Address is split into two parts:
- **Page Number (p):** High-order bits → index into the Page Table
- **Page Offset (d):** Low-order bits → displacement within the page

If virtual address size = `m` bits and page size = `2^n` bytes:
- **Number of bits for offset:** `n` bits → `Page Offset = m - n`
- **Number of pages in process:** `2^m / 2^n = 2^(m-n)` → **Page Number = m - n bits**
- **Number of frames in physical memory:** `2^p / 2^n = 2^(p-n)` (if physical address = `p` bits)

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Page Offset Bits | $n = \log_2(\text{Page Size})$ | Page size in bytes |
| 2 | Number of Virtual Pages | $N_v = 2^{m-n}$ | m = virtual address bits |
| 3 | Number of Physical Frames | $N_f = 2^{p-n}$ | p = physical address bits |
| 4 | Page Table Size | $N_v \times \text{Entry Size}$ | Entry size = 4 bytes typically |
| 5 | **Effective Access Time (with TLB)** | $EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_M) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_M)$ | h = TLB hit ratio, $T_M$ = memory access time |
| 6 | **EAT (with Page Faults)** | $EAT = (1-p) \cdot T_M + p \cdot T_{PF}$ | p = page fault rate, $T_{PF}$ = page fault service time |
| 7 | **EAT (TLB + Page Fault)** | $EAT = (1-p) \cdot [(1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB}+T_M) + h \cdot (T_{TLB}+2T_M)] + p \cdot T_{PF}$ | Combined formula |
| 8 | Page Fault Service Time | $T_{PF} = T_{trap} + T_{read} + T_{restart}$ | Trap + Disk I/O + Restart |
| 9 | Memory Access Time | $T_M$ | Typically **100 ns** |
| 10 | Disk Access Time | $T_{disk}$ | Typically **10 ms (10,000,000 ns)** |

> [!WARNING]
> When using `|` (absolute value) in markdown table rows, ALWAYS write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$. Avoid the literal `|` symbol inside table cells.

---

## 2️⃣ Page Table Structures

### (a) Single-Level Page Table
- One table per process; one entry per virtual page.
- **Problem:** A 32-bit address space with 4 KB pages needs $2^{32-12} = 2^{20}$ entries ≈ **1 million entries** per process.

### (b) Two-Level (Hierarchical) Page Table
- Splits the page number into two parts: outer (page directory) + inner.
- Saves memory because unused virtual address ranges don't need inner tables.

### (c) Inverted Page Table
- One entry per **physical frame** (not per virtual page).
- Size = size of physical memory.
- Used in systems like PowerPC, UltraSPARC.

---

## 3️⃣ Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)

A small, fast, fully-associative hardware cache that stores **recently used page table entries**.

- **TLB Hit:** Page number found in TLB → translation done in 1 cycle.
- **TLB Miss:** Page number not in TLB → consult main page table in RAM (slower).
- **TLB Reach:** `TLB entries × Page size` — amount of memory accessible directly via TLB.

---

## 4️⃣ Page Replacement Algorithms

When a page fault occurs and **all frames are occupied**, the OS must evict (replace) one page.

| Algorithm | Strategy | Pros | Cons |
|-----------|----------|------|------|
| **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) | Replace the oldest page | Simple to implement | Suffers from **Belady's Anomaly** (more frames → more page faults) |
| **Optimal (OPT / MIN)** | Replace page that won't be used for the longest future time | Theoretically minimum faults | Impossible to implement (requires future knowledge) |
| **LRU** (Least Recently Used) | Replace page used longest ago in the past | Near-optimal, no Belady's Anomaly | Expensive to implement (needs counters/stack) |
| **Clock (Second Chance)** | Approximation of LRU using reference bits | Cheap, good performance | Approximate — slightly worse than LRU |

---

## 5️⃣ Thrashing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thrashing** occurs when a system spends **more time swapping pages in/out of memory** than executing the process. It happens when the total working set of all processes exceeds the available physical memory.

### Causes:
- Too many processes competing for too little RAM.
- Poor locality of reference.
- Bad page replacement policy.

### Solution: **Working Set Model**
- Track the set of pages referenced by a process in the last `Δ` (window) memory references.
- If `Σ(Working Sets of all processes) > Total Frames` → suspend a process.

---

## 6️⃣ Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operating Systems:** Windows uses demand paging with clusters; Linux uses a modified Clock algorithm (2Q).
- **Databases:** Use virtual memory for huge datasets (with mmap).
- **Embedded Systems:** Use simpler paging or no paging (e.g., RTOS on microcontrollers).
- **Cloud Servers:** Use overcommit with swap to host more VMs than physical RAM allows (at risk of thrashing).

---

## 7️⃣ Page Fault Service Time (Detailed)

A page fault requires the OS to:

1. **Trap to OS** ($T_{trap}$): ~ 1–10 µs
2. **Check page table:** Confirm the page is invalid or just not loaded.
3. **Find a free frame** (or evict one).
4. **Issue disk I/O** ($T_{read}$): ~ 5–25 ms (depends on disk)
5. **While I/O, CPU may context-switch** to another process.
6. **Update page table & TLB.**
7. **Restart the interrupted instruction** ($T_{restart}$).

$$
T_{PF} \approx T_{trap} + T_{read\_disk} + T_{restart}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Because **disk access is ~100,000× slower than RAM access**, the page fault rate $p$ must be kept **extremely low** (typically $p < 10^{-5}$) for acceptable performance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📘 Derivation 1: Effective Access Time (EAT) with TLB

Let:
- $T_M$ = Memory access time (main RAM)
- $T_{TLB}$ = TLB lookup time
- $h$ = TLB hit ratio (fraction of accesses found in TLB)

### Step 1 — TLB Hit (probability $h$):
CPU accesses TLB (1 access), then accesses memory for data (1 access).

$$
\text{Time}_{\text{hit}} = T_{TLB} + T_M
$$

### Step 2 — TLB Miss (probability $1-h$):
CPU accesses TLB (miss), then accesses page table in memory (1 access), then accesses actual data in memory (1 access).

$$
\text{Time}_{\text{miss}} = T_{TLB} + T_M + T_M = T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_M
$$

### Step 3 — Combine by weighted average:

$$
EAT = h \cdot (T_{TLB} + T_M) + (1-h) \cdot (T_{TLB} + 2 \cdot T_M)
$$

### Step 4 — Expand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= h \cdot T_{TLB} + h \cdot T_M + T_{TLB} + 2T_M - h \cdot T_{TLB} - 2h \cdot T_M \\
&= T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T_M
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This simplified form $EAT = T_{TLB} + (2-h) \cdot T_M$ is the standard KTU answer.

---

## 📘 Derivation 2: EAT with Page Faults Only (No TLB)

Let:
- $p$ = page fault rate
- $T_{PF}$ = page fault service time
- $T_M$ = memory access time

### Step 1 — No page fault (probability $1-p$):

$$
\text{Time}_{\text{nominal}} = T_M
$$

### Step 2 — Page fault (probability $p$):

The full page fault service time dominates. After the page is loaded, the memory is accessed once more:

$$
\text{Time}_{\text{fault}} = T_{PF} + T_M
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks use $T_{PF}$ alone (since memory access is included in the service). We follow the strict KTU convention of including the final memory access.

### Step 3 — Combine:

$$
EAT = (1-p) \cdot T_M + p \cdot (T_{PF} + T_M) = (1-p) \cdot T_M + p \cdot T_{PF} + p \cdot T_M
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= T_M - p \cdot T_M + p \cdot T_M + p \cdot T_{PF} \\
&= T_M + p \cdot T_{PF}
\end{aligned}
$$

So in pure page-fault terms: $EAT = (1-p) \cdot T_M + p \cdot T_{PF}$.

---

## 📘 Derivation 3: Combined EAT (TLB + Page Faults) — KTU Standard Form

### Step 1 — No page fault (probability $1-p$):
Use TLB-aware formula from Derivation 1:

$$
T_{\text{nopagefault}} = T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T_M
$$

### Step 2 — Page fault occurs (probability $p$):

$$
T_{\text{pagefault}} = T_{PF} + T_M + T_{\text{access}}
$$

A typical KTU expression: $T_{\text{pagefault}} = T_{PF} + T_M$

### Step 3 — Combine:

$$
EAT = (1-p) \cdot \big[T_{TLB} + (2-h) \cdot T_M\big] + p \cdot (T_{PF} + T_M)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked KTU question** in Module 4. Memorize it as the **MASTER FORMULA**.

---

## 📘 Worked Example 1 (KTU Numerical — Page Fault EAT)

> **Problem:** A system has a memory access time of $T_M = 200$ ns. The page fault service time is $T_{PF} = 30$ ms = $30 \times 10^6$ ns. If the page fault rate is 1 in 1000 accesses ($p = 0.001$), find the EAT.

### Solution:

Step 1: Convert all times to same unit (ns).
- $T_M = 200$ ns
- $T_{PF} = 30 \times 10^6$ ns
- $p = 0.001$

Step 2: Apply formula $EAT = (1-p) \cdot T_M + p \cdot T_{PF}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - 0.001) \cdot 200 + 0.001 \cdot (30 \times 10^6) \\
&= 0.999 \cdot 200 + 0.001 \cdot 30{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 199.8 + 30{,}000 \\
&= 30{,}199.8 \text{ ns} \approx 30.2 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

### ⚠️ Interpretation:
The page fault slows down memory **151×** (from 200 ns to 30.2 µs). This is why minimizing page faults is critical.

---

## 📘 Worked Example 2 (KTU Numerical — TLB + Page Fault EAT)

> **Problem:** TLB lookup = 20 ns, Memory access = 100 ns, TLB hit ratio = 80%, Page fault rate = 0.01%, Page fault service time = 25 ms. Find EAT.

### Solution:

Step 1: Convert: $T_{TLB}=20$ ns, $T_M=100$ ns, $h=0.8$, $p=0.0001$, $T_{PF}=25 \times 10^6$ ns.

Step 2: Compute no-page-fault EAT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT_{\text{noPF}} &= T_{TLB} + (2 - h) \cdot T_M \\
&= 20 + (2 - 0.8) \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + 1.2 \cdot 100 \\
&= 20 + 120 = 140 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3: Apply combined formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EAT &= (1 - p) \cdot EAT_{\text{noPF}} + p \cdot (T_{PF} + T_M) \\
&= (1 - 0.0001) \cdot 140 + 0.0001 \cdot (25{,}000{,}000 + 100) \\
&= 0.9999 \cdot 140 + 0.0001 \cdot 25{,}000{,}100 \\
&= 139.986 + 2500.01 \\
&= 2640 \text{ ns} \approx 2.64 \text{ µs}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 📘 Worked Example 3 (Address Translation)
