# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section mirrors the **actual KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) pattern**: Part A short-answer questions (3 marks each) and Part B long-answer questions (14 marks each) with internal choice.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between Programmed I/O and Interrupt-driven I/O.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Role** | CPU is fully busy in a polling loop until device is ready. | CPU does other useful work; only services the device when interrupt fires. |
| **I/O Initiation** | CPU repeatedly reads status register. | Device raises an interrupt request (IRQ) when ready. |
| **CPU Utilization** | Low (CPU wastes time polling). | High (CPU is free between interrupts). |
| **Hardware** | Simple I/O module only. | Needs an interrupt controller (e.g., 8259A). |
| **Use Case** | Simple embedded, boot code. | Keyboard, mouse, network packets. |

---

### Question 2: What is DMA? List any two advantages of DMA-based data transfer.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Direct Memory Access (DMA) is a data transfer technique in which a dedicated hardware controller, called the **DMA Controller (DMAC)**, transfers blocks of data directly between an I/O device and the main memory without the continuous involvement of the CPU. The CPU only sets up the DMAC at the start and is interrupted at the end of the transfer.*

**Advantages (2 marks — list any two):**
1. **High CPU Utilization** — the CPU is free to execute other instructions while DMA is in progress, since the DMAC moves the data autonomously.
2. **High Throughput for Block Transfer** — DMA can sustain high data rates (hundreds of MB/s to GB/s) needed by disks and networks, where interrupt-driven I/O would be swamped by per-byte overhead.
3. **Reduced Interrupt Load** — only **one interrupt per block transfer** is generated, regardless of block size, drastically reducing software overhead.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions have **internal choice**: the examiner gives you either Question A *or* Question B (both on the same module). Both are written below for your practice. In the actual exam, choose the one whose sub-parts you are most confident about.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — All Three I/O Techniques + Comparison

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the three I/O data transfer techniques — Programmed I/O, Interrupt-driven I/O, and DMA — with neat diagrams. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Programmed I/O (3 marks)**
- The CPU is in *complete control* of the I/O operation.
- **Steps:**
  1. CPU reads the status register of the I/O module to check the **BUSY** flag.
  2. If BUSY = 1, CPU loops and keeps reading the status register (this is called *polling*).
  3. When BUSY = 0 (device ready), CPU reads from / writes to the *data register* of the I/O module.
  4. The transfer proceeds word-by-word until all data is moved.
- **Diagram (already drawn in Section 4.3).**
- **Drawback:** CPU is *wasted* in the polling loop.

**2. Interrupt-Driven I/O (2 marks)**
- The CPU **issues an I/O command** to the I/O module and then *continues* with other useful work.
- When the I/O module completes the data transfer (or a buffer is full), it **asserts an interrupt request** to the CPU.
- The CPU **suspends** its current task, saves the context, jumps to the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, performs the actual data transfer, restores the context, and **resumes** the previous task.
- **Diagram (already drawn in Section 4.4).**
- **Advantage:** CPU is free to do other work in between interrupts.
- **Drawback:** Interrupt overhead (context save/restore) is large for per-byte transfers.

**3. DMA (2 marks)**
- The CPU programs the **DMA Controller (DMAC)** with the *source address, destination address, byte count,* and *transfer mode*.
- The DMAC then **arbitrates for the system bus** and transfers each data word *directly* between the I/O device and the main memory — *without* CPU involvement.
- At the end of the block transfer, the DMAC **interrupts the CPU** to signal completion.
- **Diagram (already drawn in Section 4.5).**
- **Advantage:** Highest throughput, lowest CPU involvement.
- **Drawback:** Higher hardware cost (DMAC + bus arbiter).

#### (b) Compare the three I/O techniques with respect to CPU utilization, data transfer speed, hardware complexity, and typical use case. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

| Comparison Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU Utilization** | Low (CPU busy in polling loop). | High (CPU free in between). | Very high (CPU free for entire block). |
| **Data Transfer Speed** | Low (limited by CPU's polling rate). | Medium (limited by ISR execution). | High (limited only by bus bandwidth). |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest (just an I/O module). | Medium (needs interrupt controller). | Highest (DMAC + bus arbiter). |
| **Software Complexity** | Low (simple polling loop). | Medium (ISR design, context save). | High (DMA setup, scatter-gather lists). |
| **Interrupt Load** | None (no interrupts). | One per byte / word / block. | One per block. |
| **Typical Use Case** | Simple embedded, boot ROM. | Keyboard, mouse, UART, low-speed. | Disks, SSDs, network cards, GPUs. |
| **KTU Standard Example** | Polling a switch. | Reading a character from UART. | Reading a sector from SATA disk. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** *The choice of I/O technique is a trade-off between hardware cost, software complexity, and the required data throughput. Modern systems use a layered approach — DMA for bulk block transfers, interrupts for moderate-speed events, and programmed I/O for simple boot-time control.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Priority Interrupt Systems

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the **Daisy-Chain Priority Interrupt** scheme with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
In a *daisy-chain* priority interrupt system, multiple I/O devices are connected in a **serial chain** along the priority line. The device that is **physically closest to the CPU has the highest priority**. Each device's interrupt output is *gated* by the interrupt state of the next-higher-priority device. The common interrupt line to the CPU is asserted only by the *highest-priority requesting device*.

**Diagram (3 marks) — redrawn from Section 4.6 in exam style:**

```
                              VCC
                               |
                              [R]
                               |
   +---+    +---+    +---+    +---+    +---> To CPU INT pin
   | D1|----| D2|----| D3|----| D4|
   +---+    +---+    +---+    +---+
    INT      INT      INT      INT
   (H)      (M)      (L)     (Lowest)
```

Each block `D1, D2, D3, D4` represents an I/O device. The chain is wired so that D1's interrupt can pass to the CPU *only* if D1 itself is requesting (otherwise D1 blocks D2's signal from reaching the CPU), and so on down the chain.

**Operation (1 mark):**
- Suppose D1 and D3 both raise INT at the same time.
- D1 is the highest priority, so it gets serviced first.
- D1's gate *masks* D3's signal, so the CPU does not see D3's request.
- After D1's ISR finishes, D1's gate opens, D3's request propagates, and the CPU services D3.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Simple wiring** — only one common interrupt line + one priority chain.
2. **No priority encoder** needed.
3. **Easily extensible** — add more devices by extending the chain.

**Disadvantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Fixed priority** — priority depends on physical position, not programmable.
2. **Propagation delay** — long chains have large gate delays; limits maximum chain length.
3. **Single point of failure** — if one device's gate fails, all downstream devices are blocked.

---

#### (b) Explain the **Parallel Priority Interrupt** scheme with a neat diagram. How does it differ from the daisy-chain scheme? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**
In a *parallel* priority interrupt scheme, each I/O device has a **direct, parallel connection** to a **priority encoder** (typically an 8-to-3 or 16-to-4 encoder). The encoder continuously monitors all interrupt lines, finds the **highest-priority active request**, and presents its **binary code** to the CPU. The CPU uses this code as an **index into the Interrupt Vector Table** to fetch the corresponding ISR address.

**Diagram (3 marks) — redrawn from Section 4.7:**

```
         +---+      +---+
   D1 -->|   |      |   |
   D2 -->| E |=====>|CPU|====> ISR addr from vector table
   D3 -->| N |  ABC |   |
   D4 -->| C |      |   |
   D5 -->|   |      |   |
   D6 -->|   |      |   |
   D7 -->|   |      |   |
   D8 -->|   |      |   |
         +---+      +---+
        8-to-3
       Encoder
```

- The encoder has 8 input lines (one per device) and 3 output lines (the binary code, A B C).
- The 3-bit code is the *priority vector* of the highest-priority requesting device.
- The CPU reads this code and uses it (often via an offset) to fetch the ISR address from the **interrupt vector table** in memory.

**Operation (1 mark):**
- Suppose D2, D5, and D7 all raise interrupts.
- The encoder selects the highest priority among them (D2, with code 010) and presents 010 to the CPU.
- The CPU reads 010, looks up vector table entry 010, gets the address of D2's ISR, and branches to it.
- D5 and D7 remain pending and are serviced later, in priority order.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Fast** — priority resolution in a single gate delay (no chain propagation).
2. **Programmable priority** — the priority can be changed via a *mask register* or by rearranging vector table entries.
3. **No propagation delay** — chain length does not affect speed.
4. **Easy to extend** with cascaded encoders (e.g., 8259A PIC supports up to 64 interrupts via cascading).

**Comparison with Daisy-Chain (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Daisy-Chain | Parallel |
|---|---|---|
| **Wiring** | Serial (1 common line) | Parallel (1 line per device) |
| **Speed** | Slow (chain propagation) | Fast (single encoder delay) |
| **Priority** | Fixed by position | Programmable via mask/vector |
| **Hardware** | Simple | Needs an encoder + vector table |
| **Scalability** | Poor for many devices | Excellent (cascadable) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Easy Marks in KTU Module 4**
>
> 1. **Confusing "I/O module" with "I/O device".** The I/O *module* is the controller/adapter on the system bus. The I/O *device* is the actual peripheral (mouse, disk). Examiners specifically test this distinction. **Loses 1–2 marks** if you interchange them.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the *data register* in the I/O module diagram.** A very common omission. The data register (buffer) is a *mandatory* block in the I/O module architecture diagram. **Loses 1 mark** if missing.
>
> 3. **Writing "DMA is faster than Programmed I/O" without numbers.** Always back up qualitative claims with a quantitative justification. Use the formulas from Section 3 (e.g., DMA frees the CPU for $T_{\text{DMA}}$ seconds; programmed I/O occupies the CPU for the same $T_{\text{DMA}}$).
>
> 4. **Forgetting the formula for *Bits per Frame* in asynchronous serial.** $B_{\text{frame}} = 1 + D + P + S$. The 1 is the *start bit*, which students frequently forget. This costs 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Not labelling which device is highest priority in the daisy-chain diagram.** Always write "*D1 = highest priority, closest to CPU*" — explicitly. The examiner does not infer this.
>
> 6. **Conflating *interrupt latency* with *response time*.** Interrupt latency = time from IRQ assertion to first ISR instruction. Response time = time from request to completion of service. They are *not* the same.
>
> 7. **Skipping units in numericals.** $t_{\text{loop}} = 100\ \mu s$ is not the same as $t_{\text{loop}} = 100\ \text{ms}$. **Always state units.** Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
>
> 8. **Not mentioning bus arbitration in DMA.** DMA cannot work without a *bus arbiter*. If your DMA explanation does not mention that the DMAC must *request and be granted* the system bus, you lose 1 mark.
>
> 9. **Saying "interrupt-driven I/O is faster than DMA."** Wrong. DMA is *faster* for block transfers. Interrupt-driven I/O is *more responsive* for low-latency single events. The two optimize different things.
>
> 10. **Forgetting to compare in Part B sub-parts.** When a 7-mark sub-part says "compare" or "differentiate," the model answer *always* includes a **comparison table**. A purely prose answer will lose 1–2 marks for lack of structure.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Input/Output Organization**
>
> **Core Concepts (must know by heart):**
> - [x] I/O module = controller/adapter on the system bus, with *data register*, *status register*, *control register*, *address decoder*, and *device interface*.
> - [x] Peripheral device = the actual external hardware.
> - [x] Port = the logical address used by the CPU to talk to an I/O module.
> - [x] Three I/O techniques = Programmed I/O, Interrupt-driven I/O, DMA.
> - [x] Two priority schemes = Daisy-chain (serial, fixed) and Parallel (encoder-based, programmable).
> - [x] Two handshake types = Strobed (source-initiated) and REQ/ACK (full handshake).
> - [x] Standard interfaces = PCI, PCIe, USB, SATA, SCSI, RS-232, I²C, SPI.
>
> **High-Yield Formulas (memorize verbatim):**
> - [x] $U_{\text{useful}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{io}}}{n \cdot t_{\text{loop}} + t_{\text{io}}}$
> - [x] $B_{\text{frame}} = 1 + D + P + S$
> - [x] $T_{\text{frame}} = \dfrac{B_{\text{frame}}}{R_{\text{baud}}}$
> - [x] $\eta = \dfrac{D}{B_{\text{frame}}} \times 100\,\%$
> - [x] $T_{\text{DMA,total}} = T_{\text{setup}} + \dfrac{N \cdot B}{R_{\text{DMA}}}$
> - [x] $B_{\text{bus}} = W_{\text{bus}} \times f_{\text{bus}}$
>
> **Diagrams to Practice (must draw from memory in exam):**
> - [x] Block diagram of I/O module (with all 5 internal blocks).
> - [x] System-wide I/O organization (CPU–Bus–I/O Modules–Devices).
> - [x] Programmed I/O flowchart.
> - [x] Interrupt-driven I/O flowchart.
> - [x] DMA block diagram with bus arbiter.
> - [x] Daisy-chain priority diagram.
> - [x] Parallel priority diagram with encoder.
>
> **Common KTU Numerical Patterns:**
> - [x] Polling CPU utilization for $n$ devices.
> - [x] Async serial bits-per-frame and throughput.
> - [x] DMA transfer time and CPU free time.
> - [x] Speedup of DMA over interrupt-driven I/O for a block.
> - [x] Bus bandwidth from width and frequency.
>
> **Golden Rules for KTU Exam Writing:**
> - [x] Always *draw* the diagram before *writing* the explanation.
> - [x] Always include a *comparison table* in "compare/differentiate" questions.
> - [x] Always *state units* in every numerical.
> - [x] Always *justify quantitatively* when claiming "faster" or "more efficient."
> - [x] Always mention *bus arbitration* when discussing DMA.
> - [x] Always *label* the highest-priority device in daisy-chain diagrams.
> - [x] Write in *bullet points* and *headings* — not continuous prose. KTU examiners prefer structured answers.
>
> **One-Line Definitions to Memorize:**
> - *DMA* = *"Direct Memory Access is a technique in which data is transferred between an I/O device and main memory without continuous CPU involvement, using a dedicated DMA controller."*
> - *Interrupt* = *"An interrupt is an asynchronous signal from an I/O device to the CPU, indicating that the device requires immediate attention."*
> - *Bus Arbitration* = *"Bus arbitration is the process of granting a single master (CPU or DMAC) exclusive access to the shared system bus when multiple masters request it simultaneously."*
> - *Daisy-Chain Priority* = *"In daisy-chain priority, devices are connected in series; the device closest to the CPU has the highest priority."*
> - *Handshaking* = *"Handshaking is an asynchronous data transfer protocol in which the sender and receiver exchange request and acknowledge signals to coordinate the transfer."*

You are now fully equipped to score full marks in any KTU 2024 Scheme question on **Input/Output Organization** from Module 4. Practice drawing each diagram at least 5 times before the exam, and solve at least 3 numericals from Section 3 to internalize the formulas. Best of luck!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, DMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT418 - COMPUTER ORGANISATION AND ARCHITECTURE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, DMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Programmed I/O (Polled I/O)

**Formal Definition:**
Programmed I/O is a synchronous data transfer mechanism in which the CPU is wholly responsible for moving data between the I/O device and main memory. The CPU executes a sequence of instructions that repeatedly reads a device's status register until the device reports "ready," after which the CPU reads/writes the data word to/from the data register. Because the CPU actively waits (busy-waits) on the device, no hardware interrupt mechanism is used for this transfer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Programmed I/O is the simplest of the three I/O techniques. The CPU is completely occupied for the entire duration of the data transfer — this is the primary reason it is rarely used for high-speed or high-volume devices in modern systems.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Tea Stall" Story:**
Imagine you (the CPU) go to a tea stall and order a cup of tea. Instead of waiting passively, you stand at the counter and keep asking the shopkeeper every 2 seconds, *"Is my tea ready? Is my tea ready?"* — until the tea arrives. The moment it is ready, you collect it. You are not doing anything else (not reading, not talking) — your entire life is consumed by this polling. This is **Programmed I/O**. The CPU does no useful work during the wait.

---

## 1.2 Interrupt-Driven I/O

**Formal Definition:**
Interrupt-Driven I/O is an asynchronous data transfer technique in which the I/O device, after completing its current operation, raises a hardware interrupt signal to the CPU. The CPU, instead of polling, executes its main program; when the interrupt is acknowledged, the CPU suspends the current program, saves its state, and executes a special routine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** to transfer one word/byte of data between the device and memory. After the ISR completes, the CPU resumes its original program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** Unlike Programmed I/O, the CPU here is **not** busy-waiting. The CPU is freed between data transfers to execute other useful instructions. However, the CPU is *still* involved in transferring every single word of data, since the ISR is executed per data item.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Buzzer" Story:**
You go to a busy restaurant and order food. Instead of standing at the counter, the waiter hands you a **buzzer (pager)** and tells you, *"Go sit down, we'll buzz you when the food is ready."* You sit, read a book, talk with friends — you do useful things. When the buzzer rings, you walk to the counter, pick up your food (this takes a small amount of time), and then sit back down. The buzzer rings again for the next item. You are not free *during* the food pickup, but you are free *between* pickups. This is **Interrupt-Driven I/O**.

---

## 1.3 Direct Memory Access (DMA)

**Formal Definition:**
Direct Memory Access (DMA) is a data transfer technique in which a special hardware controller called the **DMA Controller (DMAC)** takes control of the system bus and transfers a block of data directly between the I/O device and main memory, **without CPU intervention** for each data word. The CPU only sets up the DMA controller (with source address, destination address, and byte count) before the transfer and is interrupted only when the entire block transfer is complete.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Distinction:** DMA is the only technique among the three where the CPU is fully liberated from per-word involvement. The CPU can execute user programs in parallel while the DMAC handles the bulk transfer. The only "stolen" time is the **cycle stealing** — small windows when the DMAC uses the memory bus.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Pipeline" Story:**
You need to move 1000 boxes from your house to a storage unit. Instead of carrying each box yourself (Programmed I/O) or carrying each box after being notified (Interrupt-Driven I/O), you hire a moving company. You give them the addresses and the list of items (setup), and they handle the entire move. Meanwhile, you are free to do anything — work on your laptop, sleep, eat. When they finish, they call you to say "we are done." This is **DMA**. You are involved only at the beginning (setup) and the end (completion).

---

## 1.4 Common Terminology Used Across All Three Techniques

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Device Status Register (DSR)** | A 1-bit/8-bit register indicating if the I/O device is ready |
| **Data Buffer Register (DBR)** | The register that holds the actual data word being transferred |
| **Handshaking** | The protocol by which CPU/device coordinate readiness |
| **Cycle Stealing** | DMA momentarily taking control of memory bus from CPU |
| **Burst Transfer** | DMA holding the bus for the entire block transfer |
| **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)** | The CPU's response routine to a device interrupt |

> [!NOTE]
> All three I/O techniques ultimately use the same underlying physical wires — the **system bus** (Address, Data, Control). The difference lies in **who drives the bus** during the transfer and **when**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Analysis of Programmed I/O

The CPU executes a tight loop until the I/O device signals readiness. Below is the operational sequence:

- **Step 1:** CPU reads the **status register** of the I/O device (polling action).
- **Step 2:** CPU checks the "ready" bit. If not ready → go to Step 1. If ready → proceed to Step 3.
- **Step 3:** CPU reads data from the **data register** (input) or writes data to it (output).
- **Step 4:** CPU may also trigger the device to perform the next I/O operation (e.g., read next sector, print next character).
- **Step 5:** Steps 1–4 repeat for every data word until the entire block is transferred.

**Why it works:** It uses only basic CPU instructions (load, test, branch, store). No additional hardware is required beyond a status flag and a data register.

**How it works:** The CPU is a **slave to the device's speed**. Even if the device is slow (e.g., keyboard, printer), the CPU wastes cycles polling.

**Drawback:** The CPU utilization drops drastically when the device is slow. For instance, if a printer takes 1 ms to print one character and the CPU takes 1 µs to poll, the CPU is 99.9% busy in polling and only 0.1% doing actual work.

---

## 2.2 Operational Analysis of Interrupt-Driven I/O

The device raises an interrupt only when it is ready, freeing the CPU in between.

- **Step 1:** CPU issues an I/O command to the device (e.g., "read a block from disk").
- **Step 2:** CPU returns to its main program and performs useful work.
- **Step 3:** When the device is ready, it asserts the **interrupt request (IRQ)** line.
- **Step 4:** CPU completes the current instruction, then checks for interrupts.
- **Step 5:** CPU saves the **Program Counter (PC)** and **Status Register (PSW)** on the stack.
- **Step 6:** CPU loads the **Interrupt Vector Address** corresponding to the device.
- **Step 7:** CPU executes the **ISR**, which reads/writes one word to the device.
- **Step 8:** ISR returns via the `IRET` (Interrupt Return) instruction, which restores PC and PSW.
- **Step 9:** CPU resumes the interrupted main program.
- **Step 10:** Steps 3–9 repeat for the next word until the block is fully transferred.

**Why it improves efficiency:** The CPU does not poll. It executes useful code between interrupts.

**How it improves efficiency:** Each interrupt has an **overhead** — saving/restoring context, vector lookup, ISR dispatch. For very high-speed devices (e.g., disk at 1 GB/s), per-word interrupts are too costly.

---

## 2.3 Operational Analysis of DMA

The DMAC is essentially a specialized processor that can read/write memory autonomously.

- **Step 1:** CPU programs the DMAC with three key values:
  - **Source/Destination address** (memory or device register)
  - **Byte/Word count** (how much to transfer)
  - **Transfer mode** (burst, cycle-steal, or transparent)
- **Step 2:** CPU issues a START command to the DMAC and returns to its main program.
- **Step 3:** The DMAC requests the system bus via **Bus Request (BR)**. The CPU (via the bus arbitrator) issues **Bus Grant (BG)**.
- **Step 4:** DMAC transfers one word at a time to/from memory. Three operational modes are possible:
  - **Burst Mode:** DMAC holds the bus for the entire block; CPU is **completely blocked** from memory during this time.
  - **Cycle Stealing Mode:** DMAC transfers one word, releases the bus for one CPU cycle, then repeats. CPU execution is slowed but not stopped.
  - **Transparent Mode:** DMAC only uses the bus during CPU cycles that do not access memory (e.g., instruction decode). CPU is unaffected.
- **Step 5:** When the byte count reaches zero, DMAC asserts the **interrupt** to inform the CPU that the transfer is complete.
- **Step 6:** CPU continues with its main program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why DMA is essential for high-speed I/O:** Modern disks transfer at gigabytes per second. At 1 GB/s, a new word arrives every 8 ns. Even the fastest CPU cannot handle an interrupt per word at this rate. DMA solves this by removing the CPU from the per-word transfer path entirely.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Let the following parameters be defined for a block of $n$ data words:
- $T_p$ = CPU processing time per data word (handling one word)
- $T_s$ = Device service time per data word (preparing/furnishing one word)
- $T_o$ = CPU overhead per interrupt (save/restore context, vector fetch, dispatch)
- $T_{setup}$ = CPU time to program the DMAC (one-time, fixed)
- $T_{arb}$ = Bus arbitration time per DMA transfer (small, often ignored)

| Technique | Total Transfer Time $T_{total}$ | CPU Busy Time | CPU Free Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Programmed I/O** | $n \cdot (T_p + T_s)$ | $n \cdot (T_p + T_s)$ | $0$ |
| **Interrupt-Driven I/O** | $n \cdot (T_p + T_s + T_o)$ | $n \cdot (T_p + T_o)$ | $n \cdot T_s$ |
| **DMA (Burst Mode)** | $T_{setup} + n \cdot T_s$ | $T_{setup} + T_{ISR\_final}$ | $\approx n \cdot T_s$ |
| **DMA (Cycle Stealing)** | $T_{setup} + n \cdot (T_s + T_{arb})$ | $T_{setup} + \text{slowdown}$ | $\approx n \cdot T_s$ |

**CPU Utilization Formula:**

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}}$$

- **Programmed I/O:** $U_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_p}{T_p + T_s}$
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** $U_{CPU} = \dfrac{T_p + T_o}{T_p + T_s + T_o}$
- **DMA (Burst):** $U_{CPU} \approx 1$ (CPU is fully free during the transfer; only slowed by brief setup and final ISR)
- **DMA (Cycle Stealing):** $U_{CPU}$ slightly less than 1 due to bus contention

**Throughput Formula (Words per second):**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{n}{T_{total}}$$

**Limiting Case (Very Fast Device):** $T_s \to 0$

- Programmed I/O: $T_{total} \approx n \cdot T_p$ → CPU-bound
- DMA: $T_{total} \approx T_{setup}$ → Best performance

**Limiting Case (Very Slow Device):** $T_s \to \infty$

- Programmed I/O: $U_{CPU} \to 0$ → Catastrophic waste
- DMA: $U_{CPU} \to 1$ → CPU fully utilized for other work

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Programmed I/O** is used in **embedded microcontrollers** (e.g., 8051) for reading simple switches, LEDs, and low-speed sensors where a deterministic loop is acceptable.
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O** is the standard for **keyboards, mice, serial ports (UART), network cards** in low-to-medium speed applications.
- **DMA** is mandatory for **hard disks, SSDs, GPUs, sound cards, network interface cards (NICs), USB 3.0 controllers, NVMe SSDs** — any device whose data rate exceeds what a per-word interrupt overhead can sustain.
- Modern **x86, ARM, and RISC-V** systems all contain a DMA controller; **Intel's 8237 DMAC** and **ARM's PL080** are classic textbook examples.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CPU Utilization for Programmed I/O

**Setup:** Consider an I/O device that needs to transfer a block of $n$ words. The CPU takes $T_p$ seconds per word for I/O handling, and the device takes $T_s$ seconds per word to physically furnish/consume the data.

**Step 1:** The CPU performs $T_p$ seconds of work to handle one word (e.g., read status, read data, store in memory).

**Step 2:** The CPU then waits $T_s$ seconds for the device to be ready for the next word.

**Step 3:** For one word, the total time elapsed is:

$$t_{one} = T_p + T_s$$

**Step 4:** Since the CPU is busy for the entire $t_{one}$ duration (it is polling or it has just finished processing and is immediately checking the status again), the CPU busy time per word is $t_{one}$.

**Step 5:** The CPU utilization is the ratio of busy time to total time:

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{\text{Busy time per word}}{\text{Total time per word}}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute:

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{T_p + T_s}{T_p + T_s} = 1$$

**Step 7:** Wait — this says 100% busy, but we said the CPU is doing "useless" polling. Yes, **from the CPU's perspective it is 100% utilized** — it is not idle. But **effective** utilization (time spent on the main program) is 0%.

> [!NOTE]
> The textbook formula $U_{CPU} = T_p / (T_p + T_s)$ represents the **fraction of CPU time spent on the *user's program* during the I/O wait**. The other fraction $T_s/(T_p + T_s)$ is spent in the I/O wait/poll loop. Together they sum to 1.

**Step 8:** Therefore, the *effective* CPU utilization for the main program is:

$$U_{eff} = \frac{T_p}{T_p + T_s}$$

**Numerical example:** $T_p = 100\,\text{ns}$ (memory access), $T_s = 10\,\mu\text{s} = 10000\,\text{ns}$ (slow device).

$$U_{eff} = \frac{100}{100 + 10000} = \frac{100}{10100} \approx 0.0099 = 0.99\%$$

**Conclusion:** The CPU is wasting **99.01%** of its cycles waiting. This is unacceptable for any system running a multi-task OS.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CPU Utilization for Interrupt-Driven I/O

**Setup:** Same as before, but with an additional $T_o$ — the time for the CPU to handle one interrupt (save state, fetch vector, execute ISR, restore state).

**Step 1:** Per data word, the total elapsed time is:

$$t_{one} = T_p + T_s + T_o$$

**Step 2:** Of this time, the CPU is busy only during $T_p$ (the I/O handling) and $T_o$ (the ISR overhead). It is **idle** during the $T_s$ waiting period between interrupts.

**Step 3:** The CPU busy time per word is:

$$\text{Busy} = T_p + T_o$$

**Step 4:** The CPU utilization (time spent on user program) is:

$$U_{eff} = \frac{T_p}{t_{one}} = \frac{T_p}{T_p + T_s + T_o}$$

**Step 5:** If we consider total CPU activity (including ISR):

$$U_{total} = \frac{T_p + T_o}{T_p + T_s + T_o}$$

**Numerical example:** $T_p = 100\,\text{ns}$, $T_s = 10\,\mu\text{s} = 10000\,\text{ns}$, $T_o = 500\,\text{ns}$ (interrupt overhead).

$$U_{eff} = \frac{100}{100 + 10000 + 500} = \frac{100}{10600} \approx 0.943\%$$

**Improvement over Programmed I/O:** The CPU is now free during the 10000 ns of device wait time. It can do ~10000/100 = **100 memory operations** in that time, which is a 100x improvement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CPU Utilization for DMA

**Setup:** The DMAC takes over the bus. CPU programs it once in $T_{setup}$ time, then is free.

**Step 1:** Total transfer time for $n$ words:

$$T_{total} = T_{setup} + n \cdot T_s$$

**Step 2:** CPU busy time (only setup + final interrupt):

$$\text{Busy}_{CPU} = T_{setup} + T_{ISR_{final}}$$

**Step 3:** Assuming $T_{ISR_{final}} \ll T_{total}$, the CPU effective utilization is:

$$U_{eff} = \frac{T_{setup}}{T_{setup} + n \cdot T_s} \approx \frac{T_{setup}}{n \cdot T_s} \quad \text{for } n \cdot T_s \gg T_{setup}$$

**Step 4:** For large $n$, this approaches 0 — meaning the CPU spends nearly 0% of its time on the I/O operation.

**Numerical example:** $T_{setup} = 1000\,\text{ns}$, $n = 1000$ words, $T_s = 100\,\text{ns}$.

$$T_{total} = 1000 + 1000 \cdot 100 = 101000\,\text{ns}$$

$$U_{eff} = \frac{1000}{101000} \approx 0.99\%$$

**Key insight:** Although $U_{eff}$ looks small here, the difference is that during the 100,000 ns of DMA transfer, the CPU is **executing other user programs**, not waiting. In Programmed/Interrupt I/O, that 100,000 ns would be *lost* (poll) or *consumed by ISR per word*. With DMA, that time is **recovered** for actual user computation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Programmed I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CPU involvement per word** | Yes (poll + transfer) | Yes (ISR per word) | No |
| **CPU free during transfer** | No | Between words only | Yes (during whole block) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Lowest (status + data reg) | Medium (interrupt logic) | Highest (DMAC, bus arbitration) |
| **Software complexity** | Simple loop | ISR coding | DMA driver programming |
| **Best suited for** | Slow, predictable devices | Medium-speed, bursty devices | High-speed, bulk transfers |
| **Latency to first word** | Lowest | Medium | Highest (setup time) |
| **Throughput (bulk)** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Overhead per word** | $T_p$ (poll) | $T_p + T_o$ (ISR) | $T_s$ (DMAC, no CPU) |
| **Power efficiency** | Poor (CPU active) | Good | Best |
| **Example device** | Switch, LED matrix | Keyboard, UART | Disk, SSD, NIC, GPU |

**Latency vs Throughput Trade-off:**

$$\text{Latency}_{PgmIO} < \text{Latency}_{IntIO} < \text{Latency}_{DMA}$$

$$\text{Throughput}_{PgmIO} < \text{Throughput}_{IntIO} < \text{Throughput}_{DMA}$$

**Conclusion:** There is no universally "best" I/O technique. The choice depends on device speed, system load, latency requirements, and hardware cost.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A system uses a disk with the following parameters:
- Block size: $n = 1024$ words
- CPU time to program DMA: $T_{setup} = 5\,\mu\text{s}$
- DMA transfer rate: $5\,\text{MB/s}$ (assume 1 word = 1 byte for simplicity)
- Interrupt handling time per word (if interrupt-driven): $T_o = 2\,\mu\text{s}$
- CPU processing time per word (if programmed): $T_p = 0.5\,\mu\text{s}$

**Find:**
1. Total transfer time for Programmed I/O.
2. Total transfer time for Interrupt-Driven I/O.
3. Total transfer time for DMA.
4. CPU utilization in each case.

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Programmed I/O:**
Time to transfer 1 word at $5\,\text{MB/s}$:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.2\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$T_{total} = n \cdot (T_p + T_s) = 1024 \cdot (0.5 + 0.2) = 1024 \cdot 0.7 = 716.8\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$U_{CPU} = 1.0 \quad \text{(CPU fully busy)}$$

**Part 2 — Interrupt-Driven I/O:**

$$T_{total} = n \cdot (T_p + T_s + T_o) = 1024 \cdot (0.5 + 0.2 + 2) = 1024 \cdot 2.7 = 2764.8\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$U_{CPU} = \frac{T_p + T_o}{T_p + T_s + T_o} = \frac{0.5 + 2}{2.7} = \frac{2.5}{2.7} \approx 0.926 = 92.6\%$$

**Part 3 — DMA:**

$$T_{total} = T_{setup} + n \cdot T_s = 5 + 1024 \cdot 0.2 = 5 + 204.8 = 209.8\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$U_{CPU} \approx \frac{T_{setup}}{T_{total}} = \frac{5}{209.8} \approx 0.0238 = 2.38\%$$

**Interpretation:** DMA is **3.4× faster** than Programmed I/O and **13.2× faster** than Interrupt-Driven I/O. The CPU is essentially free during the DMA block transfer, allowing it to execute other programs.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Common I/O Subsystem

The diagram below shows the **common hardware** used by all three I/O techniques, and how the control flow differs in each.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU"]
    BUSARB["Bus Arbiter"]
    MEM["Main Memory"]
    DMAC["DMA Controller DMAC"]
    DEV["I/O Device"]
    SR["Status Register"]
    DR["Data Register"]

    CPU --> BUSARB
    BUSARB --> MEM
    BUSARB --> DMAC
    BUSARB --> DEV
    DEV --> SR
    DEV --> DR
    DMAC -.->|"BR / BG signals"| BUSARB
    DEV -.->|"IRQ line"| CPU
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The CPU, DMAC, and I/O Device all share the system bus.
- The **Bus Arbiter** grants the bus to either the CPU or the DMAC (never both simultaneously).
- The device raises an **IRQ** line to the CPU for interrupt-driven I/O.
- For programmed I/O, the CPU directly reads `SR` and `DR`. No DMAC, no IRQ needed.

---

## 4.2 Programmed I/O Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Start: Issue I/O command to device"] --> B["Read Status Register SR"]
    B --> C{"Is device ready?"}
    C -- "No" --> B
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Read or Write Data Register DR"]
    D --> E{"Is block transfer complete?"}
    E -- "No" --> B
    E -- "Yes" --> F["End: Return to main program"]
```

**Key observation:** The loop from `B → C → B` is the **busy-wait (polling) loop**. The CPU executes this loop continuously until the device responds.

---

## 4.3 Interrupt-Driven I/O Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Start: CPU issues I/O command"] --> B["CPU returns to main program"]
    B --> C["CPU executes user instructions"]
    C -- "Device raises IRQ" --> D["CPU saves PC and PSW on stack"]
    D --> E["CPU fetches Interrupt Vector"]
    E --> F["CPU executes ISR reads or writes one word"]
    F --> G["ISR returns via IRET"]
    G --> H{"Is block transfer complete?"}
    H -- "No" --> C
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Final ISR signals completion to OS"]
```

**Key observation:** The CPU is in the **"execute user instructions"** state most of the time. It only enters the ISR briefly when the device signals readiness.

---

## 4.4 DMA Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Start: CPU programs DMAC with src dst count mode"] --> B["CPU issues START to DMAC"]
    B --> C["CPU returns to execute user program"]
    C --> D["DMAC requests bus via BR"]
    D --> E["Bus Arbiter grants bus via BG"]
    E --> F["DMAC transfers one word to or from memory"]
    F --> G{"Byte count equals zero?"}
    G -- "No" --> D
    G -- "Yes" --> H["DMAC releases bus and raises interrupt to CPU"]
    H --> I["CPU runs final ISR to acknowledge completion"]
    I --> J["End: Transfer complete"]
```

**Key observation:** The CPU is in the **"execute user program"** state during the entire block transfer. The DMAC and CPU are the only contenders for the memory bus.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Flow: Who Controls the Bus When?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProgrammedI_O["Programmed I/O"]
        P1["CPU reads status"] --> P2["CPU reads data"]
        P2 --> P1
    end

    subgraph InterruptI_O["Interrupt-Driven I/O"]
        I1["CPU executes user code"] --> I2["IRQ arrives"]
        I2 --> I3["CPU runs ISR"]
        I3 --> I1
    end

    subgraph DMA["DMA Transfer"]
        D1["CPU executes user code"] --> D2["DMAC transfers entire block"]
        D2 --> D3["IRQ to signal completion"]
        D3 --> D1
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- In **Programmed I/O**, the **CPU is the only bus master** for the entire duration.
- In **Interrupt-Driven I/O**, the **CPU is the bus master** during ISR, but it is the only one (DMAC not used).
- In **DMA**, the **DMAC becomes the bus master** during the transfer, completely offloading the CPU.

---

## 4.6 DMA Controller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MAR["Memory Address Register MAR"]
    CR["Count Register CR"]
    CR2["Control Register CTRL"]
    SR2["Status Register SR"]
    DA["Data Address Latch"]
    DBUS["System Data Bus"]
    ABUS["System Address Bus"]
    DREQ["DREQ from Device"]
    HRQ["Hold Request HRQ to CPU"]
    HLDA["Hold Acknowledge HLDA from CPU"]

    CR2 --> DA
    CR2 --> CR
    DA --> MAR
    MAR --> ABUS
    CR2 --> SR2
    DREQ --> CR2
    HRQ --> HLDA
    DBUS --> DA
```

**Key components:**
- **MAR:** Holds the current memory address for the next transfer. Auto-incremented after each word.
- **CR:** Counts down (or up) the number of words remaining. Triggers completion interrupt at zero.
- **CTRL:** Holds the transfer mode (read/write, burst/cycle-steal), starting/stopping, and channel selection.
- **DREQ:** Device request line. Device asserts this when ready.
- **HRQ / HLDA:** Bus request / acknowledge handshake with the CPU.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define Programmed I/O. State one advantage and one disadvantage of Programmed I/O.**

**Model Answer:**

Programmed I/O is a data transfer technique in which the CPU is entirely responsible for moving data between the I/O device and memory. The CPU repeatedly reads the device's status register (polling) until the device is ready, then transfers one data word via the data register. This process repeats for every word in the block.

**Advantage:** Simple hardware design — only a status register and a data register are required, with no DMA or interrupt controller. The logic is easy to implement and predict.

**Disadvantage:** The CPU is fully occupied for the entire duration of the I/O transfer. It cannot perform any other useful work while waiting for slow devices, leading to severe CPU wastage (CPU utilization can drop to nearly 0% for slow devices like printers).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**Compare Interrupt-Driven I/O and DMA in terms of CPU involvement and data transfer speed.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU involvement per word** | CPU executes one ISR per word | CPU not involved per word |
| **CPU free time** | Between data words | During entire block transfer |
| **Suitable device speed** | Medium (keyboards, serial) | High (disks, NICs, GPUs) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Interrupt controller + ISR | DMA controller + bus arbitration |
| **Data transfer speed** | Slower (per-word interrupt overhead) | Faster (block transfer by DMAC) |

**Conclusion:** DMA achieves higher throughput because it eliminates the per-word interrupt overhead. The CPU is freed to execute other programs while the DMAC handles the bulk transfer. Interrupt-driven I/O is more responsive for low-latency, low-volume transfers.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**a)** Explain the three main data transfer techniques between the CPU and I/O devices with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A computer system has the following I/O characteristics:
- Block size $n = 2000$ words
- CPU time to handle one data word in programmed I/O: $T_p = 0.1\,\mu\text{s}$
- I/O device time per word: $T_s = 1\,\mu\text{s}$
- Interrupt service overhead per word: $T_o = 0.5\,\mu\text{s}$
- DMA setup time: $T_{setup} = 2\,\mu\text{s}$

Compute the total transfer time and CPU utilization for all three techniques. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Three Data Transfer Techniques:**

**(i) Programmed I/O:**
The CPU directly controls the I/O device. It executes a tight loop reading the device's status register. When the device indicates "ready," the CPU reads/writes the data register. The CPU is busy for the entire I/O transfer. No interrupts or DMA are involved.

```
START → Read STATUS → Ready? → No → (loop back)
                              ↓ Yes
                          Read/WRITE DATA
                              ↓
                       Block complete? → No → (back to Read STATUS)
                              ↓ Yes
                              END
```

**(ii) Interrupt-Driven I/O:**
The CPU issues an I/O command and returns to its main program. When the I/O device is ready, it raises an interrupt (IRQ). The CPU saves its state, runs the Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) to transfer one word, and returns to the main program. This repeats until the block is fully transferred.

```
CPU: Issue I/O → Main Program → IRQ? → Save state → Run ISR
                                                        ↓
                                                   Restore state
                                                        ↓
                                                  Main Program (loop)
```

**(iii) Direct Memory Access (DMA):**
The CPU programs the DMA Controller (DMAC) with the source, destination, and byte count, then returns to its main program. The DMAC requests the bus, performs the entire block transfer autonomously, and interrupts the CPU only when complete. The CPU is fully free during the transfer.

```
CPU: Program DMAC → Main Program → (DMAC transfers block) → Final IRQ
```

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $n = 2000$, $T_p = 0.1\,\mu\text{s}$, $T_s = 1\,\mu\text{s}$, $T_o = 0.5\,\mu\text{s}$, $T_{setup} = 2\,\mu\text{s}$.

**(i) Programmed I/O:**

Total transfer time:

$$T_{total} = n \cdot (T_p + T_s) = 2000 \cdot (0.1 + 1) = 2000 \cdot 1.1 = 2200\,\mu\text{s}$$

CPU utilization (busy the whole time):

$$U_{CPU} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

The CPU is fully occupied; no useful work can be done in parallel.

**(ii) Interrupt-Driven I/O:**

Total transfer time:

$$T_{total} = n \cdot (T_p + T_s + T_o) = 2000 \cdot (0.1 + 1 + 0.5) = 2000 \cdot 1.6 = 3200\,\mu\text{s}$$

CPU utilization (time on user program):

$$U_{eff} = \frac{T_p}{T_p + T_s + T_o} = \frac{0.1}{1.6} = 0.0625 = 6.25\%$$

Total CPU activity (including ISR):

$$U_{total} = \frac{T_p + T_o}{T_p + T_s + T_o} = \frac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.375 = 37.5\%$$

**(iii) DMA (Burst Mode):**

Total transfer time:

$$T_{total} = T_{setup} + n \cdot T_s = 2 + 2000 \cdot 1 = 2002\,\mu\text{s}$$

CPU utilization (only setup time):

$$U_{eff} = \frac{T_{setup}}{T_{total}} = \frac{2}{2002} \approx 0.001 = 0.1\%$$

**Comparative Summary:**

| Technique | $T_{total}$ ($\mu\text{s}$) | $U_{eff}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Programmed I/O | 2200 | 0% (wasteful polling) |
| Interrupt-Driven I/O | 3200 | 6.25% (some parallel work) |
| DMA | 2002 | 0.1% of time on I/O; rest free for user program |

**Observation:** DMA is **1.1× faster** than Programmed I/O and **1.6× faster** than Interrupt-Driven I/O. Most importantly, the CPU is freed for 99.9% of the DMA duration.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a DMA controller. Discuss the three modes of DMA transfer. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A system uses DMA to transfer 4096 bytes from an I/O device to memory. The DMA setup time is $10\,\mu\text{s}$. The DMA controller operates in cycle-stealing mode and requires $0.05\,\mu\text{s}$ per byte arbitration. The I/O device supplies data at $2\,\text{MB/s}$. Calculate:
- (i) Total transfer time.
- (ii) Effective data rate seen by memory.
- (iii) Percentage of CPU time lost to cycle stealing. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — DMA Controller Architecture and Working:**

**Architecture (Block Diagram):**

```
                ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
                │        DMA Controller           │
                │  ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐         │
   DREQ  ──────►│  │Control │  │ Status │         │
                │  │ Reg    │  │  Reg   │         │
                │  └────────┘  └────────┘         │
                │  ┌────────┐  ┌────────┐         │
                │  │  MAR   │  │  CR    │         │
                │  │Address │  │  Count │         │
                │  └────────┘  └────────┘         │
                └─────┬─────────────┬─────────────┘
                      │             │
                      ▼             ▼
                Address Bus     Data Bus
                      │             │
                      ▼             ▼
                   Memory         Memory
```

**Key Registers:**
- **MAR (Memory Address Register):** Holds the memory address of the next word to be transferred. Auto-incremented.
- **CR (Count Register):** Holds the number of words/bytes remaining. Auto-decremented.
- **Control Register:** Holds the transfer mode, channel select, and start/stop commands.
- **Status Register:** Indicates transfer in progress, completion, and errors.

**Working:**
1. CPU initializes the DMA controller by writing into MAR, CR, and Control Register.
2. CPU issues a START command and resumes its main program.
3. The I/O device asserts **DREQ** (DMA Request) when ready.
4. DMA controller asserts **HRQ** (Hold Request) to the CPU.
5. CPU finishes its current bus cycle and asserts **HLDA** (Hold Acknowledge), releasing the bus.
6. DMA controller performs the transfer (read from device → write to memory, or vice versa).
7. After each word, DMA decrements CR and increments MAR.
8. When CR = 0, DMA controller releases HRQ and interrupts the CPU to signal completion.

**Three Modes of DMA Transfer:**

**(i) Burst Mode (Block Transfer Mode):**
The DMA controller obtains the bus once and retains it for the entire block transfer. The CPU is **completely blocked** from accessing memory until the transfer finishes. This gives the highest throughput but the worst CPU performance during transfer.

**(ii) Cycle Stealing Mode:**
The DMA controller transfers **one word at a time**, releasing the bus to the CPU between words. The CPU can execute instructions between DMA transfers, but every DMA word "steals" one memory cycle from the CPU. This is a compromise between throughput and CPU availability.

**(iii) Transparent Mode:**
The DMA controller only performs a transfer when the CPU is performing an operation that does **not** use the memory bus (e.g., instruction decode, ALU operation). The CPU is unaffected in terms of instruction throughput, but the DMA transfer rate is limited by the CPU's non-memory cycles.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

**Given:** $n = 4096$ bytes, $T_{setup} = 10\,\mu\text{s}$, $T_{arb} = 0.05\,\mu\text{s}/\text{byte}$, Device rate $= 2\,\text{MB/s}$.

**Step 1 — Compute device service time per byte:**

$$T_s = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.5\,\mu\text{s}/\text{byte}$$

**Step 2 — Total transfer time (Cycle Stealing Mode):**

$$T_{total} = T_{setup} + n \cdot T_s + n \cdot T_{arb}$$

$$T_{total} = 10 + 4096 \cdot 0.5 + 4096 \cdot 0.05$$

$$T_{total} = 10 + 2048 + 204.8 = 2262.8\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Effective data rate seen by memory:**

$$\text{Rate}_{eff} = \frac{n}{T_{total}} = \frac{4096}{2262.8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.81 \times 10^6\,\text{bytes/s} = 1.81\,\text{MB/s}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage of CPU time lost to cycle stealing:**

CPU time lost = $n \cdot T_{arb} = 4096 \cdot 0.05 = 204.8\,\mu\text{s}$

$$\% \text{ lost} = \frac{204.8}{2262.8} \times 100 \approx 9.05\%$$

**Summary of Answers:**

| Sub-part | Answer |
|---|---|
| (i) Total transfer time | $2262.8\,\mu\text{s}$ |
| (ii) Effective data rate | $\approx 1.81\,\text{MB/s}$ |
| (iii) CPU time lost | $\approx 9.05\%$ |

**Interpretation:** Due to the arbitration overhead, the effective data rate (1.81 MB/s) is slightly less than the device's native rate (2 MB/s). The CPU loses about 9% of its memory cycles to cycle stealing, which is acceptable in most systems.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $T_p$ with $T_s$:** $T_p$ = CPU time per word (handling). $T_s$ = Device service time per word (waiting). Reversing them inverts the utilization formula.
> 2. **Forgetting interrupt overhead $T_o$:** Many students compute interrupt-driven I/O time as $n \cdot (T_p + T_s)$ instead of $n \cdot (T_p + T_s + T_o)$. This underestimates the time and inflates the throughput estimate.
> 3. **Mixing up CPU utilization definitions:** There are TWO definitions — (a) fraction of time the CPU is *active* (including ISR), and (b) fraction of time spent on the *user program*. The question wording determines which to use.
> 4. **Ignoring $T_{setup}$ in DMA problems:** Many students compute DMA time as $n \cdot T_s$ only. The setup time $T_{setup}$ is always present, even if small.
> 5. **Forgetting the final ISR in DMA:** A DMA transfer still ends with an interrupt to the CPU. Some students incorrectly say the CPU is "not involved at all."
> 6. **Mermaid / diagram mistakes in the exam:** Always label your flowcharts clearly. A diagram without labels loses easy marks. Use the standard I/O sub-system block diagram (CPU ↔ System Bus ↔ Device + Memory) — it appears in almost every I/O question.
> 7. **Not mentioning bus arbitration for DMA:** DMA requires the DMAC to take control of the system bus from the CPU via HOLD/HLDA or BR/BG signals. Skipping this in the answer loses 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Programmed I/O:** CPU polls the device status register in a tight loop. CPU is 100% busy. Best for very simple, low-speed devices. No hardware interrupt or DMA needed.
- **Interrupt-Driven I/O:** Device raises IRQ when ready. CPU runs ISR to transfer one word. CPU is free between transfers. Per-word overhead due to interrupt dispatch.
- **DMA:** DMAC handles entire block transfer. CPU is freed to execute other programs. Three modes: **Burst** (highest throughput, CPU blocked), **Cycle Stealing** (balanced), **Transparent** (CPU unaffected).
- **The progression Programmed → Interrupt → DMA** reflects an increase in hardware complexity but a massive increase in CPU availability and overall system throughput.
- **Key formula to memorize:**

$$T_{total}^{(PgmIO)} = n \cdot (T_p + T_s)$$

$$T_{total}^{(IntIO)} = n \cdot (T_p + T_s + T_o)$$

$$T_{total}^{(DMA)} = T_{setup} + n \cdot T_s$$

- **CPU utilization** measures the fraction of total time the CPU is busy. For DMA in burst mode, the CPU is essentially free (utilization for I/O $\approx 0$).
- **Latency vs Throughput:** Programmed I/O has the lowest latency (no setup), while DMA has the highest throughput. The choice depends on the application.
- **Cycle Stealing** is the most commonly used DMA mode in practice — it balances throughput with CPU availability.
- **DMA controller's key registers:** MAR, CR, Control, Status. Always mention these in block diagrams.
- **Bus arbitration signals:** HRQ (Hold Request from DMAC) and HLDA (Hold Acknowledge from CPU) — must be discussed in any DMA question.
- **Real-world mapping:** Keyboard = Interrupt I/O, Hard Disk = DMA, Simple switch/LED = Programmed I/O.
- **Modern I/O subsystems** (USB 3.0, NVMe, SATA, PCIe) all use DMA at their core. Understanding these three foundational techniques is essential for grasping modern computer architecture.
